facts: Santa Clara, California-based electric aircraft developer Archer Aviation announced last year plans to build a manufacturing facility in Covington with hundreds of jobs.
State and local officials have offered millions of dollars in tax incentives to Archer for the project.
The incentives include a $3 million grant for machinery and equipment, contingent on job creation.
Plans are underway to train people to work in the factory building a new generation of aircraft.
The decision to choose Covington over 28 other sites in consideration around the country was led by Archer vice president of manufacturing Glen Burks.
Burks spent time in Marietta and Atlanta while in the Marine Corps.
Archer met with Newton County Industrial Development Authority officials two years ago during the site selection process.
Glen Burks stated that when he first sat down with Covington officials in August 2021, they were very excited about Archer.
The remarks were made during a luncheon at the Atlanta Aero Club in September.
Covington was one of three finalists for the Archer Aviation site, along with Tucson, Arizona, and the Alliance Airport in Fort Worth, Texas.
Elements that made Covington attractive included its location on an airport, affordability, and the town’s growth aspirations.
With plans to hire hundreds of hourly workers for the factory, Burks prioritized a low cost of living due to previous experiences with high housing costs in Maryland.
Georgia and local officials are eager for projects like Archer, which was code-named Project Jane early on.
Georgia Department of Economic Development global commerce division director Bob Kosek stated that the state and Gov. Brian Kemp have identified e-mobility as a critical industry to Georgia’s future.
Kosek noted that Georgia is creating the jobs of tomorrow by building on existing industries, including aerospace.
Kosek called Archer a natural fit for the community’s tech ecosystem, which includes Meta, Rivian, and Takeda.
Archer’s most important goal is working toward certification of its Midnight aircraft in the new electric Vertical Takeoff and Landing (eVTOL) category.
The certification process is expected to stretch into 2025.
Archer aims to get a production certificate to open the Covington facility in the middle of next year and begin building aircraft on an assembly line.
The first six to eight aircraft are being produced at San Jose for testing.
Employees working on the initial aircraft will move to Covington when the new plant opens.
These employees are being trained with a three-week curriculum developed in partnership with Georgia Piedmont Technical College.
In the first few years, Archer plans to ramp up to a few hundred workers at the Covington facility.
The workforce is expected to grow to about 650 workers by 2027.
Employment could reach as many as 1,500 workers by 2030.
Archer plans to help develop a certificate program at Georgia Piedmont Technical College to train an eVTOL manufacturing workforce.
The company will also use the state’s workforce development program Georgia Quick Start.

Chief people officer Tosha Perkins stated she does not plan to list job openings until sometime in the first half of next year when the company is confident it will get the production certificate around June.
Perkins noted that attracting people to Covington for an entirely new industry may be difficult due to its small size.
It will be important for the community to add enough housing for hundreds more workers and ensure the area is alluring enough to draw people willing to move there and raise a family.
Perkins stated that Covington is not yet built up in a way where it could currently support that influx of residents.
source_title: Electric Air Taxi Company Sets Sights on Georgia Base

facts: Proterra was selected by the Georgia Department of Administrative Services as a vendor to supply zero-emission battery-electric buses and charging stations for its Statewide Contract for Public Mass Transit & Transportation.
The state of Georgia currently operates over 1,182 buses that service customers with nearly 150 million trips per year.
Proterra was chosen by the State of Georgia as part of a “pool of providers” alongside other manufacturers to streamline local transit agencies’ access to vendors and vehicles.
Local transit agencies can now purchase Proterra zero-emission buses and charging stations through the state’s pre-established contract, bypassing future RFPs.
The contract represents Proterra’s growing foothold across the southeast region.
Prior to this award, Proterra had already signed contracts with transit agencies in Alabama, Florida, South Carolina, North Carolina, and Tennessee.
Proterra’s configurable EV platform includes adaptable battery and charging options suited for a wide range of transit and campus routes.
The Proterra Catalyst E2 series achieves an efficiency rating of 22 MPGe.
Conventional diesel buses average 3.86 MPG, compared to the Proterra Catalyst E2 series.
Proterra utilizes a lightweight carbon-fiber-reinforced composite bus body instead of industry-standard steel frames to maximize vehicle life and minimize repairs and maintenance.
Proterra offers a suite of high power DC fast chargers that comply with EV industry technology standards including J1772 CCS, J3105, and OCPP 1.6.
Proterra’s charging systems support bi-directional, vehicle-to-grid (V2G) power flow to maximize energy management capabilities and help transit agencies achieve 100 percent electric bus fleets.
Ryan Popple, CEO of Proterra, stated that implementing this transportation initiative is a huge win for Proterra, Georgia state institutions, and EV adoption in the U.S.
Ryan Popple also expressed eagerness to support the state of Georgia’s efforts to accelerate the adoption of emission-free mass transit.
Proterra designs and manufactures zero-emission heavy-duty vehicles to help bus fleet operators reduce operating costs while delivering clean transportation.
The company has sold more than 645 vehicles to 70 different municipal, university, airport, federal, and commercial transit agencies across 33 U.S. states and Canadian provinces.
Proterra’s configurable Catalyst platform is capable of serving the full daily mileage needs of nearly every transit route on a single charge.
Proterra products are designed, engineered, and manufactured in America.
Proterra maintains offices in Silicon Valley, South Carolina, and Los Angeles.
The 2018 market shift in cost and performance for electric buses enabled municipal, airport, university, federal, commercial, and utility transit agencies to adopt electric solutions.
Proterra's electric buses outperform internal combustion diesel or CNG engines in efficiency, reliability, acceleration, hill climb, and passenger-carrying power.
source_title: PROTERRA SELECTED IN ELECTRIC BUS CONTRACT BY GEORGIA DEPARTMENT OF ADMINISTRATIVE SERVICES

facts: Georgia will soon be one of the leading states in the burgeoning electric vehicle market, with gigantic investments by Rivian and SK Battery America.
The two EV companies also will be virtual neighbors, their plants about an hour’s drive apart on the outskirts of metro Atlanta.
But one of those projects almost didn’t happen, even though it was much further along. And it’s unclear how closely the two companies will work together when they open for business.
A legal dispute between Korean rivals SK and LG Energy Solution nearly scuttled SK’s $2.6 billion battery plant in Commerce that’s already under construction.
The two sides settled their dispute earlier this year but not before state officials became so worried that Gov. Brian Kemp, a Republican, asked Democratic President Joe Biden for help.
source_title: Electric vehicle maker Rivian and SK battery supplier will be Georgia neighbors but not necessarily partners.

facts: SK Battery America laid off 958 employees on Friday.
The layoffs represent about 37% of the Georgia plant's workforce.
The Georgia plant's total workforce was 2,566 people prior to the reductions.
The factory is located in Commerce, Ga.
The plant supplied cells for Ford Motor Co.’s now-canceled electric F-150 Lightning pickup truck.
The plant also supplies battery cells for Volkswagen AG and Hyundai Motor Co.

SK Battery America stated it reduced its workforce to align operations with market conditions but remains committed to Georgia and building a robust U.S. supply chain for advanced battery manufacturing.
SK is currently pursuing customers in the automotive and stationary storage sectors.
The layoffs reflect broader struggles in the U.S. EV market as EV sales slow nationally.
Elimination of federal EV tax credits and waning consumer demand have squeezed profitability for South Korean battery manufacturers.
SK Innovation stated that the end of the U.S. federal tax credit for EV buyers ate into profitability in the fourth quarter of 2025 despite increased shipments to Europe.

The company cited President Trump's elimination of purchase subsidies and rollback of fuel economy and emissions standards as factors in the unraveling of the EV transition in key markets such as the U.S.
SK has a second battery plant in Georgia that will supply Hyundai and is expected to start production in the first half of 2026.
SK's other plant in Tennessee, formerly part of a joint venture with Ford, isn’t expected to start production until 2028.
The Tennessee plant could supply both automotive and stationary storage cells.
source_title: South Korean EV battery maker SK lays off 958 workers in Georgia - Los Angeles Times

facts: Over $27.3 billion in e-mobility investments have been announced in Georgia since 2018.
Georgia has emerged as the undisputed leader in electric vehicle manufacturing investment across the United States.
Major players like Hyundai Motor Group, Kia, and Rivian have chosen Georgia as their home base for electric vehicle production.
The investments create opportunities in battery technology, charging infrastructure, and advanced manufacturing processes.
Manufacturing is Georgia's fourth-largest and sixth fastest-growing industry.
There is a projected 10% growth in manufacturing jobs over the next 10 years (till 2034).
The projected job growth encompasses traditional assembly roles to high-tech positions in quality control, process engineering, and advanced manufacturing systems management.
Georgia has an abundant workforce of approximately 289,000 production workers across multiple sectors.
These workers generate a $59.5 billion annual output, according to the Georgia Department of Economic Development.
This translates to over $205,000 in annual output per production worker.
The workforce diversity spans automotive assembly, aerospace components, food processing, textiles, and emerging technologies.
70% of occupations in the manufacturing industry require only some college or form of technical training.
Manufacturing careers offer average wages of $84,000 per worker.
Technical colleges, apprenticeship programs, and industry certifications provide direct routes into well-paying careers.
source_title: 10 Stats About Manufacturing in Georgia Over The Next Decade  - Unmudl

facts: Warner Norcross + Judd LLP provides proactive legal counsel to auto suppliers throughout Detroit, North America, and globally.
The firm adheres to a "Supplier First and Supplier Only" policy, meaning it does not represent original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) on supply chain disputes.
Warner has served as antitrust counsel for MEMA Original Equipment Suppliers (formerly OESA) for more than a decade.
The firm represents over 200 domestic and international auto suppliers conducting business in the U.S.
Tom Manganello serves as co-chair of the Warner automotive industry group and is active in the formation of MICHAuto.
Warner's attorneys handle motor vehicle safety regulations, including TREAD Act compliance, NHTSA reporting requirements, defect investigations, and recall requirements.
The firm provides legal analysis on EV industry developments, including updates titled "The Future is Electric, but What About the Charging Stations?" and "ITC Backs LG Chem in EV Battery Dispute".
Warner addresses supplier risks related to prototype testing, citing content on "Tesla Model 3: The Decision to Skip Prototype Testing Means Added Risk for Suppliers".
The firm tracks tariff impacts on the auto industry, including IEEPA tariff refund litigation, Section 301 tariffs, and Section 232 calculations.
Warner handles cybersecurity and privacy issues affecting connected vehicles and supply chain data breaches.
The firm provides legal services for automotive supplier bankruptcy, commercial litigation, contract drafting, and dispute resolution.
Warner's attorneys advise on environmental compliance including air quality regulations (NSR, PSD, NSPS, NESHAPs), brownfield redevelopment, hazardous waste management, and Superfund litigation.
The firm assists with immigration issues for multinational auto suppliers, including visas for executives, managers, and high-tech workers.
Warner hosts or participates in industry events including the "Georgia Automotive Supplier Regional Meeting".
The firm provides legal counsel on mergers, acquisitions, divestitures, joint ventures, and international business transactions for automotive clients.
Warner handles product liability litigation, class-action defense, breach of warranty, and lemon law cases involving automotive suppliers.
The firm offers services in construction, real estate acquisition, financing, zoning, condemnation, tax abatement, and equipment asset purchase for auto industry clients.
Warner provides IT procurement support, including software audits and contract processes for automotive suppliers.
The firm addresses labor and union issues, collective bargaining, strikes, picket line activity, wage and hour issues, and employee benefits for automotive employers.
Warner handles intellectual property matters such as patents, trademarks, copyrights, and infringement litigation for automotive suppliers.
The firm provides legal updates on autonomous vehicles, automated and connected mobility, and cybersecurity risks in the supply chain.
source_title: Automotive Attorneys - Warner Norcross + Judd LLP

facts: Hyundai Mobis will invest $926 million in an EV Power Electric system plant in Bryan County.
The new plant is expected to hire and employ at least 1,500 people.
This investment expands Hyundai's current operations which currently employ almost 1,200 people.
Governor Brian Kemp announced the job creation for the state.

Governor Brian Kemp stated that celebrating the groundbreaking of Hyundai’s new electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facility in Bryan County showed it would unleash transformational job creation and investment in that region.

Governor Brian Kemp noted this is the second supplier announced in just two weeks to locate in that area, expressing excitement for its impact on surrounding communities and other upcoming job creators.
The EV Power Electric system plant will increase Hyundai Mobis's geographic reach in Georgia.
Hyundai Mobis serves as the primary Tier 1 supplier to Kia Georgia.
Hyundai Mobis has operated a manufacturing facility with almost 1,200 workers in West Point since 2009.
The West Point facility provides bumper assemblies, chassis modules, and whole cockpit modules to the car industry.
H.S. Oh, Vice President of Hyundai Mobis Electric Powertrain Business Unit, stated the investment reflects an acceleration in the development of the EV supply chain in Georgia’s auto industry.
H.S. Oh stated Hyundai Mobis will be a major production player in the EV market, which will trigger more growth within the sector.
H.S. Oh stated Mobis looks forward to providing high-quality work opportunities to the growing local workforce.

At full capacity, the 1,200,000-square-foot Hyundai Metaplant facility will supply the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) factory in Bryan County, the Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama (HMMA) factory in Montgomery, and the Kia Georgia plant with over 900,000 EV Power Electric systems each year.
At full capacity, the facility will also supply over 450,000 Integrated Charging Control Units each year to those same factories.
The new factory will be built in Richmond Hill’s Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Certified Belfast Commerce Park.
Construction is scheduled to start in January 2023.
Manufacturing at the new facility will start in 2024.
Jon Seagraves, Development Authority of Bryan County Chairman, stated they could not be more excited to welcome Mobis to their community.
Jon Seagraves cited the close proximity of Belfast Commerce Park to available housing in nearby Heartwood, a top-performing public school system, interstate access, and rail as site advantages.
Jon Seagraves noted the site was purchased about two years ago with the potential to attract a special project.
Jon Seagraves highlighted nearly $1 billion of investment and the creation of over 1,500 good-paying jobs as reasons Mobis is special.
Jon Seagraves expressed gratitude to Bryan County, the City of Richmond Hill, and Raydient Places for their support and partnership on the project.
The Development Authority of Bryan County, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia Power teamed up on this competitive project.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team represented the state, with Alyce Thornhill as director of project implementation and supplier strategy.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated suppliers like Hyundai Mobis are transitioning to the jobs of the future and becoming part of Georgia’s growing EV ecosystem.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson noted that in May they predicted non-affiliated HMGMA suppliers would invest an additional $1 billion into Georgia, a prediction already blown past in about two weeks.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson expressed excitement to prepare Georgia’s workforce of tomorrow for the jobs being created now with support from partners like Georgia Quick Start and sister agencies.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson congratulated Bryan County, the City of Richmond Hill, the Georgia Ports Authority, and Hyundai Mobis.
Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric mobility industry by building on the strengths that the automotive sector offers.
In Georgia, more than 18,100 jobs have been announced as part of more than 30 EV-related projects that have invested over $13.3 billion since 2020.
In Bryan County, the first dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility of Hyundai Motor Group — Metaplant America — was fully opened on October 25.
Off-site suppliers involved in the Metaplant America project are expected to make about $1 billion in investments in other areas across the state.
Georgia has positioned itself to be a major player in electric vehicles by recruiting at least 51 EV and electric vehicle battery plants so far with billions in incentives in recent years.
Hyundai Mobis will probably be eligible for various tax credits depending on its new jobs produced and other investments, but specific information on potential incentives was not provided.
Georgia is home to more than 800 aerospace companies, including Archer Aviation, which will provide over 1,000 jobs.

facts: Rivian will hold a groundbreaking ceremony for its $5 billion manufacturing facility in Georgia.
Company executives, Governor Brian Kemp, state and local officials, and community leaders will attend the ceremony at the 2,000-acre site in southern Morgan and Walton counties.
The facility was first announced in late 2021.
The project represents the single-largest economic development investment in Georgia’s history.
The plant is expected to create 7,500 jobs once fully operational.
The project was originally slated to open in 2024 but will now be developed in two phases.
Vehicles are expected to begin rolling off assembly lines in 2028.
Once complete, the plant will be capable of producing up to 400,000 vehicles annually.

Governor Brian Kemp stated that Georgia will be home to Rivian's largest manufacturing facility, calling it the largest investment in state history and a key factor establishing the Peach State's leading role in the automotive industry for generations.
Rivian leaders describe the Georgia plant as a critical step in broadening the company’s consumer base by introducing EVs at lower price points.
The project underscores Georgia’s rise as a national leader in EV and battery sector growth, fueled by Governor Kemp’s focus on innovation and electric mobility.
source_title: Rivian Breaks Ground on $5 Billion Manufacturing Plant in Georgia | ASSEMBLY

facts: VPS, in partnership with Kia Georgia, completed a large-scale hail protection and solar canopy system at Kia’s West Point manufacturing facility.
The hail protection system covers 3.2 million square feet of automotive protection.
The solar canopy system provides 10 MW of solar capacity to strengthen resilience and sustainability at the plant.
On Feb. 24, 2026, Kia Georgia team members marked the start of production of the all-new 2027 Kia Telluride at its West Point facility.
The ceremonial 2027 Telluride vehicle represented the plant’s 5 millionth vehicle produced.
The 2027 Telluride is a hybrid electric vehicle (HEV), marking Georgia’s first-ever HEV production at the facility.
Following a $217 million expansion, Kia Georgia began assembling the all-electric 2025 EV6 SUV.
The EV6 is the plant’s second electric vehicle and fifth overall vehicle currently being assembled in West Point, Georgia.
The announcement regarding the EV6 assembly was made at the 2024 Los Angeles Auto Show on Nov. 21, 2024.
Kia Georgia began production on Nov. 16, 2009.
Since beginning operations, Kia Georgia has assembled more than 4.5 million vehicles.
Kia Georgia has helped create more than 14,000 jobs in the region.
On Nov. 20, 2024, Kia Georgia commemorated 15 years of manufacturing in Georgia.
The 2025 Kia EV9 is the first electric vehicle assembled in the state of Georgia.
The EV9 joins the Telluride, Sorento, and Sportage SUVs currently assembled at Kia’s West Point facility.
On May 30, 2024, Kia Georgia celebrated a historic milestone at its West Point assembly plant with the introduction of the EV9.
The Sportage became the fourth model produced by Kia Georgia, joining the Telluride, Sorento, and K5 to expand the plant’s product lineup.
On Feb. 1, 2022, Kia Georgia officially began production of the all-new 2023 Sportage subcompact SUV at its West Point facility.
On Dec. 17, 2021, Kia Georgia announced the retirement of President & CEO Jangsoo (Jason) Shin, effective Dec. 31, 2021.
Shin led the Georgia facility since 2017 and oversaw the inaugural launches of the Telluride and K5 models.
On Aug. 20, 2021, Kia Georgia reopened its online application process for hourly production positions.
This reopening marked the first time direct hire hourly production positions were available since 2011.
source_title: News - Kia Georgia

facts: Georgia State Transportation Board awarded US$24.4 million in federal funding to establish 26 new EV fast-charging stations across the state.
This marks the second round of deployment for the US National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure (NEVI) programme.
The funding announcement was made at the board's 20 November meeting.
The funding will support the construction and operation of fast-charging stations through public-private partnerships.
Six awardees were selected to design, install, finance, operate and maintain the stations for at least five years.
The second round of the procurement process focuses on closing strategic gaps identified after the initial 2024 awards.
The Georgia Department of Transportation identified 33 potential locations and received 41 proposals for 26 of these sites.
Selection criteria included site readiness, charging reliability and community impact.
The NEVI programme is funded through the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act and allocated US$5 billion nationwide.
Georgia received approximately US$135 million from the NEVI programme allocation.
NEVI funds cover up to 80% of project costs.
Georgia's multi-year plan focuses on rural and underserved areas where private investment in EV charging infrastructure has been lagging.
The state's NEVI deployment strategy prioritises corridor charging along major highways while addressing coverage gaps that could limit long-distance EV travel.
The 26 new stations will complement existing charging networks and support the state's goal of establishing reliable charging access every 50 miles along designated corridors.
Selected locations span multiple regions, with installations planned to begin in 2025.
Each station will feature multiple DC fast chargers capable of serving various EV models and charging standards.
The public-private partnership model ensures long-term operation and maintenance while leveraging private sector expertise in charging technology deployment.
Georgia Power has been piloting vehicle-to-grid technology and expanding EV charging capabilities as part of broader grid modernisation efforts.
Georgia Power's pilot programmes have tested bidirectional charging technology and smart grid integration, providing valuable data for scaling the deployment of EV infrastructure across the state.
source_title: Georgia awards US$24.4M for EV charging infrastructure

facts: Greentec Recycling operates a hybrid and EV battery recycling facility located at 6594 GA-42, Rex, GA 30273.
The facility provides nickel-metal hydride (NiMH) battery recycling services in Atlanta, GA.
The facility provides lithium-ion battery recycling services in Atlanta, GA.
The facility offers high voltage EV and hybrid battery drop-off services in Atlanta, GA.
Greentec Recycling serves businesses and individual electric vehicle owners seeking safe disposal of hybrid and EV batteries in Georgia.

The company's service areas in Georgia include Atlanta, Columbus, Savannah, Augusta, Athens, Acworth, Albany, Alpharetta, Americus, Brookhaven, Canton, Carrollton, Douglasville, Duluth, Dunwoody, East Point, Evans, Forest Park, Gainesville, Griffin, Hinesville, Johns Creek, Kennesaw, LaGrange, Mableton, Marietta, Milton, Newnan, Peachtree City, Peachtree Corners, Pooler, Redan, Rome, Roswell, Sandy Springs, Smyrna, South Fulton, Statesboro, Stockbridge, Stonecrest, Sugar Hill, St. Marys, Thomasville, Tucker, Union City, Valdosta, Warner Robins, and Woodstock.
Greentec Recycling specializes in green recycling methods, responsible disposal, and national battery recycling for Georgia-based clients.
The company uses safe, effective, and eco-friendly recycling and remanufacturing methods to reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
Greentec Recycling recovers valuable materials such as nickel-metal hydride, lithium, and other metals from hybrid and EV batteries.
Recovering these materials reduces the need for virgin materials and helps combat climate change.
The facility helps businesses meet all vehicle battery compliance requirements while protecting the environment.
Greentec Recycling offers fair compensation for spent hybrid and EV batteries to commercial clients.
The company processes nickel-metal hydride and lithium-ion battery packs from Toyota Prius and Ford Escape hybrid vehicles.
The recycling process includes disassembling EV battery packs from Tesla, Nissan Leaf, and Chevy Bolt models to recover valuable battery cells.
Greentec Recycling handles end-of-life large-format lithium batteries from industrial and energy storage applications.
The company supports the industry's move toward reuse and second-life applications for hybrid and EV batteries.
Greentec Recycling operates a nationwide network that spans North America while supporting local communities across Georgia.
The facility ensures safe, sustainable recycling practices for all types of lithium-ion batteries from plug-in hybrids and electric vehicles across the United States, including Georgia.
source_title: Hybrid and EV Battery Recycling in Georgia

facts: Georgia Tech researchers have discovered a novel iron chloride (FeCl3) cathode material designed to improve lithium-ion battery performance and reduce costs for EVs and large-scale energy storage systems.
Commercial lithium-ion batteries predominantly use combustible organic electrolytes and rare metal cathodes, which risk safety and sustainability while driving up costs.
Batteries account for about 50% of an electric vehicle's total cost, primarily due to reliance on expensive materials like nickel and cobalt.
Nickel and cobalt pose environmental and supply chain concerns.
The new FeCl3 cathode uses only 1-2% of traditional cathode materials like cobalt and nickel while offering comparable energy storage capacity.

Combined with a solid halide electrolyte and lithium-indium alloy anode, the material creates a fully solid-state battery that offers higher energy density, increased safety, and reduced leakage risks over conventional liquid-electrolyte Li-ions.
Early tests reveal the cathode performs as well as or better than existing commercial cathodes.
The development reduces total battery system costs by 30-40%.
The FeCl3 cathode shows stable voltage plateaus and maintains 83% capacity after 1000 cycles, with 99.95% Coulombic efficiency.
The cathode has shown operational voltages superior to commonly used lithium-iron phosphate, delivering higher electrical force and battery efficiency.
Lead researcher Hailong Chen states the technology could be ready for commercial use in electric vehicles in less than five years.
The team remains focused on laboratory refinement, including a better understanding of the underlying mechanisms and further perfecting the material.
source_title: Can an Iron-Chloride Cathode Cut EV Battery Costs? - Tech Insights

facts: Graphite is in virtually all EV batteries.
Chicago-based Anovion Technologies is opening the largest graphite production facility in North America.
Anovion is initially investing $800 million to open a 1.5 million-square-foot graphite production factory.
The factory will be located in Bainbridge, Decatur County, Georgia.
The new facility will create around 400 jobs.
This new expansion facility is the first of this scale for Anovion.
The factory will produce 40,000 metric tonnes per year of synthetic graphite anode material for lithium-ion batteries.
Anovion chose Bainbridge because of its existing and planned low- and carbon-free energy sources for power.
Anovion chose Bainbridge because of short supply chains.
Anovion chose Bainbridge because of access to existing rail infrastructure.
Eric Stopka is the chief executive officer of Anovion.
Eric Stopka stated that existing infrastructure makes Southwest Georgia an attractive location for Anovion’s new facility.
Eric Stopka noted that proximity to other battery and EV manufacturing plants will allow Anovion to further reduce its carbon footprint.
Chip Dunn is the executive chairman of Anovion.
Chip Dunn noted that Anovion is working to secure a domestic source for critical materials required for US lithium-ion battery production.
In October 2022, Anovion was awarded a $117 million grant from the US Department of Energy.
The grant was awarded under the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law to help develop the domestic battery manufacturing supply chain.
Anovion plans to expand its hydro-powered manufacturing facility near Niagara Falls, New York.
The Niagara Falls facility is one of the only qualified US sources of battery-grade synthetic graphite that’s commercially shipping product today.
source_title: North America’s largest graphite factory is launching in Georgia

facts: Mechanical engineering graduate researcher Michael Lee is part of a team at Georgia Institute of Technology rethinking an essential battery component by reshaping rubber.
The polymer acts as a new structure for the battery transport solution known as the electrolyte.
The Georgia Tech-developed electrolytes are synthesized from a liquid precursor solution consisting of plastic and salt monomers that bond to form a rubber polymer.
Through heat, the rubber transforms into what the researchers refer to as a "3D interconnected plastic crystal phase."
When the monomers turn to polymers, the plastic crystals "coarsen" within a rubber matrix, enabling the three-dimensional shape.
Lee worked alongside Georgia Tech Professor Seung Woo Lee in the development effort.
The rubber-based battery components include a lithium metal anode, rubber electrolyte, and a lithium nickel manganese cobalt oxide cathode.
The rubber electrolyte is mainly composed of lithium salt, an elastomer known as poly (butyl acrylate), and a plastic crystal called succinonitrile.
The unique structure has resulted in high ionic conductivity, improved mechanical properties, and electrochemical stability.
These three traits support the advancement of the electric vehicle.
Electric vehicles typically use lithium-ion batteries where ions are moved by a liquid electrolyte.
When damaged, conventional flammable electrolytes present a fire hazard.
Safety issues with lithium-ion batteries have led to alternative ideas like solid-state batteries that use rubber materials.
Lee and the team's rubber electrolyte can be made at low-temperature conditions, generating robust and smooth interfaces on the surface of electrodes.
The structure of the rubber electrolytes prevents damaging lithium dendrite growth and allows for faster moving ions.
This enables reliable operation of solid-state batteries even at room temperature.
Michael Lee stated that higher ionic conductivity means more ions can move at the same time.
Lee noted that increasing specific energy and energy density of these batteries can increase the mileage of electric vehicles.
Conventional solid-polymer electrolytes have been limited due to low ionic conductivity at room temperature and insufficient mechanical stability.
Rubber has not previously been considered as an electrolyte for lithium batteries due to its low ionic conductivity.
The team hypothesized that the 3D-structured rubber electrolyte simultaneously enabled the use of rubber as an electrolyte material and overcame challenges of conventional solid polymer electrolytes.
Lee described the selection of conventional materials but noted it is an unconventional material choice for battery applications.
The team believes the rubber is highly possible to be employed in current manufacturing processes.
Solid electrolytes are unable to stretch and bend due to brittleness, whereas rubber has superior mechanical stability.
Experimental data shows the rubber electrolyte can be extended up to almost 300% from its original state.

This mechanical property is a key factor for operating next-generation lithium batteries, including lithium metal batteries that replace graphite anodes with lithium metal anodes to increase energy density.
Conventional organic liquid electrolytes easily catch fire, leading to battery explosions.
Rubber electrolytes are safer due to their flame retardancy.
The rubber electrolyte-based lithium metal batteries can exhibit high energy density exceeding 410 Watt-hours per kilogram (Wh/kg electrode+electrolyte).
The batteries maintained a high energy density of 235 Wh/kg at a power density of 184 Wh/kg.
If commercialized, EVs with rubber electrolyte-based Li batteries could travel over 490 miles on a single charge compared to 310 miles for EVs with conventional Li-ion batteries.
Large-scale production of rubber electrolytes needs to be investigated at a manufacturing level to become a mainstream part of battery technology.
source_title: A Battery Breakthrough, Made from Rubber - Tech Briefs

facts: A Belgian company is expanding its operations in Augusta by building a new electric vehicle battery component factory.
Solvay Specialty Polymers announced Thursday it will build a plant next to its existing Augusta factory.
The new plant will manufacture EV-grade binders and separator coatings.
Sen Raphael Warnock announced the project in a press release.
The parts produced by the factory are critical components within the lithium-ion batteries that power EVs.
Warnock credited a $178 million federal grant awarded to Solvay for the new factory.
Warnock is a Democrat in the middle of a re-election campaign against Republican Herschel Walker.
In a prior release, Warnock stated he was proud to fight for and help secure the $178 million needed for the new facility.
Warnock pledged to continue working closely with businesses looking to start or expand in Georgia to secure critical investments that will help create local, good-paying jobs.
source_title: Georgia lands EV battery parts plant after company gets $178M grant

canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution Joint Venture
facts: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution announced Thursday they will build a $4.3 billion electric battery plant as part of Hyundai's new electric vehicle assembly plant in southeast Georgia.
The companies will split the investment for the plant.
Production is slated to start as soon as late 2025.

Hyundai Motor Co. CEO Jaehoon Chang stated the battery plant would “create a strong foundation to lead the global EV transition” and explained the company wants to speed up efforts to produce electrified Hyundai and Kia vehicles in North America.
Chang added that “Hyundai Motor Group is focusing on its electrification efforts to secure a leadership position in the global auto industry.”
In 2022, Hyundai announced it would invest $5.5 billion to assemble electric vehicles and batteries in Ellabell, just west of Savannah.
The Ellabell site is supposed to have 8,100 employees and is slated to begin producing vehicles in 2025.
Garrison Douglas, spokesperson for Republican Gov. Brian Kemp, confirmed the 3,000-job battery plant would be part of the 8,100 overall jobs.
The $4.3 billion investment will be part of the previously announced $5.5 billion total.
The Hyundai/LG plant is supposed to be able to supply batteries for 300,000 electric vehicles per year, matching the initial projected production of the adjoining vehicle assembly plant.
Hyundai has said the Georgia plant could later expand to build 500,000 vehicles annually.

Gov. Brian Kemp stated: “This is exactly what we envisioned when Georgia landed the Hyundai Metaplant in May of last year, and this project is the latest milestone in Georgia's path to becoming the EV capital of the nation.”
In addition to the assembly and battery plants, auto parts suppliers have pledged to invest more than $2 billion and hire 4,800 people in the region around the Hyundai site.
The announcements are part of an electric vehicle and battery land rush across the United States.

Under the U.S. Inflation Reduction Act, EVs must be assembled in North America, and a certain percentage of their battery parts and minerals must come from North America or a U.S. free trade partner to qualify for a full $7,500 EV tax credit.
Currently, no Hyundai or Kia vehicles are eligible for the tax credit.

Democratic U.S. Sen. Jon Ossoff of Georgia stated: “The IRA’s manufacturing incentives continue to bring jobs and investment to Georgia,” and added, “My goal remains to make Georgia the world leader in advanced energy production.”

LG said this would be its seventh battery plant in operation or under construction in the U.S., concentrating efforts to expand production in the country as an example of how federal incentives are luring manufacturers.
This is the second huge electric battery plant Hyundai is partnering to build in Georgia.
Hyundai and SK On, a unit of South Korea's SK Group, announced in December they would jointly invest $4 billion to $5 billion to build a new plant northwest of Atlanta.
The SK On plant in Cartersville is planned to begin production in 2025 and employ a projected 3,500 people.
Hyundai will need batteries for more than just vehicles made in Ellabell.
The company is already assembling electric vehicles at its plant in Montgomery, Alabama.
In April, Hyundai announced it would start assembling its electric Kia EV9 large SUV at the Kia plant in West Point, Georgia.
Partnering with LG and SK will diversify Hyundai’s supplier base, giving the automaker more than one battery manufacturer from which to buy.
Because the battery plant is part of the overall Hyundai complex, Douglas said no additional incentives would be offered.
The state of Georgia and local governments have already pledged $1.8 billion in tax breaks and other incentives for the project.
Greg LeRoy, executive director of Good Jobs First, noted it is the largest subsidy package a U.S. state has ever promised an automotive plant.
source_title: Georgia: Hyundai and LG announce 3,000-job battery plant - WJCL

facts: Southeast light-duty EV sales grew by 38 percent from January 2024 through December 2024, outpacing the national year-over-year average growth rate for the first time.
Florida is No. 1 in the region in EV sales growth, at 42 percent, and for EV market share, at 9 percent.
The Sunshine State's EV market share is hovering just below the national EV market share average, which peaked above 10 percent in 2024, setting a market record.
Georgia and North Carolina lead the region in EV investment and jobs.
Tennessee and Alabama lag behind Florida, Georgia, and North Carolina in EV investment and jobs.

The region is divided between two groups: Florida, Georgia, and North Carolina enjoying strong EV sales and market share growth; while Tennessee, South Carolina, and Alabama continue to lag far behind.
EV growth in the region is self-reinforcing, as consumer familiarity from seeing EVs on the road and talking to owners or fleet operators increases purchase likelihood.
Southeastern states, local governments, investor-owned utilities (IOUs), and EV charging companies are continuing to deploy public charging infrastructure.
80 percent of EV charging happens conveniently and affordably at home at night while EV drivers sleep.

Public charging is critical for supporting long-distance travel, providing charging for EV-driving tourists, serving local drivers needing an extra boost, and supplementing the lack of residential charging at multi-family dwellings.
Georgia is No. 1 in the region in terms of the number of public EV chargers per capita.

Florida, Georgia, and North Carolina have historically topped the region in per-capita deployment for fast charging to support long-distance travel, on-route fleet vehicle charging, and EV rideshare drivers.
In the second half of 2024, Alabama added 300 fast chargers to move ahead of North Carolina and above the national average for fast charging per capita.
EV charging deployment and EV market growth go hand in hand, each benefiting the other.
States like Georgia and Florida, which lead regional EV market share, also lead in charging infrastructure deployment.
Alabama ranks last in EV market share but is a fast charging deployment surprise.
Southeastern IOUs have invested $435.5 million in EV infrastructure through 2024.
This utility investment represents approximately 6% of the nation’s IOU investments, despite the Southeast making up about 18% of the nation’s total population.
Utilities are not proposing EV programs at scale, choosing instead to propose small pilot programs.
When utilities propose more significant investments, regulators have often required them to scale back their efforts.
On a per capita basis, all of the Southeast’s IOUs are below the national average of $38 per customer in approved EV investments.
Duke Energy Florida and Florida Power and Light are close to the national average at $36 per customer, making Florida No. 1 in utility investment.
In the Southeast, the overwhelming majority of public EV funding comes from the federal government, with states appropriating next to none of their own taxpayer dollars.
South Carolina is No. 1 in public funding because it has drawn down $44.32 per capita in federal electric transportation funding, approximately 12 percent higher than the national average.
Combined public funding from state and federal sources averages $38.23 per capita in the Southeast, which is way behind the national average of $74.01.
Southeast states’ unwillingness to commit state-appropriate funding creates a reliance on the federal government’s support of the emerging EV market.

Political uncertainty surrounds whether the federal government will honor already committed funds to states to electrify school and transit buses, deploy fast charging along highway corridors, allow government fleets to take advantage of vehicle and infrastructure tax credits, and reimburse already committed grants.
This funding uncertainty will disproportionately impact Southeast states’ ability to support EV market growth.
The degree of impact remains unclear, but this uncertainty is particularly problematic in the Southeast because investments and jobs depend on a strong EV market to succeed.
Without strong demand for EVs, there is a risk that companies could shutter existing EV and battery plants and halt new plant construction, leaving workers without jobs.
The EV market remains strong moving into 2025, with January EV sales up over 30% year over year after a record-setting December.
January EV sales topped 100,000 units sold for the tenth consecutive month.
The rapidly expanding used EV market grew over 30% year over year, with nearly 27,000 used vehicles sold in January.
The used car market accounts for two-thirds of all vehicles consumers purchase.
Used EVs are as affordable as their gas-car counterparts.
Most new EVs purchased have been leased, which will result in over 1 million EVs coming off lease and into the used market over the next two years.
These factors will enable a new and broader demographic of consumers to enter the EV market and experience low operating costs and high-performance benefits of EV ownership.
Approximately 45% of car shoppers are considering buying an EV, with EV shopper demographics becoming more mainstream.
2025 is set to be the year consumer desire collides with political turbulence and rampant misinformation.
Uncertainty looms regarding legal battles against the federal government’s attempt to claw back electric transportation and clean energy funding.
Uncertainty exists about how tariffs and the threat of tariffs impact the auto sector, vehicle prices, and investments in EV manufacturing.
Questions remain about whether new and used EV tax credits will remain available for consumers and commercial fleets.
Uncertainty remains about whether growing consumer demand for EVs will cool.
2025 will be a telling year for the trajectory of the EV market.
source_title: Hellbender Press - Southeastern electric vehicle sales and investments arc against headwinds

canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group Georgia Electric Vehicle Plant
facts: A joint development authority near Savannah voted Tuesday afternoon to approve an “economic development agreement” for automotive giant Hyundai Motor Group’s planned electric vehicle factory.
Details of the agreement were not released, as it has not been signed by the state or Hyundai.
Officials say approving the agreement is a major step for the project that allows the company to begin making improvements to the roughly 3,000-acre property near the Georgia coast.
The project’s director said it’s a “daunting” schedule to prepare the $5.5 billion plant to open in 2025.
The project’s director stated the project is so far on track.
source_title: Hyundai EV plant and local development authority reach agreement

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America produced its first IONIQ 5 electric vehicle.
The facility is an electric vehicle assembly and battery plant located in Ellabell, Georgia.
Hyundai Motor Co. is investing $5.5 billion to build the facility near the port city of Savannah.
Production at the Georgia plant started on Oct 4, 2024.
The plant's electric vehicles will be available at dealerships by the end of the year (2024).
A Hyundai supplier plans an $11 million expansion in Georgia.
Hyundai is eyeing hybrid vehicle production in Georgia as EV demand wanes.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America produces first IONIQ 5 electric vehicle - Atlanta Business Chronicle

facts: The detention of 475 workers at Hyundai's Georgia manufacturing complex occurred on Thursday.
The raid targeted the construction site of an LG Energy Solution battery plant scheduled to open next year.
Federal authorities conducted an enforcement operation at the 3,000-acre site west of Savannah.
Agents focused specifically on the construction site for the battery plant.
The battery facility is designed to be a cornerstone of domestic EV battery production capacity.
The complex was touted by Gov. Brian Kemp and other officials as the biggest economic development project in Georgia's history.
The $7.6 billion investment represents Hyundai's largest manufacturing commitment outside of South Korea.
Hyundai is already producing the IONIQ 5 at the site.
Hyundai is preparing to manufacture the three-row IONIQ 9 at the site.
The complex spans nearly 3,000 acres and includes vehicle assembly, battery production, welding operations, and extensive logistics infrastructure.
The broader ecosystem is projected to generate nearly 40,000 jobs across Hyundai and its supplier network.
Operations at Hyundai's EV manufacturing plant were not interrupted according to company representatives.
A construction halt at the battery plant poses medium-term challenges because battery production is the most supply-constrained component of EV manufacturing.
The detention operation involved a variety of different companies at the site, including subcontractors.
Workforce disruption extends beyond direct Hyundai employees to the broader contractor ecosystem supporting the facility.
Many subcontractors likely serve multiple automotive projects, meaning the impact could ripple beyond just the Georgia plant.
The majority of those detained were Korean nationals, many of whom likely possess specialized technical expertise essential for advanced battery manufacturing processes.
The loss of this knowledge base could delay the transfer of critical manufacturing technologies from Korea to the United States.
The Korean Foreign Ministry dispatched officials to the site and expressed concerns about the treatment of Korean citizens.
Tensions with South Korea represent a significant complication for supply chain strategies focused on "friend-shoring" and reducing dependence on China.
EV battery production requires highly specialized facilities with precise environmental controls, advanced automation systems, and skilled technicians familiar with complex chemical processes.
Any delays in the LG Energy Solution plant could force automakers to maintain greater reliance on imported batteries or alternative suppliers.
This dependency undermines broader U.S. goals of reducing supply chain vulnerabilities in critical clean energy technologies.
The incident highlights the tension between immigration enforcement policies and supply chain resilience objectives for advanced manufacturing.
Advanced manufacturing increasingly depends on global talent mobility, particularly for emerging technologies where expertise remains concentrated in specific regions.
The raid occurs as the U.S. directs substantial resources toward domestic EV battery production through the Inflation Reduction Act and other policies.
Success in attracting skilled workers depends partly on the ability to retain them, including those from international partners.
Workforce considerations represent an often-overlooked dimension of supply chain risk at the intersection of immigration policy, skilled labor availability, and critical infrastructure development.
The resolution will likely influence how other international companies approach major U.S. manufacturing investments.
Balancing compliant operations with access to global talent pools necessary for advanced manufacturing represents an evolving challenge shaping supply chain strategies across multiple industries.
source_title: Immigration Raid at Hyundai E.V. Plant in Georgia - Centrum-AI

facts: Hundreds of federal agents descended on a sprawling site where Hyundai manufactures electric vehicles in Georgia and detained 475 people, most of them South Korean nationals.
The raid occurred on a Thursday and targeted a manufacturing site that state officials have long called Georgia’s largest economic development project.
The detainment of South Korean nationals is notable because they are rarely caught up in immigration enforcement compared to other nationalities.
Video released by U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement on Saturday showed federal agents directing workers to line up outside the site.
Some detainees were ordered to put their hands up against a bus while being frisked and shackled around their hands, ankles, and waist.
Other detainees had plastic ties around their wrists as they boarded a Georgia inmate-transfer bus.
South Korea’s Foreign Minister Cho Hyun stated on Saturday that more than 300 South Koreans were among the 475 people detained.

Some detainees worked for HL-GA Battery Co., a joint venture between Hyundai and LG Energy Solution slated to open next year, while others were employed by contractors and subcontractors at the construction site.

Lead Georgia agent Steven Schrank stated that some detained workers had illegally crossed the U.S. border, while others had legally entered but possessed expired visas or entered on a visa waiver prohibiting work.
An immigration attorney stated that two of his detained clients arrived from South Korea under a visa waiver program allowing tourism or business stays of 90 days or less without a visa.
Attorney Charles Kuck noted one client had been in the U.S. for a couple of weeks and another for about 45 days, with both planning to return home soon.
A detainee included a lawful permanent resident kept in custody due to a prior firearm and drug offense record that could jeopardize their status under the "crime of moral turpitude" rule.

U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement public affairs officer Lindsay Williams denied reports that U.S. citizens were detained, stating the agency has no authority over them once they identify themselves.
Hyundai Motor Company stated on Friday that none of its direct employees were detained and that it is reviewing supplier and subcontractor practices to ensure compliance with U.S. employment laws.
LG Energy Solution told the Associated Press it could not immediately confirm how many of its own employees or Hyundai workers were detained.
The South Korean government expressed "concern and regret" over the operation targeting its citizens and is sending diplomats to the site.

South Korean Foreign Ministry spokesperson Lee Jaewoong stated in a televised address that "the business activities of our investors and the rights of our nationals must not be unjustly infringed in the process of U.S. law enforcement."
Most detainees were transported to an immigration detention center in Folkston, Georgia, near the Florida state line.
None of the detainees have been charged with crimes yet, though the investigation remains ongoing.
Family members and friends struggled to locate or contact detained individuals, according to James Woo, communications director for Asian Americans Advancing Justice-Atlanta.
Many detainees' families remain in South Korea because the workers were in the U.S. primarily for business purposes.
The raid resulted from a months-long investigation into allegations of illegal hiring at the site.
Search warrants and affidavits requested employment records for current and former workers, timecards, and video/photos of workers.
Court records indicate prosecutors do not know who hired the individuals, with the U.S. Attorney’s Office stating the "actual company or contractor hiring the illegal aliens is currently unknown."
Hyundai Motor Group began manufacturing electric vehicles at its $7.6 billion Georgia plant approximately one year ago.
The site currently employs approximately 1,200 people in a largely rural area located about 25 miles (40 kilometers) west of Savannah.
Agents focused on an adjacent plant still under construction where Hyundai partners with LG Energy Solution to produce EV batteries.
The plant is located in Bryan County, which had a population of nearly 47,000 in 2023 after increasing by more than a quarter in the early 2020s.
Bryan County's Asian population grew from 1.5% in 2018 to 2.2% in 2023, primarily driven by people of Indian descent.
Lead agent Steven Schrank described the Georgia raid as the "largest single site enforcement operation" in his agency's two-decade history.
According to ICE data, only 46 Koreans were deported out of over 270,000 total removals during the 12-month period ending September 30, 2024.

Governor Kemp and other Georgia Republican officials, who previously courted Hyundai and celebrated the plant's opening, issued statements on Friday emphasizing that all state employers must follow the law.
The nonprofit Asian Americans Advancing Justice-Atlanta labeled the raid "unacceptable" in a joint statement.
The advocacy group's statement noted that targeted workers are "everyday people who are trying to feed their families, build stronger communities, and work toward a better future."
Sammie Rentz, who opened a supermarket less than 3 miles (4.8 kilometers) from the Hyundai site six months ago, reported sharp business declines and fears it may not recover.
Rentz expressed concern that Korean nationals might "cut and running, or starting an exit strategy" due to the raid's impact on their pride.
Ellabell resident Tanya Cox, living less than a mile from the site, stated she held no ill feelings toward Korean nationals or other immigrant workers.
Cox noted that few local neighbors were employed at the site and believed more battery plant construction jobs should have been allocated to local residents.
Cox stated, "I don’t see how it’s brought a lot of jobs to our community or nearby communities."
source_title: ICE raids Georgia Hyundai plant and detains 475 in massive operation | AP News

facts: Hyundai Motor Group promised its electric vehicle manufacturing facility outside Savannah would be the “factory of the future.”
On Wednesday, the South Korean automaker debuted what it calls the Hyundai Metaplant to the public in a scene straight out of a sci-fi.
Robots both big and small stamped, welded and assembled autos.
EVs moved about the shop floor on self-propelled dollies that use GPS and motion sensors for navigation.
Four-legged robots that resembled medium-sized dogs inspected the work.
Employees, known as Meta Pros, monitored equipment or performed tasks too tactile for the machines to handle.
The facility is located in Bryan County, Georgia.
Hyundai Motor Group is a top EV seller.
IONIQ 5 & 6 are mentioned.
source_title: Tomorrow’s manufacturing today: Inside Hyundai’s EV plant near Savannah

facts: A Massachusetts-based electric vehicle supplier has abandoned plans to build a manufacturing facility in Bulloch County.
Aspen Aerogels Inc. manufactures thermal barriers that insulate the batteries on electric cars from fires.
Aspen Aerogels announced on Thursday via an investor call that it will cancel its plan to build a second plant near Statesboro, Georgia.
Instead of the Statesboro facility, Aspen Aerogels will increase production capacity at its East Providence, R.I., plant.
Governor Brian Kemp originally announced the Statesboro project in 2022.
The original 2022 plan called for a $325 million investment in the Statesboro plant.
The project was expected to create more than 250 jobs.
Production at the Statesboro plant had initially been scheduled to begin in late 2023.

Aspen Aerogel’s chief financial officer and treasurer, Ricardo Rodriguez, stated that in early 2023 the company decided to "right-time the construction" of the planned second aerogel manufacturing facility in Statesboro, Georgia.
Following the decision to delay construction, the company ramped up its external manufacturing capacity.
Rodriguez noted that the external manufacturing model has fully demonstrated its ability to efficiently increase aerogel supply.
The company expressed confidence that a capital-light and modular capacity plan provides the most efficient path to creating value.
Demand for electric vehicles has not grown as much in recent years as EV manufacturers originally expected.
Slower EV demand is attributed to high sticker prices, an insufficient number of charging stations, and government policies that have discouraged car buyers from purchasing EVs.
The Trump administration recently froze a $5 billion program aimed at funding the construction of EV charging stations.
source_title: EV supplier cancels plan to build plant near Statesboro - The Current

facts: Hyundai Motor Group (HMG) and SK On selected a site in Bartow County for a new EV battery manufacturing facility to supply Hyundai’s EV plants across the country.
The battery plant will create more than 3,500 new jobs through an investment of $4 billion to $5 billion.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp stated that Hyundai Motor Group and SK On are valued partners and key players in the state’s ever-growing automotive industry.
Governor Kemp noted his administration has focused on bringing jobs and opportunity to communities across the state that may have been overlooked, adding that SK and HMG share this goal.
SK On was established just last year as the lithium-ion battery subsidiary of SK Innovation.
SK Innovation is South Korea’s largest energy company and currently employs more than 2,000 Georgians at a battery plant in Commerce.
The new facility in Bartow County is expected to begin operations in 2025.
Excluding this announcement, EV-related projects in Georgia since 2020 total about $17 billion in investment and account for more than 22,800 new jobs.

Georgia Commissioner of Economic Development Pat Wilson stated the state is creating a fully integrated supply chain for automotive manufacturers while connecting battery manufacturers with recyclers to close the loop on battery manufacturing.
Commissioner Wilson expressed excitement for the future jobs the project will create for Bartow County and Northwest Georgia.

The Georgia Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce Team partnered with Georgia EMC, the Development Authority of Bartow County, the Cartersville-Bartow County Department of Economic Development, and the Technical College System of Georgia’s Quick Start program to land the project.

The Hyundai EV plant being built near Savannah in Bryan County is the largest economic development project in Georgia history, a $5.5 billion investment expected to create 8,100 jobs when fully built out.
source_title: Electric vehicle battery manufacturer coming to Bartow County | Georgia Public Broadcasting

facts: EV charging company Electrify America operates solar-powered charging stations all across the United States.
Senior Director of Sales and Marketing Robert Barrosa states that cheaper cars hitting the market and more charging station installations are making access to electric vehicles easier than ever.
Robert Barrosa said: "Electric cars are normal now. You can get into an electric vehicle and live the electric lifestyle, you don’t have to be a millionaire."
Lower income areas and regions with fewer charging stations prevent people from switching to electric vehicles.
Electric vehicle adoption in California's Central Valley is taking longer compared to other parts of the state.
Fresno currently has 5,481 electric cars on the road.
San Francisco has over 16,242 electric vehicles on the road.
Governor Newsom outlined a historic $10 billion zero-emission vehicle package aimed at accelerating the phasing out of gas-powered cars by 2035.
The Central Valley continues to experience the worst air pollution in the region.
In November 2021, a coalition of climate justice organizations sued the Environmental Protection Agency over failing to intervene in the area's climate crisis.
Robert Barrosa states that pollution and climate change are the driving forces behind Electrify America's push to increase electric vehicle adoption.

Robert Barrosa said: "Making the transition to electric vehicles is extremely important. One of the things that we wanna do, especially in areas like the Central Valley that continues to be plagued by heavy pollution levels, is to transform the transportation sector."
Electrify America works with organizations like Valley Clean Air Now and local nonprofits to make electric vehicle adoption more accessible.

The company has donated electric vehicles to community-based organizations in Bakersfield and throughout the Central Valley for services such as driving people to doctor's appointments or making deliveries.
Using these donated cars for community services allows others to experience what it is like to use an electric vehicle.
source_title: EV charging company helps reduce air pollution in the Central Valley

facts: Aspen Aerogels plans to open an advanced manufacturing facility in Bulloch County, Georgia creating over 250 manufacturing jobs.
Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Aspen Aerogels (NYSE: ASPN) plans to construct the facility at Bulloch County’s Southern Gateway Commerce Park near Statesboro.
Aspen intends to invest a minimum of $325 million and create more than 250 advanced manufacturing jobs at the new site.
Production at the new Georgia facility is expected to begin in late 2023.
Governor Brian P. Kemp stated that Aspen Aerogels has put confidence in Georgia, its people, and the growing electric mobility sector in the state.
Combined with Aspen’s original East Providence, Rhode Island plant, the planned Georgia facility is designed to triple Aspen’s nameplate aerogel capacity.
The expansion supports Aspen’s thermal barrier expansion plans in the fast-growing electric vehicle (EV) market.
Aspen aims to capitalize on increased demand for its Aerogel Technology Platform™ as EV OEMs adopt PyroThin® aerogel thermal barrier solutions.
Don Young, President and CEO of Aspen, noted the company is pleased to site, develop, and construct a PyroThin materials plant in Georgia.
The City of Statesboro and Bulloch County region offer access to efficient ports, a strong available workforce, and secure low-cost utilities and critical raw materials.

Aspen is increasingly being asked to provide broader solutions for improved battery performance and safety centered on PyroThin materials that provide thermal management, mechanical stability, and fire protection properties to EV battery systems.
Aspen plans to build a 500,000-square-foot advanced manufacturing facility situated on a 90-acre parcel in southeast Georgia.

The company plans to begin hiring personnel in the second half of 2022 across skilled manufacturing operators, advanced process engineers, quality and maintenance technicians, and professional support roles.
Billy Allen, Vice Chair of the Development Authority of Bulloch County, welcomed Aspen Aerogels to the Statesboro-Bulloch County community and region.
Allen cited great site and logistics advantages as reasons for Aspen's decision, citing the region's commitment to innovation in the electric mobility ecosystem.
Allen expressed confidence that education resources including Georgia Southern University and Ogeechee Technical College will provide critical support to help Aspen flourish.

Assistant Director Alyce Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce division on this project in partnership with the Development Authority of Bulloch County, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia EMC.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson welcomed Aspen Aerogels’ innovative, sustainable solutions for the electric vehicle and energy storage markets to Georgia.
Wilson stated they look forward to Georgia employees collaborating with Aspen’s network of world-class scientists, engineers, and additional skilled workforce to solve mobility challenges.

Southern Gateway Commerce Park is a Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Certified park requiring due diligence and specific certification requirements prior to prospective business visits.
Aspen is a technology leader in sustainability whose aerogel technology enables customers to achieve objectives around resource efficiency, electrification, and sustainability.
Aspen’s PyroThin® thermal barrier products enable solutions to thermal runaway challenges within the electric vehicle and energy storage markets.
The company’s carbon aerogel program seeks to increase the performance of lithium-ion battery cells to enable EV manufacturers to extend driving range and reduce EV costs.
Aspen’s Spaceloft® sustainable building materials provide industry-leading energy efficiency and fire safety to building owners.
Cryogel® and Pyrogel® products are valued by the world's largest energy infrastructure companies.
Aspen’s strategy is to partner with world-class industry leaders to leverage its Aerogel Technology Platform™ into additional high-value markets.
The company is headquartered in Northborough, MA, and manufactures products at its East Providence, R.I. facilities.
source_title: Manufacturing company to open facility in Bulloch Co.

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced during an economic development trip to Israel that Hanon Systems will create 160 new jobs.
Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Hanon Systems will invest more than $40 million in a new manufacturing facility in Bulloch County.
Georgia’s automotive and e-mobility industries continue to grow as the state welcomes Hanon Systems.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that as Georgia furthers its position as the EV capital of America, new jobs and opportunities are coming to hardworking Georgians across the state.
Min Sung, President and Chief Executive Officer of Hanon Systems, stated the company is pleased to contribute to the local economy of Bulloch County through job creation.
Min Sung thanked the State of Georgia and the cooperation between its agencies that has guided the project.
Min Sung noted that today’s announcement aligns with Hanon Systems' strategy to support the industry shift toward electrification while growing its North America footprint.
Hanon Systems will build a new facility at Gateway Regional Industrial Park in Bulloch County to assemble automotive thermal solutions.
Operations at the new Hanon Systems facility are expected to begin in May 2024.
Billy Allen, Chair of the Development Authority of Bulloch County, stated that Statesboro-Bulloch County is honored by Hanon Systems’ decision to locate its new facility there.
Billy Allen noted that the community is excited to welcome another automotive supplier working closely with the Hyundai Metaplant and the electric vehicle industry.
Billy Allen stated that this decision exemplifies the resources offered by their community and region to help companies succeed.
Griff Lynch, Executive Director of the Georgia Ports Authority (GPA), stated that the announcement further solidifies Georgia’s position as a growing hub for auto manufacturing.
Griff Lynch added that GPA is proud to support the operations of Hyundai Motor Group and its suppliers as they deliver jobs and opportunity for Georgia residents.
Alyce Thornhill, Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy, represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The GDEcD worked in partnership with the Development Authority of Bulloch County, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Power, and Georgia Quick Start on this project.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that suppliers like Hanon Systems successfully support larger industry ecosystems in Georgia through partnership-driven solutions ranging from workforce training to logistics systems efficiencies.
Pat Wilson noted that Bulloch County, as part of the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority, has been part of a growing success story from the beginning.
Pat Wilson stated that Georgia’s history of partnership continues to create opportunity for current and future generations.
Building on the assets that make the automotive industry successful, Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric mobility industry.
Since 2020, over 40 EV-related projects have announced more than $22.7 billion in investment and 28,400 jobs in Georgia.
On October 25, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground in neighboring Bryan County on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility.
source_title: GA: Automotive Supplier Hanon Systems to Create 160 New Jobs in Bulloch County | Trade and Industry Development

facts: Hanon Systems will invest more than $40 million for a manufacturing facility in Bulloch County.
The move is expected to create 160 jobs.
Hanon Systems specializes in automotive climate control systems.
Another Hyundai supplier is coming soon to the Savannah area.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp’s office announced the investment on Tuesday.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated that Georgia’s automotive and e-mobility industries continue to grow as they proudly welcome Hanon Systems to Georgia.
Gov. Brian Kemp noted that as the state furthers its position as the EV capital of America, new jobs and opportunities are coming to hardworking Georgians all over the state.
source_title: Hanon Systems to bring 160 jobs to Bulloch County

facts: North Carolina is suing to try to regain control of a nearly 2,000-acre Chatham County economic development site from VinFast.
The suit alleges that VinFast won't be able to have its planned car manufacturing facility open by July 2026.
The suit alleges that VinFast won't be able to employ 1,750 people at the Moncure site by the end of this year.
Those deadlines are part of an option built into the state's sales agreement for three parcels that make up the megasite.
By failing to meet them, the state says it should be able to re-purchase the site.
North Carolina officials point to a clause requiring "vertical construction" on buildings and structures needed by Jan. 1, 2024.
The state alleges that while VinFast has graded the site and poured some concrete, nothing has been built above ground.

Attorney General Jeff Jackson wrote in a statement: “VinFast agreed to build a factory and create jobs for North Carolinians – it didn’t do either. When North Carolina makes a deal, we build in protection for taxpayers. VinFast broke the deal, so we’re using that protection to find a project for this site that will create jobs."
VinFast has said publicly that it could open the factory by 2028, two years after the deadline in its agreement with North Carolina.
On May 10, 2024, VinFast first asked North Carolina for an extension on the 2026 target.
At that meeting, VinFast said a slowdown in global electric vehicle sales was making it difficult to secure financing.
State officials told VinFast they couldn't change the agreed-upon timeline.
In the lawsuit, North Carolina officials express skepticism that VinFast would even be able to meet the 2028 target.
Officials argue that even if VinFast could build a factory by then, it falls outside of the maximum 18-month "cure" period the state is able to grant.
State officials allege that while VinFast cleared the site of trees in 2023, it stopped any further work there in December 2024.
A clause in the site development agreement says the state can claw back funds used for site prep if work halts for more than 12 consecutive months before 2032.

The lawsuit states: "VinFast has failed to take any concrete action that shows it can and will fulfill its obligation to the State. VinFast abandoned work on the site for over a year. Not only did the Company fail to create jobs — it made zero progress towards the construction of any buildings or structures,"
The lawsuit was filed Thursday in Wake County Superior Court.
A request for comment sent to VinFast's general communications portal was not returned Thursday afternoon.
VinFast was founded in 2017 and quickly looked to open a factory in the United States.
When the Chatham County factory was announced in 2022, VinFast said it expected to finish the first phase by 2024.
At the time of the 2022 announcement, VinFast was the largest economic development project in North Carolina history.
VinFast said it planned to invest $4 billion and employ 7,500 people.
The site would feature not only a car factory but also a bus manufacturing facility and an electric vehicle battery production plant.
VinFast's agreement with North Carolina required that it start constructing vertical buildings on its first phase by January 1, 2024.

When asked for proof of vertical construction, the suit says VinFast sent photos of concrete footers being poured or retaining walls instead of anything showing free-standing structures built above ground.
The lawsuit states: "VinFast has never provided photos of any upright columns or vertical structures built into the building footers,"
North Carolina officials argue that even finishing a foundation for the facility would require hundreds more footers to be poured.

The lawsuit states: "VinFast cannot begin vertical construction of a manufacturing facility until it has completed the underground foundation of the building. The company has yet to advance beyond the preliminary stage and has now run out of time to do so,"
VinFast has never actually been issued a building permit.
Chatham County was poised to issue one on May 29, 2024, but required VinFast to inform the county which contractors would be working on the site.
The company never responded to that request.
The January 1, 2024, deadline is important because a potential 18-month "cure" period offered under the agreement has already lapsed.
In a new building permit application for a foundation submitted to Chatham County on March 6, VinFast reduced the scope of the project.
The application said the facility would cover about 236 acres of the site and be built to employ 1,400 people.
That projected employment is fewer than the 1,750 people VinFast's agreement with North Carolina says it needs to employ at a manufacturing facility on the site by this year.

Governor Josh Stein said in a written statement: “VinFast has not fulfilled its commitments. Today’s action is about protecting taxpayers and getting the Chatham County mega-site back on the market to support future good-paying manufacturing jobs,"
As part of the economic development package, the N.C. General Assembly appropriated $450 million of taxpayer dollars to the project for site preparation.

That included up to $125 million to VinFast, as well as up to $250 million to the N.C. Department of Transportation and up to $75 million to the City of Sanford for water and sewer extensions to the site.
The site development agreement states that if construction halts for at least a year, the state can claw back any funds it sent to VinFast.

North Carolina is alleging that VinFast failed to do anything at the site for a full calendar year, meaning the state can claw back about $80 million it sent to the company for grading and other site preparation.
The suit points to Clayco, the company's previous contractor, terminating its contract with VinFast on June 28, 2024, due to delays from the company and its failure to prove financing.
In a lawsuit, Clayco officials alleged that while they were contracted to perform construction, the facility's design was only 50% complete in June 2024.
