VinFast also terminated its agreement with construction company Barnhart in December 2024.

In April 2025, VinFast told North Carolina that no work had been done since Barnhart left and said it was considering using the site as a "supplier park" instead of the announced manufacturing facility.
On December 11, 2025, VinFast executives met with North Carolina officials.
At that meeting, North Carolina told VinFast that based on the lack of progress at the site, it was ready to begin discussions about triggering its option to purchase the megasite.
VinFast officials pushed back, saying they still planned to build a manufacturing facility on the site.
Attorney General Jeff Jackson sent VinFast a letter on Jan. 6, 2026, formally informing the company that North Carolina believed it was in violation of the economic development agreement.
In that letter, Jackson told VinFast the state would seek to claw back the $80 million if the company "did not comply with the state's exercise of the option."

VinFast officials pushed back in February, arguing work was ongoing at the site, including erosion control, and that VinFast was working to secure financing and sign new agreements with contractors for the project.
In a series of letters over the ensuing months, VinFast made claims about the scope of the project, potential financing and what work was being conducted.
None of those satisfied North Carolina officials, particularly VinFast's insistence that it plans to finish construction in 2028.
The lawsuit states: "Far from assuring the state of its ability to fulfill its agreements, the company repeatedly repudiated its agreements throughout its responses,"
source_title: NC sues VinFast in effort to regain control of Chatham County megasite | WUNC News

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that PHA, a global auto parts manufacturer that will supply the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America in Bryan County, will create 402 new jobs and invest more than $67 million in a new facility in Chatham County.

In a single month prior to this announcement, Georgia’s economic development community had announced more than 1,900 new jobs for hardworking Georgians, with the bulk of those jobs related to the Hyundai Metaplant, according to Governor Brian Kemp.

Governor Brian Kemp thanked partners in the local communities represented by the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority for their ongoing commitment to seeing businesses and Georgians prosper together.
PHA is a Tier-1 automotive parts supplier for Hyundai Motor Group, Kia, and many other original equipment manufacturers (OEMs).
PHA is a Korea-based company with locations throughout the world, including multiple facilities within the United States.
Since its establishment in 1985, PHA has grown into a global company in the field of automotive door moving systems through research and development, innovative technology, and aggressive investment.
Sangtae Kim, Chairman of PHA, stated it is an honor for PHA to establish a new production facility in Chatham County, Georgia.
PHA produces innovative products for the automotive world and the growing EV market.
PHA expressed gratitude to Chatham County and the State of Georgia for their support in making the partnership happen and launching it to bring new jobs and advance regional economic development.
PHA’s new facility will be located at the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center, a Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Certified site.
The new manufacturing facility will produce door modules, tailgate latches, and hood latches for OEMs, including Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
Operations at the new facility are expected to begin in 2024.

Savannah Economic Development Authority President and CEO Trip Tollison announced that PHA is the second Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America supplier to locate in the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center.
Trip Tollison noted that PHA is well-regarded in the industry and expressed anticipation for working with them as they build their new facility and begin operations in the Savannah region.

Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy Alyce Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project in partnership with the Savannah Economic Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia Power.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson welcomed PHA to Georgia as the second Hyundai supplier at the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson emphasized that projects like Hyundai and its suppliers underscore the importance of site preparation to attract jobs, noting companies prioritize locations with completed GRAD certification to meet demand and accelerate speed-to-market.
On October 25, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground in neighboring Bryan County on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility.
Offsite suppliers connected to the Hyundai Metaplant project were predicted to invest approximately $1 billion in other locations around the state.
Projects announced to date for the Hyundai Metaplant ecosystem have exceeded the $1 billion supplier investment projection by more than $800 million.
Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric mobility industry, building on the assets that make the automotive industry successful.
Since 2020, more than 35 EV-related projects have announced more than $21 billion in investment and 27,400 jobs in Georgia.
source_title: Hyundai supplier PHA to create more than 400 jobs in Chatham County - SEDA

facts: A Vietnamese automaker announced plans to build a $4 billion electric-vehicle assembly and battery-manufacturing plant.
The facility will be located at Triangle Innovation Point in Moncure, Chatham County, North Carolina, approximately 30 miles west of the Triangle region.
VinFast selected North Carolina for its first North American automotive assembly and battery manufacturing plant.
The project is expected to create 7,500 jobs.
The company intends to invest up to $2…
VinFast is an electric vehicle company headquartered in Los Angeles, California.
VinFast is part of Vingroup, the largest privately held company in Vietnam.
The North Carolina Railroad Company (NCRR) invested $1 million in a new auto ramp in Chatham County.
NCRR is one of several partners named in the announcement for VinFast's first U.S.-based EV assembly and battery manufacturing facility.
source_title: March 2022 – Chatham County, NC

entity_name: Hyundai-SK On Battery Plant Joint Venture
canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group and SK On Joint Venture
facts: Gov. Brian Kemp announced Thursday that Hyundai Motor Group and SK On chose a location in Bartow County for a new facility to help supply automotive manufacturers' plants across the country.
Officials say the new facility will be one of the largest economic development projects in Georgia history.
The project will invest approximately $4 to $5 billion into Bartow County.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated that Hyundai Motor Group and SK On are valued partners and key players in Georgia's ever-growing automotive industry.
Gov. Brian Kemp noted his administration has focused on bringing jobs and opportunity to overlooked communities and is proud the companies are investing further in the No. 1 state for business.
Officials estimate the facility will create more than 3,500 new jobs.
The facility will be located at Bartow Centre, an industrial site on Highway 411.
The company facility is expected to begin operations in 2025.
Bartow County Sole Commissioner Steve Taylor expressed pleasure with the decision and thanked the owners of the former Bartow Centre Industrial property.
Steve Taylor stated the project will have regional benefit for job seekers and establishes Bartow in Georgia’s EV ecosystem in a significant way.
SK On is the lithium-ion battery branch of SK Innovations.
SK On currently employs over 2,000 people at its SK Battery America facility in Commerce, Georgia.
The construction of the plant has faced controversy, with the FOX 5 I-Team investigating allegations of worker safety issues at the plant's construction site.
Hyundai Motor Group announced earlier this year plans to invest over $5.5 billion into an electric vehicle manufacturing facility in Bryan County.
The Bryan County investment led to multiple parts suppliers announcing new facilities to supply that plant.
A headline on the page states that Hyundai breaks ground on a $5.5B electric car manufacturing plant in Georgia.
source_title: HSBMA

facts: SK On and Ford Motor have agreed to unwind their BlueOval SK joint venture in the United States.
The partnership was launched in 2022 to build large-scale battery manufacturing capacity for electric vehicles.
The three battery plants developed under the BlueOval SK banner will be split between the two companies, with ownership and operations separated.
SK On will assume full ownership of the battery plant under construction in Tennessee, located within Ford’s BlueOval City campus.
Ford will take over the two plants in Kentucky through a wholly owned subsidiary.
The Kentucky plant officially opened its doors in August.
A second plant in Kentucky is still under development.
The Tennessee plant was supposed to be completed this year; however, production there has yet to start.
The companies aim to complete their parting by the end of the first quarter of 2026, subject to regulatory approvals and customary closing conditions.
Once the transfer is finalized, the Tennessee site will become SK On’s second wholly owned battery factory in the US.
SK On’s other US battery facility is SK Battery America in Commerce, Georgia.
SK On’s standalone battery production capacity in the US is expected to rise from 22 gigawatt-hours to 67 gigawatt-hours annually.
The company’s only remaining US joint venture will be its battery project with Hyundai Motor Group in Georgia.
Despite the dissolution of the joint venture, SK On and Ford are expected to continue their supplier relationship.
The Tennessee plant is set to provide batteries for Ford’s next-generation electric vehicles under existing agreements.
The BlueOval SK project was originally announced as an investment of around $11 billion.
The combined planned capacity is up to 120 gigawatt-hours per year.
The planned capacity is sufficient to supply batteries for more than one million electric vehicles annually.
Timelines for the Tennessee plant and the second Kentucky facility had been pushed back.
In 2023, Ford announced that it would postpone its investment in the second Kentucky plant and thus the start of construction.
The reason for the postponement was weaker-than-anticipated demand for EVs across the US.
For that same reason, Ford agreed to let competitor Nissan use its battery production in Kentucky earlier this year.
SK On currently focuses on nickel-cobalt-manganese battery chemistries for electric vehicles.
SK On supplies cells to several automakers, including Hyundai Motor Group, Kia, Volkswagen, Nissan and US EV startup Slate.
The Tennessee plant could support energy storage system production, reflecting rising demand for grid-scale storage in the US.

A spokesperson told The Korea Herald: “With our 45-gigawatt-hour Tennessee plant, we aim to supply batteries for both electric vehicles and energy storage systems not only to Ford but to a wider range of customers, strengthening profitability and operational resilience in the North American market.”
Ford published what is probably the shortest company statement ever: “We are aware of SK’s disclosure and we have nothing further to share at this time.”
source_title: SK On and Ford split US battery venture - electrive.com

facts: SK On, a major South Korean battery manufacturer, has laid off nearly 1,000 workers at its US battery plant in Georgia amid slowing electric vehicle (EV) demand, according to a company filing on Saturday.

SK Battery America, SK On's US unit, dismissed 968 employees at its battery manufacturing facility in Commerce, Georgia, per a Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification (WARN) notice posted on a Georgia state website.
The layoffs account for about 37 per cent of the plant's total workforce of around 2,500 employees, according to Yonhap news agency.
SK On stated the workforce reduction is part of restructuring efforts prompted by slowing EV sales and changing market conditions.
The company made the decision to adjust operations while maintaining its commitment to the state of Georgia and efforts to build a strong U.S. supply chain for advanced battery manufacturing.
The Georgia plant has been supplying EV batteries to automakers including Germany's Volkswagen and South Korea's Hyundai Motor.
The plant also supplied batteries for Ford Motor's F-150 Lightning electric pickup truck.
Profitability was affected after Ford canceled production plans for the F-150 Lightning model.
SK On is currently building a second battery plant in Georgia to supply batteries to Hyundai Motor, with production scheduled to begin in the first half of this year.
Another SK On plant in Tennessee is expected to begin production in 2028.
In October last year, Governor Brian Kemp of Georgia met with the head of South Korean battery maker SK On to discuss ways to enhance cooperation and strengthen business ties.
Governor Kemp and his delegation visited SK On's headquarters in central Seoul earlier that day.
Governor Kemp held talks with Lee Seok-hee, chief executive officer (CEO) of SK On.
Governor Kemp and Lee Seok-hee previously met in June 2024 during Kemp's visit to Seoul.
SK On operates a 22 gigawatt-hour battery plant in Commerce, Georgia, through its U.S. unit, SK Battery America.
SK On is building another facility jointly with Hyundai Motor Group in Bartow County.
source_title: SK On Layoffs: SK On lays off nearly 1,000 workers at US battery plant amid EV slowdown, ETHRWorld

facts: Volkswagen AG called itself an "unintended victim" in a battle between two battery suppliers and urged the U.S. government to extend a reprieve to buy batteries key to its planned U.S. electric vehicle production.

The U.S. International Trade Commission (ITC) sided with LG Chem in a trade secrets case but permitted SK Innovation to import components for domestic production of lithium ion batteries for Ford Motor Co's EV F-150 program for four years, and for Volkswagen of America’s electric vehicle line for two years.
VW requested its carve out be extended to at least four years to give an adequate transition period.
VW expressed hope that the two suppliers will settle the dispute outside of the courtroom.
The German automaker has invested $800 million to build electric vehicles in Tennessee and is hiring hundreds of employees.
Volkswagen stated it will take all necessary steps to fulfill its commitment to provide skilled jobs to proud Tennessee workers.
Ford Chief Executive Jim Farley publicly encouraged LG Chem and SK Innovation to reach a settlement on Thursday.
VW and Ford previously warned the dispute could disrupt supplies of key EV parts and cost U.S. jobs during the COVID-19 pandemic.

Georgia Governor Brian Kemp called on President Joe Biden to overturn the ITC ruling, warning the long-term prospects for the $2.6 billion SK Innovation battery plant in Jackson County, Georgia, would be "harmed significantly."
The SK Innovation plant will eventually build batteries for Volkswagen and Ford.
LG Energy Solution counsel Dave Callahan said the company remains committed to negotiating a fair settlement for the theft of its trade secrets and stated the Georgia plant will be able to operate.
Dave Callahan added that the only obstacle to resolving the matter and securing the plant's long-term future is SK Innovation's refusal to acknowledge its wrongdoing and make amends.
source_title: VW 'unintended victim' in battery supplier dispute, seeks four-year reprieve - Times of India

entity_name: Georgia EV and Battery Supply Chain
canonical_name: Georgia EV and Battery Supply Chain
facts: The US has historically shied away from establishing an industrial policy on lithium-ion battery production but policymakers now support domestic supply chains due to stiff competition from China and the EU.
Beijing unveiled its ‘Made in China 2025’ strategy in 2015, and Brussels launched its ‘Strategic action plan on batteries’ three years later.

American plants are projected to deliver 486 gigawatt hours of battery capacity per year (GWh/y) by 2030, compared with China’s 2,681 GWh/y and the EU’s 778 GWh/y according to Benchmark Mineral Intelligence.
The capacity gap is equivalent to China building one new battery megafactory every week while the US builds one every four months.

Simon Moores, managing director of Benchmark Mineral Intelligence, states the US has been relegated to a “bystander” in the global battery arms race despite China holding only 9% of world lithium reserves but processing and manufacturing 55% of global lithium chemical compounds domestically.
The US Department of Energy published a 10-year national strategy in June 2021 to onshore key inputs including extraction processes, electrode manufacturing, and cell manufacturing.
President Joe Biden’s plan has created bullish sentiment for building a domestic lithium-ion economy in the US with connective thinking from the top of the government regarding supply chains.
Outside Tesla’s Nevada gigafactory, the greatest inroads toward electrification are being made in Georgia and Tennessee.
Korean energy company SK Innovation plans to become a top three global EV supplier with its cornerstone $2.6bn investment in two battery plants based in Jackson County, Georgia.
SK Innovation’s Georgia plants will supply automakers Ford and Volkswagen.
US car manufacturer General Motors announced in April it will build a second $2.3bn battery plant in Spring Hill, Tennessee as part of a joint venture with Korean battery maker LG Energy Solution.
The GM facility is set to be 2.8 million square feet and open in late 2023.

A spokesperson for LG Energy Solution notes the US is one of the biggest EV battery markets by size with potential to grow at the fastest rate among major markets due to the Biden administration’s emphasis on clean energy and the EV battery value chain.
Georgia has 130,000 people working in the automotive sector, and the transition to EVs is forcing automakers to rethink operations and supply chains.

Pat Wilson, commissioner of the Georgia Department of Economic Development, states the industry will see more change over the next 20 years than in the previous 100 years and the state wants to be part of the process across the entire supply chain from refining rare minerals to automobile assembly.
Bob Rolfe, commissioner of the Tennessee Department of Economic and Community Development, shares this view stating every single company is going all-in on EVs.

Battery chemistry advancements include Volkswagen seeing lithium-ion battery energy density double since 2014 and the International Energy Agency reporting unit cost per kWh has fallen by 87% in the past decade.

Vanessa Witte, senior analyst at Wood Mackenzie, notes battery packs are registered as hazardous goods and can be up to a quarter of a vehicle’s weight, rendering them unsuitable for shipping in bulk over long distances.
The risks of just-in-time supply chain models that sacrifice resilience for efficiency have been highlighted by events such as the pandemic.
Companies are trying to shore up local supply chains due to international supply chain blind spots.

Benchmark Mineral Intelligence estimates global annual raw lithium supply of 440,000 tonnes will meet EV market demand of 432,000 tonnes for 2021, but demand will outstrip supply by 60% (2.4 million tonnes to 1.5 million tonnes) by the end of the decade.

Hau Thai-Tang, Ford’s chief product platform and operations officer, told CNBC in May 2021 that while Ford doesn’t have to scale today to justify a dedicated battery plant, by 2025 volume from the F-150, E-Transit, and another announced BEV will justify it.

Simon Moores expects consolidation in the space with automakers acquiring battery manufacturers from initial joint ventures, but limiting factors will be lithium compound shortages or lagging consumer demand.
Federal tax credits to incentivize electric passenger cars were phased out for most automakers at the end of 2019, dramatically raising costs for consumers.
The US government needs to find the right mix of policies to make EVs affordable and build out domestic supply chain capacity.
source_title: fDi Intelligence – Your source for foreign direct investment information - fDiIntelligence.com

facts: Battery manufacturer LG Energy Solution will lay off 170 workers from its Holland, Michigan, site.
The layoffs come as the company expands and transitions some production lines from their current production facility in Holland to a second factory within the site where fewer staff are needed.
A company statement cited a production gap and automakers realigning the speed of the EV transition as reasons for the layoffs.
The phased layoff will occur between next month and January 2024.
South Korea-based LG Energy Solution is rapidly expanding its production capacity in the U.S., including in Holland.
The company announced plans last month to invest $3 billion in its Holland campus as part of a new lithium battery supply deal with Toyota.
The battery maker has been aggressively building its U.S. manufacturing network through a combination of owned operations and joint ventures.
The company is building joint venture battery factories with Hyundai, Honda and GM.
The company is also establishing its own factories in Holland and Arizona.
Like others in the EV space, LG Energy Solution has been adjusting operations to mitigate slower-than-expected demand.
The company’s revenue fell 6% in Q3 compared to the previous quarter, which it blamed on slowing sales in Europe despite what it called a robust market in North America.
Executives forecasted a slow final quarter this year during a recent earnings call.
Other battery makers are also making labor cuts in a slow market.
SK Battery will furlough an unspecified number of workers from its Jackson County, Georgia, site due to a drop in demand.
Production at the SK Battery site in Jackson County, Georgia, will not stop as a result of the furloughs.
source_title: LG Energy Solution to lay off 170 in Michigan | WardsAuto

facts: Georgia Gov. Brian P. Kemp announced that Daesol Ausys Georgia will establish a new manufacturing facility in West Point.
The facility will create more than 140 new jobs and $72 million in investment in Harris County.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that West Point has become an automotive capital in its own right, and he is excited that Daesol Ausys will join other innovators in Harris County.

Governor Brian Kemp noted that Georgia's automotive industry employs over 50,000 hardworking Georgians and will continue to grow as e-mobility suppliers choose all corners of the state for the jobs of the future.
Daesol Ausys was established in Korea in 2017.
The company specializes in designing and manufacturing motor and electric vehicle interior components and accessories.
It is a key supplier for Kia Georgia, Hyundai Motor Group, and General Motors.
Min Ho Kwon, CEO of Daesol Ausys, stated the company is committed to delivering world-class services that exceed its clients’ requirements and expectations.
Min Ho Kwon expressed excitement about this project in the Northwest Harris Business Park and looking forward to continuing the partnership with the State of Georgia and Harris County.
Daesol Ausys’ new facility will produce luggage boards and luggage covers.
Operations are expected to begin in December 2024.
The company will be hiring for positions in production, purchasing, quality control, maintenance, human resources, and management.
Robert Grant, Chairman of the Harris County Commission, stated that Daesol Ausys’ decision to locate in the Northwest Harris Business Park represents a commitment to Harris County.

Robert Grant noted that West Point and Harris County have a wonderful relationship with Daesol Ausys’ sister companies Daehan Solutions and Daesol Materials, and they have been working closely with company officials to get this new venture in West Point.

Craig Greenhaw, Chairman of the Harris County Development Authority, extended a gracious welcome to Daesol Ausys and expressed looking forward to a long and mutually beneficial partnership with their newest corporate citizen.
Craig Greenhaw stated that West Point and Harris County continue to work well together, calling this venture another successful one that brings jobs and capital investment into the region.

Alyce Thornhill, Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy, represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project in partnership with the Harris County Commission, Harris County Development Authority, City of West Point, Georgia Power, and Georgia Quick Start.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that economic development is a team sport that is driven by the local level, and congratulated Harris County and community partners for helping bring Daesol Ausys to West Georgia.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson noted that the automotive industry is providing generational change in the region and across the state, and expressed excitement for the continuation of this growth and the opportunities that Daesol Ausys will provide to the people of Harris County.

For over a century, Georgia has fostered healthy industry practices, encouraged collaboration and innovation, and positioned the state as a leader in developing and harnessing emerging technologies for the evolving automotive and mobility industry.
As the market transitions to electric vehicles, Georgia has pursued the entire supply chain, creating more than $25 billion in investments and 30,000 jobs since 2018.
In fiscal year 2023, job creation in the automotive industry increased by 324 percent when compared to FY21.
Suppliers attracted by Georgia’s mobility original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) resulted in over $2 billion in investment across the state in FY23.
source_title: GA: Automotive Supplier Daesol Ausys Georgia to Invest $72 Million in Harris County | Trade and Industry Development

facts: The US has roughly 3,000–4,000 active Tier-2 automotive suppliers, the large majority of them privately held companies with 50–500 employees.
Tier-2 suppliers make components such as stamped metal parts, plastic housings, machined castings, and wire harnesses that get built into Tier-1 systems.
The US Census assigns NAICS codes at the establishment level for automotive suppliers under NAICS 3361–3363.
NAICS code 3361 covers Motor Vehicle Manufacturing.
NAICS code 3362 covers Motor Vehicle Body and Trailer Manufacturing.
NAICS code 3363 covers Motor Vehicle Parts Manufacturing.
NAICS code 336310 covers Motor Vehicle Gasoline Engine and Engine Parts.
NAICS code 336320 covers Motor Vehicle Electrical and Electronic Equipment.
NAICS code 336330 covers Motor Vehicle Steering, Suspension, and Interrelated Parts.
NAICS code 336340 covers Motor Vehicle Brake System Parts.
NAICS code 336350 covers Motor Vehicle Transmission and Power Train Parts.
NAICS code 336360 covers Motor Vehicle Seating and Interior Trim.
NAICS code 336390 covers Other Motor Vehicle Parts.
Some Tier-2 facilities also carry adjacent NAICS codes such as metal stamping (332116), rubber product manufacturing (326291), or precision machined components (332721).
GM operates assembly plants in Flint (MI), Lansing (MI), Fort Wayne (IN), Bowling Green (KY), Spring Hill (TN), Wentzville (MO), Fairfax (KS), Arlington (TX), and Hamtramck/Factory Zero (MI).
Ford operates assembly plants in Dearborn (MI), Chicago (IL), Kansas City (MO), Louisville (KY), Flat Rock (MI), Hermosillo (MX adjacent but worth noting for AZ/TX reps), and Wayne (MI).
Stellantis operates plants in Detroit (Mack/Jefferson, MI), Belvidere (IL — idled/reopening), Toledo (OH), Windsor (ON adjacent), and Brampton (ON adjacent).
Toyota operates assembly plants in Georgetown (KY), Princeton (IN), San Antonio (TX), and Buffalo (WV).
Honda operates assembly plants in Marysville (OH), East Liberty (OH), Lincoln (AL).
Nissan operates assembly plants in Smyrna (TN) and Canton (MS).
Hyundai/Kia operates assembly plants in Montgomery (AL) and West Point (GA).
BMW operates an assembly plant in Spartanburg (SC).
Mercedes-Benz operates an assembly plant in Vance (AL).
Volkswagen operates an assembly plant in Chattanooga (TN).
Tesla operates assembly plants in Fremont (CA) and Austin (TX).
Rivian operates an assembly plant in Normal (IL).
EV battery plants include BlueOval (Marshall, MI + Stanton, TN), Ultium (Spring Hill, TN + Lordstown, OH + Lansing, MI + Tennessee), Toyota Battery (Liberty, NC), and Honda/LG (Jeffersonville, OH).
Each of these OEM and battery plants anchors a 100–250 mile supplier radius.
The Midwest and upper South are by far the densest regions for Tier-2 facilities, with the MI–OH–IN–KY–TN corridor containing the majority of US Tier-2 facilities.
GM's Spring Hill plant produces the Cadillac XT5, XT6, and Chevy Colorado.
GM's Spring Hill plant is surrounded by a dense Tier-1 and Tier-2 cluster across middle Tennessee, northern Alabama, and southern Kentucky.
Querying suppliers around Spring Hill, TN can include state filters for TN, AL, KY, GA.
Nissan Smyrna overlaps with the Toyota Georgetown radius in terms of supplier states (TN, AL, KY).
Hyundai Montgomery's key supplier states include AL, GA, TN.
BMW Spartanburg's key supplier states include SC, NC, GA.
Mercedes Vance's key supplier states include AL, GA, MS.
Tesla Austin's key supplier states include TX, OK.
Rivian Normal's key supplier states include IL, IN, OH, MO.

The US automotive supply chain is mid-transition from ICE to EV, creating two distinct prospect segments for automation vendors: high automation urgency (ICE-specific Tier-2s) and growth investment mode (EV-adjacent Tier-2s like battery pack components, power electronics, thermal management).
Facilities Finder indexes every physical plant as its own record with AI-enriched data including 35,000+ industry classifications and 7 million+ products.
Facilities Finder covers 600,000+ US industrial facilities.
source_title: How to Build a List of Tier-2 Automotive Suppliers by Region

entity_name: SK On and Hyundai EV Battery Plant Joint Venture
canonical_name: SK On-Hyundai Georgia Battery Plant Joint Venture
facts: SK Innovation and Hyundai Motor Group plan a sprawling electric vehicle battery plant in Bartow County, northwest of Atlanta.
The project is expected to create more than 3,500 jobs.
The project will rank as the third-largest corporate investment in state history.
Gov. Brian Kemp’s office announced the project on Thursday.
The joint venture is between SK subsidiary SK On and Hyundai.
The project adds to Georgia’s stature as an emerging EV and battery manufacturing hub.
Georgia’s status as an emerging EV and battery manufacturing hub shows no signs of slowing down.
Automakers are betting their futures on electrification.
SK Innovation previously announced a $5.8 billion investment in Atlanta, Georgia on February 7, 2025.
Hyundai announced a $4.5 billion investment in automotive components manufacturing for two new plants.
The joint venture between SK On and Hyundai announced the project at a press conference in Bartow County, Georgia on February 11, 2025.
Gov. Brian Kemp said one of the largest investments in the state will be made by Hyundai and SK On in automotive manufacturing.
The company will also add three new jobs for the first three years of this project and up to 845,000 jobs over the lifetime of the project.
The project is in addition to multibillion-dollar investments SK and Hyundai have already announced in Georgia.
source_title: SK and Hyundai plan Georgia electric vehicle battery plant, 3,500 jobs

facts: Hyundai Motor Group has tied a significant share of Georgia’s economy to its fate and decisions for almost 17 years.
In 2006, Hyundai decided to build a $1 billion Kia automobile assembly plant in West Point.
In 2022, Hyundai decided to build an electric vehicle (EV) assembly plant in Bryan County.
Kia Georgia’s investment in the 2,200-acre West Point site has grown to $1.8 billion since its first car rolled off the line in 2009.
The West Point plant produces 340,000 K5, Sportage, Sorento, and Telluride vehicles a year.
Kia Georgia employs nearly 3,000 people working in three shifts around the clock.
Kia claims to have spurred 14,000 plant and supplier jobs in the region, well above original projections.
Troup and Harris counties are historically preferred locations for suppliers, especially for Kia’s 39 Tier 1 suppliers.
Kia’s Tier 1 suppliers provide primary components for the production process.
About 70% of Kia’s Tier 1 suppliers are subsidiaries of South Korean companies.
Kia’s first suppliers included those that already served Hyundai’s then-new Montgomery, Ala., plant.
In 2013, the Alabama Department of Commerce listed 106 Kia suppliers and vendors on its website, concentrated along the I-85 corridor linking West Point and Montgomery.

West Point hosts Kia suppliers including Hyundai Mobis, Hyundai TRANSYS Georgia Powertrain, Hyundai Transys Georgia Seating System, Hamco America Inc., Daehan Solution Georgia, Daesol Material Georgia, Adient, and JinTech.
LaGrange in Troup County hosts suppliers including Sejong Georgia, GLOVIS Georgia, Sewon America, Seoyon E-Hwa Interior Systems, and Daeha-America Corp.
The COVID pandemic disrupted global supply chains and challenged Kia’s management of its suppliers.
Many materials and parts for Kia are imported from Canada, Mexico, and abroad due to different regional restrictions.
Kia Georgia currently makes only gas-powered internal combustion engines (ICE) but will soon change.
In April 2023, Kia announced the West Point plant will begin assembling the EV9 in 2024.
The EV9 will be the first Kia EV assembled locally in North America.
Kia has not yet stated how the workforce will change, how suppliers will be affected, or what the new investment will total for the EV9 transition.
Stuart Countess, president and CEO of Kia Georgia, stated the EV launch will create unique processes requiring changes to existing tooling and equipment.
An EV does not require a fuel tank or circulation system, so specific process area changes will be necessary.
Kia will adopt new testing and validation processes to maintain high-quality standards for the EV9.
Thomas Kurfess, professor and executive director of the Georgia Tech Manufacturing Institute, noted that suppliers failing to pivot toward EV components will lose out to those who do.
Hyundai’s $5.54 billion EV manufacturing “Metaplant” in Bryan County will produce EVs for its Hyundai, Genesis, and Kia brands.
The Bryan County Metaplant will employ 8,100 workers.
Joon Georgia announced a $317 million investment near Statesboro in Bulloch County to create 630 jobs as part of the first wave of EV suppliers.
Ecoplastic Corporation announced plans for a $205 million facility in Bulloch County to make automotive interior and exterior plastic parts and hire 456 workers.
Hyundai Mobis is building a $926 million Bryan County plant generating 1,500 jobs to make EV power systems and integrated charging-control units.
Seoyon E-Hwa will create 740 jobs at a $76 million plant in Chatham County.
Sewon America is investing $300 million in an Effingham County facility expected to generate 740 jobs.
Chatham County won a $67 million, 400-worker investment from South Korea’s PHA, a global auto parts manufacturer.
Hyundai’s EV battery plant will be located in Bartow County rather than on-site at the Bryan County Metaplant.
The Bartow County battery facility is a $4 billion to $5 billion joint venture with SK On, a subsidiary of SK Innovation.
SK On’s existing SK Battery America plant in Commerce was Georgia’s first EV battery plant.
The new Bartow County plant is expected to create 3,500 jobs and begin operating in 2025.
In January, Hyundai senior executives held a town-hall meeting with staff in Korea explaining a strategy transition to electrification and software-centered systems (software-defined vehicles).
Hyundai set a goal by 2040 of offering only zero-carbon EVs in major markets and producing “flawless” software-defined vehicles.
Hyundai-Kia announced that as of 2025, it will apply over-the-air software update functionality to all its EV models.
A company spokesperson in Korea stated they are currently focused on building world-class electric vehicles when asked if the zero-carbon goals apply to the Bryan County Metaplant.
Thomas Kurfess noted that future EV costs will increasingly be in software and recalls may become software updates.
Stuart Countess identified charging station availability and charging time as the main challenges to EV acceptance, requiring changes in consumer lifestyle and behaviors.
The Biden administration plans to install 500,000 EV charging stations across the U.S. by 2030.
The federal government is setting aside $5 billion in formula funding for states and $2.5 billion in competitive grants to help cities, tribes, and states build charging infrastructure.
Federal officials released new domestic manufacturing standards for federally funded EV chargers.
The Georgia Department of Transportation will deploy federal funds to build charging stations filling gaps along interstates per its EV Infrastructure Deployment Plan.
Georgia attracted the North American headquarters of Heliox, a Netherlands-based company that makes e-mobility charging systems.
The U.S. Department of Transportation requires final assembly in the U.S. for new charging stations to be eligible for federal funds.
Georgia Power owns about 3% of the 1,700 charging stations in Georgia.
Bentina Terry, senior vice president of customer strategy and solutions for Georgia Power, stated the company builds public charging infrastructure in low-income or underserved areas where necessary.
Georgia Power’s primary role is encouraging EV use through incentives, rebates, and guidance, with a motto to “enable, empower and educate.”

Kristi Brigman, deputy commissioner of Global Commerce at the Georgia Department of Economic Development, stated that Georgia consistently targets entire industry ecosystems and one investment often spurs further investment directly or indirectly.
source_title: Industry Spotlight: Spurring Increased Investment - Georgia Trend Magazine

facts: Hanwha Advanced Materials Georgia (HAGA) will invest $147 million to build a plant in Cartersville.
The HAGA plant is located just down the road from Qcells’ new manufacturing facility.
The new plant is set to open in 2024.
The plant will create more than 160 jobs that will include engineering and manufacturing line operator positions.
HAGA and Qcells share the same parent company, the South Korea-based conglomerate Hanwha Group.
The HAGA plant will make a specialized film coating for Qcells panels that helps increase their long-term durability.
Once online, the facility will be the only one in the U.S. making the material.
The plant is a key part of Qcells’ plan to build the first fully-integrated solar manufacturing supply chain in the U.S. in Georgia.
Qcells North America’s president HG Park stated that Qcells is doubling down on building a complete, domestic solar supply chain and this recent investment is critical to making that happen.
Earlier this year, Qcells announced it would spend $2.5 billion on a massive expansion of its Georgia production footprint.
Qcells’ expansion will bring 2,500 jobs to the state.
Company and federal and state officials billed Qcells’ expansion as the largest ever investment in clean energy manufacturing in U.S. history.
As part of that expansion, Qcells is building a brand new, 3.3-gigawatt plant in Cartersville about an hour northwest of Atlanta.
Qcells broke ground on that facility earlier this year.
The plant is set to open its production lines in 2024.
At the same time, Qcells is working on expanding its existing factory in Dalton to add 2.1 gigawatts of additional capacity.
Once both projects are complete, Qcells will be able to produce 8.4 gigawatts of solar panels in Georgia.
This production capacity is enough to meet the demands of roughly 30% of the solar installations currently planned around the country.
Qcells has said the passage of President Joe Biden’s Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) last year helped spur their new investment in Georgia.
Georgia’s two Democratic U.S. senators, Jon Ossoff and Raphael Warnock, were key to the bill’s passage in a narrowly divided upper chamber.
Senator Jon Ossoff wrote several of the law’s solar provisions.
President Biden connected the dots between the IRA and the new investment in Georgia in a statement on HAGA’s plant.
The IRA provides $10 billion in tax credits for building new solar manufacturing facilities.
The IRA provides billions more for manufacturers to make components for solar panels, batteries, and wind turbines in the U.S., among a host of other clean energy incentives.

President Biden stated that with the support of Senators Warnock and Ossoff and Georgia House Democrats, clean energy manufacturing announcements in Georgia are creating thousands of good-paying jobs making solar panels and components here at home and tackling the climate crisis.
During Gov. Brian Kemp’s tenure in office, Georgia has pitched itself as a major hub for clean energy and electric vehicle manufacturing projects.

With generous incentive packages, the state has secured billions in investments from companies like Rivian, Hyundai Motor Group and SK Battery America — along with a constellation of suppliers — to build new plants and bring tens of thousands of jobs in Georgia.
Gov. Kemp framed the arrival of Qcells’ new supplier as another sign of the state’s growth as a major clean energy player.
Gov. Kemp stated that Georgia is leading the nation in attracting next generation jobs.
Since Gov. Kemp first welcomed Qcells to the state in 2018, Georgia has announced more than 4,000 related jobs for hardworking Georgians.
Hanwha Advanced Materials is adding to that growing number as it becomes a valued member of the Bartow County community.
source_title: Solar parts supplier for Qcells building $147M plant northwest of Atlanta

facts: A multibillion-dollar EV factory under construction west of Savannah, Georgia, spans 17 million square feet, which is about two and a half times larger than Disneyland, and will create more than 8,000 jobs.
Near Atlanta, a $2.5 billion battery factory that will begin making batteries for renewable energy storage later this year will create hundreds more positions.
In another Atlanta suburb, a solar panel factory under construction plans to hire 2,500 workers.

In the year since the Inflation Reduction Act passed, clean energy companies have announced nearly 20 major new projects in Georgia totaling more than $12 billion, according to E2 (Environmental Entrepreneurs).
Other projects already underway, like the massive electric vehicle plant near Savannah, have accelerated construction to begin production earlier.
Hyundai, which is building the EV plant near Savannah, announced plans to build a $5 billion battery plant with SK On after the Inflation Reduction Act passed.
Georgia Senator Jon Ossoff stated that Georgia will benefit more than just about any state in the country from these policies.
Georgia’s governor is reluctant to acknowledge climate change, and none of the state’s Republican representatives in Congress voted for the Inflation Reduction Act.
Other states with Republican leaders who voted against the IRA, including South Carolina and North Carolina, are also at the top of the list in new clean energy investments.
In Georgia and South Carolina alone, 37 major new projects are already in development or have broken ground.
Investment is moving more slowly in some states that have led on climate policy; California currently trails Oklahoma and Indiana.
Georgia’s clean energy economy was growing even before the IRA as state and local leaders worked aggressively to attract solar, battery, and EV companies to build factories and create new jobs.
Governor Brian Kemp has said he wants Georgia to be the “electric mobility capital of America” while courting new factories, even though he has done little to get EVs on roads.
The IRA made investments surge and sped up some existing projects.

Jeremy Bezdek, EVP of global corporate development and president of Freyr Battery U.S., stated that the company decided to move forward on a fast-track project to get production up to a year earlier because the math from the IRA motivated them to move faster.
The Inflation Reduction Act offers clean energy tax credits that won’t expire for at least 10 years, unlike previous credits that were more uncertain because they had to be renewed repeatedly.

Bezdek noted that if it was a 5-year program it would make pursuing large capital projects more challenging, but the fact that it’s a 10-year program helps them get comfortable with the CapEx spend required.
Korean solar panel manufacturer Hanwha Qcells opened its first factory in Georgia in 2019.
After the IRA passed, Hanwha Qcells decided to expand its facility and build a second large plant in Cartersville, Georgia, investing another $2.5 billion in the state.
The company made the decision because the IRA included solar manufacturing tax credits that were drafted by Ossoff.
Along with Georgia Senator Rafael Warnock, Ossoff pushed to ensure that EV and battery production were priorities in the bill.
Ossoff has led trade delegations to South Korea and met individually with businesses there twice to recruit investment to Georgia.

Aaron Brickman, a senior principal at the nonprofit RMI, stated that Georgia’s long track record of attracting new businesses put the state in a position to take advantage of the IRA quickly due to its economic development infrastructure.
State and local government in Georgia supports training for workers and partnerships with schools.
One program with the Technical College System of Georgia is designed to quickly train new employees while a factory is being built.
The state and counties help companies find sites, guide them through permitting, and offer grants and other incentives.

Freyr Battery U.S. considered 170 different industrial site locations in 34 states for its factory, looking at factors like the cost of power and the availability of skill development programs, before eventually choosing to work in Georgia.

Some companies are choosing Georgia because of the concentration of existing clean energy companies; Ascend Elements, for example, with a massive new battery recycling facility, wanted to work near battery manufacturers.
Brickman is working with seven Great Lakes states, from Minnesota to Pennsylvania, as the region aims to become more competitive for clean energy development.
The opportunity for industrial transformation is huge, with an incredible amount of manufacturing jobs and commensurate CapEx in building out domestic manufacturing supply chains.
For every mega project, there will be 10 or 20 smaller projects.
The jobs are well-paid and offer opportunities to advance quickly, according to Bob Keefe, executive director of E2.
Keefe recently visited Qcells’s first solar factory in Dalton, Georgia, where he spoke with a worker who had been in the carpet industry along with the rest of his family.
The town calls itself the “carpet capital,” but lost thousands of jobs after the Great Recession as the industry contracted.
The worker had never gotten a promotion in his carpet factory job, but has already been promoted three times over the past year at Qcells.
Dalton used to be nothing but flooring and carpets, but now other companies are coming in that no one would have ever imagined could come to a place like that.
Before the Inflation Reduction Act passed, U.S. leadership in clean energy had waned, but now only a year later, a lot of clean energy is looking at the U.S. as the place to go.
source_title: How GOP-dominated Georgia became a hub for clean energy jobs - Governors' Wind Energy Coalition

entity_name: Georgia EV and Battery Manufacturing Projects
facts: Hyundai announced plans on November 24, 2022 to invest nearly $1 billion ($926 million) in a new manufacturing plant in Bryan County, Georgia.
The plant will manufacture EVs and EV powertrains.
Construction for the facility is scheduled to start early in 2023.
Production at the facility is scheduled to begin in 2025.
The plant is expected to produce 900,000 EV power systems and 450,000 integrated charging control units.
The Hyundai Mobis plant will be located in Belfast Commerce Park in Richmond Hill, Georgia.
Georgia Governor Kemp announced on November 11, 2022 that Freyr Battery is planning to invest about $1.6 billion in a new EV battery manufacturing plant in Newnan, Georgia (Coweta County).
The company is calling the Freyr Battery project “Giga America”.
The first phase of the Giga America project will be valued at $1.7 billion.
The company plans to start engineering work on the project in a few months from the November 2022 announcement.
Georgia provided financial incentives to attract the development of the Freyr Battery project.
Hyundai announced on May 22, 2022 its plan to build a $6.5 billion Hyundai EV Car Assembly Plant and EV battery Manufacturing Plant located just south of Savannah, Georgia.
Information regarding the Savannah plant is hosted on the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority website.
Over $5 billion of work related to the Savannah plant is available for bidding through the Joint Development Authority.

Invitations to bid for Savannah plant work

source_title: Georgia - Bid Notices - Welcome to US Public Works -

facts: Ascend Elements has opened its first commercial-scale lithium-ion battery recycling facility in Covington, Georgia.
The Massachusetts-based company claims its Base 1 facility cost $50 million to build.
Ascend Elements claims the Base 1 facility is the largest electric vehicle (EV) battery recycling facility in North America.
Partial operations at the facility began in August 2022.
The facility now has an annual capacity to process 30,000 metric tons of used lithium-ion batteries and manufacturing scrap.
This processing capacity is equivalent to 70,000 EV batteries per year.
The facility is powered by renewable energy.
The facility features onsite wastewater recycling and treatment systems.
Ascend Elements claims it can recover up to 98 percent of critical battery metals in used EV batteries and gigafactory manufacturing scrap using its patented Hydro-to-Cathode process.
The company states that recycling used lithium-ion batteries and scrap can decrease the carbon footprint of new EV battery cathode materials by up to 90 percent.
Mike O’Kronley, CEO of Ascend Elements, stated that the facility is an important part of America’s EV battery infrastructure and 'this is just the start.'
O’Kronley emphasized the industry's need to continue building a closed-loop battery materials supply chain to make electric vehicle batteries cleaner and more sustainable.
The company currently employs about 100 people at the facility.
Ascend Elements plans to increase the facility's workforce to 185 employees by 2024.
source_title: Ascend Elements opens Li-Ion battery Recycling facility in Georgia | Energy Tech

facts: Rivian’s planned production facility near Social Circle will produce a new SUV model expected to be more affordably priced than its current vehicles.
The company plans for the Georgia facility to cover 2,000 acres in Walton and Morgan counties.
The Georgia facility will serve as the production site for the future R2 platform, a more accessibly priced mid-sized SUV targeting global markets.
Prices are in the $70,000 range for new Rivian-produced vehicles.
A lower-priced model could allow it to better compete with other automakers that are planning or already offer electric vehicles in the $30,000 range.
CEO CJ Scaringe stated the California-based company was “on a clear path” to the beginning of production at its planned Georgia facility despite its stock falling to an all-time low recently.
The company announced that production would begin in 2024.
Scaringe said the company plans for its next new model, based on the R2 platform, to be produced beginning in 2025 at the new facility.
Analysts attributed the stock price decline to prominent investor Ford Motor Co. announcing it was selling around 8% of its Rivian shares.
The company reported a $1.59 billion net loss on revenue of $95 million in the first quarter of 2022.
Scaringe stated the company had $17 billion in cash on hand.
The company is working to overcome supply chain issues that have lowered vehicle production numbers at its Normal, Illinois, plant.
Supply chain issues at the Normal, Illinois, plant have lowered the company's profits.
Based on demonstrated production rates and progress, if all supply constraints were resolved, the Normal plant would run at two times the currently expected output for the remainder of 2022.
The company is producing vehicles at low volumes on production lines designed for higher volumes, experiencing negative gross profit related to significant labor and overhead costs.
The company expects negative gross profit dynamics to continue in the near term but improve on a per vehicle basis as production volumes ramp up faster than future labor and overhead cost increases.
The company is investing in technologies and capabilities at its current Normal, Illinois, facility that are designed to benefit future platforms, including R2.
The company signed an Economic Development Agreement with the state of Georgia and the four-county Joint Development Authority (JDA).
The agreement allowed Rivian to move forward with development of the Georgia plant.
The deal included $1.5 billion in local and state incentives for the project.
Rivian expects the new facility to have a transformative impact on the local economy and believes the investment represents compelling benefits for all stakeholders.
The agreement requires the company to produce at least 80% of the 7,500 jobs it promised by 2028.
The agreement requires the company to maintain the 7,500 jobs for 20 years or face the loss of its tax breaks.
The company will give the JDA payments in lieu of taxes (PILOTs) that the JDA will redistribute to the four counties that are part of the agency, including Newton.
There was no mention in the report of local opposition to construction from an organized group centered on Morgan County and the nearby city of Rutledge.

The opposing group has opposed construction for a variety of reasons: lack of transparency in negotiations, environmental concerns, potential loss of the area’s rural nature, and the state’s control of the project which allows it to bypass local reviews.
The state has vowed to comply with all environmental regulations and constraints and be as transparent as possible.
Opponents have regularly scoffed at the state's claim regarding compliance and transparency.
source_title: Rivian plans new SUV model for facility near Social Circle - The Covington News

facts: Ozburn Electrical is a family-owned electrical contracting company founded by Harold T. Ozburn in 1984.
The company provides commercial and industrial electrical services across Social Circle, Georgia; Monroe, Georgia; Walton County, GA; and metro Atlanta.

Ozburn Electrical offers EV Charging Stations, including Level 2 EV Chargers and Fast DC EV Charging, for industrial, commercial, manufacturing, retail, apartments, and office spaces in Social Circle, GA and Walton County.
The company provides Electric Vehicle (EV) Charger Installation services, specifically covering Level 2 and Level 3 Fast DC Charging.
Ozburn Electrical performs electrical work for assembly line and manufacturing operations.
The firm provides automation and PLC work tailored for manufacturing facilities.
All electrical installations and upgrades are performed in compliance with the National Electrical Code (NEC), State, County, Municipality, and Electrical Power Provider Guidelines.
Electricians complete a 2-year or 4-year electrical apprenticeship program established by the Independent Electrical Contractors Association (IEC).
The company works directly with facility managers to ensure EV charger and industrial electrical projects are completed correctly and within budget.
source_title: Electrician in Social Circle, GA | Ozburn Electrical

facts: HSBMA is a major development in Georgia's industrial sector.
HSBMA is a joint venture company between Hyundai Motor Group and SK On.
The joint venture has established an electric vehicle battery cell manufacturer covering 26,136,000 square feet at 5059 HWY 411, Kingston, Georgia 30145.
Choosing this location shows a focus on efficiency and innovation, with easy access to HMG's vehicle production site.
The factory will produce 50 million battery cells.
Production capacity supports around 300,000 electric vehicles.
HSBMA is preparing to fully launch in 2025.
HSBMA is committed to promoting sustainable transportation and boosting the local economy.
HSBMA was named "2023 Georgia’s Deal of the Year".
The company's mission is to drive the future of sustainable transportation by producing high-performance EV batteries using SK On’s cutting-edge battery technology.
source_title: HSBMA

facts: Freyr Battery was set to build a major manufacturing facility in Coweta County.
Freyr Battery must repay Georgia incentives after nixing its planned Coweta plant.

facts: Isuzu selected Greenville County, S.C., to establish its new U.S. production base.
The company's $280 million investment will create more than 700 new jobs.
Isuzu is a subsidiary of Isuzu Motors Limited, one of the world’s largest manufacturers of medium- and heavy-duty trucks.
Isuzu supplies commercial vehicles for the United States and Canadian markets.
Isuzu trucks entered the North American truck market in 1984.
The company sold 44,000 units in the last fiscal year, its highest total ever.
Isuzu purchased a 1 million-square-foot facility on over 200 acres of land on Augusta Road in Greenville County.
The company is converting the purchased facility into a state-of-the-art assembly plant to expand vehicle supply capabilities in North America.
The new plant will include a variable-model, variable-volume production line that produces both internal combustion engine vehicles and electric vehicles.
When completed, the plant will produce the company’s N-Series Gas, N-Series Electric, and F-Series Diesel trucks.
The completed plant will have a production capacity of 50,000 units annually.
source_title: Southern Auto Corridor - Winter 2025 | Southern Business and Development

facts: Norwegian battery company Freyr announced plans to build a $2.6 billion battery factory in Coweta County, Ga., in November 2022.
In February 2025, Freyr announced it was canceling the project and putting up for sale a large swath (368 acres) of woodlands it owns.
The property sale in Coweta County could garner $50 million.
Freyr will use part of the sale proceeds to pay back about $27 million it has already received in incentives for the project.
The property is located at Interstate 85 and Bethlehem Church Road, south of the Newnan-Coweta County Airport.
Freyr was set to receive a total of $358 million in grants and incentives for its clean energy tech project.
source_title: Southern Auto Corridor - Winter 2025 | Southern Business and Development

facts: Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama (HMMA) celebrated the grand opening of its new Care Center in the winter quarter.
The $14 million facility is designed to enhance the health, wealth and well-being of its 4,000 team members.
The Care Center is located at a new central site on the southwest corner of the general assembly building on the HMMA campus.

Chris Susock, HMMA’s President and CEO, stated the facility represents a commitment to its Alabama workforce and serves as a resource for wellness, fitness, physical therapy, fire prevention and emergency response.
source_title: Southern Auto Corridor - Winter 2025 | Southern Business and Development

facts: HydroFleet announced it will build a production and fueling station for heavy-duty trucks in Pooler, Ga., near the Port of Savannah and Hyundai’s massive new plant there.
The facility will be able to refuel about seven to 14 trucks a day.
The company plans to eventually expand this capacity to 50 trucks a day.
HydroFleet provides fleet solutions including forklifts and heavy-duty trucks, such as Hyundai Class 7 and 8 trucks, and hydrogen-powered charging.
HydroFleet will invest $33 million for the new facility.
source_title: Southern Auto Corridor - Winter 2025 | Southern Business and Development

facts: A nearly $6 billion federal loan, granted under the Biden administration, should help Rivian restart construction on a $5 billion, 7,500-job project east of Atlanta in Covington.
Rivian's announcement represented the largest development project by value in Georgia history.
The project status is precarious at best since Rivian's stock has tanked.
source_title: Southern Auto Corridor - Winter 2025 | Southern Business and Development

facts: Toyota Motor Manufacturing Kentucky (TMMK) announced in the winter quarter it will build a new advanced paint facility at its massive assembly plant in Georgetown, Ky.
Gov. Andy Beshear said Kentucky and Toyota have formed a winning partnership over the last 40 years that has been transformational for families throughout the commonwealth.
The new $922 million facility will add 1 million square feet of capacity while decreasing carbon emissions by 30 percent.
The paint facility project is scheduled to open in 2027.
The project follows a $1.3 billion investment announced in 2024 at the Georgetown site to bring assembly of an all-new, three-row electric SUV to the U.S. market in 2026.
Both plants directly support Toyota’s goal of zero carbon emissions by 2050.
Toyota is close to opening its $14 billion North Carolina battery plant.
Chief Financial Officer Yoichi Miyazaki provided a third-quarter financial report showing the Japanese automaker expects to open its battery plant in Randolph County, N.C., by the summer quarter.
The 5,000-employee plant is Toyota’s 11th manufacturing site in the United States and the first to make batteries for hybrid, plug-in hybrids, and battery all-electric vehicles in the United States.
source_title: Southern Auto Corridor - Winter 2025 | Southern Business and Development

facts: Shelbyville Battery Manufacturing announced the largest project in Shelby County (Louisville MSA) history in the winter quarter.
The project is a state-of-the-art, 6-gigawatt-hour battery cell and module plant in an existing 1 million-square-foot building in Shelbyville.
The $712 million plant will house almost 1,600 new workers.
source_title: Southern Auto Corridor - Winter 2025 | Southern Business and Development

facts: Murakami Manufacturing USA, a supplier of side-view exterior mirrors and automotive components, announced plans to expand its operation in Taylor County with a $3.4 million investment.
source_title: Southern Auto Corridor - Winter 2025 | Southern Business and Development

facts: The West Tennessee Ford plant being built near a site between Memphis and Jackson, Tenn., is still two years away from opening.
Electric pickups will be assembled at the West Tennessee site along with battery manufacturing.
Ford electric vehicle sales got off to a strong start in 2025, continuing to improve year over year after a record 2024.
The Michigan-based company sold more than 5,600 EVs in January 2025, a 24 percent increase from the same month a year prior.
Ford also sold more than 13,000 hybrid vehicles in January 2025, a 19 percent jump year over year.
Ford classes EVs and hybrids as “electrified” vehicles.
BlueOval SK’s Kentucky One plant should come online this year.
BlueOval SK’s Tennessee facility is scheduled to come online near the end of this year.
Those facilities are expected to significantly increase Ford’s EV production capacity.
source_title: Southern Auto Corridor - Winter 2025 | Southern Business and Development

facts: FREYR Battery, a developer of clean, next-generation battery cell production capacity, will create 723 new jobs over the next seven years in Coweta County.
The new facility will be located at the Bridgeport Industrial Park site and will use U.S.-based 24M Technologies to simplify the production process and increase manufacturing efficiency.

Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that FREYR Battery will invest $2.57 billion into Georgia’s sustainable technology ecosystem and create 723 new jobs over the next seven years at a manufacturing facility in Coweta County.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that job creators and innovators from all over the world bring their operations to Georgia because they know they will have success there.

Governor Brian Kemp expressed pride in welcoming FREYR to the Peach State as the latest company to bring transformational investments and opportunity, noting Georgia is the No. 1 state for business and looking forward to its impact on Coweta and the surrounding region.
FREYR is dedicated to supporting a domestic supply chain for renewable power sources.
The company’s battery cells can be used for stationary energy storage (ESS), electric mobility, and additional applications.
FREYR’s Co-Founder and Chief Executive Officer Tom Einar Jensen stated the company is deeply committed to decarbonizing the transportation and energy sectors with U.S. partners.

Tom Einar Jensen noted that as they advance their U.S. expansion plan with key stakeholders, they expect meaningful investments to spur job creation and eventual development of localized, decarbonized supply chains in the U.S. to enhance energy security and economic activity.
FREYR’s new facility will be located at the Bridgeport Industrial Park site, which has been zoned for a mix of heavy and light industrial use since 2014.
The company will be hiring for technicians, operators, and engineers as well as executive and administrative roles.
The facility, to be called Giga America, will have a planned first phase production of approximately 34 gigawatt hours.
The facility will use the U.S.-based 24M Technologies SemiSolidTM platform to simplify the production process and increase manufacturing efficiency.
FREYR is currently evaluating clean power supply solutions for the new facility, including the potential use of renewable energy from solar installations.

Coweta County Development Authority Chair John Daviston stated the authority is extremely pleased to welcome FREYR Battery and excited about the opportunities, quality highly-skilled jobs, and significant stimulus to the local economy.
Metro Atlanta Chamber President and CEO Katie Kirkpatrick welcomed FREYR Battery to metro Atlanta, noting it has quickly become a hub for battery cell research and production.
Katie Kirkpatrick stated FREYR will benefit from the vibrant research ecosystem in the region and the diversity of talent ready to support next-generation efforts.

Project Manager John Soper represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project in partnership with Coweta County Development Authority, the Metro Atlanta Chamber, Georgia EMC, and Georgia Quick Start.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated companies like FREYR are driving a global revolution based on sustainable technology and solutions.

Pat Wilson noted that electrification is changing how we power entire cities and that Georgia is developing a battery ecosystem with manufacturers, recyclers, and customers to support electrification in home and commercial storage.

Georgia’s prime location, extensive infrastructure, skilled workforce, and business-friendly climate has made it an attractive location for diverse rapidly developing industries focused on creating a sustainable future.
Georgia is cultivating a vertically integrated supply chain that will help companies increase efficiencies by reducing reliance on imported materials.
FREYR Battery aims to provide industrial scale clean battery solutions to reduce global emissions and is listed on the New York Stock Exchange.
FREYR’s mission is to produce green battery cells to accelerate the decarbonization of energy and transportation systems globally.
FREYR has commenced building the first of its planned factories in Mo i Rana, Norway.
FREYR announced potential development of industrial scale battery cell production in Vaasa, Finland, and the United States.
FREYR intends to install 50 GWh of battery cell capacity by 2025, 100 GWh annual capacity by 2028, and 200 GWh of annual capacity by 2030.
source_title: Battery Manufacturer to Invest $2.57B, Create Over 700 Jobs in Coweta County - Location Georgia

facts: K&D Electric helps businesses transition to electric mobility with professional EV charging station installation.
The company serves properties across Coweta County with Level 2 and DC fast-charging solutions for retail, office, fleet, and multi-family applications.
K&D Electric installs 208/240V charging stations for retail, office, and multi-family properties, which are ideal for overnight and workplace charging.
The company provides high-power 480V charging for fleet operations, public charging hubs, and highway corridor locations.
K&D Electric offers scalable charging solutions for commercial fleets, including depot charging and route optimization.
The company conducts comprehensive site evaluations to determine optimal charger placement and required electrical upgrades.
K&D Electric has deep expertise in commercial electrical, HVAC, and petroleum systems since 1994.
The company manages projects from concept to completion under a one-contractor model for total accountability.
K&D Electric provides full-service electrical and industrial HVAC under one roof for seamless coordination.
The company is City of Atlanta Small Business Enterprise certified for public-sector projects.
K&D Electric is fully licensed, bonded, and insured in Georgia and Florida.
The company handles new construction, tenant build-outs, and full rewiring.
K&D Electric performs 200A–400A+ service upgrades and load analysis.
The company installs commercial lighting, LED retrofits, and emergency systems.
K&D Electric provides standby generators for mission-critical facilities.
The company specializes in commercial EV charging and fleet infrastructure.
K&D Electric offers design-build engineering for new construction.

The Bainbridge location provides customers with information on available fuel types and updated prices.

facts: Club Car is headquartered in Augusta, Georgia.
Club Car is a global leader in golf, consumer, and utility vehicles with a history spanning over six decades.
Club Car serves as the Worldwide Supplier of the Ryder Cup, marking 26 years of partnership that began at Valderrama, Spain in 1997.
For the 2023 Ryder Cup held September 25 – October 1 at Marco Simone Golf & Country Club in Rome, Italy, Club Car assembled and delivered a fleet of 550 vehicles.
The 2023 Ryder Cup fleet included six red and six blue team cars for captains Luke Donald and Zach Johnson and their vice-captains.
The fleet also included hundreds of golf, turf, and utility cars supporting media, broadcasting, security, and medical transportation at the event.
Club Car prepared and worked towards the 2023 Ryder Cup event over a four-year period.
Kevin Hart is the Golf Sales Director for Club Car in Europe, Middle East, and Africa.
Paul Dunstan is the Ryder Cup Operations Director.
For the first time at the Ryder Cup, the majority of Club Car vehicles were charged using solar-powered stations on-site.
The Ryder Cup has a sustainability policy and commitment to be carbon neutral by 2040.
Club Car supplies many of the world’s top golf destinations with sustainable electric vehicles.
Club Car is an official supplier to the DP World Tour, PGA, and St Andrews Links Trust.
The Club Car product portfolio includes vehicles for commercial, personal, and street-legal low speed use.
The Club Car Vehicle Group includes the luxury electric vehicle brand Garia and the lightweight electric utility vehicle brand Melex.

facts: Power & Light Services LLC offers various electrical services supporting EV charging stations in the continental USA.
The company intends to provide impetus to the public to help spur the migration to the electric vehicle from reliance on the internal combustion engine.
Power & Light Services LLC offers QA/QC-type support in an effort to ensure that the electric vehicle charging infrastructure is dependable, reliable, and robust.
The company hopes to monitor and support charging stations across the continental USA so that more people will start to buy electric vehicles.
Power & Light Services LLC is committed to providing the driving public with support, quality control, and assistance.
Early experimental carriages were built in the 1830s.
The 1888 Flocken Elektrowagen is widely recognized as the first true four-wheeled electric car.
By the early 1900s, electric cars made up roughly a third of all vehicles on U.S. roads.
