facts: GLOVIS Georgia, LLC is a subsidiary of Hyundai GLOVIS and operates as a vital logistics and supply chain solutions provider strategically located in West Point, Georgia.
The company specializes in supporting the automotive manufacturing sector, particularly Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia (KMMG).
Services encompass inbound parts logistics, warehousing, sequencing, just-in-time (JIT) delivery, and finished vehicle logistics.

GLOVIS Georgia functions as the primary operational hub and headquarters, managing comprehensive logistics services including parts consolidation, sequencing, inventory management, and transportation for major regional automotive manufacturers.

The facility includes extensive warehousing equipped with advanced material handling systems, direct proximity to major automotive manufacturing plants, and specialized areas for parts sequencing and just-in-time delivery operations.
GLOVIS Georgia serves as a critical logistics linchpin for the Southeast automotive industry, directly supporting the KMMG plant and contributing significantly to regional supply chain efficiency.

Through its parent company Hyundai GLOVIS, it contributes to a global network providing ocean transportation, air freight, rail and road transportation, warehousing, supply chain consulting, and specialized logistics for automotive, steel, and heavy machinery across Asia-Pacific, the Americas, Europe, and the Middle East.
The headquarters address is 1000 GLOVIS Drive, West Point, GA, USA, with no additional office locations available.
As of April 2025, specific leadership details for GLOVIS Georgia LLC are not publicly listed, as executive changes are typically managed within the broader Hyundai GLOVIS corporate structure.

On February 2, 2024, Hyundai GLOVIS announced a dedicated U.S. corporation for electric vehicle (EV) battery collection and recycling, an initiative that will significantly utilize logistics capabilities in Georgia where GLOVIS Georgia LLC operates to support EV market growth and sustainability efforts.

On August 3, 2023, Hyundai GLOVIS announced expansion of its U.S. electric vehicle (EV) battery logistics business, with GLOVIS Georgia LLC expected to play a key role by providing battery transportation, storage, and pre-treatment for recycling using existing Georgia infrastructure.

On December 7, 2022, Hyundai GLOVIS announced a significant investment to open a new packing and logistics facility in Bryan County, Georgia, to support the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, highlighting its growing footprint in the state.
source_title: Where is GLOVIS Georgia LLC Located? HQ, Global Offices & Company Insights

facts: A Korean-speaking real estate agent moved to Pooler in 2023 to provide temporary housing for hundreds of workers building Georgia’s largest economic development project.

For nearly two years, temporary housing for Korean construction workers, technicians, and engineers near the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America site in Bryan County included mobile homes on rural plots, rented rooms in owner-occupied homes, and a makeshift bed and breakfast.
In September, the U.S. Department of Homeland Security raided the Ellabell electric vehicle factory and detained more than 300 Korean workers.
Construction on the EV battery plant halted alongside the raid, as many specialized employees worked there.
The real estate agent's temporary housing business came to a halt following the September raid.
Ripple effects from the federal raid impacted at least a half dozen small businesses in counties surrounding the metaplant.
Local business owners reported double-digit percentage drops in revenue after their Korean customers were detained.
Some local entrepreneurs who capitalized on the Korean workforce's arrival are now facing bankruptcy.
Hyundai Motor Group leadership stated commitment to expanding production and work opportunities at the metaplant despite acknowledging short-term struggles to increase output.
Bianca Johnson, HMGMA manager of public relations, declined to comment on disruptions to local businesses following the ICE raid.
Georgia and southeast coastal counties are subsidizing Hyundai and its suppliers with $2.1 billion in tax breaks, construction costs, discounted land, and other perks.
Governor Brian Kemp announced the subsidy deal would produce good-paying jobs for Georgians.
Hyundai Metaplant, the LG joint-venture battery facility, and five affiliated Hyundai suppliers must collectively invest $5.545 billion and hire at least 8,500 workers by 2031.
The hired workers must earn an average annual salary of $58,105.
The created jobs must be retained until 2048.
Chip Johnson, CEO of the Turnstone Group, invested in the only hotel at the I-16 exit leading to the Hyundai plant, expecting steady customers during construction.
Since opening in July 2025, the hotel has received only one booking from a metaplant worker.
Last year, owners of Savannah's Viet Huong grocery store purchased an old Piggly Wiggly in Ellabell across the highway from the Hyundai plant to stock goods for the Korean workforce.
For nearly a year, the Ellabell grocery store served as a preferred meeting spot for Korean workers buying snacks and lunch in the parking lot before work and groceries for dinner afterward.
Following the federal raid, the Ellabell grocery store's customer base dropped to a daily trickle.
Local Bryan County residents showed no interest in Korean food, leaving the store with little cash flow to restock.
A Korean American-owned restaurant in Pooler experienced spoiled inventory after visiting Korean engineers and managers stopped coming following the raid.
By the end of September, the anonymous Pooler restaurant owner's weekly income dropped by 18%.
The restaurant owner, who originally came to Georgia as a Hyundai worker, is uncertain about keeping his business open.
Fear from the raid caused additional Korean workers to return home, and new arrivals from Korea stopped coming entirely.
A second Pooler restaurant previously hosted groups of 20 to 30 Korean workers daily but now rarely sees groups larger than five.
The second Pooler restaurant owner noted that provided food is going to waste because customers stopped eating there.
Hispanic construction workers and their families also stopped visiting the second Pooler restaurant, likely due to fear from the raid.
A Hispanic employee at the second Pooler restaurant was shaken after a traffic stop where police requested her immigration documents.
Hyundai announced a goal to increase metaplant production from 300,000 to 500,000 vehicles annually.
Current metaplant output is limited to one shift per day.
The LG joint-venture battery plant will not open until next year at the earliest due to difficulties finding qualified personnel to finish the high-tech production line build-out.
Partnered construction companies are struggling to replace specialized Korean labor with local hires.
A supplier site manager confirmed the battery plant has no backup plan for completing construction.
On Oct. 1, Reuters reported that the U.S. agreed to allow South Korean workers with existing temporary visas to work on equipment at U.S. investment sites like the Hyundai metaplant.
Korean suppliers are working to improve their reputation for compliance with U.S. labor laws and environmental requirements.
Multiple Korean suppliers and subcontractors previously failed to report workplace accidents at the metaplant in violation of U.S. law.
On Oct. 8, Chris Susock, Hyundai Motor North America’s chief manufacturing officer, suggested creating a steering committee for the Joint Development Board of county officials.
The proposed steering committee would include CEOs and senior leadership from all metaplant companies to improve communication and planning.
Chris Susock stated the committee's purpose is to set policy, guidance, and procedures for site management, covering safety, security, operations, communications, and public relations.
It remains unclear how the new steering committee will impact employment recruitment across the Hyundai supply chain.
One month after the raid, HMGMA hosted a job fair at Savannah Technical College.
More than 350 job seekers attended the HMGMA job fair at Savannah Technical College seeking stability amid recent International Paper mill closures and timber industry upheaval.
Cody Bennington, a truck driver from Millen, attended the job fair seeking a stable career.
Dasia Mosley, recently laid off from Daniel Defense (located across I-16 from the metaplant), is willing to commute an hour to work at the plant for a good salary.
Hyundai Glovis employee Jazzlyn Javis reported severe retention issues, noting she trained nearly 500 people over four years but only five remain employed there.
Jazzlyn Javis stated they lack sufficient staff to complete work and cannot achieve stability while constantly losing employees.
Bruce Hall, a 62-year-old veteran from Pooler, viewed the job fair as evidence of Hyundai's long-term commitment to the community.
The real estate agent moved back to Atlanta at the end of September due to a foreseeable long-term regional slowdown tied to Hyundai's operations.
The real estate agent retains hope for a second golden period in the next couple of years if visa regulations relax and Hyundai resumes flying in an immigrant workforce from South Korea.
The real estate agent stated that all revenue plans in Coastal Georgia have stopped with no known timeline for resumption.
source_title: ICE raid leaves ripple effects across Coastal Georgia communities - The Current

entity_name: Hyundai-LG Energy Solution EV Battery Plant Joint Venture
canonical_name: Hyundai-LG Energy Solution Georgia EV Battery Plant Joint Venture
facts: Hyundai and LG Energy Solution will collaborate to create a $4.3 billion electric car battery plant in Bryan County, Savannah, Georgia.
The new plant is expected to create 3,000 jobs.
The plant site will be adjacent to the currently under-construction Hyundai EV "Meta Plant."
Both companies signed a joint venture memorandum to produce electric vehicle batteries in the United States to accelerate electrification efforts in North America.
The agreement was signed at LG Energy Solution's headquarters in Seoul on May 26th.
Youngsoo Kwon, CEO of LG Energy Solution, and Jaehoon Chang, President and CEO of Hyundai Motor Company, were present at the signing.
$4.3 billion will be invested in the project, with each company holding a 50% stake in the joint venture.
The joint venture plans to begin construction on the project in the second half of 2023.
The plant is scheduled to produce batteries by the end of 2025.
The yearly production capacity of the plant will be 30GWh and will have the potential to support 300,000 EVs.
Hyundai believes its annual capacity will increase to 500,000 EV productions later.
Hyundai Mobis will assemble these cells into battery packs and supply them to Hyundai production units for Kia and Genesis EV models.
Hyundai and LG Energy Solution have worked together in the EV battery sector since 2009 when Hyundai unveiled its Elantra Hybrid EV.
LG Energy Solution provided batteries for the Hyundai Elantra and later contributed batteries to the Kona Electric and IONIQ 6 EVs.
Both companies began constructing a separate $1.1 billion battery cell manufacturing plant in Karawang, Indonesia, in September 2021.
The Indonesian project is scheduled to begin operating in the first part of 2024.

Under the US Inflation Reduction Act, EVs must be built in North America with a specific portion of battery components and minerals originating in North America or a US free trade partner to qualify for a $7,500 tax credit.
Hyundai's current EVs, including Kia and Genesis models, do not qualify for the tax credits as they do not meet domestic manufacturing requirements.
Democratic US Senator Jon Ossoff of Georgia stated that the IRA's manufacturing incentives continue to bring jobs and investment to Georgia.
Senator Ossoff added that his goal remains to make Georgia the world leader in advanced energy production.
Jaehoon Chang stated that the battery plant has the potential to "create a strong foundation to lead the global EV transition."
Chang further stated that Hyundai Motor Group is focusing on its electrification efforts to secure a leadership position in the global auto industry.
Youngsoo Kwon stated, "Two strong leaders in the auto and battery Industries have joined hands, and together we are ready to drive the EV transition in America."
Kwon added that LG Energy Solution will commit its best efforts to offering ultimate sustainable energy solutions by advancing product competitiveness and global operational expertise.
source_title: Hyundai, LG Energy to Build $4.3b EV Battery Plant in Georgia

canonical_name: LG Energy Solution - Hyundai Motor Group Joint Venture Battery Plant
facts: US ICE authorities arrested around 475 people during a raid at an in-construction battery plant jointly operated by Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution in Georgia.
Just under 300 of those detained were South Korean nationals who were aiding in the construction.
Following the incident, LG instructed its employees to suspend all business trips to the US.
LG directed employees already in the US to either remain in their accommodations or return to South Korea immediately.
LG Energy Solution and South Korean Foreign Minister Cho Hyun responded to the raid, with Minister Cho calling an emergency meeting in Seoul.
South Korean officials have been in contact with US authorities to push for the release and safe return of detained South Korean nationals.
A plane has been chartered to fly the detained South Korean citizens home.
LG issued an official statement confirming the suspension of further business trips to the US and warning workers to leave or stay secure in their lodgings.
The incident is expected to continue damaging US and foreign relations regarding trade and economics.
The raid has stoked anti-immigration and anti-business sentiments, compounding pressures from Trump's tariffs and leaving many in South Korea outraged.
South Korea has invested billions into American industries, including a $26 billion investment promised by Hyundai from 2025 to 2028.
South Korean Foreign Minister Cho Hyun stated that Korea is 'deeply concerned and feel a heavy sense of responsibility over the arrests of our nationals.'
Minister Cho Hyun warned that the incident may negatively affect business between the US and South Korea in the short term.
In a press conference this week, Minister Cho Hyun stated that if the completion of the Hyundai factory is delayed, the United States will suffer significant losses.
Construction of the joint Hyundai and LG battery plant in Georgia is currently on pause due to the raid.
source_title: LG instructs employees to suspend U.S. business trips following ICE raid at Georgia EV battery plant | Shacknews

entity_name: Hyundai-LG Energy Solution Georgia EV Battery Plant Joint Venture
canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution Joint Venture
facts: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution have announced plans to construct a $4.3 billion electric vehicle (EV) battery plant in the United States.
The joint venture aims to capitalize on tax credits and adhere to new U.S. sourcing requirements for EV battery components and critical minerals.
By meeting these criteria, buyers of Hyundai and Kia vehicles will qualify for up to $7,500 in tax credits under the Inflation Reduction Act.
Currently, vehicles from Hyundai Motor and its sister company, Kia Corp, are not eligible for these tax credits.
Hyundai and LG Energy Solution will commence construction of the battery plant in the state of Georgia in the second half of 2023.
Battery production is expected to commence by the end of 2025.
The plant will boast an annual production capacity of 30 gigawatt-hours (GWh).
The 30 GWh capacity is sufficient to power approximately 300,000 EVs.
The new plant in Georgia will be established in Bryan County.
Bryan County is where Hyundai’s existing joint factory with LG Energy Solution is located.
Both companies will own an equal 50% stake in this joint venture.
LG Energy Solution is a key supplier to prominent automakers like Tesla and General Motors.
The collaboration between these two industry leaders signifies a significant step towards driving the EV transition in the United States.
LG Energy Solution’s CEO, Youngsoo Kwon, expressed confidence in this partnership and emphasized their commitment to accelerating electrification efforts in America.
In April, Hyundai Motor finalized a separate $5 billion EV battery joint venture with SK On, the battery unit of SK Innovation Co Ltd.
This separate JV further reinforces Hyundai's commitment to advancing electrification in its largest market.
Hyundai Motor Group is currently the world’s third-largest automaker by vehicle sales.
Hyundai Motor Group has been actively investing in EV and battery manufacturing facilities.
source_title: Hyundai & LG Energy to Build $4.3 Billion EV Battery Plant in US : Automotive Addicts

facts: Groundbreaking of secondary smelter for multimetal recycling in the US occurred on June 18, 2022, in Augusta, Richmond County, Georgia.
Total investment in Augusta, Richmond County in Georgia is estimated at €300 million (approx. $320 million).
The plant is expected to be fully operational by mid-2024.
Plant operational in the first half of 2024 and creates over 120 new jobs directly.
Capacity to annually process up to 90,000 tons of complex metals containing recycling materials, including printed circuit boards.
Aurubis’ metals are crucial enablers to promote global market trends such as renewable energy, digitalization and electric vehicles.
Strong focus on environmental protection: State-of-the-art technology exceeds highest State of Georgia and federal government’s environmental standards.

Representing one of the biggest international investments in the state of Georgia, 150-year-old, German-based Aurubis AG broke ground today in the United States’ first large secondary smelter for multimetal recycling.
Once fully on-line, Aurubis Richmond, USA, will have the capacity to process about 90,000 tons of complex recycling materials annually.
Processed materials include printed circuit boards, copper cables, and other recycling materials containing metals such as copper, nickel, tin, and other industrial and precious metals.
Currently, much of these materials in the US are either shipped to Asia and Europe or trucked to local landfills.
The US currently produces about 6 million tons of recycling materials that contain valuable metals.
Copper is 100-percent recyclable without any loss in performance.
Aurubis’ facility will both reduce waste, and direct key metals to industry including renewables such as solar and wind, along with electric vehicle and battery manufacturers.
Further, Aurubis’ state-of-the-art environmental technology meets or even exceeds the highest State of Georgia and the federal government’s standards.
Aurubis Richmond will have a very low impact on the air quality in the region (less than 0.2 % share of dust emissions in Richmond county).
Governor Brian Kemp congratulated our state and local economic development teams on bringing this sustainability-focused company to Augusta.

As the largest single investment made by a German company in Georgia history, this groundbreaking represents what is made possible in the Peach State by our strong partnerships and world-class business assets.
Aurubis’ Georgia facility is expected to become a major player in the North American supply chain and a significant employer in greater Augusta, GA.
It is also expected to further elevate Aurubis’ gold-standard leadership for major multi-metal recycling and environmental stewardship across the world.
source_title: Aurubis AG starts construction of a € 300 million multimetal recycling plant in Augusta, Georgia

facts: Germany-headquartered Aurubis announced on Nov. 10 its project to build a secondary smelter in Augusta, Ga.
Aurubis is one of the largest copper recyclers in the world and stated it will be the first secondary smelter for multi-metal recycling in the U.S.
This announcement marks the second major downstream e-scrap project in Georgia in recent weeks, following Igneo Technologies' plan to build a large metals recovery plant in the same area.
Igneo Technologies is connected to French e-scrap metals recycling company WEEE Metallica.
The Augusta facility will process 90,000 metric tons of recovered materials annually.
At full capacity, the facility will produce up to 35,000 metric tons of blister copper per year.
Blister copper is an intermediate copper product that is partly purified and has a blistered surface from the smelting process.
The smelter will use metallurgical technologies described as proven in the market yet still innovative.
The Georgia facility is located a two-hour-and-15-minute drive from the Port of Savannah.
The plant will ship some intermediate metal products to Aurubis's European smelters for further refining into pure industrial and precious metals.
The Georgia plant also expects to sell some of its output to U.S. companies for further processing.
The new site is located in Richmond County, Ga., and will be named Aurubis Richmond, USA.
Aurubis plans to invest about 300 million euros (worth about $345 million at Nov. 10 exchange rates) in the project.
The facility will create over 100 jobs.
The site will be built on 150 acres at the Augusta Corporate Park, according to an announcement from the governor’s office.

Aurubis CEO Roland Harings stated that the investment sets a clear example of sustainable growth and positions the company as a forerunner for multi-metal recycling in the U.S., mirroring its existing role in Europe.
Construction is scheduled to begin in summer 2022.
Commissioning is set for early 2024.
Full production capacity could be reached in the 2025-26 fiscal year, according to Aurubis.
source_title: Aurubis says it's going to build a US e-scrap smelter - Resource Recycling

facts: German-based Aurubis AG has broken on its latest investment – a new plant for multimetal recycling in Augusta, Richmond County in Georgia.
The company is investing an estimated EUR 300 million in the project.
The plant is expected to be fully operational by mid-2024.

Roland Harings, CEO of Aurubis AG, stated that the company has doubled down on its commitments to use its technology and experience to become a forerunner for recycling valuable materials containing copper, nickel, tin, and other industrial and precious metals locally in the US.
Once fully on-line, Aurubis Richmond, USA will have the capacity to process about 90,000 tons of complex recycling materials annually.
The processed materials include printed circuit boards, copper cables, and other recycling materials containing metals.
Currently, much of these materials in the US are either shipped to Asia and Europe or trucked to local landfills.
Roland Harings noted that the US currently produces about 6 million tons of recycling materials that contain valuable metals.
Aurubis aims to take advantage of this potential and help ensure that these materials get reused as critical input materials for many industries in the US.
Copper is 100% recyclable without any loss in performance.
Secondary smelter recycling at an industrial scale requires significant long-term investment.
source_title: Aurubis starts building its €300M multimetal recycling plant

facts: Aurubis AG is a leading global provider of non-ferrous metals and one of the world’s largest copper recyclers.
The company hosted its Capital Market Day in London on Wednesday, October 8, 2025.
CEO Dr. Toralf Haag outlined a revised strategy titled “Performance 2030: Forging resilience. Leading in multimetal” to guide the company’s future growth and market position.

Aurubis is positioned to capitalize on structural demand surges for copper and multimetals driven by electrification, AI, advanced technology, energy infrastructure modernization, and geopolitical shifts.
The company can recover 20 different metals and elements from a wide range of primary and secondary raw materials.
Aurubis operates an integrated smelter network across Europe and the US to provide scale, flexibility, and operational resilience.
The company is shifting its focus from strong investment-driven growth to delivering impact from investments and pursuing smart, targeted growth aligned with core strengths.

Initiatives include expanding commercial reach and service level to access new material streams, strengthening existing ones, and doubling down on securing product competitiveness to maximize profitability.
Aurubis will boost its position as a leading global multimetal provider by maximizing technical expertise and optimizing its smelter portfolio.

A focus on operational excellence will allow Aurubis to create more efficient material flows, alleviate system bottlenecks, and draw on internal expertise in processing low-grade material to maximize multimetal yields and process more complex intermediates in-house.
The company expects to consistently grow its earnings and cash flow generation with an emphasis on strengthening resilience and profitability.
Aurubis presented an update on its US expansion, highlighting the start of production at a new, first-of-its-kind multimetal recycling facility in Richmond, Georgia.
The Richmond facility represents a major milestone and is Aurubis’s largest investment outside Europe at approximately €740 million.
The facility significantly increases recycling capacity and secures Aurubis’s strong position in one of the most attractive global metals markets.
The site is designed to meet surging North American demand for sustainable metal supply driven by infrastructure investment, supportive industrial policy, and resource security needs.
CEO Dr. Toralf Haag stated that Aurubis built the first greenfield smelter in the US in over a century and considers their timely entry into the US market a strong foundation for further growth.
Financial guidance for fiscal year 2025/26 includes operating EBITDA between €580 million and €680 million.
Guidance for 2025/26 includes operating EBT between €300 million and €400 million, aligned with market expectations.
Operating ROCE guidance for 2025/26 is set between 7% and 9%.
Net cash flow guidance for 2025/26 ranges from €640 million to €740 million.
The company targets a free cash flow break-even before dividend for fiscal year 2025/26.
Mid-term financial outlook commits to a maximum net leverage of 3.0 and an equity ratio above 40%.
Aurubis has set a long-term ROCE target of 15%.
The company introduced a mid-term dividend payout ratio of up to 30% of operating consolidated net income after taxes.
For fiscal year 2024/25, the company aims for a dividend payout ratio of 25% of operating consolidated net income after taxes.
CFO Steffen Hoffmann commented that Aurubis combines strong cash generation with a very solid balance sheet and multiple earnings drivers underpinning its business model strength.
Corporate Communications contacts listed are Christoph Tesch (Head of Corporate Communications) and Meino Hauschildt (Manager Corporate Communications, Spokesperson).
source_title: Aurubis sets course for multimetal growth and stronger returns

facts: Duckyang is opening a $10 million facility in Georgia.
Georgia has already started attracting more manufacturers in the electric vehicle supply chain.
SK Innovation’s massive battery plant is gearing up for production.
EV battery recycling is sparking opportunities for startups.
Georgia is primed to become a leader in the electric vehicle industry.
SK Innovation's Georgia battery plant will employ 1,000 by the end of the year.
An automotive parts manufacturer is adding 678 jobs in a Georgia expansion.

facts: Governor Brian Kemp announced that Seoyon E-Hwa, a global auto parts supplier, would bring almost $76 million and more than 740 new jobs to Chatham County through a new manufacturing facility.
The move would nearly double Georgia employment for the company.

Governor Brian Kemp stated: "When I announced the largest economic development project in Georgia history last year, I promised it was just the start in bringing even more jobs and opportunity to hardworking Georgians."

Governor Brian Kemp added: "As we work to ensure our state is the e-mobility capital of the nation, projects like this will continue to choose the No. 1 state for business and benefit communities in just about every zip code of Georgia."
The new facility is slated to open at the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center.
The facility will produce automotive interior parts as early as October 2024.
This location marks Georgia's second Seoyon facility.
The company's first location in LaGrange is expected to continue supporting 630 full time jobs there.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated: "With its second Georgia facility, Seoyon is committing to nearly doubling the number of jobs it supports in the state and extending its partnership with our business community."

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson noted that Hyundai’s new EV facility is drawing an experienced and dedicated supplier network to the region, adding to the state’s industry expertise and shaping a larger skilled workforce.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson expressed excitement to work with partners across the state and near Savannah to create future jobs.
More than two-thirds of the new jobs represent permanent roles in human resources, development, quality, and production personnel.
Interested applicants can learn more about Seoyon E-HWA by visiting their career page.
Seoyon E-Hwa Co., Ltd. is a Korean automotive parts company established under the name Hanil E-Hwa Co., Ltd. in 1972.

The company specializes in supplying interior parts like door trim, headlining, seats, C/PAD, bumpers and other parts for car companies including Hyundai Motor Group, Kia, Mercedes-Benz, Ford, Volkswagen, and others.
Seoyon operates 32 corporations across North America, South America, Europe, and Asia.
On July 2, 2014, the company split and began a spin-off new establishment under its current name.
In Gov. Kemp's 2023 State of the State Address, he pledged to focus on job growth specifically in manufacturing electric vehicles.
The governor announced plans to bring two giant electric vehicle (EV) assembly plants to Georgia.
Officials hope to build on $23 billion in announced projects to make Georgia the center of electrified transportation.
The governor's office reported that more than 35 EV-related projects have announced over $21 billion in investment and 26,700 jobs in Georgia since 2020.
On October 25, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America broke ground in Bryan County.
The $5.5 billion manufacturing plant is the first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing plant for the country.
Offsite suppliers connected to the Hyundai project have already exceeded the $1 billion in investment predicted from other locations around the state by more than $600 million.
In September 2022, Georgia’s EV Infrastructure Deployment Plan was approved after the Biden Administration announced that $5 billion would be spent putting charging stations on major highways.

The Georgia Department of Transportation announced on July 21, 2022 that U.S. 441 from Cornelia in northeast Georgia to Dublin in east central Georgia is the strip of highway where the first set of charging will go.
U.S. 82 from Brunswick in coastal Georgia to Albany in southeast Georgia is designated as the second highway for charging infrastructure.
source_title: Over 700 auto parts manufacturing jobs coming to Georgia | FOX 5 Atlanta

facts: A fifth automobile parts supplier announced Tuesday that it will open a factory in coastal Georgia to support Hyundai Motor Group’s future $5.54 billion electric vehicle plant.
Sewon America committed to a $300 million factory in Rincon.
The factory will employ 740 workers, according to a news release.
It is the first automobile supplier announced for Effingham County.
Sewon America’s announcement means parts-makers are in the pipeline in each of the four counties surrounding Hyundai’s future factory.
Gov. Brian Kemp calls Hyundai’s future factory the largest economic development project in Georgia history.
Gov. Brian Kemp said: “Today we not only celebrate delivering on our promise to create jobs in all four counties in the JDA, but also the growth of a valued partner in our business community.”
The JDA refers to the Savannah Harbor Interstate-16 Joint Development Authority.
Sewon America is a Korea-based company.
The facility will be located at the Savannah River Plant site near Rincon.
It will provide new component parts for Hyundai's future EV plant.
source_title: Sewon becomes latest supplier to Hyundai’s Georgia EV plant

facts: Sewon Company was founded in 1991 in Pyeongtaek-si, South Korea.
The company specializes in manufacturing critical automotive parts and supplies major automotive manufacturers globally.
Corporate headquarters are located at 56, Sandan-ro 64beon-gil, Pyeongtaek-si, Gyeonggi-do, South Korea.
Early product focus included capacitors, radiator caps, and head brackets for air conditioning systems.
As of March 31, 2025, Sewon Co., Ltd. reported a trailing 12-month revenue of $158 million.
For fiscal year 2024, Sewon reported revenue of $149.98 million, EBITDA of $18.019 million, and net income of $5.893 million.
Sewon Corporation (KOSDAQ:024830) recorded Q1 2025 revenue of 45.46 billion KRW, representing 8.09% growth.
Trailing twelve-month revenue for Sewon Corporation reached 234.31 billion KRW, up 26.55% year-over-year.
In 2024, annual revenue for Sewon Corporation was 230.91 billion KRW, showing 19.61% growth.
Sewon Precision Industry Co., Ltd. (KRX:021820) reported trailing 12-month revenue of $116 million as of March 31, 2025, and fiscal year 2024 revenue of $121.76 million.
Early product launches included oil filter modules, spin-on and ECO oil filters, air and fuel filters, air intake systems, and transmission, suction, and cabin filters.
Sewon Precision Industry specializes in automotive stamping parts such as dash, radiator, and side inner panel assemblies, along with cowl crossbar assemblies.
In February 2023, Sewon America announced an investment exceeding $300 million for a new manufacturing plant in Rincon, Georgia.
The Rincon facility is set to begin operations in 2025 and will produce EV body parts for OEMs including Hyundai Motor Group’s Metaplant America.
The new Rincon plant is expected to create 740 new jobs.
This facility marks Sewon America's second U.S. location, following its first facility in LaGrange, Georgia which opened in 2008 and has since doubled in size.
Sewon Precision Industry Co., Ltd. was founded in Daegu, South Korea in 1985, specializing in automotive body parts and molds.
Sewon Co., Ltd. (Pyeongtaek) was listed on the KOSDAQ stock exchange in 2015.
In March 2024, Sewon Co.,Ltd released full year 2024 earnings with an EPS of ₩147.
The first shipment from the Rincon, Georgia facility to the new Hyundai Metaplant is expected in October 2024.
New equipment installed at Sewon America-Rincon enables the production of lighter aluminum components.
The shift to aluminum components aims to reduce vehicle weight and enhance safety by minimizing spark output during welding.
Sewon is strategically focusing on electric vehicle (EV) components to meet growing demand from major clients like Hyundai Motor Group.
The company faces challenges including maintaining profitability, adapting to rapid technological advancements, and managing global supply chain complexities.
The Rincon facility is expected to support a total of 1,600 jobs in Georgia.
source_title: What is Brief History of Sewon Company? – businessmodelcanvastemplate.com

facts: Sewon America will invest more than $300 million into a new manufacturing facility in Rincon.
The investment will bring more than 700 jobs to Effingham County.
Bryan, Bulloch, and Chatham Counties each have a Hyundai supplier committed to building a facility in their county.
Effingham County joins Bryan, Bulloch, and Chatham Counties as the fourth location for a Hyundai supplier in the JDA.
Gov. Brian Kemp announced the Sewon America investment on Tuesday.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated that the announcement celebrates delivering on the promise to create jobs in all four counties in the JDA and the growth of a valued partner in the business community.
Gov. Brian Kemp had previously congratulated Chairman Kim on the company's then-recent expansion during his first international trip as governor to Korea.
During that trip, Gov. Kemp discussed how they could continue to work together with Chairman Kim and Sewon America.
Those discussions led to the announcement of Sewon America continuing to grow their Georgia operations.
source_title: Effingham County gets first Hyundai supplier for Bryan County megasite

facts: Hanon Systems announced a commitment to build a factory in Gateway Regional Industrial Park.
The announcement brings the number of tier-one Hyundai Motor Group suppliers locating in Bulloch County to three.
It also brings the number of new manufacturers of all types building in the county to five.
The five projects' job creation commitments add up to a projected 1,566 employees.
Their promised capital investments within Bulloch total $937 million.
All five projects have been announced within 16 months.

All but one of the projects were announced in the 12 months since Hyundai's electric vehicle Meta Plant America was revealed for the four-county Joint Development Authority site in northern Bryan County.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp’s office issued a news release around 1:30 p.m. Tuesday announcing Hanon's commitment.
Hanon is a maker of “automotive thermal and energy management” equipment, which includes air-conditioning components.
Hanon will invest more than $40 million in the factory.
Hanon will create 160 jobs.
The governor's news release was datelined “Israel” because Kemp is on a weeklong visit there.
Hanon Systems, like Hyundai Motor Group, is based in South Korea.
The Development Authority of Bulloch County (DABC) met at its headquarters upstairs at the Queensborough National Bank, Statesboro branch to discuss the project.
DABC members went into closed session to discuss a memorandum of understanding with Hanon and unanimously approved it upon reopening.

Billy Allen, chair of the DABC, stated that Statesboro-Bulloch County is honored by Hanon's decision and excited to welcome another automotive supplier working closely with the Hyundai Metaplant and electric vehicle industry.
Steve Rushing, attorney for the Development Authority, summarized the contents of the memorandum before the board voted.
Hanon’s new facility will be built next to the Cardinal Glass plant at the Gateway site on 34 acres provided by the DABC in a lease-to-own arrangement.
The DABC is selling the land at a discount rather than giving it away, similar to other Hyundai suppliers locating in the area.
After internally valuing the land at $50,000 an acre, the DABC offered it to Hanon for $25,000 an acre.
The company will pay over time $850,000 for a site the DABC considered to be worth $1.7 million.
By maintaining legal ownership of the land and facility for 10 years, the tax-exempt DABC will provide the site free of county government property tax to Hanon for that period.
The company is expected to make payments to the county equal to the Bulloch County Board of Education share of property tax and the fire protection district tax.
The DABC agrees to provide temporary facilities, such as office space, free to the company up to a rental value of $10,000.
The DABC will provide $20,000 worth of additional workforce training through Ogeechee Technical College, separate from state Quick Start program training.
The Development Authority already funded an initial environmental study of the site at a cost of $17,625.
The DABC is to apply for an EDGE Grant from the OneGeorgia Authority for up to $525,000 to assist Hanon.
The DABC will make its bond-issuance power available to help finance the company’s investment in Bulloch.
In exchange for incentives, the company is expected to meet the announced capital investment and job creation goals.
Average wages are expected to be at least equal to Bulloch County’s average wage of $21.65 an hour, as stated by the DABC.
Steve Rushing noted that if there is a shortfall in meeting goals, there is a clawback calculation where the company would pay back a portion of the incentives.
The authority and company expect to close on the deal by July 31.
The governor’s announcement states that the plant is expected to begin operations in May 2024.
Gateway Regional Industrial Park is located on the west side of U.S. Highway 301 south of Statesboro and is the authority’s oldest current industrial park.
A surge of new industries has filled the newer Bruce Yawn Commerce Park on the other side of U.S. 301 at the I-16 interchange, prompting the DABC to buy additional sites.
DABC CEO Benjy Thompson acknowledged that total promised jobs have increased in a county with a low unemployment rate.
Thompson stated they are constantly looking for real estate opportunities matching community growth and working with partners to ensure the workforce is trained and skilled to fill incoming jobs.
Timelines for construction of the factories are short.
The Hyundai Motor Group Meta Plant itself is projected to start production in January 2025 and eventually employ 8,100 people.
Thompson noted that job creation numbers are for plants operating at full capacity, which will take time to reach.
Thompson emphasized that companies won't open next year and immediately be at full capacity, requiring time to settle and ramp up over time.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated that Georgia’s automotive and e-mobility industries continue to grow and that the state furthers its position as the EV capital of America with new jobs coming to Georgians.
Aspen Aerogels was announced on Feb. 17, 2022, with construction underway on a 90-acre site in Bruce Yawn Commerce Park.

Aspen Aerogels has committed to a $325 million investment and at least 250 manufacturing jobs, and will make aerogel insulation used in and around electric vehicle batteries, though it is not linked to the Hyundai factory.

Ecoplastic America Corporation, a supplier of injection-molded plastic automotive body parts to Hyundai Motor Group, intends to build a $205 million plant that will create 456 jobs phased in over eight years on a DABC-owned site south of Statesboro where site preparation is underway.

Ajin USA, a supplier of metal auto body parts to Hyundai, has committed to invest $317 million and eventually employ 630 people at its Joon Georgia plant on an 83-acre site beside the Aspen Aerogels location in Bruce Yawn Commerce Park.

revalyu Resources, headquartered in Germany and not connected to Hyundai, plans to build a PET plastic-bottle recycling plant on a 43-acre site in Gateway Regional Industrial Park that is expected to create 71 jobs and require a $50 million investment in Phase 1.
Hanon, Ecoplastic, and Ajin are all tier-one suppliers because they deal directly with Hyundai Motor Group, whereas a tier-two supplier would supply materials to a tier-one supplier.
source_title: Hanon brings Bulloch’s developing industries to 5; jobs total tops 1,500 - Coastal Courier

facts: Hanon Systems announced the development of the world’s first fourth-generation heat pump system for electric vehicle thermal management.
The new heat pump system is the first to employ a parallel heat source recovery method that uses waste heat from the motor and battery along with heat from external air.
Recovered heat from the system is used for heating, cooling, and battery temperature management to significantly enhance EV driving range.
The modular heat pump system incorporates multiple patented or patent-pending technologies.
The system integrates the Refrigerant Control Module (RCM) and Coolant Control Module (CCM) into a unified control unit for comprehensive thermal system management.
Hanon Systems pioneered the mass production of heat pump systems with waste heat recovery in 2015.
The company’s second-generation heat pump system was launched in 2018 and added battery thermal management functions.
The third-generation system was developed in 2021, applied to a client’s EV-dedicated platform, and was the first to incorporate an 800-V electric compressor to reduce EV charging times.
The fourth-generation heat pump system was first implemented in the Kia EV3 in South Korea.
Hanon Systems plans to expand production of the fourth-generation heat pump system to the US and Europe.

Subu Nagasubramony, co-CEO of Hanon Systems, stated that continually refining their heat pump design improves efficiency and reduces power consumption requirements, contributing to extended EV driving range.
Hanon Systems is the world’s second-largest thermal management company.
The company introduced Thin HVAC technology, a world first that reduces the overall HVAC system size by approximately 30%.
The reduced HVAC system size minimizes exposure under the dashboard, providing more passenger legroom while ensuring a comfortable driving experience.
source_title: New fourth-generation heat pump system for EV thermal management - EV Engineering & Infrastructure

facts: Joon Georgia Inc. is a new unit of established auto body parts manufacturer Ajin USA.
Joon Georgia Inc. plans to build a $317 million factory in the Bruce Yawn Commerce Park.
The company will receive a 10-year partial break from Bulloch County property taxes on the planned factory.
The tax incentive is provided in exchange for the anticipated, eventual creation of 630 jobs.
The Joon Georgia factory plan moves forward with the county tax break.
source_title: Joon Georgia factory plan moves forward with county tax break - Statesboro Herald

facts: Hyundai Mobis announced plans to build a manufacturing facility with 1,500 more automotive jobs near the Georgia coast.
The factory will be located in Richmond Hill in Bryan County.
It will manufacture powertrain systems for vehicles.
Components will supply the future Hyundai EV factory near Ellabell.
Components will also be provided to the Kia factory in West Point and Hyundai’s plant in Alabama.

Gov. Brian Kemp stated that Hyundai’s new electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facility groundbreaking would unleash transformational job creation and investment in that entire region of the state.
Kemp noted this is the second supplier announced in just two weeks to locate in that area, expressing excitement over their impact on surrounding communities and other upcoming job creators.
The project supports Georgia's goal to become an e-mobility capital with significant incentives for automotive component manufacturing.
source_title: Hyundai Mobis announces EV powertrain plant near Georgia coast

facts: Ecoplastic is a Korean-based auto parts manufacturer.
The company is investing $205 million into a manufacturing facility in Bulloch County.
The company operates as a tier-1 supplier for Hyundai.
Ecoplastic produces products such as consoles, bumpers and more.
The facility will be located in Statesboro, Bulloch County.
The plant will be able to serve the Hyundai Metaplant well from Statesboro.
On January 11, 2023, Georgia Governor Brian Kemp welcomed Ecoplastic to the state's growing ecosystem of manufacturers, logistics professionals, suppliers and more.
Governor Kemp noted the site's close proximity to Georgia's ports system as a key advantage for the company.
source_title: Hyundai supplier Ecoplastic invests in new plant in Bulloch County GA

facts: Automotive parts manufacturer Ecoplastic Corp. is investing $205 million in two new manufacturing facility near Register, Ga.
source_title: Hyundai supplier Ecoplastic invests $205M in new Georgia facility - Plastics News

facts: Ascend Elements celebrated the grand opening of its first commercial-scale lithium-ion battery recycling facility in Covington, Georgia, on March 29, 2023.
The $50 million Base 1 facility located in Covington, Georgia, is North America's largest electric vehicle battery recycling facility.
The facility began partial operations in August 2022.
The facility has an annual capacity to process 30,000 metric tons of used lithium-ion batteries and manufacturing scrap.
The processing capacity is equal to 70,000 electric vehicle batteries per year.
Ascend Elements CEO Mike O'Kronley stated that the facility is an important part of America's EV battery infrastructure and 'just the start.'
O'Kronley emphasized the industry's need to continue building a closed-loop battery materials supply chain to make electric vehicle batteries cleaner and more sustainable.
Ascend Elements recovers up to 98% of the critical battery metals in used electric vehicle batteries and gigafactory manufacturing scrap.
Using its patented Hydro-to-Cathode™ process, Ascend Elements can reduce the carbon footprint of new electric vehicle battery cathode materials by up to 90%.
The facility is powered by renewable energy and features onsite wastewater recycling and treatment systems.
The facility currently employs about 100 team members.
The company plans to increase the facility's headcount to 185 by 2024.
source_title: Ascend Elements Opens North America's Largest Electric Vehicle Battery Recycling Facility in Georgia - Middle Georgia CEO

facts: Ascend Elements has announced plans to launch a new lithium recovery line at its Covington, Georgia, facility in 2025.
The new line will produce over 99% pure lithium carbonate (Li2CO3) recovered from used lithium-ion batteries.
With a planned output of up to 3,000 metric tons annually, it will be the first large-scale commercial production of recycled Li2CO3 in the US.
Ascend Elements was founded in 2015 with headquarters in Massachusetts.
The company utilizes Hydro-to-Cathode direct precursor synthesis technology to efficiently produce new cathode materials from spent lithium-ion cells.
Eric Gratz, co-founder and Chief Technology Officer of Ascend Elements, states that this is the first new, domestic source of lithium carbonate in the 21st century.
Eric Gratz adds that this new domestic supply will help US industries meet growing demand while avoiding the possibility of tariffs on imported materials.
Li2CO3 is a key ingredient in advanced batteries powering EVs, grid-scale energy storage systems, boats, and aircraft.
Currently, the only other US source of Li2CO3 is a mining operation in Nevada.
Ascend Elements' lithium recovery process produces just 2.27 kilograms of CO2 emissions per kilogram of Li2CO3.
This method is significantly less carbon-intensive than conventional techniques like spodumene mining (16.7 kg CO2/kg Li2CO3) or Chilean brine extraction (3.6 kg CO2/kg Li2CO3).

Eric Gratz highlights that their current process of lithium extraction from used lithium-ion batteries is about 86% less carbon-intensive compared to spodumene mining and 37% less carbon-intensive compared to Chilean brine extraction.
The Covington plant has been operational since August 2022.
The Covington facility is one of North America's largest lithium-ion battery recycling facilities.
The Covington plant has the capacity to recycle up to 30,000 metric tons of lithium-ion battery materials annually.
The Covington plant can process approximately 70,000 EV battery packs annually.

Eric Gratz states that this new capability aligns with their vision of creating a circular economy for battery materials and calls it a win for the industry, the environment, and the nation's energy future.
source_title: Ascend Elements' bid to Launch US Lithium Recovery Line | EV Magazine

facts: Ascend Elements is a recycling company focused on Li-ion batteries.
The company was founded in 2015 as a spin-out of the Worcester Polytechnic Institute to develop commercially its research activities ongoing since 2011 to develop NMC recycling methods.
Its industrial activities were first implemented in its headquarters state of Massachusetts before expanding to Michigan and Georgia from 2020 onward.
Recycling of Cathode Active Materials (CAM) is Ascend Elements’ core business, represented by the introduction of its Hydro-to-Cathode® technology for CAM precursors (pCAM) synthesis process.
The company delivered its first commercial shipment of refined pCAM in the form of Lithium Carbonate (Li2CO3) in early 2025.
Ascend Elements expanded its view overseas to Europe, partnering with Strategic Elemental Metals to establish a joint venture on Polish ground.

The commercial expansion led to the creation of the joint venture AE Elements in partnership with Elemental Strategic Metals to further develop industrial capabilities in Europe, starting from Poland and planning to build another facility in Germany.

Ascend Elements’ published patent portfolio comprises 35 patent families grouping 119 individual patents, with most focusing on protecting CAM-related methods and processes and a clear predominance of lithium ternary oxides.
Most patents were published over the past 3 years, with a noticeable peak in 2024, and the USA remains the main jurisdiction where patents are filed, followed by Europe, China, and South Korea.
The years 2023-24 saw the most IP activity, with over half of the total Ascend Elements’ patent publications, while 2025 saw patent filings only in the USA and PCT applications.
One patent family from Ascend Elements has been cited an impressive 232 times, while a handful of others have received up to 30 citations each.

A key patent (US10522884) discloses a method and apparatus for LIB recycling that allows recovering elements such as Co, Ni, Mn, and Al at the same time from black mass recovered from Li-ion batteries.

The patented invention isolates key metals from Cathode Active Materials without a preferred cathode chemistry, though it is particularly apt to recover metals commonly used in CAM such as NMC, NCA, and LCO.

The method involves preliminary leaching with Sulfuric Acid and Hydrogen Peroxide at mild temperatures, followed by precipitation of metals hydroxides using Sodium Hydroxide, pH adjustment, calcination to obtain pristine CAM, and isolation of Lithium Carbonate.

Another patent (JP7649364) reports a method to recover Graphite from black mass resulting from crushing Li- or Na-based batteries, involving impurity removal, alkaline treatment, washing, and sintering for high purity.

A patent from partner Elemental Strategic Metals (PL2146860) describes an electrochemical method to recover cobalt, nickel, and manganese(IV) oxide from electrolytes obtained from leaching black mass, depositing Co and Cu on the cathode and MnO2 on the anode in the first step, followed by Ni deposition on the cathode.
Dr. Filippo Farina, a Technology Analyst at KnowMade, highlighted that IP competition across the globe for LIB recycling is fierce, fast-paced, and multi-faceted.

links.url: Le Drakkar, 2405 route des Dolines, 06560 Valbonne Sophia Antipolis, France

source_title: Ascend Elements: Strategic Player in Li-ion Battery Recycling and High-Value IP for CAM Technologies

facts: Chicago-based Anovion Technologies plans a 1.5 million-square-foot factory in Bainbridge.
The planned factory will employ 400 people.
source_title: Anovion Technologies plans $800 million Georgia EV battery factory - Atlanta Business Chronicle

facts: US battery materials company Anovion Technologies is building a production facility for its graphite anode material for use in lithium-ion batteries in Decatur County in southwest Georgia.
The project involves an initial investment of $800 million.
Anovion is based in Chicago, Illinois, and has been producing anode-grade synthetic graphite near Niagara Falls, New York, and Clarksburg, West Virginia, since 2021.
In October 2022, Anovion received a grant for a total of 117 million US dollars from the US Department of Energy to expand its production capacities.
The new plant in Georgia is planned to start with an annual production capacity of 40,000 metric tonnes.
Information on the schedule for the Georgia facility is not yet available.

The initially 1,500,000 square foot facility was chosen based on its proximity to existing and planned low- and carbon-free energy sources for power, short supply chains, access to existing rail infrastructure, and a highly valuable skilled workforce.

Eric Stopka, Chief Executive Officer of Anovion, stated that the company conducted a thorough search to identify a location addressing energy, transportation, logistics, and human capital needs to ensure an ecosystem for producing the highest quality and sustainable synthetic graphite anode material on the market today.

Eric Stopka noted that existing infrastructure makes Southwest Georgia attractive and that proximity to other battery and EV manufacturing plants will allow Anovion to further reduce its carbon footprint.

Chip Dunn, Executive Chairman of Anovion, stated the company's long-term strategic vision is to focus on growth and innovation, positioning Anovion as a leader in the US energy transition and securing a domestic source for critical materials required for US lithium-ion battery production.

Chip Dunn added that from continued research and development in electrochemistry to technology improvements in production processes and climate tech initiatives, the goal is to become carbon-neutral over time, mitigate greenhouse gas emissions, and position Anovion as the climate tech leader of the future.
source_title: Anovion to build battery material plant in Georgia - electrive.com

facts: Anovion announced plans on Monday to develop an $800 million factory in Southwest Georgia as it aims to be a significant supplier of graphite for electric vehicle batteries.
The Chicago-based advanced materials company aims to produce synthetic graphite anode in Bainbridge, with plans to start production in 2025.
The planned 1.5-million-square-foot plant in Bainbridge will aid the company in producing premium synthetic graphite, the most-used material in EVs by mass.
Synthetic graphite surpasses copper, manganese, nickel, cobalt, and lithium in usage by mass in EVs.
The facility will be the first-ever of its scale and size in North America.
The new plant will have an initial annual production capacity of 40,000 metric tonnes once fully operational.
Anovion CEO Eric Stopka explained that the company selected Southwest Georgia due to its closeness to other battery and EV production plants on top of the state’s existing infrastructures.

Anovion CEO Eric Stopka stated: “Anovion conducted a thorough search to identify a location that addressed our energy, transportation, logistics, and human capital needs that would ensure we have the ecosystem in place to produce the highest quality and sustainable synthetic graphite anode material on the market today.”
The project is expected to generate 400 jobs in the state.
Governor Brian Kemp expressed his enthusiasm for the investment.

Governor Brian Kemp stated: “We’re proud to welcome Anovion to the No. 1 state for business that is fast becoming the e-mobility capital of the nation. Job creators like them are recognizing that every corner of our state has the resources and workforce needed for success. Having met their team personally, I look forward to celebrating their partnership with the hardworking Georgians of Decatur County.”
The US Department of Energy granted Anovion $117 million under the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law in October 2022.
The DOE grant is meant to aid the country’s battery production supply chain, boosting electric vehicle adoption.
The planned Bainbridge plant is expected to start production by late 2025.
source_title: Anovion announced plans to build an $800 million production plant in Southwest Georgia

facts: Solvay S.A., based in Brussels, Belgium, confirms plans to build a new battery-grade PVDF facility in Augusta, Georgia.
More than half of U.S. car sales are projected to be electric by 2030.
The facility will produce U.S.-made PVDF, a thermoplastic fluoropolymer, to supply the rapidly growing EV battery market and meet domestic energy storage needs.
At full capacity, the new operations will provide material for more than 5 million EV batteries per year.
The project will create hundreds of jobs throughout the value chain.
Solvay and Orbia have signed a joint venture agreement for this project.
The partnership secures Orbia's supply of needed materials for Solvay to manufacture suspension-grade polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF).
Suspension-grade PVDF is used as a lithium-ion binder and separator coating in electric vehicle batteries.
Solvay will contribute its process technology and global market know-how to the venture.
The combined efforts leverage Solvay’s Solef PVDF innovations and Orbia’s raw material assets and production expertise.
The delivered PVDF enables electric vehicles to travel farther on each charge, extends battery life, and improves battery safety.
Solvay and Orbia intend to utilize two production sites in the southeastern U.S. for this project.
One of the two production sites will be dedicated to raw materials, while the other will produce finished products.
Both plants are expected to become operational in 2026.
Solvay and the U.S. Department of Energy’s Office of Manufacturing and Energy Supply Chains finalized an agreement for a $178 million grant to Solvay.
The $178 million grant will help build the facility at Solvay's existing site in Augusta, GA.
The grant was awarded under the U.S. Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA).
The funding aims to expand U.S. domestic manufacturing of batteries for electric vehicles and the electrical grid.
The project will fill a significant supply gap in the domestic market.
The project builds upon favorable regulatory conditions that promote regional production and material security.
source_title: Solvay and Orbia progress plans for new PVDF facility in Georgia - Chemical Engineering | Page 1

facts: April 25, 2024: The Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) joined executive leadership from Syensqo and local/state representatives to celebrate the start of construction on Syensqo’s new battery-grade PVDF manufacturing facility in Augusta.

Governor Brian Kemp stated that Georgia is developing an e-mobility supply chain to position the state as a leader in the industry for years to come, and expressed excitement for Syensqo to become part of the ecosystem and community.
The new Augusta facility will create approximately 100 new full-time, highly skilled local manufacturing jobs in Richmond County.
The facility’s products will support Georgia’s growing EV manufacturing ecosystem by providing material for more than 5 million EV batteries per year at full capacity.
The project will create hundreds of jobs throughout the value chain.
Syensqo was born from the demerger of Solvay in December 2023 and is now a standalone listed company.
Syensqo has a portfolio addressing electrification, lightweighting, advanced connectivity, resource efficiency, improving quality of life, and sustainable sourcing.
The company’s U.S. headquarters in Alpharetta and its existing manufacturing facility in Augusta support more than 800 jobs in Georgia.

Mike Finelli, Chief Technology & Innovation Officer and Chief North America Officer at Syensqo, thanked GDEcD for their support in expanding the Augusta footprint, noting it enables new jobs, reinforces the local economy, and underscores Augusta's strategic importance in the EV supply chain along the Battery Belt.
The new facility is located at 3702 Clanton Road in Augusta.
The facility will supply polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), used as a lithium-ion binder and separator coating in electric vehicle batteries.
The plant will be the largest PVDF production facility in North America.
The facility will be hiring mechanics, technicians, engineers, and more.
Augusta Mayor Garnett Johnson noted Syensqo has been part of the community for over two decades as a premier employer, expressed excitement about the expansion and quality jobs, and thanked partners.

Augusta Economic Development Authority Chairman Wayne A. Gossage, Jr. stated Syensqo fits the goal of creating well-paying, skilled manufacturing jobs, highlighted Augusta-Richmond County as the right place for manufacturing to grow, and thanked local leadership.

Senior Project Manager Adela Kelley represented GDEcD’s Global Commerce team on this competitive project in partnership with the Augusta Economic Development Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia EMC.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated companies are making once-in-a-generation investments for electrification, expressed excitement that Syensqo selected Georgia for a critical EV supply chain piece, noted Georgia's forefront position in attracting e-mobility and battery investments thanks to state leadership and Governor Kemp, and congratulated all parties.
The Syensqo Augusta site is a partner of the Giving Your Best Award with WJBF, Piedmont Hospital, and Security Federal Bank for volunteerism recognition.
Syensqo is supporting higher education institutions in Georgia, including Augusta Tech, with workforce development projects.
In 2022, Syensqo opened its New Applications Development Lab at its R&I Center in Alpharetta to support customers in the EV, renewable energy, and aerospace industries.
The inauguration ceremony for the Alpharetta lab was attended by Her Royal Highness Princess Astrid of Belgium, high-ranking Belgian officials, and State of Georgia leadership.
In the last four years, Syensqo has invested more than $30 million in the Alpharetta site.
source_title: Syensqo Breaks Ground on New EV Material Facility in Augusta - Augusta Economic Development Authority

facts: Vice President Kamala Harris visited an expanding solar panel factory in northwest Georgia on Thursday and announced an order for millions of Peach State-built panels.
Harris toured the Qcells plant near I-75 in Whitfield County, one of two northwest Georgia locations where the South Korean company is expanding its solar panel production.

The Qcells Dalton expansion and a new facility in Bartow County represent the largest ever investment in clean energy manufacturing in U.S. history, according to company leaders and elected officials.
The $2.5 billion expansion will result in 2,500 additional jobs.
This investment is part of the White House’s “Investing in America” tour promoting President Joe Biden’s legislative agenda ahead of a likely 2024 re-election campaign.

Harris highlighted a historic commitment from Qcells and Virginia-based commercial solar company Summit Ridge Energy to deploy 1.2 gigawatts of community solar power, which is enough electricity to power 140,000 homes and businesses.
Harris stated this deployment is the largest community solar purchase in U.S. history.

Community solar projects involve residents, businesses, or groups pooling resources to deploy and connect to solar arrays built elsewhere, with participants buying or leasing access or receiving utility bill credits.
There are several such community solar arrangements currently operating in Georgia.
The Qcells and Summit Ridge Energy commitment requires the construction of 2.5 million solar panels.
Harris noted this output represents roughly eight months’ worth of production from Qcells’ current Dalton facility.
The bulk of these panels will be installed in community projects across Illinois, Maryland, and Maine, though specific project locations are still being assessed.
Harris cited administration achievements including growing jobs, strengthening the economy, addressing climate change, boosting infrastructure spending, and encouraging domestic manufacturing.
President Biden visited Minnesota on Monday to highlight how federal policies have revitalized blue-collar communities.
Dalton is famous as the flooring capital of the world and is located in a deep-red corner of Georgia that supports former President Donald Trump and represents U.S. Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene.

White House National Climate Advisor Ali Zaidi noted the irony that the investment is taking place in a district represented by someone who did not vote for the responsible tax incentives but wants to repeal them.

A Republican National Committee spokeswoman criticized the Biden and Harris agenda as “out-of-touch,” claiming Democrats are crushing Peach State families with higher prices, lower savings, and tighter incomes.
President Biden won Georgia in 2020 by approximately 12,000 votes, and the state is expected to be a key battleground in 2024.
Harris stated that communities like Dalton are full of people with incredible ambition who just need an opportunity to show what they can do.
Georgia has become a leader across the clean tech sector, particularly in solar panel and electric vehicle industries.
Hyundai Motor Group, Rivian, SK Innovation, Freyr Battery, and Qcells have announced billion-dollar facilities across Georgia over the past few years.
Kia, a subsidiary of Hyundai, announced it will manufacture a new electric SUV at its existing West Point factory starting in 2024.

President Biden stated Georgia is ground zero for building a clean energy economy and industries of the future, citing investments that create good-paying jobs, unleash private sector investments, and produce Made in Georgia and Made in America products.
Solar panel production has historically been dominated by China.
Qcells’ expansion makes it the largest solar panel manufacturing operation in the Western Hemisphere and brings the entire chain of panel production onto U.S. soil, according to experts.
Georgia Republicans led by Gov. Brian Kemp and prominent statewide Democrats have each claimed credit for the state’s clean energy manufacturing surge.
The Inflation Reduction Act included historic investments to fight climate change and promote domestic clean tech manufacturing.
The legislation includes the Solar Energy Manufacturing for America Act, championed by U.S. Sen. Jon Ossoff, intended to boost solar panel production.
Sen. Ossoff stated his solar manufacturing law continues to expand opportunity in Georgia and nationwide through this new partnership.
State and local governments have aggressively incentivized clean technology companies with billions of dollars in tax abatements, job tax credits, and free infrastructure.
Gulfstream Aerospace Corporation plans a $150 million expansion that will add 1,600 jobs to its workforce in Chatham County.
Local governments provided financial incentives to Gulfstream to encourage the projects, according to officials at the Savannah Economic Development Authority.
source_title: Vice President Harris visits Georgia today to laud clean energy growth

facts: LG Energy Solution Vertech and Qcells announced a partnership to deploy 5 GWh of battery energy storage systems (BESS) across the United States.
The agreement will see LG Energy Solution Vertech supply lithium iron phosphate (LFP) battery systems for Qcells’ utility-scale engineering, procurement and construction (EPC) arm.
Projects under the agreement are scheduled between 2028 and 2030.
The deal reflects a continued focus on domestic supply chains in the U.S. renewable energy market.
Battery cells for the installations will be manufactured at LG Energy Solution facilities in Michigan and Arizona.
The battery systems will be deployed alongside Qcells solar modules produced at the company’s Georgia manufacturing hub.
Chris Hodrick, CEO of Qcells EPC, stated that Qcells is proud to partner with LG Energy Solution on a project that shows what’s possible when energy development supports U.S. manufacturing.

Chris Hodrick noted the collaboration reflects a shared commitment to building clean energy by creating American jobs, strengthening supply chains, and delivering reliable, affordable power for communities across the country.
This is the second significant agreement between Qcells and LG Energy Solution Vertech in recent years.
In May 2024, Qcells and LG Energy Solution Vertech signed a 4.8 GWh supply deal.
Reports from Wood Mackenzie and the American Clean Power Association (ACP) noted that the U.S. installed more than 35.4 GWh of energy storage in the first nine months of 2025 alone.
The 35.4 GWh installed in the first nine months of 2025 surpassed the total installed in all of 2024.
In late 2025, the U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) reported that total capacity served by energy storage installations increased by nearly 14 GW in the preceding 12 months.
The EIA forecast the installation of an additional 22 GW of capacity in the following 12-month period.
Qcells is a subsidiary of Korean giant Hanwha Solutions.
Qcells secured a massive 12 GW solar module supply agreement with Microsoft in 2024.
Qcells offers manufactured products, project development and installation services for the residential, commercial, and utility-scale sectors.
Qcells provides installation financing through its Enfin lending arm.
Qcells says its annual solar panel production capacity in the U.S. will reach 8.4 gigawatts when the new Cartersville facility is complete.
LG Energy Solution manufactures products across multiple U.S. sites for the electric vehicle, energy storage, mobility, and IT device sectors.
LG Energy Solution Vertech was formed in 2022 following its acquisition of NEC Energy Solutions and operates as a system integrator.
LG Energy Solution maintains a major manufacturing partnership with General Motors.
A deal for LG Energy Solution to supply batteries to Ford Motor Company fell through in late 2025 when Ford discontinued its F-150 Lightning EV truck.
source_title: Qcells, LG Energy Solution Vertech ink 5 GWh US battery storage deal - Energy Storage

facts: Qcells announced the official launch of Qcells New Homes, a new division designed to work with homebuilders to integrate onsite, end-to-end clean energy solutions for new residential construction across the U.S.
The new division aims to create manufacturing jobs while addressing homebuyer affordability challenges.

The Qcells New Homes platform provides a turnkey, vertically integrated package including solar panels manufactured at Qcells’ Georgia factory, domestically produced battery systems, financing, installation support, and long-term system monitoring.
Qcells New Homes is positioned as the only direct-from-manufacturer solar and storage partner available to homebuilders.
The division is backed by the A-rated financial strength of parent company Hanwha Group to provide bankability and long-term stability for homebuilders.

David Shin serves as President of Qcells North America and stated that the launch raises the bar for convenience, compliance, and quality by offering a reliable, single-source partner for clean energy in new communities.
Qcells is helping builders integrate advanced energy solutions seamlessly into new construction to improve long-term value for homeowners as energy demands evolve.
The platform addresses household energy affordability, operating costs, and grid reliability through a homebuyer-friendly operating lease with in-house financing.

Integrated battery storage allows power produced by solar to be stored and sold to the grid to enhance local community reliability in states supporting onsite clean energy programs like NEM 3.0 in California.
Qcells New Homes offers battery configurations that can provide reserved backup during outages while supporting broader grid stability needs.

Phil Narodick is President of Qcells New Homes and noted that the company is cutting out intermediaries to deliver solar panels assembled in America directly to new communities, leveraging decades of homebuilding industry experience.
Qcells is headquartered in Seoul, South Korea, with international manufacturing facilities in the U.S., Malaysia, and South Korea.
The company provides renewable electricity retail services and packages to end customers globally across utility, commercial, governmental, and residential markets.
The company operates a global business network spanning Europe, North America, Asia, South America, Africa, and the Middle East.
source_title: Qcells launches New Homes solar platform in U.S.

facts: Hanwha Advanced Materials Georgia (HAGA) will build a new advanced materials manufacturing facility in Bartow County, Georgia.
HAGA will supply Qcells with Encapsulant film, which is used to encapsulate solar cells and ensure long-term panel durability.
The project represents a $147 million investment.
The investment is expected to create more than 160 new jobs.
This investment is critical to supporting Qcells plans to develop a fully integrated solar supply chain in America.
HAGA will be the only company in the United States manufacturing solar EVA.
Inhwan Kim, CEO of Hanwha Advanced Materials, stated that the products they make are an important piece of the clean energy supply chain puzzle and they are excited to meet this need.
Inhwan Kim said building cutting-edge advanced materials in Georgia will create new careers in solar and help bring more affordable, reliable clean energy to customers across the country.
Justin Lee, CEO of Qcells, stated that Qcells is doubling down on building a complete, domestic solar supply chain and this recent investment is critical to making that happen.
Justin Lee said working with Hanwha Advanced Materials will allow customers to confidently know that the solar they buy from them was made right here in America.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that Georgia is leading the nation in attracting next generation jobs.
Governor Kemp noted that since welcoming Qcells to the state in 2018, more than 4,000 related jobs have been announced for Georgians.
Governor Kemp expressed pride that Hanwha Advanced Materials is adding to that growing number as it becomes a valued member of the Bartow County community.
Senator Jon Ossoff stated that his solar manufacturing law continues to deliver jobs, investment, and wealth for Georgians.
Senator Ossoff called the project a continuation of the largest clean energy manufacturing investment in American history.

U.S. Senator Reverend Warnock stated he is thrilled that Georgians are reaping the economic benefits of the Inflation Reduction Act that has helped pave the way for advanced manufacturing companies like QCells to invest and expand in the state.
Senator Warnock said he looks forward to continuing to partner with QCells as the state becomes the premier destination for 21st century jobs.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Hanwha Advanced Materials provides key components for the solar supply chain in Georgia, supporting the state's commitment to making energy solutions more affordable to businesses and consumers.
Commissioner Wilson noted that over the past five years, clean-tech jobs have been one of the fastest growing manufacturing sectors in Georgia.
Commissioner Wilson expressed excitement that Hanwha Advanced Materials will add another crucial part of the solar supply chain to Georgia's already robust ecosystem.
Mayor of Cartersville Matt Santini stated that the community is thrilled to be adding another Hanwha affiliate to their growing family of industries.
Mayor Santini said their investment in bringing quality jobs to the community is greatly appreciated.

Mayor Santini noted that the mix of industries in Cartersville and Bartow County is further diversified by adding them to the local mix, and one of their goals from the beginning was to recruit world-class companies to the area.
