facts: Georgia has over 50 years of international partnerships to help companies scale up quickly in the United States.
International companies accounted for one in four new locations or expansions in FY 2025.
Georgia facilitated more than $210.7 billion in international trade in 2025.
The Port of Savannah is the fastest growing single-terminal port in the nation.
The Port of Brunswick is the No 1. Ro/Ro port for automotive and heavy equipment.
Georgia has connections via inland ports and the most extensive rail system in the Southeastern United States.
Products can reach 80% of the U.S. market by a two-day drive via highways.
Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport serves more than 150 U.S. destinations and more than 70 international destinations in 43 countries.
Georgia consistently ranks as the No. 1 State for Business due to its business-friendly environment and smooth permitting process.
More than 60 international diplomatic and trade offices are located in Georgia.
Georgia maintains international representatives in more than a dozen locations covering over 60 markets worldwide.
The State of Georgia established its first international offices in Japan and Europe more than 50 years ago.
Georgia offers a skilled workforce pipeline, low cost of living, and robust infrastructure for moving goods globally.
source_title: Georgia's Global Advantage | Georgia

facts: The Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 (Public Law 117-169) amended the Qualified Plug-in Electric Drive Motor Vehicle Credit (IRC 30D), now known as the Clean Vehicle Credit.
A new requirement for final assembly in North America took effect on August 17, 2022.
The final assembly requirement applies to new electric, fuel cell electric, and plug-in hybrid electric vehicles acquired, delivered, and placed in service after August 16, 2022.
For vehicles placed in service on or after January 1, 2023, Clean Vehicle Credit provisions are subject to updated guidance from the IRS and the U.S. Department of the Treasury.
Beginning January 1, 2024, buyers can reduce the vehicle's upfront purchase price by the amount of their Clean Vehicle Credit by transferring the credit to the dealer.
Before 2024, eligible vehicle buyers could only receive the credit amount after filing their tax return.
Starting January 1, 2024, dealers must submit information to the IRS through IRS Energy Credits Online to determine vehicle eligibility and credit amount at the point of sale.
Without dealer submission to the IRS, buyers cannot claim a tax credit on their return nor transfer it to a dealer.
A dealer must provide the buyer a copy of the IRS's approval of the dealer's submission.
The list of EVs with final assembly in North America applies only to vehicles purchased and delivered between August 17 and December 31, 2022.
Model Year 2022 and 2023 vehicle eligibility is based on Fuel Economy labeling information and Part 583 data submitted by manufacturers to the EPA and NHTSA as of December 31, 2022.
Verification of final assembly for vehicles purchased beginning January 1, 2023, should use the VIN decoder.
Manufacturer sales caps were met for the 2022 Chevrolet Bolt EUV, Chevrolet Bolt EV, GMC Hummer EV Pickup, GMC Hummer EV SUV, Tesla Model 3, Tesla Model S, Tesla Model X, and Tesla Model Y.

Eligible 2022 models include the Audi Q5, BMW 330e, BMW X5 xDrive45e (PHEV), Chrysler Pacifica PHEV, Ford E-Transit, Ford Escape PHEV, Ford F-150 Lightning, Ford Mustang MACH E, Jeep Grand Cherokee 4xe, Jeep Wrangler 4xe, Lincoln Aviator PHEV, Lincoln Corsair PHEV, Lucid Air, Nissan Leaf, Rivian EDV, Rivian R1S, Rivian R1T, and Volvo S60 Recharge.

Eligible 2023 models include the BMW 330e, BMW X5 xDrive45e (PHEV), Cadillac Lyriq, Chevrolet Bolt EV, Ford E-Transit, Jeep Grand Cherokee 4xe, Jeep Wrangler 4xe, Lincoln Aviator PHEV, Lucid Air, Mercedes EQS SUV, Nissan Leaf, Rivian R1S, Rivian R1T, Tesla Model 3, Tesla Model S, Tesla Model X, Tesla Model Y, and Volkswagen ID.4.
The list will be updated as vehicle manufacturers submit Fuel Economy labeling information and Part 583 vehicle identification information to relevant government agencies.
Manufacturers can use the website contact form to request changes to this vehicle list.
Final assembly in North America is not required for tax credit eligibility for vehicles purchased before August 17, 2022.
The build location of a particular vehicle should be confirmed by the VIN or an information label affixed to the vehicle.
The U.S. Department of Transportation's NHTSA provides a VIN decoder to identify a vehicle's build plant and country of manufacture.

Verification of North American final assembly is an initial step toward identifying vehicles eligible for the Clean Vehicle Credit, but further eligibility conditions may apply depending on the purchase date.
For some manufacturers, the build location may vary based on the specific vehicle, trim, or production date because some models are produced in multiple locations.
To be eligible for the Clean Vehicle Credit, a vehicle must have undergone final assembly in North America.
In general, North America includes the United States and Puerto Rico, Canada, and Mexico for determining the location of final assembly.
The information on the page should not be viewed as an official or legally binding document.
source_title: Alternative Fuels Data Center: Electric Vehicles with Final Assembly in North America

facts: Manufacturers know the cost of downtime but few calculate the cost of workforce decay.
A weakening technician pipeline causes manufacturers to lose capacity, predictability, and profit.
Signs of a weakening technician pipeline include more overtime, slower ramp-up, and rising rework rates.
Over time, hidden inefficiencies from workforce decay can drain millions from annual output.
Unmudl’s Technician Pipeline Health Check™ helps manufacturers quantify invisible losses before they hit the balance sheet.
The Health Check connects people metrics (hiring speed, onboarding flow, retention) with operational impact (productivity, safety, uptime).
Every unfilled technician role is a capacity constraint.
Every delayed hire is lost throughput.
Every turnover event is a restart cost.
The Health Check converts these realities into an early-warning system to uncover where the technician ecosystem is leaking value and identify targeted interventions for double-digit ROI.
Workforce risk has become a board-level concern, making technician health a financial leading indicator.
Manufacturers who track technician health like a P&L line item will outlast those who treat it like a staffing statistic.
source_title: Manufacturing Georgia | 2025

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced on April 20, 2026, that Blue Ops, Inc. will invest $30 million in a new manufacturing facility in Valdosta.
The investment is expected to create 200+ jobs over the next several years in Lowndes County.
Blue Ops operates as the maritime division of Red Cat Holdings, Inc. (Nasdaq: RCAT), a U.S.-based provider of advanced all-domain drone and robotic solutions for defense and national security.
Blue Ops is leasing the former Regal Boats facility located at 601 Gil Harbin Industrial Boulevard in Valdosta.
The leased manufacturing facility spans 155,000 square feet.
The facility will manufacture the company’s uncrewed surface vessels (USVs).
Blue Ops plans to hire up to 100 people in 2026.
Red Cat recently announced a partnership with HADDY to accelerate Blue Ops’ USV manufacturing capacity.
The HADDY partnership will create demand for hiring personnel to complete boat production after hulls are built using large-scale 3D printers.
Barry Hinckley, President of Blue Ops, stated the investment aims to build a modern, scalable, and domestic USV manufacturing base from the ground up.
Blue Ops is leveraging advanced production technologies to accelerate delivery of mission-ready maritime systems for defense and national security customers.
The company will create high-quality local jobs, reinforcing Red Cat’s commitment to a secure, domestic supply chain.
Valdosta Mayor Scott James Matheson welcomed Blue Ops to Valdosta, citing the area's strong workforce, strategic location, and powerful partnerships driving local economic growth.
Niki Ogletree, president and CEO of the Valdosta-Lowndes County Development Authority, stated the announcement marks a significant community milestone.
Valdosta-Lowndes County has experienced tremendous economic momentum over the past several years by attracting major investments and creating hundreds of new jobs.
Bringing Blue Ops to the region diversifies the local economy, creates higher-wage opportunities, and positions the community for continued growth in advanced manufacturing and technology.
Senior Regional Project Manager Andrea Taylor represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development on this competitive project.
The project was developed in partnership with the City of Valdosta, Valdosta-Lowndes County Development Authority, and Georgia Quick Start.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson highlighted that expanding the defense and national security manufacturing base is critically important.
Georgia maintains a military presence across nine installations statewide, providing a stable foundation for companies in this ecosystem to grow and compete.
Blue Ops’ location decision highlights the depth and diversity of innovation occurring across Georgia's defense sector.

Through wholly owned subsidiaries Teal Drones and FlightWave Aerospace, Red Cat develops American-made hardware and software supporting military, government, and public safety operations across air, land, and sea.
Red Cat’s family of systems delivers tactical capabilities in small, unmanned aircraft systems (sUAS).

Through Blue Ops, Inc., Red Cat is expanding into the maritime domain to innovate uncrewed surface vessels (USVs) that deliver integrated platforms enhancing safety and multi-domain mission effectiveness.

links.context: Deputy press contact for Governor Kemp

source_title: Red Cat’s Blue Ops, Inc. to Bring Hundreds of New Jobs with Lowndes Co | Georgia

facts: Mativ Holdings, Inc. has its principal executive offices located at 100 Kimball Place, Suite 600, Alpharetta, Georgia 30009.
On May 6, 2026, Mativ Holdings, Inc. issued a press release announcing its financial results for the quarter ended March 31, 2026.
Shruti Singhal serves as President and Chief Executive Officer of Mativ Holdings, Inc.
Mativ Holdings, Inc. common stock trades under the symbol MATV on the New York Stock Exchange.

canonical_name: Department of Energy MESC Funding Application Guide
facts: The guide explains the steps needed to get started on a funding application for DOE opportunities.
Each funding opportunity is labeled as either a Funding Opportunity Announcement (FOA) or a Partnership Intermediary Agreement (PIA) on the funding page.
Applicants are recommended to use only one account per organization or business unit as the contact point for each submission when creating an account with Infrastructure eXCHANGE.
Questions or technical difficulties with submissions should be directed to the Infrastructure eXCHANGE helpdesk.
Each applicant must register with the System for Award Management (SAM) to obtain a Unique Entity ID (UEI) before submitting an application, unless excepted under 2 CFR 25.110.
The SAM account must be maintained with up-to-date information in order for a federal award to be made.
Applicants who have not fully complied with SAM requirements by the time DOE is ready to make a federal award will not receive a federal award.
Designating an Electronic Business Point of Contact and obtaining a Marketing Partner ID Number (MPIN) are important steps in SAM registration.
Entity legal business name and address validations for UEI requests and SAM registrations are taking longer than expected due to high volume.
Entities should start the UEI and SAM registration process as soon as possible.
Technical difficulties with UEI validation or SAM registration should be addressed using the HELP feature on SAM.gov.
SAM.gov will address service tickets in the order in which they are received, and entities should not create multiple service tickets for the same request or technical issue.
Applicants must register with Grants.gov to receive email updates on FOA amendments, though applications will not be accepted through Grants.gov.
Applying to a Funding Opportunity Announcement is a multi-step process that can take several weeks.
Applicants should review the relevant Funding Notice on Infrastructure eXCHANGE for all submission deadlines and resources.
Joining the ENERGYWERX ecosystem provides notifications and updates about funding opportunities, with a recommendation to use only one account per organization or business unit per submission.
Questions regarding ENERGYWERX should be directed to ENERGYWERX directly.
Applications for MESC funding opportunities can be found via links on the funding page, with the application type listed as PIA if applying through ENERGYWERX.
Applicants using ENERGYWERX must pay close attention to provided instructions, which vary per funding opportunity.
The 48C tax credit applies to investments in advanced energy projects as defined in 26 USC § 48C(c)(1).

The Energy Efficient Transformer Rebate Program and Extended Product System Rebate Program support the installation of energy efficient distribution transformers and extended product systems that use equipment with electric motors, such as pumps, air compressors, and fans.

These rebate programs aim to help domestic manufacturers, utilities, tribes, hospitals, schools, and other operators finance equipment upgrades to conserve energy, reduce costs, and slash greenhouse gas emissions across multiple sectors of the economy.

The funding page hosts all MESC funding opportunities, tax credits, rebates, requests for information, notices of intent, and selections, which can be filtered by content type, status, funding source, and program area.
By default, funding opportunities that are open for applications are listed on the top of the page and clearly marked as "Open".

Requests for Information (RFIs) represent the initial fact-finding and need-identifying phase, with MESC periodically announcing them to collect information about market dynamics and test program design concepts.
Perspectives supported by facts and evidence carry the most weight among RFI submissions.
RFI statuses include Open (public can submit input), In Review (input is under consideration), and Closed (next steps have been taken; process is complete).

A Notice of Intent (NOI) serves as a hypothetical heads-up for applicants to understand MESC's areas of interest for a potential funding opportunity, though one is not guaranteed, has no deadline, and requires no required action.
FOAs can be applied to through Infrastructure eXCHANGE, while PIAs are accessed through ENERGYWERX.

Funding opportunity statuses include Open (open for applications), In Review (no longer open for application; MESC is reviewing submitted applications), and Selected (MESC has selected applicants as potential awardees; applicants will undergo negotiation).
Tax credits managed by MESC can have statuses of Open, In Review, or Closed (no longer active).
Rebates managed by MESC can have statuses of Open, In Review, or Closed.
source_title: Application Guide | Department of Energy

facts: Hitachi Astemo Americas Inc. announced an expansion of its operation in Madison County with a $153 million investment creating 167 new, quality Kentucky jobs.
The project will see company leaders invest in the current manufacturing operation in Berea to support site improvements and renovations.
Renovations include additional production lines and equipment in response to increased market demand and growth within the EV industry.
Hitachi’s investment will create 167 full-time jobs for Kentuckians, bringing the company’s total Kentucky employment across the Harrodsburg and Berea facilities to over 2,100 workers.
Tim Clark, president of Hitachi Astemo Americas, stated the announcement is a bold step in the direction of vehicle electrification.
In January 2021, Hitachi Automotive Systems, Keihin, Showa and Nissin Kogyo completed a business merger to form the global mega-supplier Hitachi Astemo.
The company provides advanced mobility solutions including electric powertrains for automobiles, advanced chassis, autonomous driving/advanced driver assistance systems, and systems for motorcycles.
Headquartered in Tokyo, Japan, the company’s entry into the Americas began in 1972 with a subsidiary established in Brazil.
Hitachi expanded into the U.S. market in the mid-1980s with its first factory in Kentucky.
The company serves as a global mega-supplier to some of the largest automobile manufacturers in the world and has more than 20 manufacturing sites in the United States, Canada, Mexico and Brazil.
Hitachi Astemo operates two locations in Kentucky.
Madison County Judge/Executive Reagan Taylor expressed pleasure at the company's decision to expand its Berea facility.
Berea Mayor Bruce Fraley noted the decision speaks to the talent of the local workforce and economic climate.

Donna Angel, business development director for the Business and Economic Development Office of the city of Berea, highlighted the investment will bring quality jobs and economic sustainability to the community.
Kentucky’s administration has seen nearly 600 private-sector new-location or expansion projects commit to invest over $22.9 billion and create more than 30,000 jobs.
Gov. Andy Beshear announced there are more than 2 million jobs filled in Kentucky, the most in state history.
57,000 new jobs have been filled since the beginning of Gov. Beshear’s administration.
Kentucky set a historic low unemployment rate of 3.7% on May 18.
Since the beginning of his administration, Gov. Beshear has announced more than 860 private-sector new-location and expansion projects totaling $26.8 billion in announced investments.
The average incentivized hourly wage in Kentucky in 2022 was $26.78 before benefits, an 11.5% increase over the previous year.
Gov. Beshear and leaders from Ford Motor Co. and SK On celebrated a transformative $5.8 billion investment that will create 5,000 jobs in Hardin County in September 2021.
Gov. Beshear joined leadership at AESC to announce a $2 billion investment that will create 2,000 jobs in Warren County in April 2022.
These announcements solidified Kentucky as the EV battery production capital of the United States.
In May 2023, Gov. Beshear and leaders at Toyota announced a $591 million investment across multiple projects as Georgetown introduces its first battery electric vehicle.
In June, Gov. Beshear joined INFAC North America to break ground on a $53 million investment to support EV-related production in Campbellsville.
KEDFA preliminarily approved a 10-year incentive agreement with Hitachi Astemo under the Kentucky Business Investment program in March 2023.

The performance-based agreement can provide up to $2.4 million in tax incentives based on annual targets of maintaining 167 Kentucky-resident, full-time jobs for 10 years and paying an average hourly wage of $25.71 including benefits.
KEDFA approved Hitachi Astemo for up to $200,000 in tax incentives through the Kentucky Enterprise Initiative Act (KEIA).
KEIA allows approved companies to recoup Kentucky sales and use tax on construction costs, building fixtures, equipment used for research and development, and electronic processing.
The company may claim eligible incentives against its income tax liability and/or wage assessments.

Hitachi Astemo can receive resources from Kentucky’s workforce service providers, including no-cost recruitment and job placement services, reduced-cost customized training, and job-training incentives.

entity_name: DOE and GE Hitachi Nuclear Energy
canonical_name: U.S. Department of Energy and GE Hitachi Nuclear Energy
facts: The U.S. Department of Energy announced $5.8 million in funding to develop three construction technologies that together can reduce the cost of new nuclear builds by more than 10 percent.
Construction costs and schedule overruns have plagued new nuclear builds for decades, according to Dr. Kathryn Huff, Acting Assistant Secretary for Nuclear Energy at DOE.

Leveraging advanced construction technologies can drive down costs and speed the pace of advanced nuclear deployment to tackle global climate change and meet the President’s goal of net-zero carbon emissions by 2050.
The project team is led by GE Hitachi Nuclear Energy.
One technology is vertical shaft construction, a best practice from the tunneling industry that could reduce construction schedules by more than a year.
Another technology is Steel BricksTM, modular steel-concrete composite structures similar to high-tech LEGO pieces, which could significantly reduce the labor required on site.
A third technology is advanced monitoring coupled with digital twin technology, which can create a 3D replica of the nuclear power plant structure.
These technologies can be applied to a variety of advanced reactor designs to significantly improve the economics of bringing advanced reactors to market.

GE Hitachi Nuclear Energy leads a proposal team that includes Black & Veatch, the Electric Power Research Institute, Purdue University, Caunton Engineering, Modular Walling Systems Limited, University of North Carolina at Charlotte, Nuclear Advanced Manufacturing Research Centre, and the Tennessee Valley Authority.
Jon Ball, executive vice-president of GE Hitachi Nuclear Energy, stated that the funding will significantly benefit the commercialization of SMRs and pave the way for other advanced reactors.
The work is funded and managed through DOE’s National Reactor Innovation Center (NRIC), which was established in 2019 to enable advanced reactor demonstration and deployment.
The project, referred to as the Advanced Construction Technology initiative, will be conducted in two phases.
The initial phase will focus on technology development and preparation for a small-scale demonstration.
Pending successful completion of the first phase and future appropriated funds, a second phase is planned to carry out the demonstration within three years of this award.
source_title: DOE and GE Hitachi Team Up to Lower Costs of Building New Nuclear Reactors | Department of Energy

facts: Georgia’s Department of Economic Development welcomed representatives from the state’s 12 international offices abroad for a weeklong visit to Georgia during the last week of April 2023.
The representatives are placed in 12 strategic markets, including Brazil, Canada, Chile, China, Colombia, Europe, Israel, Japan, Korea, Mexico, Peru, and the United Kingdom and Ireland.
Georgia’s Japan and Europe offices were opened by then-Governor Jimmy Carter 50 years ago.
In 2022, 52% of Georgia exports and 57% of Georgia’s total trade involved markets where Georgia maintains international representation, according to the 2022 Trade Report.
Every county in the state exports goods, and small businesses make up 85% of Georgia’s exporters.
Over the course of the week, 22 representatives met with local communities and businesses during their journey from Atlanta to Savannah.
The group connected with GDEcD’s Atlanta office for training and project update sessions.

The group attended receptions hosted by Georgia EMC, the Metro Atlanta Chamber, Georgia Allies, the Savannah Economic Development Authority (SEDA), and the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority.
Penelope Kim from the Korea Office stated: “This week is like going to ‘Georgia school!’”
The state tour featured multiple industry visits, including companies that support a few of Georgia’s key exports such as automotive products.
JD Irving / Irving Consumer Products industry visit took place in Macon.
Irving Tissue has successfully expanded in the state multiple times, helping the company reach key markets to drive growth while also setting high standards for community involvement.
The business currently employs over 300 Georgians and over the last six years has invested nearly $1 billion into a community and state that have acted as a partner to this international business.
Denmark-based company Dinex hosted the representatives for a tour of their operations in Dublin Willie Paulk Industrial Park.
Since locating in Dublin, Dinex has invested more than $6.5 million in machinery and equipment and its employment has grown to nearly 100 people.
Dinex manufactures emission control equipment for OEM and aftermarket truck and heavy equipment brands, representing a staple in Dublin-Laurens County’s automotive sector.
The Dublin facility represents Dinex’s manufacturing presence in North America.
Before departing Dublin, the group viewed the Georgia Highway 257 Rail Served Site and ADFORS/Saint-Gobain and Erdrich, both located on Willie Paulk Parkway.

At the Georgia Advanced Manufacturing Training Center, Georgia Quick Start’s latest training facility in the state, representatives observed how the program provides customized training free-of-charge to qualified new, expanding, and existing businesses in the manufacturing industry.

Various new electric vehicle related companies announced and under construction around the state are eligible to utilize Quick Start as a partner, including the nearby Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
The group met with partners at the Georgia Ports Authority (GPA) for a tour of the Port of Savannah, the largest single-terminal container facility of its kind in North America.
More than $174 billion in goods flowed through the Ports of Savannah and Brunswick in 2022.
GPA continues to invest in port capacity and infrastructure to maintain the port’s position as a global gateway.
In February, GPA welcomed the largest ship-to-shore cranes on the East Coast at the Port of Savannah.
GPA is planning $1.8 billion in investments in the ports this year (referring to 2023).

In Bryan County, the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority (JDA) briefed the representatives about Hyundai’s plans for its first dedicated electric vehicle (EV) and battery manufacturing facility in Georgia.

As a sign of the state’s growing presence in international business and its position as a leader in the electric mobility industry, seven Tier-1 suppliers have announced investments in the EV ecosystem around the state since Hyundai’s May 2022 announcement.
In the last three years, at least 38 EV investments have been announced in the state totaling over $21.9 billion in investment and creating more than 28,000 new jobs in Georgia.
source_title: Representatives From Georgia’s 12 International Offices Convene | Georgia

facts: An In-Plant Training focused on process heating will be hosted at Hollingsworth & Vose Company in Hawkinsville, GA.
Each INPLT is designed to help companies train and empower their staff on optimizing industrial systems.
The training underscores the host company's commitment to reliable production.
Interested individuals are instructed to contact their TAM if they are interested in attending.
The event is part of the Better Buildings & Better Plants Initiative.
source_title: Hollingsworth & Vose Company / Hawkinsville, GA | Better Buildings & Better Plants Initiative

entity_name: Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) FY22 Economic Development Record
canonical_name: Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) FY22 Economic Development Record
facts: During fiscal year 2022 (July 1, 2021, to June 30, 2022), Georgia achieved record economic development investments and job creation supported by GDEcD’s Global Commerce team through 358 projects.
Investments in expansions and new locations totaled more than $21.2 billion.
51,132 jobs were created statewide during fiscal year 2022.
Excluding the two largest projects in state history—Rivian and Hyundai Motor Group—Georgia jobs and investments for the year totaled 35,532 and $10.7 billion, respectively.
There were 12 automotive projects that created a total of nearly 16,000 new jobs across the state.
Eighty-five percent of the investments and more than 30,000 new jobs came from outside the 10-county metro Atlanta region.
Companies choosing to expand in Georgia accounted for 74 percent of total projects.
New locations created 33,846 jobs across the state.
The advanced manufacturing sector added more than 10,000 new jobs in the state.
Both the automotive and advanced manufacturing industries include manufacturers and suppliers for the electric vehicle (EV) industry.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America’s (HMGMA) development includes EV battery manufacturing along with affiliate suppliers on-site.
Component recyclers Aurubis and Ascend Elements have joined HMGMA as part of Georgia’s efforts to build a sustainable, reliable supply chain for its electric mobility ecosystem.
Foreign direct investment accounted for over $8 billion in investments created in fiscal year 2022.
The top five sources of foreign direct investment were South Korea, Germany, Japan, France, and the Netherlands.
Korea-based projects represented the highest job creation and investment from a single country.
Germany produced the second most jobs and investments after Korea.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson led economic development missions to the United Kingdom, South Korea, and Germany during FY22.
Missions included meetings with economic development drivers like Hyundai Motor Group and SK On.
The state discussed new opportunities for development with the German state of North Rhine-Westphalia and the Liverpool City Region Freeport.
GDEcD offers 'Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development' (GRAD) certification to recognize pre-qualified industrial sites for which due diligence has been completed prior to a prospect visit.
Through GRAD, Georgia has certified more than 60 industrial sites as ready for fast-track industrial projects.
Georgia’s Quick Start program provides specialized training tailored specifically to business needs and has operated for 55 years.
Access to Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport, the Port of Savannah, and a robust network of highways and rail lines supports the logistics and distribution industry.
source_title: Georgia Shatters Investment and Job Records in FY22 | Georgia

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced on November 1, 2023 that Hyundai Industrial Co., Ltd., a global auto parts supplier, will create 100 new jobs and invest more than $24 million in a new manufacturing facility near Newnan in Coweta County.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that partners like Kia and Hyundai are attracting a large network of suppliers, creating jobs and opportunity for hardworking Georgians across the entire state.

Governor Brian Kemp noted that these suppliers have now committed to bringing over 5,300 jobs to Georgia, mostly to communities outside of large urban areas, since last year's Hyundai Metaplant announcement.
Hyundai Industrial was established in 1978 and is headquartered in Ulsan, Korea.
The Hyundai and Kia supplier specializes in manufacturing car seats, arm rests, and head rests.

Mr. Woosuck Kim, Team Leader at HDI America Inc., stated that Hyundai Industrial has more than four decades of experience providing comfortable and safe experiences for drivers and passengers with some of the world’s biggest automotive brands.

Hyundai Industrial expressed excitement about being part of the e-mobility transition in Georgia and looking forward to working with Coweta County to create mutually beneficial growth for the community and future employees.
Hyundai Industrial has purchased an existing building at 83 Amlajack Way in Coweta County.
The purchased building will be retrofitted to produce arm and head rests for OEMs, including Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
Hyundai Industrial will focus on filling manufacturing production roles at the new facility.
Coweta County Board of Commissioners Chairman John Reidelbach welcomed Hyundai Industrial Co., Ltd. to Coweta County.
The new facility will join thriving manufacturing industries and have close proximity to I-85, making transport to nearby automobile plants effortless.

Director of Korean Investment Yoonie Kim represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project in partnership with the Coweta County Development Authority and Georgia EMC.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that with Kia in West Georgia and Hyundai on the eastern coast, suppliers like Hyundai Industrial can locate almost anywhere in the state and still be center of at least two major manufacturers.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson noted that companies are able to select a community that’s the right fit for them, and vice versa.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson described the relationship between Coweta County and Hyundai Industrial as another classic example of Georgia’s partnership approach to economic development, matching a community with an existing asset and plan to a company whose goals aligned.

For over a century, Georgia has fostered healthy industry practices, encouraged collaboration and innovation, and positioned the state as a leader in developing and harnessing emerging technologies for the evolving automotive and mobility industry.
As the electric vehicle market continues to grow, Georgia has pursued the entire supply chain, creating more than $25.1 billion in investments and 29,000 jobs since 2020.
In fiscal year 2023, job creation in the automotive industry increased by 324 percent when compared to fiscal year 2021.
Suppliers attracted by Georgia’s mobility original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) resulted in over $2 billion in investment across the state in FY23.
Hyundai Industry Co., Ltd. aims to manufacture automotive car seats that not only provide comfort but also play a critical role in safety.

Hyundai Industrial creates safer and more comfortable experiences through developing cutting-edge products based on talent and advanced technology while also striving for mutually beneficial growth for its employees and the community.
source_title: Hyundai, Kia Supplier to Invest in Manufacturing Facility in Coweta | Georgia

facts: The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) announced an additional $14.8 million investment in the Industrial Training and Assessment Centers (ITAC) Implementation Grants program.
This funding will support 57 small- and medium-sized manufacturers (SMMs) across the country to implement energy and efficiency projects.
Projects include installing onsite solar and heat pumps, improving lighting and heating, and electrifying industrial equipment and fleets.
The grants are funded by the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law and administered by DOE’s Office of Manufacturing and Energy Supply Chains (MESC).
The ITAC Implementation Grants program provides grants of up to $300,000 per project to manufacturers to implement recommendations from DOE and other qualified energy assessments.
These grants aim to help small businesses lower energy costs, improve efficiency, reduce harmful emissions, and strengthen the domestic manufacturing sector.

U.S. Secretary of Energy Jennifer M. Granholm stated that the Biden-Harris Administration is bringing energy saving improvements to small businesses across the nation to drive down costs and strengthen the manufacturing backbone of many communities.
Small- and medium-sized manufacturers (SMMs) represent the vast majority of the nation's manufacturing base.
The 57 selected projects are expected to abate more than 33,000 tons of carbon dioxide emissions per year, equivalent to about 11 small businesses’ annual emissions.
The $14.8 million federal investment will leverage an additional $33.2 million from industry to support these projects.
18 of the selected projects are located in disadvantaged communities.

This investment supports the Biden-Harris Administration’s Justice40 Initiative, which sets a goal that 40% of the overall benefits of certain federal investments in climate and clean energy flow to disadvantaged communities.
The ITAC program is currently open for additional applications.
DOE announced four additional entities to provide and coordinate energy assessments, including one with cybersecurity expertise.
For more than four decades, the ITAC program has provided more than 21,000 energy assessments at SMMs.
The program typically identifies more than $150,000 in potential annual energy savings opportunities for every manufacturer.
Since launching the implementation grant program in 2022, 219 businesses across 38 states have been selected to receive a combined total of more than $40 million through the ITAC program.
Past grants have been matched by more than $83 million in industry investment.
These past improvements are expected to save $21.2 million in annual energy savings and abate about 88,000 tons of carbon dioxide emissions per year.
Selection for award negotiations is not a commitment by DOE to issue an award or provide funding.
Before funding is issued, DOE will ensure compliance with the National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA).
DOE, its partnership intermediary ENERGYWERX, and applicants will undergo a negotiation process, during which DOE may cancel negotiations and rescind the selection for any reason.

DOE is working with ENERGYWERX under an innovative Partnership Intermediary Agreement (PIA) set up by the DOE's Office of Technology Transitions to broaden engagement and facilitate clean energy solutions.
SMMs can register to attend “office hours” on January 7, 2025, from 2:00-3:00 pm EST.
The MESC mission is to strengthen and secure manufacturing and energy supply chains needed to modernize the nation’s energy infrastructure and support a clean and equitable energy transition.
source_title: Biden-Harris Administration Announces Nearly $15 Million to Further Bolster Domestic Manufacturing and Support Small- and Medium-Sized Businesses | Department of Energy

facts: Hyundai Motor Group signed a memorandum of understanding (MOU) with SK On Co., Ltd. to secure a supply of EV batteries in North America.
The MOU signing took place at SK Group Headquarters in Korea.
Heung-soo Kim, Executive Vice President and Head of Corporate Future Growth Planning Division & EV Division of Hyundai Motor Group, participated in the signing.
Young-chan Choi, Chief Administrative Officer of SK On, participated in the signing.
Under the MOU, both parties will cooperate to provide SK On batteries to Hyundai Motor Group’s plants in the U.S. after 2025 for EV production.
Executive Vice President Kim stated that the EV battery MOU will accelerate efforts to secure EV leadership in the North American market.
Hyundai expects the stable supply of EV batteries from SK On to contribute to emissions reduction and meet climate goals in the market.
SK On Chief Administrative Officer Choi Young-chan stated that the cooperation between SK On and Hyundai Motor Group is expected to create significant synergy.
Both companies expect to hold a solid position in the process of electrification in the North American auto market.
Hyundai Motor Group has set a global target to sell 3.23 million full electric vehicles annually by 2030.
To realize its electrification goals, Hyundai Motor Group plans to establish a global EV production network to ensure a stable supply of EVs around the globe.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group Signs MOU with SK On to Secure EV Battery Supply for North America

facts: On April 29, 2026, Hyundai Motor Group and Georgia Tech announced an expansion of their collaboration to advance hydrogen-powered transportation, deepen applied research and education, and accelerate the use of zero-emissions vehicles in Georgia.
The partnership will bring hydrogen fuel cell vehicles and fueling infrastructure to campus, turning Georgia Tech into one of the nation’s most prominent campus-based examples for hydrogen mobility.

Ken Ramírez, Executive Vice President and Head of Global Energy and Hydrogen Business at Hyundai Motor Group (a Georgia Tech alumnus, class of 1991, and current member of the Georgia Tech Advisory Board), stated the collaboration combines university research with Hyundai’s global experience to create real-world solutions for the energy transition and inspire future mobility leaders.

Randy Parker, President and CEO of Hyundai Motor North America, donated a handful of NEXO fuel cell SUVs to expand the campus clean mobility footprint and provide real-world experiences with zero-emissions transportation technology.

Ángel Cabrera, President of Georgia Tech, stated the expanded partnership advances hydrogen research, reduces emissions on their campus, and strengthens Georgia’s role in the future of clean mobility.
The partnership includes the donation of four Hyundai NEXO fuel cell electric SUVs by Hyundai Motor North America.
A hydrogen electrolyzer project will be installed at Georgia Tech’s North Avenue Research Area (NARA).

The donated vehicles and installed infrastructure will support campus operations and interdisciplinary research focusing on engineering, sustainability, energy systems, and public policy related to hydrogen adoption.
Faculty and students will study the real-world performance of hydrogen technology, infrastructure requirements for large-scale deployment, and environmental impacts of hydrogen energy systems.
Insights gathered from the initiative aim to inform and accelerate the widespread use of hydrogen technology in campuses, fleets, cities, and freight corridors.
The initiative supports Georgia Tech’s strategic plan goal of expanding the use of zero-emissions vehicles powered by sustainable energy sources.
Beyond hydrogen energy, the partnership aims to advance innovation in autonomous driving, electric vehicle (EV) batteries, charging infrastructure, materials science, and cybersecurity.

Georgia is home to the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) and serves as a hub for zero-emission transportation through HTWO Logistics, a clean logistics partnership operating Hyundai XCIENT fuel cell heavy-duty trucks in logistics operations near Savannah.
The collaboration reinforces the link between education, research, and Hyundai’s long-term goal of achieving carbon neutrality by 2045.
The partnership aims to further clean mobility research, generate workforce talent, and position Georgia as a national leader in hydrogen and zero-emissions transportation.
Additional announcements regarding the partnership’s research projects, educational programs, and vehicle deployment are expected in the coming months.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group and Georgia Tech Sign MOU to Further Collaborate on Hydrogen Mobility Development  | Press Releases & Insights | Worldwide | HTWO

facts: General Motors is executing an unprecedented transformation focused on an accelerated transition to electric and autonomous vehicles.
GM invested billions to develop the Ultium Platform, a combined EV architecture and propulsion system that enables EVs at scale for every lifestyle and price point.
GM delivered its first Ultium-powered EVs to customers.

Ultium-powered vehicles include the GMC HUMMER EV Pickup, BrightDrop Zevo 600 and Zevo 400, Wuling Hong Guang Mini EV (the best-selling EV in China), Cadillac LYRIQ, Chevrolet Silverado EV, and Chevrolet Equinox EV.
By the end of 2025, GM expects to have more than 2 million units of EV capacity globally, with 1 million in North America and another 1 million in China.
GM recently announced investments of nearly $7 billion for a significant expansion of battery cell and EV assembly capacity in the United States.
The $7 billion U.S. expansion is expected to create 4,000 new jobs and retain 1,000 others.
This investment is part of GM's larger commitment to invest more than $35 billion in EVs and AVs from 2020 to 2025.
GM is investing aggressively in other growth platforms and new businesses, including Cruise, BrightDrop, GM Defense, OnStar Insurance, and Ultifi.
GM named Kyle Vogt as the CEO of Cruise.
Under Kyle Vogt's leadership, driverless Cruise test vehicles are rapidly accumulating miles and transporting members of the public throughout San Francisco.
Cruise is one permit away from being able to provide paid ride-hailing services in San Francisco.
GM recently increased its stake in Cruise.
GM announced plans to be carbon neutral in its global products and operations by 2040.
GM plans to eliminate tailpipe emissions from new light-duty vehicles globally by 2035.
GM set SBTi-validated science-based emission reduction targets for scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions that align with the most ambitious goals of the Paris Agreement.
GM announced a plan to source 100 percent renewable energy to power its U.S. facilities by 2025.
The target to source 100 percent renewable energy for U.S. facilities is five years ahead of GM's previous target and 25 years ahead of its initial target.
GM aims to source 100 percent renewable energy globally by 2035.
GM updated its executive compensation program to better link the long-term compensation of executives to its EV strategy.
Jane Mendillo informed GM she will not stand for re-election and will retire from the Board following the Annual Meeting, having provided insights that helped accelerate the Company's EV future.

links.context: Governance, Compliance, ESG Policies, and Political Contributions Disclosures

source_title: DEF 14A

facts: The production share of vehicles with all-wheel or 4-wheel drive has increased rapidly over the past 10 years, reaching 59% in 2022.
All-wheel drive/4-wheel drive systems are popular because they can improve traction and handling performance, especially in inclement weather.
All-wheel drive/4-wheel drive systems have extra components that increase weight and mechanical friction, which can negatively impact fuel economy.
Manufacturers have made technological improvements to minimize the impact on overall vehicle efficiency.
In 1975, rear-wheel drive was the predominant drive type, installed on 91% of all light-duty vehicles produced.
By 1985, front-wheel drive became the most common drive type and remained dominant until 2017.
Since 2017, all-wheel drive/4-wheel drive has become the most common drive type in light-duty vehicle production.
Data for 2022 are preliminary.

Sources include the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency's The 2022 EPA Automotive Trends Report (December 2022) and an SAE Technical Paper comparing tailpipe emissions and fuel economy for 2WD versus AWD vehicles.
source_title: FOTW #1277, February 13, 2023: Nearly 60% of Light-Duty Vehicles Produced in 2022 had All-Wheel or 4-Wheel Drive | Department of Energy

facts: Governor Brian Kemp and First Lady Marty Kemp joined Hyundai Motor Company President/Global COO and Hyundai/Genesis Motor North America President/CEO José Muñoz to celebrate Hyundai Motor Group’s impact on Georgia and update progress on Metaplant America.
The facility will officially start vehicle production in the fourth quarter of this year.

Governor Brian Kemp stated Hyundai has been an incredible partner for decades, helping recruit businesses, giving back to communities, and working with colleges and universities to connect Georgians with opportunities.
Hyundai and Genesis electric vehicles on display included the IONIQ 5, IONIQ 6, GV60, and Electrified GV70.
A hydrogen-powered XCIENT Fuel Cell Truck will be used for logistics needs between Hyundai’s manufacturing facilities and suppliers.
Governor Kemp officially declared February 26 as Hyundai Day throughout the state.
The Georgia General Assembly passed a Resolution commending Hyundai Motor Group’s dedication to Georgia and recognizing February 26 as Hyundai Day at the State Capitol.
José Muñoz stated Hyundai’s investments in Georgia will create tens of thousands of great-paying American jobs and bring tens of billions of dollars in economic impact for decades to come.
Since the Metaplant America project was announced in May 2022, LG Energy Solution was named as the joint venture partner in the manufacture of battery components.
An additional expansion of the LG Energy Solution battery facility was announced.
Hyundai and SK On are building an EV battery manufacturing facility in Bartow County to supply Hyundai Motor Group’s U.S. plants.
Hyundai Motor Group’s planned investment in Georgia has reached more than $12.6 billion.
The project is bringing with it the creation of 12,000 direct, long-term jobs.

Lieutenant Governor Burt Jones and House Speaker Jon Burns attended alongside legislative leaders, GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson, local elected officials from the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority, and company representatives.
Lt. Governor Burt Jones applauded Governor Kemp, state agencies, and the University System of Georgia, especially Georgia Tech, for their partnership with Hyundai.
Up to 300,000 Hyundai, Genesis, and Kia EVs are expected to be produced annually at the Metaplant.
17 HMGMA suppliers have announced projects across 10 Georgia counties spanning beyond southeast Georgia, with further announcements expected.
Supplier facilities are expected to create more than 6,700 new jobs and more than $2.5 billion in investment.
Supplier facilities will be operational prior to the opening of HMGMA.
Speaker of the House Jon Burns credited Governor Kemp and the General Assembly for sound policy direction that enabled Hyundai’s investment and expansion in Southeast Georgia.
The Metaplant will serve as a force multiplier for additional investments in education, healthcare, housing, and transportation.
The nearly 3,000-acre Metaplant site is located in Bryan County, Georgia.
Four building exteriors are complete at the site, and outfitting for internal operations is underway.
The Metaplant’s architecture incorporates technologically advanced elements alongside design features representing the Savannah area, Hyundai’s brand identity, nature, and clean energy.
HMGMA is hiring full-time employees, referred to as "Meta Pros," at a rapid pace for training and skills on machinery, office and clerical work, and numerous other employment roles.
HMGMA currently has 566 full-time employees.
Approximately 75 percent of the current workforce are from the Greater Savannah area.
An additional 37 employees are from Georgia but outside of the local area.
Georgia Quick Start broke ground on its customized training center at the Metaplant site on February 8.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated Georgia fulfilled one of its commitments to Hyundai Motor Group and Georgians with the training center groundbreaking.
HMGMA partnered with local technical colleges to prepare students for entry-level employment at the Metaplant.

Electrical Vehicle Professional Technical Certificate of Credit (EVP TCC) training is available at Coastal Pines Technical College’s Baxley and Waycross campuses, Southeastern Technical College’s Vidalia campus, Savannah Technical College, and Ogeechee Technical College.

TCSG Commissioner Greg Dozier stated the Technical College System of Georgia is providing comprehensive training programs tailored to the growing electric vehicle industry in partnership with Hyundai.

In September 2023, Georgia Institute of Technology (Georgia Tech) and Hyundai Motor Company signed a memorandum of understanding to collaborate on research and applications for sustainable mobility, hydrogen economy development, workforce development, and smarter cities.
U.S. News & World Report ranked Georgia Tech as the top public university and No. 3 nationally in energy and fuel research.
Georgia Tech President Ángel Cabrera stated the strategic partnership with Hyundai will advance science powering key sectors and produce the next-generation workforce needed for these industries.
Since 1998, Hyundai has directed over $6 million towards local Georgia communities through philanthropic programs and partnerships.
The company is promoting STEM programs in local K-12 schools and hosting educational programs on alternative energy sources.
Hyundai is supporting local organizations dedicated to affordable homeownership and focusing on environmental stewardship.

Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority’s Trip Tollison stated Hyundai has given more than $1 million to regional nonprofits and formed key partnerships with local educational institutions.
Trip Tollison noted Hyundai committed to $7.59 billion in capital investment in Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America and will create 8,500 jobs.
More than 550 Meta Pros have already been hired, with 75 percent coming from the Savannah region.
Beginning with mass production, the electricity used to power the Metaplant will come from 100 percent clean power sources.
This aligns with Hyundai’s commitment to carbon neutrality by 2045.
A 41-acre park will be featured in front of the plant, including trails, running tracks, environmental art displays, sports fields, and other amenities for employee use.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America is Hyundai Motor Group’s first dedicated Electric Vehicle mass-production plant.
The new plant will boast a highly connected, automated, and flexible manufacturing system that organically connects all elements of the EV ecosystem.
The Georgia facility will become an intelligent manufacturing plant where order collection, procurement, logistics, and production are optimized utilizing AI and data.
The innovative manufacturing system will help create a human-centered work environment with robots assisting human workers.
Hyundai Motor Group is a global enterprise with about 250,000 employees worldwide and mobility brands including Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis.
source_title: Governor Kemp, state and local leaders celebrate Hyundai Motor Group’s historic impact on Georgia – Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority

facts: OSHA cited Hyundai Transys Georgia Powertrain Inc. – operating as Powertech America Inc. – for exposing employees to struck-by and fall hazards after a fatality at the company’s West Point, Georgia, facility.
The automobile transmission manufacturer faces $68,194 in penalties.
An employee suffered a fatal injury when a transmission turnover machine activated while the employee performed maintenance.
OSHA cited the company for failing to train employees to control the release of hazardous energy and use energy isolation devices.
OSHA also cited the company for failing to provide lockout procedures to employees in their native language, provide fall protection and implement a hearing conservation program.
OSHA conducted the inspection in conjunction with the Agency’s Regional Emphasis Program for Safety Hazards in Auto Parts Industry.

OSHA Atlanta-West Area Director Jeffery Stawowy stated that failure to adequately control hazardous energy is a leading cause of serious injuries and fatalities in the auto parts industry, and employers must provide workers with the tools and information needed to ensure safety for themselves and coworkers.

The company has 15 business days from receipt of the citations and proposed penalties to comply, request an informal conference with OSHA’s area director, or contest the findings before the independent Occupational Safety and Health Review Commission.

links.context: More information about OSHA's role and standards

source_title: U.S. Department of Labor Cites Georgia Transmissions Manufacturer For Exposing Employees to Safety Hazards After Fatality | U.S. Department of Labor

facts: Georgia has 61 laws and incentives related to alternative fuels and advanced vehicles.
Georgia has 8,761 stations with alternative fuels.
There are 0 public and 3 private biodiesel (B20 and above) fueling stations in Georgia.
There are 16 public and 33 compressed natural gas (CNG) fueling stations in Georgia.
There are 7,529 public and 1,020 private electric charging ports in Georgia.
There are 74 public and 9 ethanol (E85) fueling stations in Georgia.
There are 0 public and 0 hydrogen fueling stations in Georgia.
There is 1 public liquefied natural gas (LNG) fueling station in Georgia.
There are 69 public and 7 propane (LPG) fueling stations in Georgia.
There are 0 public and 0 renewable diesel (R20 and above) fueling stations in Georgia.
Georgia has one Clean Cities and Communities coalition, a U.S. Department of Energy partnership to foster transportation energy choices nationwide.

Gasoline fuel consumption data for Georgia is sourced from the State Energy Data System based on beta data converted to gasoline gallon equivalents of petroleum (GGEs) for the transportation sector from the U.S. Energy Information Administration.
In 2024, there were 120,000 electric vehicle (EV) registrations in Georgia.
In 2024, there were 26,400 plug-in hybrid electric vehicle (PHEV) registrations in Georgia.
In 2024, there were 206,300 hybrid electric vehicle (HEV) registrations in Georgia.
In 2024, there were 101,500 biodiesel vehicle registrations in Georgia.
In 2024, there were 705,700 ethanol/flex (E85) vehicle registrations in Georgia.
In 2024, there were 300 compressed natural gas (CNG) vehicle registrations in Georgia.
In 2024, there were 200 propane vehicle registrations in Georgia.
In 2024, there were 0 hydrogen vehicle registrations in Georgia.
In 2024, there were 0 methanol vehicle registrations in Georgia.
In 2024, there were 8,261,200 gasoline vehicle registrations in Georgia.
In 2024, there were 205,900 diesel vehicle registrations in Georgia.
In 2024, there were 74,900 unknown fuel vehicle registrations in Georgia.
Vehicle registration counts are sourced from TransAtlas with data from Experian Information Solutions.
As of October 2025, the average price for biodiesel (B20) in the Lower Atlantic PADD region was $2.67/gallon ($2.43/GGE), compared to a national average of $3.74/gallon ($3.40/GGE).
As of October 2025, the average price for biodiesel (B99–B100) in the Lower Atlantic PADD region was $4.50/gallon ($4.46/GGE), compared to a national average of $4.30/gallon ($4.26/GGE).
As of October 2025, the average price for ethanol (E85) in the Lower Atlantic PADD region was $2.50/gallon ($3.53/GGE), compared to a national average of $2.63/gallon ($3.71/GGE).
As of October 2025, the average price for natural gas (CNG) in the Lower Atlantic PADD region was $2.53/GGE, matching the national average.
As of October 2025, the average price for propane in the Lower Atlantic PADD region was $3.06/gallon ($4.22/GGE), compared to a national average of $3.42/gallon ($4.72/GGE).
As of October 2025, the average price for gasoline in the Lower Atlantic PADD region was $2.89/gallon ($2.89/GGE), compared to a national average of $3.14/gallon ($3.14/GGE).
As of October 2025, the average price for diesel in the Lower Atlantic PADD region was $3.57/gallon ($3.21/GGE), compared to a national average of $3.74/gallon ($3.37/GGE).
Fuel price data is sourced from the Alternative Fuel Price Report.
Transportation projects in Georgia include Advancing Equitable Access to Electric Vehicles, Infrastructure, and Jobs in Underserved Communities in Georgia.
Transportation projects in Georgia include the DeKalb County / Metropolitan Atlanta Alternative Fuel and Advanced Technology Vehicle Project.
Transportation projects in Georgia include Developing Replicable, Innovative Variants for Engagement for EVs in the USA (DRIVE Electric USA).
Transportation projects in Georgia include Development of a National Liquid Propane (Autogas) Refueling Network, Clean School Bus/Vehicle Incentive, and Green Jobs Outreach Program.
Transportation projects in Georgia include the East Coast Commercial Zero-Emissions Vehicle Corridor Planning Partnership.
Transportation projects in Georgia include the Maryland Hybrid Truck Goods Movement Initiative.
Transportation projects in Georgia include NFPA Spurs the Safe Adoption of Electric Vehicles through Education and Outreach.
Transportation projects in Georgia include No One Silver Bullet, But a Lot of Silver Beebees.
Transportation projects in Georgia include the Southeast Alternative Fuel Deployment Partnership.
Transportation projects in Georgia include the Southeast Propane Autogas Development Program.
Transportation projects in Georgia include the Southeast Regional Alternative Fuels Market Initiatives Program.
Transportation projects in Georgia include Southeast Regional Readiness.
Electricity sources and annual emissions per vehicle data for Georgia is sourced from the Electricity Sources and Emissions Tool based on assumptions with 2024 data from the EIA.

Additional resources for Georgia include the Georgia Data – U.S. Energy Information Administration, Georgia State Energy Office, Georgia Electric Vehicle Charging Plan, Georgia – National Association of State Energy Officials (NASEO), Maps and Data – Alternative Fuels Data Center, Maps – National Laboratory of the Rockies, Electric Vehicle Infrastructure Toolbox (EVI-X), and State Electrification Planning and Funding.
