facts: Automotive parts company Hwashin will establish a new manufacturing facility in Georgia.
Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Hwashin will establish its new manufacturing facility in Dublin, Laurens County.
The new facility will create over 460 new jobs.
The project involves more than $176 million in investment in Laurens County.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that Georgia's goal is to become the e-mobility capital of the nation.
Hwashin operates as a Tier-1 supplier specializing in crafting chassis and body parts.
Hwashin's customers include Hyundai Motor Group, Kia, and international original equipment manufacturers (OEMs).
Hwashin is headquartered in Korea and has established production facilities across the United States, Asia, and South America.
Seo Jin Jung serves as the CEO of Hwashin.
Hwashin draws from 50 years of expertise in its collaboration with Georgia and Laurens County.
Hwashin aims to position the Dublin-Laurens County region at the forefront of the evolving automobile industry.
Hwashin's new Georgia facility is scheduled to begin production in late 2025.
The facility will be located at the Georgia Highway 257 Rail Served Site.
The site is the first Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Select site in the state.
A “GRAD Certified Select” status indicates a site has met or exceeded more rigorous certification requirements.
The facility will primarily manufacture chassis components for OEMs, including Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America and Kia Georgia.
Dublin Mayor Joshua Kight welcomed Hwashin to the community.
The Dublin-Laurens County Development Authority staff and board assisted in presenting Dublin-Laurens County as a business location to Hwashin.
Jeff Davis serves as the Chairman of the Laurens County Board of Commissioners.
Hwashin will be the seventh international company to call Dublin and Laurens County home.
The project demonstrates a partnership and "can-do attitude" between Laurens County, the City of Dublin, and the Dublin-Laurens County Development Authority.
Griff Lynch is the President and CEO of the Georgia Ports Authority.
The Georgia Ports Authority welcomes Hwashin as the newest member of its manufacturing community.
Alyce Thornhill is the Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy at the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team.
The project was executed in partnership with the Dublin-Laurens County Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia EMC, and Georgia Quick Start.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Hwashin will have direct interstate access to at least two long-term automotive partners from its chosen site.
Locating on a GRAD Select site allows Hwashin to pursue an accelerated construction timeline.
In fiscal year 2023, job creation in Georgia's automotive industry increased by 324 percent compared to FY21.
Suppliers attracted by Georgia’s mobility OEMs resulted in over $2 billion in investment across the state in FY23.
Since 2020, Georgia has created more than $25.1 billion in investments and 29,000 jobs pursuing its entire EV supply chain.
Hwashin Co., Ltd. was founded in 1975.
The company actively engages in research and development for chassis and body parts.
Hwashin has expanded its product offerings to include lightweight aluminum components and eco-friendly solutions.
Hwashin Co., Ltd. employs more than 4,200 personnel worldwide.
source_title: Hawashin to create 460 jobs with new GA facility

facts: Lilly invested $1 billion into a new injectables factory in North Carolina in January.
Lilly is investing $2.1 billion to build two new manufacturing sites at LEAP Lebanon Innovation and Research District in Boone County, Indiana.
The company announced the Indiana expansion on Wednesday.
Lilly is headquartered in Indianapolis, Indiana.
The new facilities will broaden Lilly’s manufacturing network for active ingredients plus new modalities like genetic medicines.
The sites are intended to help Lilly respond to heightened demand for its current and future drugs.
Lilly highlighted 17 new drug approvals in the past eight years, including this month's approval for tirzepatide (christened Mounjaro) for Type 2 diabetes.
Lilly expects to hire 500 new employees to staff the Indiana plants.
Lilly currently employs more than 3,700 manufacturing workers in Indiana.
CEO David Ricks stated: “More than 146 years after our founding, Lilly remains committed to investing and innovating in Indiana.”
CEO David Ricks stated: "These new sites will add capacity in support of our growing pipeline of innovative medicines, while also creating more high-tech jobs for Hoosiers."
Lilly’s most recent expansion in Indiana was unveiled in 2019.
Prior to the 2019 expansion, Lilly made cash outlays in research and manufacturing around Indianapolis totaling more than $2.5 billion over five years, which generated additional manufacturing jobs.
For the Boone County project, Lilly plans to partner with several organizations, including the Indiana Economic Development Corporation.
Lilly expects each new company role to generate “an additional four indirect jobs.”
The project is estimated to require about 1,500 construction jobs during plant construction.
In late January, Lilly announced it was investing around 400 million euros ($445.3 million) into a new biologics ingredients plant in Limerick, Ireland.
Shortly after the Ireland announcement, Lilly telegraphed a $1 billion build-out of an injectables facility in Concord, North Carolina.
The North Carolina facility plans to recruit approximately 600 new hires.
Construction on the North Carolina plant is poised to begin in 2022 and will take several years to complete.
Lilly anticipates “additional future investments in manufacturing to address growth expected from potential new medicines to treat diabetes, Alzheimer’s disease, cancer and autoimmune conditions.”
In April, CEO David Ricks criticized Indiana's education attainment during a speech before The Economic Club of Indiana, stating: “Our education attainment in the state is not good.”

Regarding workforce reskilling, Ricks stated: “The ability to reskill the workforce, I think, could improve. Health, life and inclusion, overall, I think conditions rank poorly nationally in our state. And also workforce preparedness, also related to reskilling, is a liability for us.”
Ricks noted Indiana’s healthcare costs outstrip those of neighboring states, making it unattractive to potential employers.
The article was updated with additional comments from Lilly CEO David Ricks.
source_title: Lilly is boosting manufacturing in IN with $2B investment

facts: Kia maintains manufacturing facilities in eight countries along with research centres in South Korea, the United States, Japan, and Germany.
In 2022, Kia announced an additional $480 million to open at least five new production facilities while $67 million of the existing investment will be used to improve commuting conditions.
Kia Manufacturing Georgia (KMMG), Kia's plant in West Point, Georgia, is capable of producing 350,000 cars annually for the North American and global markets.
It was announced in 2006 and went online in 2010, with the state of Georgia providing significant incentives.
At an initial cost of $1 billion (US) the facility comprises 2.2 million-square-feet on more than 2,200 acres (8.9km2) of land near Interstate 85.
In addition to four main areas (stamping, welding, paint, and assembly), the facility also includes a transmission shop, module shop, and a two-mile (3km) test track.
The assembly area features more than a half mile of height-adjustable conveyors and wood flooring.
Kia Georgia is home of the Telluride SUV, Sorento SUV, Sportage SUV, all-electric Kia EV6 SUV, and the all-electric EV9 SUV.
This advanced manufacturing facility represents a $2.8 billion investment on a 2,200-acre site, operating 24 hours a day.
Kia's Georgia plant recorded cumulative production of 1 million units in 2013 and exceeded 3 million units in September 2019.
On December 31, 2021, President Jangsu Shin retired and a new CEO, Stuart Countess, was appointed effective January 2022.
On May 20, 2022, Kia's parent company Hyundai announced plans to construct a new electric vehicle plant near Savannah, Georgia.
Construction of the sprawling site took two years, with production starting in late 2024, and the site is now dubbed the Hyundai Metaplant.
source_title: List of Kia design and manufacturing facilities - Wikipedia

facts: Over $1 billion investment positions Honda to build ICE, hybrid, and EV models on same production line.
All-new Acura RSX SUV will be first battery electric model produced at Honda EV Hub in late 2025.
Production models based on Honda 0 Saloon and Honda 0 SUV prototypes introduced at CES 2025 will follow the Acura RSX.
Retooled production facilities will provide an enhanced work environment for associates and even more sustainable production processes and equipment.
Honda has provided a first look at its innovative approach to manufacture electric vehicles (EV) by late 2025 at the Honda EV Hub in Ohio.
The flexible approach uniquely positions Honda to build internal combustion engine (ICE), hybrid-electric, and battery electric vehicles (EVs) on the same production lines.
Honda is retooling the Marysville Auto Plant (MAP), East Liberty Auto Plant (ELP) and Anna Engine Plant (AEP).
The investment will reach over $1 billion.

Honda has reimagined manufacturing to create a work environment that is more human-friendly and environmentally responsible while achieving the highest levels of quality, efficiency and value for customers.

The Honda EV Hub provides Honda with the flexibility to produce ICE, hybrid-electric and EV models on the same production lines so we can quickly respond to shifting customer needs and market conditions, said Bob Schwyn, senior vice president, Honda Development & Manufacturing of America, LLC.

Beyond adding the capability to produce EVs, Honda completely reimagined its approach to manufacturing, transforming the Honda production environment with more human-friendly processes and sustainable manufacturing practices, according to Bob Schwyn.

The Honda EV Hub will begin EV production in late 2025 with the all-new Acura RSX EV followed by Honda EV models based on the Honda 0 SUV and Honda 0 Saloon prototypes the company debuted on January, 7 at CES 2025 in Las Vegas.
Honda also debuted its original vehicle operating system (OS), the ASIMO OS, for use in Honda 0 Series models.
The use of the ASIMO name pays homage to the beloved Honda ASIMO robot and the "Wise" value of the Honda 0 Series.
Honda announced plans to establish the Honda EV Hub in Ohio in October 2022.
Honda's vision is to make zero emissions vehicles represent 100% of its vehicle sales by 2040.
Honda and LG Energy Solution committed to invest $3.5 billion in a new JV battery facility, L-H Battery Company.
The overall investment for the L-H Battery Company facility is projected to reach $4.4 billion.
The L-H Battery Company facility will have an annual capacity of approximately 40GWh.
Production at the L-H Battery Company facility is scheduled to start in late 2025.

In addition to serving as the starting point for the global production of the new Honda 0 Series EVs, the Honda EV Hub is playing a critical role in developing the knowledge and expertise for EV technology and production to be shared with other Honda plants in North America and globally.

Honda engineers have utilized the opportunity of reimagining manufacturing at the Honda EV Hub to implement energy-saving and other environmentally responsible initiatives in line with the company's Triple Action to Zero approach.
Triple Action to Zero is a comprehensive strategy aimed at achieving zero environmental impact by 2050, through three critical action areas: carbon neutrality, clean energy, and resource circulation.
All three plants in the Honda EV Hub, MAP, ELP and AEP, play pivotal roles in this initiative and have already begun adopting new processes and equipment to reduce the company's environmental impact.
Honda is targeting net-zero CO2 emissions from its products and operations by 2050.
As part of the retooling of the Honda EV Hub, actions to advance the company toward carbon neutrality include high efficiency melt furnaces installed at AEP that reduce consumption of natural gas.
Honda selected the new melt furnaces due to their potential to be modified in the future to Plasma Arc Melting that would further reduce CO2 emissions.
New air HVAC handlers, more efficient lighting, and energy-saving equipment was introduced at all plants to reduce the company's carbon footprint.

The Honda goal to use 100% sustainable and/or carbon-free energy sources is already offsetting more than 60% of the electricity used in its North American manufacturing operations through long-term virtual power purchase agreements (VPPAs) for renewable wind and solar power.
Honda aims to utilize 100% sustainable materials by reprocessing products back to raw materials and reusing those materials in the creation of new products.
This approach is also applied at the Honda EV Hub.
Honda recycles scrap material at all plants, including aluminum at AEP that is melted onsite and reused to make battery cases and engines.
Honda also is transforming associate uniforms into sound-absorbing insulation for use in Honda and Acura automobiles.
Honda has produced automobiles in Ohio for over 40 years, beginning in November 1982 with the start of automobile production at the Marysville Auto Plant.
Honda established U.S. manufacturing operations in Marysville in 1979 with the start of motorcycle production.
Today, over 12,000 associates support Honda manufacturing in Ohio.
Five plants represent a total capital investment of $13 billion.

The facilities have the capacity to produce 460,000 Honda and Acura automobiles, 1.18 million automobile engines, and over 1 million automobile transmissions and two-motor hybrid systems, using domestic and globally sourced parts.

The company's five Ohio manufacturing facilities include three auto plants, the Marysville Auto Plant, East Liberty Auto Plant and Performance Manufacturing Center, as well as the Anna Engine Plant and transmission plant in Russells Point.

The company has invested more than $1.4 billion in these facilities over the past six years, continually modernizing equipment and adopting new technologies to advance quality and efficiency for Honda and Acura customers here and around the world.

Honda is establishing a new EV Hub for the start of EV production in the U.S. including an investment of over $1 billion to re-tool its existing auto and powertrain plants in Ohio for production of electric vehicles.
Honda is also investing $3.5 billion with LG Energy Solution to establish a joint venture EV battery plant in Fayette County, Ohio.
Honda began auto production in America over 40 years ago at the Marysville Auto Plant (MAP), and the plant is once again serving as a pioneer for the company and the auto industry.
Honda has implemented several new technologies at MAP to support flexible manufacturing.
This began in December 2023, when MAP, East Liberty Auto Plant (ELP) and Anna Engine Plant (AEP), began removing equipment and infrastructure to make way for innovation.
MAP consolidated its two production lines, removing much of Line 2 to create the capability to produce EVs on the same line with ICE and hybrid vehicles.
Consolidation also created more versatile space on the production line for associates to work and enable efficient parts delivery.

Retooling one of MAP’s two weld lines involved installing new weld robots and technology to enable welding EV bodies on the same line with ICE models, while enhancing quality on all vehicles built at MAP.
Changes included securing the EV battery case in the vehicle floor, which required welding side sills to the floor comp just for EV models.
MAP also is adding CDC welding for the first time at Honda globally.

This technology, added to existing weld robots, will expand the use of lightweight and high-strength materials to enhance collision safety performance and improve electrical efficiency by reducing the body frame weight.
Honda engineers renovated an area of MAP to create a workspace for the new role of assembling battery packs for EVs built exclusively at Marysville Auto Plant.

This process will combine the battery module from the joint venture battery facility, L-H Battery Company, with the battery case megacast at the Anna Engine Plant to create EV battery packs for midsize and large vehicles that will be used for EV production at both MAP and ELP.
The battery pack assembly will have the longest automated guided vehicle (AGV) line at Honda.
A new dedicated area on the production line was established for installation of battery packs into EVs at the Marysville Auto Plant.
ICE and hybrid vehicles will pass through this new area without stopping, allowing for multi-platform flexibility.

A new hybrid-electric battery assembly area was established, moved closer to the installation area on the vehicle assembly line, and expanded to meet the record customer demand for hybrid-electric models.
The Vehicle Quality Department (VQD) department has evolved and expanded to enhance quality inspections and include more human-friendly functions.
This involves new optical aiming station improvements to check the alignment and functioning of new safety and connectivity technologies in EVs and the future software-defined vehicles (SDV).
This change accommodates the radar and optical aiming verification required for the new L3 automated driving features on Honda 0 Series vehicles.
The changes provide more space for VQD associates and for vehicles, to create an ideal flow to easily conduct quality checks on 100% of the vehicles produced at the plant.

MAP associates have begun new training programs to prepare for the skills required for EV production, EV battery pack assembly, and quality checks for SDVs, thus maintaining stable employment and a strong team to meet customer demand.
AEP is adding megacasting of EV battery cases to the long list of components the plant has produced since it began production of motorcycle engines 40 years ago.
This history includes engines and engine components, transmissions, camshafts, crankshafts, and high-tech CVT components.
The EV Battery Case will serve as the main frame structure for the floor of Honda and Acura EVs.
AEP has installed six, 6,000-ton high-pressure die cast machines that will megacast EV battery case parts, a mass production first for global Honda.
The battery case is much larger than anything Honda has die cast before and is made in two halves, which are welded together at a later process at AEP.
This enables Honda to produce cases in two different sizes for midsize and large EVs – with one-half of the case common to each size.
The massive presses will offer a new mass production capability, with future flexibility to produce other aluminum parts, such as body frame parts and ICE or hybrid-electric engine components.

Installation of the die-cast machines required modifications to the plant, including the placement of pilons 80 feet below the concrete surface of the plant floor to provide a stable foundation for the 31-feet tall machines.
The use of high-efficiency natural gas melt furnaces that provide the molten aluminum were made with the potential to modify in the future to Plasma Arc Melting (PAM) to further reduce CO2 emissions.
The machines also reduced compressed air usage compared to current AEP melt furnaces and die cast machines used for ICE and hybrid-electric engine production.
Honda is also recycling 100% of the scrap aluminum from the process to be re-used in-house for case and engine production.

In another mass production first for global Honda, AEP is using Friction Stir Welding (FSW) to join the two halves of the aluminum battery case, using an environmentally responsible method to produce high-strength welds that reduces the amount of energy required for welding, and with minimal heat impact on the material.
AEP will use the new weld technology both to join the two halves of the battery case (butt joint) and to attach the aluminum water jacket (lap joint) which is stamped at MAP.
The water jacket has the critical function of maintaining the optimal battery module temperature to maximize battery efficiency.

To quickly and accurately visually analyze the quality of each battery case, AEP will employ a new multi-camera machine to perform Photogrammetry, the science and technology of obtaining reliable analysis of physical objects through the process of recording, measuring and interpreting photographic images and patterns.
AEP will use Photogrammetry systems in two locations to achieve 100% quality confirmation.
Following the die-cast process, four machines equipped with 90 cameras will visually analyze the part shape to ensure 100% quality of the surface of the battery case surface.

After final assembly and prior to shipping of the case to the Marysville Auto Plant, two machines equipped with 120 cameras will visually analyze each case to ensure mountability of the case to the vehicle frame at MAP and ELP.
Photogrammetry machines were originally utilized by the aviation industry.
The quality checks adopt a human-friendly approach that does not require associates to lift or manually check the heavy battery cases.
A machining process was adopted at Honda for the first time for the aluminum battery case that reduces energy use and requires less lubricant to machine parts.
This environmentally responsible approach to manufacturing supports a commitment to delivering superior, high-quality products in a sustainable manner.
Honda currently uses MQL technology on ferrous parts such as crankshafts.
ELP is also undertaking significant innovation as it prepares for EV production.
A new paint department was built at ELP and engineers utilized the time to strengthen carriers for the heavier EV bodies.
The project includes a phosphate and E-coat expansion that is nearing completion.
This expansion will allow ELP to achieve a higher quality of paint appearance.
ELP constructed a new building north of the plant, as the facility did not have space for the expanded department.
As at MAP, the new operation includes a new optical aiming station to check the alignment and functioning of new safety and connectivity technologies in EVs and the future SDVs.
The new building provides more space for VQD associates and for vehicles, to create an ideal flow to easily conduct quality checks on 100% of the vehicles produced at the plant.

Drop lifts, conveyors, and support beams are all being strengthened and improved to handle the additional weight of heavier EV models and offer the flexibility to produce hybrid-electric and ICE models.
Other modifications include a new approach with AGVs on the final assembly line to provide a stronger, larger body carrier for EVs.
The Battery Pack Mount Mezzanine was created to enable EV models to disengage from the main assembly line for EV battery pack installation.
Once the battery pack is installed, EVs will rejoin the existing assembly line to be produced along with ICE and hybrid-electric models.

In addition to making the same changes as the MAP weld department to accommodate EVs, additional changes to weld at ELP include reinforcement and modifications to existing drop lift, transfers, and conveyor systems.
source_title: Honda EV Hub Prepares for New Level of Flexible Production in Reimagined Manufacturing Environment

facts: Anthro Energy and EnPower Inc. signed a memorandum of understanding to establish a long-term, multi-phase partnership.
The companies plan to jointly develop, manufacture, and scale high-performance lithium-ion cells for robotics applications.
EnPower will combine its U.S.-based electrode and pouch cell manufacturing capabilities with Anthro Energy’s Proteus electrolyte platform.
The partnership aims to support longer mission times, safer system designs, and operation in more demanding environments.

David Mackanic, co-founder and CEO of Anthro Energy, stated the partnership is about reindustrializing American manufacturing and rebuilding a domestic battery supply chain for the next generation of energy storage.

The partnership integrates a paradigm-shifting battery platform with U.S. manufacturing capabilities to accelerate commercialization and deliver high-performance energy solutions for defense, robotics, and autonomous systems.
EnPower and Anthro Energy plan to produce the batteries in the U.S.
Alameda, Calif.-based Anthro Energy develops advanced polymer electrolytes to enhance lithium-ion battery performance and safety.
Indianapolis-based EnPower is a developer of lithium-ion battery cell design, integration, and manufacturing.
The partners plan to create a fully domestic pathway from advanced electrolyte chemistry through finished cell production.
A vertically integrated U.S. cell stack will address growing concern over foreign dominance of the lithium-ion supply chain.
The collaboration aims to support the reindustrialization of American battery manufacturing for defense and dual-use applications.
The companies anticipate scaling production capacity beyond 750 MWh.
This capacity would make it one of the largest U.S.-based advanced lithium-ion cell platforms outside of automotive supply chains.
According to a 2026 industry report, the artificial intelligence and robotics market in aerospace and defense is projected to grow from $26.9 billion in 2025 to $29.7 billion in 2026.
This reflects a 10.5% annual growth rate driven by increased adoption of autonomous drones, robotics, and AI-enabled defense systems.
Each platform depends on high-performance, domestically manufactured batteries, a category currently underserved by the U.S. supply base.
Anthro Energy and EnPower will collaborate across multiple phases to deliver high-performance and high-power cells.
A separate agreement will define product specifications, performance targets, and commercial timelines.
The partners are developing high-performance pouch cell designs for mission-critical applications.
Cells will exceed 350 watt-hours per kilogram (Wh/kg) for high-energy applications while maintaining industry-leading safety.
EnPower and Anthro Energy will achieve this by combining synergistic electrolyte and separator technologies.
The companies will develop high-rate or ultra-high rate capability for applications needing lift (drones) or ride-through power (AI data centers).
Target applications include defense, unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), unmanned underwater vehicles (UUVs), drones, robotics, embodied AI systems, and public safety equipment.
Adrian Yao, founder and CEO of EnPower, stated EnPower is strategically positioned to serve as an integrator of domestic battery technologies and a vector for rapid commercialization.
Anthro Energy recently announced plans to establish a large-scale, U.S.-owned and operated advanced electrolyte manufacturing facility in Louisville, Ky.
The Louisville facility will be just two hours away from EnPower’s 92,000 sq. ft. (8,547 sq. m) Indianapolis facility.
source_title: Anthro Energy partners with EnPower to develop lithium-ion cells in the U.S. - The Robot Report

facts: In 2023, Honda announced that Accord production would be moved to the factory in Greensburg, Indiana, to make way for electric vehicle production at the Marysville, Ohio, plant.
source_title: Honda Accord - Wikipedia

facts: Georgia secured Kia's first US auto manufacturing facility via diligence, prompt action, and close collaboration.
The KMMG project announcement came six months after Kia requested a special meeting with representatives of the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD).

Georgia was chosen for its skilled workforce, world-class transportation infrastructure, top-ranked state-sponsored employee training program, pro-growth corporate environment, and dedication to fostering innovation.
The facility has reached capacity thanks to these resources.
More than 3 million automobiles have been produced by KMMG since operations started in 2009.
KMMG produces the Sorento, K5 (formerly Optima), and Telluride at its 2,200-acre, $1.1 billion plant in Troup County, Georgia.
The plant employs 2,700 workers.
More than 14,000 jobs have been created in the West Georgia region by Kia and its suppliers.
More than 40% of all Kia automobiles sold in the US are now produced by KMMG.
940 dealerships in the US and Canada receive more than 90% of vehicles built in West Point.
Vehicles are shipped to Mexico, countries in the Caribbean, and the Pacific.
GDEcD's Trade Division assisted Kia in shipping Tellurides to the Middle East via the Brunswick port with the Georgia Ports Authority.
Jangsoo (Jason) Shin serves as President and CEO of KMMG.
Shin stated that "the total support of the state agencies has been nothing short of spectacular."

Kia has received assistance from over a dozen local organizations, including Labor, Transportation, and Community Affairs Departments, Georgia Ports Authority, Office of the Attorney General, and the state's technical college system.
KMMG is a founding partner of Georgia MadeTM, a GDEcD program offering logistics, production, and marketing assistance for goods made in Georgia.
Governor Brian Kemp visited the facility to support Georgia MadeTM's launch.
Georgia has been rated the best state for business for seven years straight.
Leaders commend TCSG's Quick Start program, which built a 70,000 square foot Kia Georgia Training Center and collaborates with Kia on its operation.
Quick Start uses cutting-edge methods to offer specialized, advanced industrial training to KMMG employees.
Shin states that Georgia Quick Start serves as a model for workforce development initiatives.
The state's first online application process handled more than 125,000 applications for Kia positions.
Georgia Quick Start created an industrial maintenance certification program taught at West Georgia Technical College to help KMMG overcome a labor shortage.
Kia's workforce development activities led to the creation of hundreds of co-op programs for college and technical school students.
Along with Troup County's THINC College and Vocational Academy, KMMG sponsors STEM initiatives in the neighborhood school system.
KMMG's corporate social responsibility program addresses diversity, wellness, environment, and education.
"The Ray" is a stretch of I-85 in West Georgia serving as a testing ground for new transportation technology.
At the state-run West Point Visitor Information Center, KMMG provides a free solar panel charging station and a tire inspection station.
KMMG modified operations to create face shields during the COVID-19 crisis, producing about 550,000 units with assistance from GDEcD and Georgia Tech Manufacturing Institute.
KMMG gave thousands of life-saving equipment items (face shields, masks, gloves, surgical caps, alcohol pads, BioHazard bags) to the Georgia Emergency Management and Homeland Security Agency.
Six Kia vehicles were made available by the company to transport medical supplies across the state.
Pat Wilson is Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner.

Wilson stated that Kia inquired how they could help during the pandemic, stepped up to offer life-saving equipment, and expressed appreciation for Kia's support as a member of the Georgia MadeTM family.

Shin cites Wilson's emphasis on interpersonal connections inside Georgia, noting success begins with confidence developed from them and that there is a wonderful network of accessibility between state and local economic groupings.
KMMG is Kia Motors Corporation's first manufacturing facility in North America, located in West Point, Georgia, on 2,200 acres.
KMMG started mass production on November 16, 2009, with an annual capacity of 340,000 units.

The Kia Motors assembly factory in West Point was forced to halt operations due to a semiconductor chip shortage caused by COVID-related work stoppages in Asia, costing the plant two weeks of production that year.
source_title: How Much Do Kia Plant Workers Make - GearShifters

canonical_name: Honda Development & Manufacturing of America, LLC
facts: Honda produced its 30 millionth vehicle in America in early 2023.
The achievement was based on associates at eight auto manufacturing facilities in Alabama, Georgia, Indiana, and Ohio.
Honda's first auto plant in America was the Marysville Auto Plant in Ohio, established 40 years ago.
Honda's total investment in new facilities over the past four decades totals almost $20 billion.
Honda's vision is to make battery-electric and fuel cell electric vehicles represent 100% of its vehicle sales in North America by 2040.
Honda plans to produce those electrified vehicles in America within the next few years.
Bob Schwyn joined Honda 35 years ago (in 1988) as an equipment engineer in the paint department at the Ohio plant, when cumulative production was less than 500,000 vehicles.
Advanced technology is now the cornerstone of auto production, requiring vast amounts of computing power.
Modern Honda vehicles contain more computing power in safety sensing and connected car technologies than the entire Ohio plant had when Schwyn joined.
Honda opened a second auto plant in Ohio.
Honda established a new auto and engine production facility in Alabama, with engineers from Ohio and Canada helping construct and launch it.
Over the past 25 years, eight plants in Ohio, Alabama, and Indiana produce some 12 Honda and Acura vehicles, along with engines, transmissions, and hybrid-electric systems.
The Alabama Auto Plant began production in 2001.
Lamar Whitaker is a home-grown Honda associate who joined the company in 2001 and succeeded Schwyn as Plant Lead of the Alabama Auto Plant.
Honda is establishing an EV Hub in Ohio with a $700 million investment to re-tool three plants for producing EVs and EV components.
The EV Hub will include a new EV battery plant, which is a joint venture investment of $3.5 billion with LG Energy Solution to make batteries for EVs built in Honda auto plants.
These investments focus on attracting and preparing the next generation of skilled associates for the future manufacturing workforce.
Bob Schwyn holds the title of Senior Vice President at Honda Development & Manufacturing of America, LLC.
source_title: 40 Years and 30 Million Vehicles Made in America:  How did we get here and where are we going?

entity_name: Honda Transmission Manufacturing Plant in Georgia (TMP-G)
canonical_name: Honda Transmission Manufacturing Plant in Georgia
facts: Honda Transmission Manufacturing Plant in Georgia (TMP-G) began production in Tallapoosa in 2006.
The plant started production of 5-speed transmissions on May 12, 2006.
Since its opening, Honda associates in Georgia have built more than five million transmissions.
The Georgia plant is the exclusive source of the advanced 10-speed automatic transmission for Honda and Acura vehicles built in the U.S.
Honda has grown its workforce at the Georgia facility to nearly 500 associates.
The Tallapoosa facility employs about 480 associates.
The plant has a total investment of more than $485 million.
The facility has an annual capacity to build 375,000 transmissions.
TMP-G was formerly called Honda Precision Parts of Georgia (HPPG).
The plant produced 6-speed automatic transmissions before adding a second assembly line in 2017.
TMP-G is the first Honda plant in the world to produce the advanced 10-speed automatic transmission (10AT).
Five years ago, the facility revamped its original production line to increase production of the 10AT.
The 10AT transmission is featured in the Honda Pilot, Passport, Odyssey, and the Acura MDX.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that since its grand opening, the facility has provided hundreds of jobs for the community while serving as a strategic location for Honda's global production process.
Arturo Valdes, TMP-G Plant Lead, noted the facility will play an important role in the future supporting North American production.
Last year relative to the article, Honda awarded more than $950,000 in funding to non-profit community-based organizations in Georgia.
TMP-G associates volunteered over 3,200 hours at numerous events across the region last year.
The Tallapoosa facility is one of two Honda automobile transmission plants and one of 12 major Honda manufacturing facilities in America.
The plant serves as the lead global facility for production of the company’s state-of-the-art 10-speed automatic transmission.
The plant uses domestic and globally made parts for its transmissions.
Over the past 20 years, the plant added the casting and machining of transmission cases to its initial assembly operations.
The facility spans 575,000 square feet.

links.context: Contact email for marketing and events

source_title: Honda Transmission Plant in Georgia Celebrates 20 Years of Operation  - Greater Haralson Chamber

facts: Hyundai Motor Group plans to deploy Atlas humanoid robots at its Metaplant America facility in Georgia.
The Atlas robots are developed by Boston Dynamics and are designed to perform tasks traditionally carried out by humans.
This initiative marks a significant milestone in Hyundai’s ongoing partnership with Boston Dynamics, which is aimed at scaling up robotic manufacturing and design at its facilities.
Last month, Hyundai committed to purchasing tens of thousands of robots from Boston Dynamics, a company it acquired from SoftBank in 2021.
At the Georgia plant, Hyundai already uses Spot robots for industrial inspections.
The facility features a dedicated robot that removes car doors before vehicles enter General Assembly.
A fixed robot reinstalls the doors toward the end of the process, a technology unique to the Georgia facility.
Hyundai has not disclosed how many Atlas robots will be deployed or what specific tasks they will perform.
The company plans to further expand the use of robots across its global manufacturing facilities to streamline processes and enhance efficiency.
The automation drive is part of Hyundai’s $21 billion investment plan in the U.S.
$6 billion of the investment is specifically earmarked for innovation, automation, and strategic partnership.
U.S. President Donald Trump’s tariffs on imported autos has also forced Hyundai to ramp up these efforts.
North America remains Hyundai’s largest market.
Despite a global sales slump of 2 percent last year, Hyundai’s sales in the U.S. rose by 4 percent, reaching 1.9 million units.
The company’s strong performance in North America was a key driver behind its record consolidated revenue last year.
Hyundai’s vehicles are known for their price competitiveness, especially in the midrange segment, and continue to appeal to U.S. consumers.
Hyundai aims to manufacture 300,000 electric and hybrid vehicles annually at the new Metaplant America facility.
At its recent Grand Opening Ceremony, the company announced plans to ramp up production to 500,000 units over time, without specifying a timeline.
Hyundai expects to produce 1.2 million vehicles annually on U.S. soil in total.
This represents a 70 percent increase from the estimated production volume of roughly 700,000 vehicles in 2024.
The automaker is mulling building capacity at its Alabama plant.
Lee Jae-il, an analyst at Eugene Investment & Securities in South Korea, stated that hybrid vehicles with favorable cost performance have contributed significantly to Hyundai’s brand strength.
The company remains vulnerable to the impact of tariffs on imports from South Korea, which make up a large portion of Hyundai’s U.S. sales.
Hyundai President Jose Munoz confirmed in April that the company will not raise prices until June.
A correction dated May 5, 2025, clarified that the correct annual production target for HMGMA is 300,000 units, not 100,000 as previously stated.
A further correction clarified that the doors at HMGMA are installed robotically but not by Atlas robots.
source_title: Hyundai bets $21B on Atlas humanoid robots for US car assembly

canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Company Georgia EV Facility
facts: Almost 500 people were arrested at a Hyundai factory in Georgia by immigration authorities during the largest workplace raid of President Donald Trump's second term.
The 3,000-acre site was built by the Korean company to manufacture electric vehicles and had been operational for one year at the time of the raid.
A majority of those detained at the facility are Korean nationals.
South Korea expressed 'concern and regret' over the operation and urged the US government to respect the rights of its citizens.
The Department of Homeland Security stated agents executed a search warrant due to allegations of 'unlawful employment practices and other serious federal crimes'.

Steve Schrank, special agent in charge of Homeland Security Investigations in Atlanta, said at a Friday news conference that it was not an immigration operation but a multi-month criminal investigation where evidence, interviews, and documents were gathered for a judicial search warrant.
Schrank described the raid as 'the largest single-site enforcement operation in the history of homeland security investigations'.
The raid highlights a tension between President Trump's priorities of building US manufacturing and cracking down on illegal immigration, potentially stressing relations with a key ally.
President Trump stated in the Oval Office on Friday: 'They were illegal aliens and ICE was just doing its job.'

Regarding Seoul's reaction, Trump told reporters: 'Well, we want to get along with other countries, and we want to have a great, stable workforce. And we have, as I understand it, a lot of illegal aliens, some not the best of people, but we had a lot of illegal aliens working there.'
Trump added that the detained workers 'came through with Biden' and 'came through illegally'.
Immigration officials reported that 475 people who were in the country illegally or working unlawfully were detained.
The detainees were held at a US Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) facility in Folkston, Georgia, pending further decisions on their placement.
Of the detained individuals, 300 are reported to be Korean nationals.
Hyundai Motor Company issued a statement saying it was 'closely monitoring the situation and working to understand the specific circumstances'.
Hyundai stated that as of the report date, none of those detained are directly employed by the company.
Reuters reported that Hyundai's electric vehicle production at the site was not affected by the raid.
LG Energy Solutions, Hyundai's partner in a battery joint venture at the site, paused construction work following the raid.
South Korea dispatched diplomats to the site and contacted the US embassy in Seoul, urging the US 'to exercise extreme caution' regarding the rights of Korean citizens.

The South Korean foreign ministry stated that 'the economic activities of Korean investment companies and the rights and interests of Korean citizens must not be unfairly infringed upon during US law enforcement operations'.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp had previously touted Hyundai's new electric vehicle operation as the biggest economic development project in the state's history, noting it employs 1,200 people.
South Korean companies have promised to invest billions of dollars in key US industries in the coming years, partly to avoid tariffs levied by Trump.
The raid occurs amid Trump's broader post-return-to-office effort to round up individuals thought to be in the US illegally, hold them in detention facilities, and frequently deport them.
source_title: South Koreans detained in ICE raid at Hyundai electric vehicle site in Georgia

facts: U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement raided Hyundai Motor Group's Metaplant America in eastern Georgia on Sept. 4.
The agency detained 475 workers on allegations that they were employed in the United States illegally.
The raid shocked the auto industry as automakers have already been grappling with tariff turmoil due to the Trump administration's stance on trade.
Hyundai Motor Company calls its Georgia plant a "key pillar" of the group's $12.6 billion investment in Georgia.
The vehicle assembly and battery plant was set to produce up to 500,000 hybrid and electric vehicles for the company's portfolio of brands.
Hyundai committed to an additional $21 billion investment in U.S. manufacturing growth from 2025 to 2028.
The decision to raid the South Korean automaker's plant puzzled some political observers considering President Donald Trump's push for private and foreign investment.
On Sept. 11, a plane from Atlanta carrying over 300 Hyundai employees headed to South Korea.
Hyundai Motor Company President and CEO Jose Munoz said production at the battery plant will likely be delayed 2 to 3 months.
A production delay could mean that the plant won't produce as many of their popular EVs such as the Hyundai Ioniq 5 and Kia EV6.
The delay could also cause a delay in production of planned new electric vehicle nameplates for 2026.
Delayed battery production may force Hyundai to import batteries and parts, which could have cost implications for the automaker and consumers.
As of August 2025, Hyundai sold 32,683 Ioniq 5 small electric SUVs.
As of August 2025, Hyundai sold 8,318 Ioniq 6 small electric sedans.
August Ioniq 5 sales increased a whopping 61% compared to 2024, according to sales report data.
August Ioniq 6 sales increased by 30% compared to August 2024.
Hyundai introduced the Ioniq 9 electric midsize SUV this year and sold 3,102 units for the year as of August.
Tesla remains the undisputed sales champ in the EV space.
Chevrolet's Equinox EV has been a success for the U.S. brand.
Competitors from rivals Honda, General Motors, and Ford continue to gain momentum in the EV market.
source_title: ICE raid hits Hyundai EV output — What it means for car buyers

facts: Georgia marked a record year with $26.3 billion in pledged investment, while the share of that total coming from international companies decreased compared to recent years.

Of the 23,200 new private-sector jobs announced by companies in the state in the year ended June 30, 6,500 (about 28 percent) came from companies headquartered in Canada, South Korea, and Japan, which were the top three.
This represents a far lower share than two years ago, when foreign investors accounted for nearly half of new jobs announced and two-thirds of foreign investment in capital terms.

Prior years appear to have been FDI outliers due to massive investments from Hyundai Motor Group in fiscal 2022 and related suppliers for its Metaplant continuing into 2023, which contributed to South Korea emerging as the top investor nation.
Without Hyundai, fiscal 2022 would have seen only $3 billion in announced foreign investment, driven by projects like Korean solar panel maker Qcells, Norway’s Freyr Battery, and Finland’s ADMARES.
That $3 billion total is roughly the same as the amount of foreign investment the state saw announced in fiscal 2025.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development notes that the number of international projects is up.
More than a quarter of the 423 projects the state worked on this year involved international companies, totaling 115, which is up from 114 last year and 76 in 2022.

Most international projects were expansions, with about two-thirds of new international investment (about $3 billion) coming from existing investors like Canada’s Irving Tissue, which committed another $600 million to its Macon facility and added 100 new jobs.
In metro Atlanta, Germany’s Mercedes-Benz announced plans to expand its Sandy Springs headquarters, hire 500 additional people, and develop a separate R&D center elsewhere in the region.
Pat Wilson, commissioner of the Georgia Department of Economic Development, stated that the state's stable and reliable business climate gives companies confidence to plan for the long term.
Pat Wilson added that whether a company is new to Georgia or already there, domestic or international, the question is no longer if they will expand in the state, but when.
Expansions made up three-quarters of the 423 projects and perennially account for most new jobs.
The companies that expanded already accounted for 50,000 jobs in the state, which is a key reason the state aggressively pursues major projects.
New greenfield projects included Greenbox Systems’ $144 million automated warehouse in Butts County backed by Japan’s SoftBank, and beauty company Kiss USA’s fulfillment center in Bryan County.
The Greenbox Systems and Kiss USA projects have promised 300 and 395 jobs, respectively.
The state’s Global Commerce figures carry a caveat that while many announced expansions are underway, most are multi-year projects that do not commence hiring and production until years later.
As market conditions and company plans change, the actual number of jobs created and capital deployed may not match initial pledges.

High-profile examples of stalled or altered projects include Freyr pulling the plug on its $2.6 billion plant in Newnan, Sungeel Hi-Tech reconsidering a Toccoa recycling facility, and Aspen Aerogels moving to source from China instead of building a new $325 million plant in Georgia.
All three stalled projects faced difficulty as the EV market tightened, threatening Gov. Brian Kemp's signature EV strategy amid the Trump administration's pullback on EV incentives.
The state does not announce when prior investment pledges fall through, but it has recourse to recoup taxpayer funding spent on wooing those projects.
While money for site preparation may already be spent, states and communities can use “clawback” provisions to recoup some tax incentives calculated on baseline job and capital investment levels.
Gov. Brian Kemp’s office issued the news release detailing the state's investment and job figures.
source_title: Foreign firms' share of Georgia investment falls in record year overall - Global Atlanta

canonical_name: Hyundai-LG Energy Solution Georgia Battery Joint Venture
facts: South Korean President Lee Jae Myung ordered "all-out efforts" to respond to the arrest of hundreds of its citizens by US ICE during a raid at a factory under construction in Georgia to manufacture car batteries.
South Korean Foreign Minister Cho Hyun stated the government established a task force following the arrest of 300 South Korean nationals.
Cho Hyun indicated he would travel to Washington if necessary and expressed feeling a "heavy responsibility" regarding the arrests.
US immigration officials reported that ICE arrested approximately 475 workers during the raid.
South Korea's LG Energy Solution (LGES) suspended business travel for its US-bound employees after 47 of its staff were detained.
The Georgia factory is designed to manufacture batteries to power Hyundai, Kia and Genesis electric vehicles.
LGES instructed employees already in the US to return to South Korea immediately.
An LGES executive planned to travel to the US on Sunday to address the detention of its staff.
The raid constitutes the largest single-site enforcement operation in the history of the US Department of Homeland Security (DHS).
While US immigration officials stated on Friday there were 475 arrests, the Korea Economic Daily previously reported approximately 560 workers at the Hyundai facility and LGES were detained.
The Thursday raid caused a significant setback to the joint venture's substantial project in Georgia.
The operation represented a dramatic escalation of the Trump administration's immigration crackdown.
The facility represents the largest industrial investment in Georgia's history.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp hailed the project as a huge boost for the economy.
The raid highlighted the disruptive impact of the Trump administration's mass deportation agenda on businesses, contrasting with White House efforts to attract foreign investment.
A DHS agent stated that ICE, the FBI and the DEA conducted a judicially authorized enforcement operation due to an active investigation into unlawful employment practices.
Steven Schrank, special agent in charge of homeland security investigations for Georgia, confirmed during a news briefing that arrests were ongoing.
A South Korean government official stated that the arrested individuals were being held at an ICE detention facility.
The Georgia battery factory is a joint venture between South Korean battery maker LGES and Hyundai Motor.
According to LGES, the facility was due to start operations at the end of this year.
The arrests risk exacerbating tensions between Washington and Seoul, a key ally and investor in the US.
The US and South Korea have been at odds over the details of a trade deal that includes $350bn of investments.
At a summit last month, South Korea pledged $150bn in US investments, including $26bn from Hyundai Motor.
A spokesperson at the Hyundai-GA battery company stated the joint venture is cooperating fully and has paused construction work.
DHS officials stated the arrested workers were barred from working in the US after crossing the border illegally or overstaying visas, following an investigation lasting several months.
A Hyundai Motor spokesperson clarified that none of the people detained was employed directly by the automaker.
Hyundai Motor stated it complies with all laws and regulations wherever it operates.
Hyundai Motor reported its production of electric vehicles at the sprawling site was not affected.
Steven Schrank confirmed the detained workers were being held at ICE's facility in Folkston, Georgia.
In 2023, Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution (LGES) announced the $4.3bn venture to produce EV battery cells, with each company holding a 50% stake.
The battery factory is part of Hyundai's $12.6bn investments in Georgia, including the automaker's just-opened car factory.

The project is described as "the largest economic development project in the state's history" and aligns with broader South Korean investments in US industry announced in March as Trump was ramping up tariffs.

Social media video footage showed an individual wearing a vest with the letters HSI telling workers in yellow safety vests they had a search warrant for the whole site and demanding construction cease immediately.
While President Trump stated his goal is to deport "the worst of the worst" criminals, ICE figures have shown a rise in non-criminals being picked up.
Rights advocates have denounced these types of immigration raids.
HSI led the raid with assistance from the Georgia department of public safety and state troopers.
source_title: Seoul promises to help hundreds of Korean workers arrested in US in Ice raid | US immigration | The Guardian

facts: Hyundai announced plans to invest a fresh $20 billion in the US.
The investment includes a new $5.8 billion steel plant near Donaldsonville, Louisiana, about 40 miles south of Baton Rouge.
The Louisiana steel facility would be Hyundai's first such plant in North America and will provide metal supplies to neighboring states.
President Trump claimed the steel facility would create more than 1,400 jobs as part of the investment.
Hyundai already operates factories in Alabama and Georgia.
President Trump stated that Hyundai will massively increase its auto manufacturing in Georgia.
President Trump stated that Hyundai will invest billions of dollars in advanced American technology companies.
The investment announcement was made to avoid looming 25% tariffs on foreign-made steel and aluminum announced by the administration in February.
Metals produced in the US will be exempt from the 25% tariff, which applies without exceptions or exemptions to all countries.
Chung Eui-sun, executive chairman of Hyundai Motor Group, congratulated Trump on his administration and stated the company shares a vision for "American industrial leadership."
CEO José Muñoz said the president's promised tariff policies incentivized the company to invest big time in America as the most important market to increase localization.
The announcement coincided with an April 2 deadline for reciprocal tariffs on global goods, which the administration promoted as "Liberation Day."
South Korea has a massive trade surplus with the US and is likely to be affected by the April 2 tariffs.
President Trump indicated he may give breaks to many countries regarding the April 2 tariffs but did not specify which nations would be affected.

Other foreign companies, including Japan's Softbank and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co., recently announced major planned US investments to curry favor with the administration before the tariffs take effect.
source_title: Hyundai plans fresh $20 billion investment in US, including new Louisiana steel plant, as tariff threats loom

facts: South Korea's Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution Ltd. will invest 5.7 trillion won (US$4.3 billion) in a battery cell plant in the United States.
The investment is a move to better deal with the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) amid growing demand for electric vehicles.
The two companies will set up a 50:50 joint venture within this year (2023) to build the plant.
The plant will have an annual capacity of 30 gigawatt-hours, enough to power 300,000 EVs.
The plant will be located near Hyundai Motor Group's dedicated electric vehicle and battery plant under construction in Georgia.
Construction of the plant is planned to begin later this year (2023).
The companies aim to start production at the plant in late 2025.

Hyundai Motor President and Chief Executive Officer Chang Jae-hoon stated that Hyundai Motor Group is shifting its focus to electrification and aims to become a leader in the global EV market based on this battery cell plant project.
LG Energy Solution CEO Kwon Young-soo stated that the two industry leaders have joined hands to take the lead in the North American EV market.
The joint company will supply battery cells for EVs manufactured at Hyundai Motor Co.'s Alabama plant.
The joint company will also supply battery cells for EVs manufactured at Kia Corp.'s Georgia plant.
The joint company will supply battery cells for the upcoming dedicated EV and battery plant named the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA).
The move responds to IRA tax credits of up to $7,500 to buyers of EVs assembled only in North America.
There are concerns that Hyundai Motor and Kia could lose ground in the U.S. market because they currently make most of their EVs at domestic plants for export to the U.S.
In October, Hyundai Motor Group began construction of a separate 300,000-unit-a-year EV and battery plant in Georgia.
The separate Georgia plant aims to begin commercial production in the first half of 2025.
Early this year (2023), Hyundai Motor began producing all-electric GV70 SUVs under its independent Genesis brand at the Alabama plant.
Last month (April 2023), Hyundai Motor Group and SK Group's battery unit SK On announced plans to build a 6.5 trillion-won EV battery plant in Bartow County, Georgia.
The Hyundai-SK On Georgia plant aims to start production in the second half of 2025.
source_title: (LEAD) Hyundai Motor, LG to invest 5.7 tln won in EV battery plant in U.S.

facts: Hyundai Motor Group is spending approximately USD 5.54 billion to build an EV plant and a battery manufacturing facility in Bryan County, Georgia.
Hyundai Motor Group entered an agreement with the State of Georgia to build full electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facilities in Bryan County.
Commercial production at the new facilities is scheduled to begin in the first half of 2025.
The new facilities will have an annual capacity of 300,000 units.
Hyundai Motor Group aims to produce a wide range of innovative EV models at the new facility.
The Group aims to become a leading provider of electrified mobility solutions with this U.S. investment.

An official signing ceremony was held in Bryan County, attended by Georgia Governor Brian P. Kemp, other Georgia officials, Hyundai Motor Company President and CEO Jaehoon Chang, and Hyundai Motor President and Chief Operating Officer José Muñoz.
Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung joined the signing ceremony virtually.
Georgia Governor Brian P. Kemp stated that the project is the largest in the state's history and will deliver high-quality jobs on the leading edge of mobility to Georgians.
Hyundai is locating a world-class project in Bryan County within a year of Georgia’s investment in the Bryan County Megasite.
Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung stated that the new facilities will be the first dedicated full EV and battery manufacturing facilities in the U.S. for the Group.
The new facility is expected to break ground in early 2023.
The battery manufacturing facility will be established through a strategic partnership, with details to be disclosed at a later stage.
Local EV production will increase U.S. consumer accessibility to the Group’s innovative EVs.
Through the battery manufacturing facility, the Group aims to establish a stable supply chain and build a healthy EV ecosystem in the U.S.
The EV and battery manufacturing plant will be located on a dedicated 2,923-acre site in Bryan County, Georgia.
The site has immediate access to I-95 and I-16 highways, creating easy access to 250 major metro areas.
The site is located less than 50 kilometers from the Port of Savannah.
The Port of Savannah is the single-largest and fastest-growing container terminal in the U.S., with two Class I rail facilities on-site provided by Georgia Central Railway.
West Point, Georgia is already home to the Group’s Kia manufacturing hub in the U.S.
Hyundai Motor Group selected Georgia due to favorable business conditions, including speed-to-market, a talented workforce, and an existing network of Group affiliates and suppliers.
Georgia is the Southeastern leader for EV registration per 1,000 registered automobiles.
Georgia already offers more publicly available EV charging outlets per capita than anywhere else in the Southeast.
The project is expected to create about 8,100 new jobs.
The EV plant investment is part of the Group’s 2021 announcement to invest USD 7.4 billion by 2025 to foster future mobility in the U.S., including EV production and smart mobility solutions.
Hyundai Motor Group has a global target to sell 3.23 million full electric vehicles annually by 2030.
To realize this goal, the Group plans to establish a global EV production network to ensure a stable supply of EVs around the globe.

Hyundai Motor President and CEO Jaehoon Chang stated that the new EV plant is the future of their business and will help meet growing U.S. customer demands for leading edge design and safe, zero-emissions vehicles.
With additional U.S. EV and battery production capabilities, the Group aims to become one of the top three EV providers in the U.S. by 2026.
The investment aligns with the U.S. government’s roadmap to accelerate electrification.
Last year, the U.S. government announced an initiative requiring sales of zero-emission vehicles to account for at least half of OEM new vehicle sales by 2030.
The new plant will feature a highly connected, automated, and flexible manufacturing system that organically connects all elements of the EV ecosystem.
The Group plans to implement advanced intelligent manufacturing technologies currently under test at the Hyundai Motor Group Innovation Center in Singapore (HMGICS).
All production processes, including order collection, procurement, logistics, and production, will be optimized utilizing AI and data.
The innovative manufacturing system will create a human-centered work environment with robots assisting human workers.
As part of its sustainability commitment, the plant will mainly rely on renewable energy sources and use emission-reduction technologies to meet RE100 requirements.
source_title: Hyundai To Build First Dedicated EV Plant and Battery Manufacturing Facility in the U.S. | ⚡ Ford Lightning Forum ⚡

facts: Hyundai Motor Group will invest US$5.54 billion to build a dedicated electric vehicle and car battery manufacturing plant in the United States.
Construction on the 300,000-unit-a-year EV and battery manufacturing plant is planned to start in January 2023.
Production at the new Georgia plant is scheduled to begin in the first half of 2025.
Hyundai Motor Group selected Georgia due to the state's speed-to-market, workforce, and ability to meet the company's carbon neutrality standards.
Georgia is home to an existing network of Hyundai subsidiaries and suppliers.

Hyundai Motor Group Chairman Euisun Chung stated that the U.S. holds an important place in the Group's global strategy and expressed excitement to partner with the State of Georgia for electrified mobility and sustainability goals.
Governor Brian Kemp described Hyundai's investment as the largest economic development project in Georgia's history.
Non-affiliated suppliers will invest an additional $1 billion in the plant.
The project is expected to generate at least 8,100 new jobs in Georgia.
The new EV plant will help Hyundai Motor Co. and Kia Corp. strengthen their lineup by launching all-electric SUVs such as the Hyundai IONIQ 7 and the Kia EV9 in the U.S.
Hyundai currently operates a manufacturing plant in Alabama, while Kia operates a plant in Georgia.
Last month, Hyundai Motor announced a $300 million investment in its Alabama plant to produce the Santa Fe gasoline hybrid model and the all-electric GV70 SUV under the Genesis brand later that year.
Hyundai plans to roll out 17 EV models by 2030, including six Genesis models.
Kia is scheduled to release 14 EVs by 2027.
The investment aligns with the Biden administration's drive to attract more EV investment and generate jobs in the industry.
The U.S. government aims to have half of the vehicles sold in the U.S. be electrified by 2030.

Hyundai operates seven domestic plants in Korea and 11 overseas plants (four in China, one each in the U.S., Czech Republic, Turkey, Russia, India, Brazil, and Indonesia) with a combined capacity of 5.65 million vehicles.
Kia operates eight domestic plants and seven overseas ones (three in China, one each in the U.S., Slovakia, Mexico, and India) with an overall capacity of 3.84 million units.
The announcement coincided with U.S. President Joe Biden's visit to South Korea as part of his first trip to Asia.
source_title: (LEAD) Hyundai to build US$5.54 bln EV, battery plant in Georgia | Yonhap News Agency

canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group / LG Energy Solution Joint Venture
facts: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution will spend an additional $2 billion and hire an extra 400 workers to make batteries at Hyundai’s U.S. EV plant under construction in Georgia.
The announcement expands on a partnership launched three months ago to produce batteries at the same site west of Savannah, where Hyundai plans to start EV production in 2025.
The news brings the companies’ total investment in the Georgia plant to more than $7.5 billion and the site’s overall planned workforce to 8,500.

José Muñoz, president and global chief operating officer of Hyundai Motor Company, stated that the incremental investment in Bryan County reflects their continued commitment to create a more sustainable future powered by American workers.
In 2022, Hyundai said it would invest $5.5 billion to assemble electric vehicles and batteries on 2,900 acres (1,170 hectares) in the community of Ellabell.
The additional investment and jobs do not mean the Hyundai/LG battery plant will produce more batteries than originally planned.
The companies still expect the plant to supply enough batteries for 300,000 EVs per year, matching the capacity given when the joint venture was first announced in May.
Hyundai spokesperson Michael Stewart said the plant has potential to expand depending on market conditions and demand.
Stewart added that Hyundai remains very optimistic about the transition to electric vehicles.
Hyundai’s Georgia plant initially plans to produce 300,000 vehicles per year but could later expand to build 500,000 annually.
Landing Hyundai’s first U.S. plant dedicated to EV manufacturing was hailed as the largest economic development project in Georgia’s history when it was first announced last year.
Suppliers have pledged to invest nearly $2.2 billion and hire 5,000 people for the project.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp stated that they are building on that success as they continue to make Georgia the e-mobility capital of the nation.

Jessica Atwell, spokesperson for the state Department of Economic Development, said Georgia and local governments will kick in additional incentives to be disclosed next week after documents are finalized.
State and local governments have already pledged $1.8 billion in tax breaks and other perks.
The new investment will allow more tax breaks by law, including another $10 million in state income tax credits at $5,250 per job over five years.
State and local governments could also end up spending more on discretionary incentives like infrastructure aid and equipment subsidies.
The Hyundai package has been described as the largest subsidy package a U.S. state has ever promised an automotive plant, according to Greg LeRoy, executive director of Good Jobs First.
The announcements are part of an electric vehicle and battery land rush across the United States.

Under the U.S. Inflation Reduction Act, EVs must be assembled in North America, and a certain percentage of their battery parts and minerals must come from North America or a U.S. free trade partner to qualify for a full $7,500 EV tax credit.
Currently, no Hyundai or Kia vehicles are eligible for the tax credit unless they are leased.
Hyundai opposed having foreign-made vehicles excluded, in part because it is building American factories.
Hyundai will need batteries for more than just vehicles made in Ellabell.
The company is already assembling electric vehicles at its plant in Montgomery, Alabama.
In April, the company announced it would start assembling its electric Kia EV9 large SUV at the Kia plant in West Point, Georgia.
source_title: Hyundai and LG will invest an additional $2B into making batteries at Georgia electric vehicle plant | AP News
