facts: The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers is launching a “reevaluation” of a key environmental permit for four proposed Floridan Aquifer wells.
The wells are intended to supply up to 6.6 million gallons of water per day to Hyundai Motor Company’s Bryan County manufacturing facility and related development around the nearly 2,500-acre site.

USACE's move is in response to a threatened legal challenge by a coastal Georgia conservation group regarding regulatory approval for the South Korean automaker’s electric vehicle and battery operation near Savannah.
State and local development officials omitted the site’s projected water use in their application for a federal permit required by the U.S. Clean Water Act.
At maximum capacity, the four Bulloch County wells would remove more than 6.6 million gallons per day from the aquifer.
USACE determined that new information has surfaced regarding the effects the project may have on municipal and private water supplies, warranting a reevaluation of its permit decision.
The Ogeechee Riverkeeper organization informed USACE in June that it intended to challenge the October 2022 approval of a permit for the $7.6 billion Hyundai project.
The organization accused USACE of “not completing required steps and overlooking water supply concerns during the permitting process” for the site.
The Clean Water Act requires USACE to collect pertinent information and analyze permit requests that impact or disturb “waters of the U.S.,” including wetlands.

The organization challenged USACE’s findings that it would be reasonable to assume that the Bryan County water supply is adequate to support the plant and related growth without requiring water withdrawals or a permit from the EPD.

Applicants for the federal permit included Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority Secretary Trip Tollison and Pat Wilson, commissioner of the Georgia Department of Economic Development.

Tollison and Wilson knew months before the permit was approved that the Hyundai plant and related development would require up to 6.6 million gallons of water per day and the drilling of four new wells.

This water requirement information was included in a confidential “letter of intent” dated April 25, 2022, addressed to Robert Boehringer, managing partner at consulting firm KPMG International, and signed by Tollison and Wilson.
The water needs were not mentioned in the federal permit application submitted on June 7, 2022, which was also signed by Tollison and Wilson.
USACE approved a revised version of the permit on Oct. 4, 2022, including findings that no additional water or wells would be needed.

A Friday letter from USACE requests the JDA provide an impact assessment of the Megasite’s water supply wells to show they will not impact the Floridan Aquifer, its users, aquatic resources, and environment.
The required impact assessment must include any groundwater and surface water modeling or data collected by the JDA.
USACE will assess the submitted report to determine if the permit needs adjustment, and the JDA may be required to compensate for any resulting impacts.
The Ogeechee Riverkeeper organization welcomed the Corps' decision to revisit the permit.

Damon Mullis, riverkeeper and executive director of ORK, stated that these considerations should have been taken into account from day one and urged the Corps to independently vet information transparently during the reevaluation.
A spokeswoman in USACE’s Savannah office did not respond to a request for comment about the reevaluation or how it could affect the Hyundai project.

Trip Tollison defended the application process, stating that the JDA has been working with local governments and the state on water service since the Georgia Environmental Protection Division is responsible for drinking water and water usage, not the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers.

Experts and federal agency comments suggest Tollison’s defense missed the point, as the Clean Water Act directs USACE to account for “probable impact including cumulative impacts” on factors including “water supply and conservation.”

Stan Meiburg, former deputy administrator at the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency and now executive director of the Sabin Family Center for Environment and Sustainability at Wake Forest University, explained that while USACE cannot dictate where a project gets its water, it must weigh those factors when reviewing applications for permits under Section 404 of the Clean Water Act.

The EPA concurred with Meiburg’s assessment via an email from its Washington office, confirming that the Corps and EPA consider potential effects on potable water supplies when reviewing projects for compliance with Clean Water Act Section 404 regulations.
The Clean Water Act regulates the discharge of dredged or fill material into “waters of the United States,” including wetlands.
The Clean Water Act applied to the Hyundai project because the site contained more than 300 acres of “jurisdictional wetlands” as defined by the law.
USACE is charged with weighing the impact on waters around the site, including those potentially affected by the new wells.
Significant withdrawals from underground aquifers can impact surface waters, which are at the heart of Section 404 permits.

In its Friday letter, USACE directed Tollison and Wilson to provide an assessment of effects on municipal and private water supplies, including whether the anticipated drawdown of the Floridan aquifer would result in any drainage of aquatic resources.
source_title: Permit for Hyundai's Georgia wells to get second look from feds

facts: The Georgia Environmental Protection Division (Georgia EPD) approved permits for wells that will support Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) and related developments last week.
The Georgia EPD conducted a rigorous analysis assessing the impact these developments will have on the area’s water supply.
The Georgia EPD determined that four wells can meet interim water needs while maintaining the integrity of the natural resources.
HMGMA reassured the community that the Georgia EPD’s comprehensive analysis and the establishment of a mitigation fund reflect a commitment to protecting local homes and properties.
Officials are working to build confidence among residents regarding the thoroughness of the analysis and the measures being implemented.
This effort aligns with the findings from a decade of site studies, which demonstrate sufficient water to support industry and agriculture while protecting the environment.
The Georgia EPD permits are separate from wetland permits currently under review by the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers (USACE).
USACE permits focus on the potential impact the metaplant may have on surrounding wetlands.
USACE has requested additional information about the wells to aid in its assessment of the wetland permits, which have been provided, and a response from USACE is expected soon.
HMGMA will bring a $7.59 billion investment to the region.
HMGMA is expected to create 8,500 well-paying jobs.
The company remains committed to working closely with the local community to ensure the area is developed responsibly and sustainably.
source_title: Georgia EPD Issues Permits for Water Wells to Support HMGMA - Savannah Chamber

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America is gearing up for the start of production this fall.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America has been lauded as Georgia’s largest economic project-to-date.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America workforce is up to 861 employees

facts: For automakers and their suppliers, other manufacturers and logistics operations, Georgia’s ports are a key factor in the decision to bring jobs to the Peach State.
GPA President and CEO Griff Lynch stated that GPA's mission is to drive success for the entire state, not just Savannah or Atlanta.
GPA and GDEcD announced 350 projects from potential port users in Fiscal Year 2023.
These announcements will bring more than 33,000 manufacturing and logistics jobs to the state.
These announcements will bring $21 billion in investment to 96 counties across Georgia.
GPA, GDEcD, and Georgia Department of Transportation are working to foster responsible growth delivering good jobs while preserving quality of life.
Georgia Ports is investing $29 million in an overpass that will route trucks leaving Ocean Terminal in Savannah directly onto U.S. 17, avoiding community streets.
GDEcD's Global Commerce division states Georgia’s growing electric vehicle ecosystem has touched every corner of the state.
This EV ecosystem growth is led by announcements associated with Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
Hyundai Metaplant America locations have spread across more than 10 counties in the state.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated companies, especially in e-mobility, are prioritizing speed-to-market solutions.
Investing in site development and obtaining “Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development” certification are ways communities can prepare for new opportunities.
Anovion Technologies is a synthetic graphite manufacturer creating 400 jobs for Southwest Georgia in Decatur County.
Anovion Executive Chairman Chip Dunn described the state’s recruitment effort that swayed the company’s decision to locate in rural Georgia.
The recruitment effort included meetings with Gov. Brian Kemp and other factors like GPA’s inland port system, and access to waterway and land transport.
GPA’s inland port system includes the Appalachian Regional Port in Crandall, Ga.
The inland port system also includes the Blue Ridge Connector rail yard now under construction near Gainesville, Ga.
The inland port system supports the movement of large, heavy products from centrally located communities to the state’s deepwater ports.
In the Savannah area alone, industrial property inventory totals more than 124 million square feet.
More than 14 million square feet of industrial property in the Savannah area was under construction at the close of Calendar Year 2023.

Stephen Ezelle, a broker for Cushman & Wakefield, stated in a fourth-quarter report that the Savannah regional economy continues to experience expansion and strong absorption in the industrial sector despite a slowing U.S. economy.
Ezelle stated the outlook for Savannah’s industrial market is favorable due to continued growth at Georgia Ports with conservative projections to eclipse 8 million TEUs of throughput by 2030.
Operational efficiency offered by GPA and the shift in import cargo from the West Coast and NY/NJ are key drivers in the growth of Savannah’s industrial market, according to Ezelle.
The report indicates Savannah is expected to continue balanced expansion thanks to the Hyundai announcement and GPA’s growing national market share.
Ezelle predicts continued absorption of well-located light industrial warehouse space.
Savannah’s industrial sector ended 2023 with an overall vacancy rate of 7 percent.

GPA Director of economic and industrial development Stacy Watson stated being within 30 miles of the port is great, but not required to be competitive in drayage, lease rates, and cost of doing business.
Watson stated forty or 50 miles away from Savannah is an easy drive, attributed to the port's location upriver of the Savannah population center and GDOT's efforts making truck routes less congested.
Direct truck routes from Port of Savannah connect to Interstates 95 and 16.
On-terminal service from two Class I railroads expedite cargo moving to and from the marine terminal.
Watson noted available properties in Bryan, Liberty, McIntosh counties, and points west offer interstate and rail links to the port and inland markets.
GPA's Stacy Watson cited Augusta as an example, noting a majority of manufacturers there are port users.
Cal Wray, president of Development Authority of Augusta, Ga., stated the region is ripe for growth.

Wray stated Augusta-Richmond County provides an ideal location for port users due to available, affordable industrial land, a key base for manufacturers with over 350 already calling Augusta home, and easy rail/highway connections to the port.
Wray noted Augusta has a large industrial base with recent announcements including Aurubis Richmond (metal smelting, 250 jobs, $600 million investment).
Recent announcement includes Denkai America (copper film producer, 250 jobs, $440 million investment).
Recent announcement includes Purecycle Technologies (recycling plastic into like-new pellets for reuse, 100 jobs, $400 million investment).
A stand-out industry ecosystem in Augusta is electric vehicle manufacturing, with EZGo Textron and Club Car and their related suppliers in Richmond and Columbia counties.
Metals and chemical manufacturing are two other important industries in Augusta.
Wray stated GPA supports efficient operation of existing industries and local economic development efforts.

Wray stated GPA is a key partner in ongoing recruitment of new industry and a great resource to existing manufacturers who have invested billions in the region, many announcing significant further investments in the last five years.
source_title: Georgia Ports Highlights Site Selection Opportunities — Savannah Traffic Club

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Chairman Euisun Chung outlined the company’s strategic direction and commitments for 2021 and beyond in a New Year’s message to global employees.
The Group aims to transform into a new growth engine by expanding market share in the eco-friendly market, securing future technology capabilities, and strengthening business competitiveness.
Chairman Chung emphasized making "quality and safety" a top priority for all staff and business partners.
2021 is positioned as an inflection point and decisive year for long-term sustainable investments and innovations in the post-pandemic era.

The Group’s vision includes stressing eco-friendliness, future technology, and business competitiveness; reinforcing electrification leadership with E-GMP based EV models; striving for carbon neutrality via hydrogen fuel cell technology; realizing the safest and most innovative mobility technologies; expanding new mobility through investments in new growth fields; and rationalizing its business portfolio.

Hyundai Motor Group plans to launch dedicated electric vehicles on the E-GMP (Electric-Global Modular Platform), including Hyundai Motor’s IONIQ 5, Kia’s crossover EV, and Genesis luxury brand’s crossover EVs.
The E-GMP platform offers a multi-charging system allowing both 800V and 400V charging capabilities.
E-GMP technology is the world’s first patented system to operate the motor and inverter to boost 400V to 800V for stable charging compatibility.

Battery electric vehicles based on E-GMP can achieve a maximum range of over 500 kilometres (WLTP standard) and high-speed charge up to 80 percent in 18 minutes, adding 100 kilometers of range in five minutes.
Hyundai Motor Group plans to expand its battery electric vehicle lineup from eight models to 23 by 2025.
The Group aims to sell 1 million EV units annually in global markets.
Hyundai Motor Group will set up 20 high-speed charging stations in Korea by 2021 and expand its charging network with energy companies.
Overseas, the Group plans to optimize EV charging infrastructure with partners including IONITY, Europe's leading high-power charging network in which it made a strategic investment.
The Group will accelerate the creation of a hydrogen energy ecosystem by expanding the supply of hydrogen fuel cell systems for ships, generators, trains, and global automakers.
Hyundai Motor Group recently introduced a dedicated HTWO brand for its hydrogen fuel cell systems.
The Group will realize the safest and most innovative mobility technology by strengthening autonomous driving, connectivity, and software capabilities.
Hyundai Motor Group will invest in new growth fields such as urban air mobility (UAM) and robotics to expand the realm of new mobility.
The Group plans to apply Level 3 autonomous driving technology to mass production models in 2022.
Hyundai Motor Group will push for commercialization projects for autonomous driving technologies, such as robotaxi services, in 2023.
Motional Inc., a joint venture between Hyundai Motor Group and Aptiv, plans to conduct Level 4 driverless tests on public roads in Nevada, U.S.
In 2023, Motional will work with U.S. car-sharing provider Lyft to launch autonomous driving commercialization services in major U.S. regions.
The Group will focus on core next-generation software technologies including vehicle software for driving assistance, precision map-linked navigation, and connected infotainment services.
Hyundai Motor Group recently signed a deal to acquire the controlling stake in Boston Dynamics.
Hyundai’s wearable robot technology and industrial/logistics automation technology are expected to maximize synergies with Boston Dynamics’ capabilities.
Robotics technology will be applied to autonomous driving, UAM, and PBV (Purpose Built Vehicle) to help establish a leading position in mobility areas.

Hyundai Motor Group affiliates will enhance future business capabilities through electrification and self-driving core components and software specialization, while exploring smart city development and renewable energy projects.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group chairman outline strategic growth vision for 2021 and beyond | Autocar Professional

facts: Georgia state government and the Hyundai group agreed on incentives of some 2 trillion won ($1.6 billion), which is the authority’s largest benefit for a company.
The incentive package includes free land, property tax cuts, additional tax reduction when meeting job creation targets, and financial support for job training.
On May 20, Hyundai announced a plan to invest about $5.5 billion to build a new EV plant with an annual capacity of 300,000 units as well as a battery production complex in Bryan County, Georgia.
Earlier, Georgia committed to providing Rivian Automotive Inc., a US EV manufacturer, with a total $1.5 billion worth of incentives for pledging to invest $5 billion in the state.
Rivian Automotive Inc. manufactures R1T pickup trucks and R1S sport utility vehicles.
Hyundai is likely to receive better incentives than those offered to Rivian because the South Korean company’s investment and job numbers are higher than those of the US EV maker.
On May 22, US President Joe Biden pledged not to disappoint Hyundai after a meeting with group Chairman Chung Euisun, indicating large-scale incentives.
The size of Hyundai's incentives could shrink due to possible opposition by US taxpayer groups and the midterm elections in November.
Georgia is one of the most business-friendly states in the US, named No. 1 for business for eight straight years since 2014 by Area Development.
Global information technology giants including Microsoft Corp. and Google have operations in Georgia.
South Korean company SK On Co., the world’s fifth-largest EV battery maker, has also set up units in Georgia.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development offers a one-stop service for site selection, business permissions, links with infrastructure and incentive information.
The state provides additional tax incentives to companies that use ports in Georgia and develop new products or services there.
Higher population growth and a lower minimum wage compared to the US average are also attractive factors for Hyundai's site selection.
The US Democratic Party is seeking legislation for a $4,500 tax credit for EVs made in a union plant proposed by the Biden administration.
The existing US plants of Hyundai Motor Co. and Kia Corp. do not have unionized workers.
Hyundai is likely hoping workers at the planned Georgia EV plant will not set up a labor union.
Georgia adopts a right-to-work law that gives workers the freedom to choose whether or not to join a union in the workplace.
An industry source stated that Hyundai will inevitably lose price competitiveness if the Democratic Party's bill is passed, which would remove an additional tax break.
If the legislation goes through, Hyundai may expect additional incentives because Biden is aware they won't benefit from the tax credit.
Some sources predicted that the group may allow workers to establish a union in the new factory.

links.context: Contact email for reporters Il-Gue Kim and Han-Shin Park

source_title: Georgia state to provide $1.6 bn in incentives to Hyundai - KED Global

facts: Hyundai begins supplying its electric vehicles with lithium battery packs made by SK Battery at a Georgia factory.
The use of these Georgia-made batteries makes Hyundai's electric vehicles eligible for a $7,500 federal tax credit.
More than 60% of the components in these battery packs are now made domestically.
A West Virginia-based electric school bus maker has produced only 12 of 91 contracted buses for state school systems.
The West Virginia-based electric school bus manufacturer has fallen short of production expectations.
source_title: Hyundai lands Georgia-made batteries to secure key tax credit

facts: Hyundai Motor Group is building its largest and most modern electrified car manufacturing plant in the United States in Savannah, Georgia.
The modern metaplant is expected to be ready to make cars by the end of 2024 or beginning of 2025.
Hyundai will produce a diverse range of innovative Hyundai, Genesis, and Kia electric vehicles (EVs) at the facility.
The plant will also build the most important component of electric cars, the battery, in a joint venture with LGES, LG Energy Solution.
Vehicles expected to be built locally include Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis EV models like the IONIQ 5, IONIQ 6, GV60, and Electrified GV70.
The hydrogen-powered XCIENT Fuel Cell Truck will be used for logistics needs between Hyundai’s manufacturing facilities and suppliers around the metaplant.
The new car manufacturing plant will be open to the public, offering free tours to see the facility.
The plant site, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, LLC (HMGMA), is located at 10484 US Highway 280, Ellabell, GA 31308.
The site is located just about 40 minutes drive from Savannah, Georgia.
The new plant covers a site of about 3,000 acres.
Hyundai named the facility "Metaplant" to signify transformation or transcending, focusing on electrification, social impact, employee motivation, and being eco-friendly.
The official name of the factory in Bryan County, Georgia, is Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA).
It is Hyundai Motor Group’s largest plant in the United States.
The project is supported by José Muñoz, President and Global Chief Operating Officer for Hyundai Motor Company and also President and CEO for Hyundai, and Genesis Motor North America.
CEO Jose Muñoz has given guidelines to give back to the community and invest in the well-being of children, education, and the environment.
"Georgia is like a second home to us," said Jose Muñoz.
Hyundai’s future global goals include becoming the world’s leading mobility solutions provider, implying social responsibility and producing a positive impact in the Georgia community.
Joe LaMuraglia, Communications, and PR Manager for HMGMA, discussed benefits including donations to charity organizations with tons of social impact.
The venture includes educational programs with high schools and colleges.
The plant will provide training for new employees.
The project will create more than 8,000 new jobs for the area.
Joint ventures and third-party suppliers will help the local economy grow.
Brandon Ramirez, Director of Corporate Social Responsibility and External Relations at Hyundai Motor America, highlighted building a bridge between the plant and the Georgia community.

Hyundai has donated more than one and half million dollars to charity initiatives in Georgia through Hyundai Hope, Hyundai Hope On Wheels, Genesis Gives, Genesis Inspiration Foundation, and Kia Accelerate the Good.

Recent beneficiaries of Hyundai Motor America’s donations include The Salvation Army of Savannah, Frank Callen Boys & Girls Club, Bulloch County Boys & Girls Club, and Coastal Empire Habitat for Humanity.
Hyundai Motor America provided STEM scholarships to Georgia Southern University.
The Hyundai Hydrogen STEM Program has come to action in Bryan County and Effingham County near the new Metaplant.
Through this program, middle school students learn about hydrogen, build their own hydrogen vehicles, and race them on a Hyundai track.
Hyundai has already invested more than one million dollars in different organizations in Georgia near Bryan County and Savannah.
Vanessa Perez and Ciara Calub from HMNA (Hyundai Motor North America) provided feedback on benefits and cultural influences the plant will bring to the area.
The new Metaplant is targeting production starting end of 2024 into 2025 with an annual capacity of 300,000 cars.
Production capacity can be expanded to almost double if the market requires it.
The investment into this Georgia facility is part of Hyundai’s pledged $10 billion investment into the U.S. mobility market.
Approximately $1 billion is anticipated to be invested by non-affiliated, HMGMA suppliers in connection with the project in other locations.
More production in the United States means less production in South Korea, Hyundai's homeland.
Hyundai has Headquarters in California and two large plants in Alabama and Georgia.
Hyundai plans to lead the EV market in sales, design, technologies, EV and battery production capabilities, and mobility solutions.
Hyundai wants to evolve beyond an automaker into the world’s leading mobility solutions provider.
The new Metaplant consolidates Hyundai Motor Group as one of the leaders in the U.S. auto market’s EV transition.
Hyundai maintains its position as one of the top three EV providers in the U.S.
The Group’s electrification goal includes a global target to sell 3.23 million fully electric vehicles annually in the next few years.
Employees at the plant are called "Meta Pros" by Hyundai Motor Group.
The Metaplant is expected to employ more than eight thousand people from Bryan County, Georgia, and Savannah, Georgia.
Partner companies working next to the Metaplant will be a source of employment for small businesses and independent contractors.
Hyundai partnered with four technical colleges in the Savannah area to train students in the Electrical Vehicle Professional (EVP) Technical Certificate of Credit (TCC).
After graduation from the EVP TCC program, students have the opportunity to be employed at the new Hyundai Metaplant with higher wages and positions.
The short 8-credit-hour certificate includes 3 courses: Automotive Technology Introduction, Automotive Electrical Principles, and Introduction to EV/Hybrid Vehicles and Safety Protocols.
Savannah Technical College offers the program at its Savannah and Liberty County Campuses.
Southeastern Technical College offers the program at its Vidalia Campus.
Coastal Pines Technical College offers the program at its Baxley and Waycross Campuses.
The certificate program prepares students for entry-level employment in the electrified vehicle production industry, focusing on safe shop operations, electrical principles, and EV operation/service.
Hyundai’s first U.S. assembly plant is Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama, LLC (HMMA) in Montgomery, Alabama.
The new Bryan County, GA facility is larger and smarter than the Alabama plant and is completely oriented towards electrified cars, electric batteries, and components.
The new MetaPlant will cooperate with the previous Montgomery, Alabama facility, which produces over 300,000 vehicles annually.
This new facility will double Hyundai’s current vehicle output in the U.S.
The Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) under the Biden administration influenced the decision to build the plant in the U.S.
The IRA stipulates that EV vehicles must be domestically produced in the U.S. to qualify for federal incentives.
Tax incentives from the act allow manufacturers to transfer savings to consumers, offering lower prices and winning competition.
Producing cars locally in Georgia will help lower transportation costs and speed up inventory delivery to car dealers in the U.S.
Local production ensures parts, batteries, and important electric car components become available much faster than if produced in Mexico or Asia.
source_title: Hyundai's new Metaplant in Georgia gives Millions of Dollars in Social Impact - AutoNews-Wire.com

facts: Georgia Governor Brian Kemp will announce on Friday that Hyundai Motor Group is investing more than $7 billion in the state.
The announcement coincides with a visit by President Joe Biden to Seoul.
Hyundai Motor Group declined to comment on the announcement.
The South Korean auto group, which houses Hyundai Motor Co and Kia Corp, will build a plant to make electric vehicles (EVs) in south Georgia.
Kemp’s office said on Wednesday he would make a “special economic development announcement” in Bryan County, near the port city of Savannah, on Friday.
The Atlanta Journal Constitution reported the investment would create more than 8,500 jobs.
Reuters reported on May 9 that Hyundai was in talks to build an EV-manufacturing plant in the United States and had held discussions with Georgia officials.
The Associated Press said on Friday that Hyundai could also build some cars powered by gasoline engines at the site.

White House National Security Adviser Jake Sullivan said Biden “will engage with technology and manufacturing leaders in Korea who are mobilising billions of dollars in investment here in the United States to create thousands of good-paying American jobs.”

The Hyundai group, the world’s third-biggest automaker by vehicle sales, said last year it planned to invest $7.4 billion in the United States by 2025 to produce EVs, upgrade production facilities and expand investment in smart mobility solutions.
Smart mobility solutions are described as technology to improve different modes of transportation in cities.
The group said on Wednesday it would invest 21 trillion won ($16 billion) in South Korea through 2030 to expand the production capacity of EVs in the country to 1.44 million vehicles per year.
This accounts for roughly 45% of its forecast 2030 global EV production capacity of 3.23 million vehicles.
The cited exchange rate is $1 = 1,274.4700 won.
source_title: Hyundai to invest $7 billion in Georgia, create 8,500 jobs

entity_name: LG Energy Solution and Hyundai Motor Group Joint Venture
canonical_name: LG Energy Solution and Hyundai Motor Group Joint Venture
facts: South Korea's Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution announced they will build a $4.3 billion electric vehicle battery plant in the United States.
The companies are building the plant amid a push to take advantage of tax credits.

Manufacturers must adhere to new U.S. sourcing requirements for EV battery components and critical minerals so that buyers of their vehicles can qualify for up to $7,500 in tax credits under the Inflation Reduction Act.
Vehicles from Hyundai Motor and sister automaker Kia are currently not eligible.
Construction of the factory in the state of Georgia will begin in the second half of 2023.
Battery production at the facility is scheduled to start at the end of 2025 at the earliest.
The plant will have an annual production capacity of 30 gigawatt-hours.
This capacity is enough for 300,000 EVs.
Hyundai Motor Group, the world's third-largest automaker by vehicle sales, is building EV and battery manufacturing facilities in Bryan County in Georgia.
The joint factory with LGES will be based in Bryan County.
LGES and Hyundai Motor Group will each own 50% of the joint venture.
Hyundai Motor Group houses Hyundai Motor, Kia, and auto parts maker Hyundai Mobis.
LGES supplies automakers including Tesla and General Motors.
LGES CEO Youngsoo Kwon said in a statement, 'Two strong leaders in the auto and battery industries have joined hands, and together we are ready to drive the EV transition in America.'
In April, Hyundai Motor finalized a $5 billion EV battery joint venture in the United States with SK On.
SK On is a battery unit of SK Innovation.
The joint venture with SK On is intended to boost electrification efforts in Hyundai's largest market.
source_title: Hyundai, LG Energy to build $4.3 billion EV battery plant in U.S.

facts: Hyundai Transys Georgia Powertrain Inc. is a global automotive powertrain and transmission manufacturer specializing in advanced drivetrain systems, electrified powertrains, and automated transmission solutions for major automakers worldwide.

The facility has been focusing on expanding production capacity for electrified powertrain components while maintaining strong transmission manufacturing operations to support growing demand from automotive manufacturers transitioning to hybrid and electric vehicle platforms.
The company expanded its workforce by 36% to support increased production demands and new supply contracts with major automotive manufacturers.
Hyundai Transys Georgia Powertrain Inc. enhanced quality control systems and manufacturing processes to meet stringent requirements for next-generation transmission technologies.
The facility integrated hybrid transmission production capabilities aligned with the parent company's development of TMED-II systems and electric drive solutions.
The operation strengthened community partnerships and local workforce development programs to support sustained growth in West Point, Georgia.
Hyundai Transys Georgia Powertrain Inc. is making strategic efforts to expand market presence and reach new customer segments.
The company is implementing advanced technologies to improve service delivery and customer experience.

Hyundai Transys Georgia Powertrain Inc. serves as a critical manufacturing hub in North America, producing full-quality automatic transmissions through advanced production processes that maximize efficiency and quality control.
The facility was established in 2008 as the first overseas powertrain plant for Hyundai Transys, marking a significant expansion into the U.S. market.
The Georgia operation employs 230 people and represents a vital component of Hyundai Transys' global manufacturing network spanning 33 locations across 11 countries.
The facility produces transmissions for Hyundai, Kia, and various other automotive manufacturers, contributing to the parent company's position as the 34th largest global auto parts supplier.
The facility is located in West Point, Georgia.
The company operates as a private entity.

facts: A Hyundai auto parts subsidiary will establish a new manufacturing facility in western Georgia to produce vehicle transmissions.
Hyundai Transys expects to invest $240 million to expand its current operations in West Point, Georgia.

Hyundai Motor Group’s Kia Motors operates a plant in the city, and Hyundai’s Montgomery, Alabama, plant is located 90 miles away.
The 620,000-square-foot project is expected to create 678 full-time jobs, along with 165 related positions in construction, handling, and inspection.
Once completed, Hyundai Transys alone is slated to employ more than 1,700 people in the state.
Hyundai Transys Georgia Powertrain President Sangkil Jung stated, “We are excited for the opportunities ahead as we expand our presence in the West Point community.”
The subsidiary could qualify for state and local tax incentives, but a Georgia Department of Economic Development spokeswoman told the Associated Press that terms have not been finalized.

Kia received hundreds of millions of dollars in state incentives to establish its West Point plant more than a decade ago and accounts for more than 4,300 jobs in the county between the automaker, its affiliates, and suppliers.

Suppliers Hyundai Mobis and Hyundai Transys Georgia Powertrain work in seamless tandem with Kia to ensure parts and components are delivered just-in-time.
Kia and its suppliers clearly offer employee-centered job opportunities across the skills spectrum in a clean, modern, and ergonomical environment.

facts: Hyundai TRANSYS Georgia Seating System, LLC will invest $9 million in expanding its seating manufacturing operations in West Point, Georgia.
The expansion will create more than 150 jobs in Troup County.
Hyundai TRANSYS Georgia Seating System manufactures seats and seat foam pads for the Hyundai Santa Fe, KIA Sorento, and other models.
The company supplies seating structures to automobile manufacturers and leading electric vehicle makers.
The expansion will take Hyundai TRANSYS' total employment in Georgia to more than 1,890 jobs.
Hyundai TRANSYS Georgia Seating System is continuing to hire for careers in production seat assembly, foam, quality, and logistics.
Senior Project Manager Amanda Fields represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce division on this project.
The project was executed in partnership with the West Point Development Authority, Georgia Power, and Georgia Quick Start.
Governor Brian Kemp announced the investment and praised Georgia's highly skilled workforce for attracting manufacturing jobs statewide.
Hyundai TRANSYS President and CEO Sangkil Jung stated the company is excited to expand its presence in the West Point community and proud of its partnership with the State of Georgia.
West Point Development Authority Chairman Kevin Patrick thanked Hyundai TRANSYS for its continued investment, noting automotive and existing industries are the backbone of local economic success.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that contributing more than $3 billion to Georgia’s economy, the state is committed to growing Georgia’s robust automotive ecosystem and creating solid manufacturing jobs of the future.
Georgia is home to more than 200 automotive-related facilities.

Georgia remains a national leader in advanced manufacturing, outpacing the United States in 10-year GDP growth in the manufacture of machinery, electrical equipment and components, and fabricated metals.
Hyundai TRANSYS produces critical automobile parts including transmissions, axles, and seats.
Hyundai TRANSYS is the only company in the world specializing in transmissions.

Hyundai TRANSYS develops and produces top quality seats for Genesis, Santa Fe, Sorento, and other models.

facts: Hyundai Transys announced plans for a new transmission factory in West Point, Georgia.
The company will invest more than $240 million to build the plant.
The new plant will have a capacity of 620,000 square feet (58,000 square meters).
The plant will be located on the Kia campus just off Interstate 85 in West Point, near the Alabama state line.
Hyundai Transys plans to hire 678 workers for the new facility.
The new plant will build eight-speed transmissions.
The new factory will adjoin an existing Hyundai Transys factory and a Kia Motors Corp factory.
Both plants are part of Hyundai Motor Group, a Korean auto conglomerate that sells cars under the Kia and Hyundai brands.
Hyundai Transys was created by a merger of two Hyundai affiliates last year.
Hyundai Transys also manufactures axles and vehicle seats.
The company has operations in South Korea, the United States, and seven other countries.
The investment will create 165 additional jobs for Hyundai Transys.
Once the new plant is complete, Hyundai Transys's total employees in Georgia will rise above 1,700.
West Point Mayor Steve Tramell expressed thrill over Hyundai Transys's long-term investment and the new jobs being created.
Many supplier plants are clustered near the Hyundai plant in Montgomery, Alabama, and the Kia plant in West Point.
Kia, its affiliates, and suppliers had more than 4,300 employees in Georgia's Troup County in 2018, according to county financial documents.
This is the largest industrial announcement by investment value made so far this year in Georgia.

Hyundai TRANSYS Georgia Powertrain President Sangkil Jung stated that years of capital investment, employment, training support, and unmatched opportunities made Georgia a natural choice for the company.
Final incentives for Hyundai Transys have not been agreed on yet, according to Marie Hodge Gordon, spokesperson for the Georgia Economic Development Department.
Hyundai Transys will qualify for a state income tax credit allowing it to deduct $3,500 per job from its Georgia income taxes for five years.

If Hyundai Transys does not incur $2.37 million in state income taxes each year, it can use the credit to receive cash payments redirected from money withheld from employee paychecks for state income taxes.
This tax credit applies because the plant is located in an area classified by the state as less-developed.
Workers must earn at least $28,000 a year for Hyundai Transys to qualify for the tax credits.
The state will pay to train Hyundai Transys workers.
The state could contribute money to pay for infrastructure.
Hyundai will automatically qualify for tax breaks saving it millions in sales and use taxes on machinery.
Local officials could also abate property taxes.
West Point's financial statements show that in 2018, Kia, its affiliates, and suppliers were receiving $2.85 million in property tax breaks from the city alone.
Kia received subsidies and tax breaks worth more than $450 million to locate its plant in West Point, which opened in 2009.
The state aimed to revitalize a region hit hard by the closure of textile and apparel plants.
Because the plant is only miles from the Alabama state line, a significant number of its workers live in Alabama.

entity_name: Hyundai-LG Energy Solution Georgia Battery Plant
canonical_name: Hyundai-LG Energy Solution Georgia Battery Plant
facts: A battery plant co-owned by Hyundai is facing a minimum startup delay of two to three months following an immigration raid last week, according to Hyundai CEO Jose Munoz.
The Georgia plant is operated through a joint venture between Hyundai and South Korea’s LG Energy Solution.
The plant was at the center of the largest single-site enforcement operation in the U.S. Department of Homeland Security’s history last week.
The plant is part of a $7.6 billion factory complex to make battery-powered models.
The plant was slated to come online later this year (2025).
Hyundai CEO Jose Munoz said he was surprised when he heard the news and immediately inquired if Hyundai workers were involved.
The company discovered that the workers at the center of the raid were mainly employed by suppliers of LG.
About 475 workers, mostly South Korean nationals, were arrested, according to U.S. immigration officials.
It is typical for an automotive battery plant to employ these workers as it is getting off the ground, according to Munoz.
For the construction phase of plants, specialized people are needed because there are a lot of skills and equipment that cannot be found in the United States, according to Munoz.
Hyundai will source batteries from other plants while waiting for the LG plant to start up, including from a Georgia plant co-owned with Korean battery-maker SK On.
Fallout from the raid has cascaded across the country, with Reuters reporting that workers at other LG plants, including those co-owned by GM, were asked to return home.
source_title: Hyundai battery plant faces at least 2-3 month startup delay following raid, CEO says | CNN Business

facts: The Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) serves as the state's lead agency for business recruitment and expansion.
Georgia's primary cash incentives include the REBA (Regional Economic Business Assistance) grant for closing assistance on major projects.
OneGeorgia Authority grants fund infrastructure projects supporting business expansion or location in rural Georgia counties.
The Job Tax Credit and Quality Jobs Tax Credit are statutory rather than discretionary.
Georgia operates one of the most active state film-and-television production grant/credit ecosystems.
REBA is a discretionary grant fund used for site preparation, infrastructure, and other closing assistance on major economic-development projects.
REBA grants are negotiated through GDEcD project managers and are not broadly available as a small-business grant.
OneGeorgia Authority grant applications must be submitted by local governments, with businesses benefiting through site readiness at qualifying locations.
The state primarily relies on federal small-business certifications (SBA 8(a), HUBZone, WOSB/EDWOSB, SDVOSB/VOSB) for set-aside eligibility.

State agency procurement may apply federal certifications where federal funds pass through, but no state-administered formal small-business set-aside program operates beyond the standard MBE/WBE/DBE registries.
Georgia does not currently operate a statewide SBIR/STTR Phase 0 or Phase I match program.
Eligible small businesses apply directly to federal SBIR/STTR through participating federal agencies (DoD, NIH, NSF, DOE, NASA, etc.).
Georgia state-administered grant programs typically use program-specific application portals on individual agency sites rather than a single statewide grants portal.
Federal pass-through funds (HUD CDBG, EDA, USDA Rural Development) route through Grants.gov for the federal half, then through the state sub-recipient process.
HUD CDBG-State dollars are administered by GDEcD and sub-granted to localities for economic development.
EDA public-works and economic-adjustment grants flow through regional EDA offices.
USDA Rural Development provides Business and Industry loan guarantees and Rural Business Development Grants for rural-county operations.
SBA programs (7(a), 504, Microloan, CDFI) are accessed via local lenders.
Business eligibility for federal grant channels depends on entity size, location (rural vs urban), and use-of-funds.
The Community Development Block Grant (CDBG) program flows from HUD to GDEcD, which sub-grants to localities.

Businesses do not apply directly to the state for CDBG dollars; they apply to participating cities or counties for economic development sub-awards focused on job creation, blight remediation, and low-to-moderate-income workforce support.
Most flagship Georgia grant and incentive programs require either an existing operation, a defined hiring commitment, or capital investment milestones.
Pure pre-revenue startups should usually pair federal SBIR/STTR (where R&D-eligible) with state innovation match programs (if available) and CDFI lending.
Some Georgia regional or university-based innovation programs may provide application support for federal grants.
Most state-administered programs require pre-registration with a state vendor identification number before an application can be submitted.

links.context: Tool to filter federal grants by entity type, sector, and award size

source_title: State Grants in Georgia (2026): Agency, Programs, Set-Asides, SBIR Match, Portal | GrantProbe

facts: An army of armed immigration and law enforcement authorities descended on the Hyundai-LG Energy plant construction project in Georgia on Sept. 4.
The raid rounded up 475 people.
The majority of those rounded up were South Korean workers.
The event ignited an international incident.
source_title: In our opinion: Theatrical immigration enforcement hurts business - Automotive News

facts: Three workers have died since Hyundai Motor started construction of its $7.6 billion auto plant in Georgia in 2022, according to a Wall Street Journal review of federal records.
More than a dozen workers have suffered serious injuries at the site, including from falling without wearing harnesses and getting crushed by forklifts.
A Hyundai Motor North America spokesperson stated the company conducted a comprehensive audit and strengthened safety oversight across the site.
The spokesperson added that contractor vetting, training, and accountability were enhanced to ensure all partners meet Hyundai’s standards and legal requirements.
Dozens of current and former workers, many of them safety coordinators who helped oversee construction, told the WSJ that the work environment involved many inexperienced immigrant laborers.
These workers reported lax safety standards and frequent accidents at the site.
The plant is operated through a joint venture between Hyundai and South Korea’s LG Energy Solution.
The facility has been in the limelight since an immigration raid last month that detained hundreds of South Korean workers.
The immigration raid was the largest single-site enforcement operation in the U.S. Department of Homeland Security’s history.
Workers interviewed by the WSJ said Hyundai did not ensure that people were properly trained on site.
Safety regulators did little to prevent worksite violations, according to the report.
Hyundai CEO Jose Munoz told the newspaper he acted immediately and comprehensively to prevent anything like this from happening again.
Jose Munoz stated he traveled to Georgia to tell his team directly that their safety comes before production schedules, costs, profits, and everything else.
Construction on the electric vehicle and battery plant is ongoing.
The plant is located in Bryan County, about 30 miles west of Savannah.

links.context: Read more from our local writers → MSU Board Crisis - Michael Balow

source_title: Three workers died at Hyundai's Georgia plant since 2022, before US immigration raid, WSJ reports

entity_name: Hyundai Motor Group EV Plant in Georgia
canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group EV Manufacturing Plant
facts: Hundreds of federal agents detained 475 people at the Hyundai EV manufacturing site in Georgia during a large-scale immigration raid, with most detainees being South Korean nationals.
The raid occurred on a Thursday and targeted a manufacturing site that state officials have long considered Georgia's largest economic development project.

U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement released video on Saturday showing federal agents directing workers to line up outside, frisk them, and shackle or tie their hands, ankles, waists, and wrists before boarding Georgia inmate-transfer buses.
South Korean Foreign Minister Cho Hyun stated on Saturday that more than 300 of the 475 detained individuals were South Korean nationals.

Some detained individuals worked for HL-GA Battery Co., a joint venture between Hyundai and LG Energy Solution for a battery plant slated to open next year, while others were contractors or subcontractors at the construction site, according to Steven Schrank, lead Georgia agent of Homeland Security Investigations.
Lead agent Steven Schrank stated that some detained workers had illegally crossed the U.S. border, while others entered legally with expired visas or on visa waivers prohibiting work.
Immigration attorney Charles Kuck stated that two of his detained clients arrived from South Korea under a visa waiver program allowing tourism or business stays of 90 days or less without a visa.
Attorney Charles Kuck noted one detained client had been in the U.S. for a couple of weeks and another for about 45 days, with both planning to return home soon.

A detained lawful permanent resident was kept in custody due to a prior firearm and drug offense record that could jeopardize their status under the "crime of moral turpitude" rule, according to ICE public affairs officer Lindsay Williams.
ICE public affairs officer Lindsay Williams denied reports of U.S. citizens being detained, stating that once citizens identify themselves, ICE has no authority to detain them.

Hyundai Motor Company stated on Friday that none of its direct employees were detained and that the company is reviewing supplier and subcontractor practices to ensure compliance with U.S. employment laws.
LG Energy Solution told the Associated Press it could not immediately confirm how many of its own employees or Hyundai workers were detained.
The South Korean government expressed "concern and regret" over the raid targeting its citizens and is dispatching diplomats to the site.

South Korean Foreign Ministry spokesperson Lee Jaewoong stated in a televised address that "the business activities of our investors and the rights of our nationals must not be unjustly infringed in the process of U.S. law enforcement."
Most detained individuals were transported to an immigration detention center in Folkston, Georgia, near the Florida state line.
Lead agent Steven Schrank confirmed that none of the detainees had been charged with crimes yet and that the investigation remains ongoing.
Family members and friends struggled to locate or contact detained individuals, according to James Woo, communications director for Asian Americans Advancing Justice-Atlanta.
Many detained workers were in the U.S. solely for business purposes, leaving their families primarily located in South Korea.
The raid resulted from a months-long investigation into allegations of illegal hiring at the site.
Search warrants and affidavits requested employment records for current and former workers, timecards, and video/photos of workers.
Court records indicate prosecutors do not know who hired the individuals, with the U.S. Attorney’s Office stating the "actual company or contractor hiring the illegal aliens is currently unknown."
Hyundai Motor Group began manufacturing EVs at the $7.6 billion plant approximately one year prior to the raid.
The site currently employs approximately 1,200 people in a largely rural area located about 25 miles (40 kilometers) west of Savannah.
Federal agents specifically targeted an adjacent plant currently under construction where Hyundai and LG Energy Solution are partnering to produce EV batteries.
The plant is located in Bryan County, which had a population of almost 47,000 in 2023 after increasing by more than a quarter in the early 2020s.
Bryan County's Asian population grew from 1.5% in 2018 to 2.2% in 2023, with growth primarily driven by people of Indian descent, according to Census Bureau data.
Lead agent Steven Schrank described the Georgia raid as the "largest single site enforcement operation" in Homeland Security Investigations' two-decade history.
The majority of detained individuals were Korean; ICE data shows only 46 Koreans were deported out of over 270,000 total removals in the 12 months ending Sept. 30, 2024.

Governor Brian Kemp and other Georgia Republican officials, who previously courted Hyundai and celebrated the plant's opening, issued statements on Friday asserting that all state employers must follow the law.
The nonprofit Asian Americans Advancing Justice-Atlanta called the raid "unacceptable" in a joint statement.
Advocacy groups stated that workers targeted at Hyundai are "everyday people who are trying to feed their families, build stronger communities, and work toward a better future."
Sammie Rentz, who opened a supermarket less than 3 miles (4.8 kilometers) from the plant six months prior, reported sharp business declines and fears it may not recover following the raid.
Local business owner Sammie Rentz expressed concern that Korean nationals might leave the area due to pride and the raid's impact.
Ellabell resident Tanya Cox, living less than a mile from the site, stated she held no ill feelings toward Korean or other immigrant workers.
Resident Tanya Cox noted that few local neighbors were employed at the site and believed more battery plant construction jobs should have been allocated to local residents.
Local resident Tanya Cox stated she did not see how the plant had brought significant jobs to her community or nearby areas.
source_title: What to Know About a Large-Scale Immigration Raid at a Georgia Manufacturing Plant

facts: Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia, Inc. (KMMG) provided $400,000 in funding support to the Troup County Development Authority.

The Troup County Development Authority has always been supportive of KMMG and has helped to make its success in Troup County possible, according to Randy Jackson, the Kia plant’s vice president of human resources and administration.

KMMG considers the Troup County Development Authority a critical partner for KMMG and a big part of its team at the facility along with the City of West Point, Troup County, Harris County and the State of Georgia.
Ricky Wolfe, the chairman of the Troup County Commission, thanked KMMG for the support and for the plant’s commitment to Troup County.
Ricky Wolfe stated that KMMG is committed to this area’s growth and success and expressed that they look forward to many years of continued partnership with KMMG.
Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia, Inc. (KMMG) is the first manufacturing site in North America for Kia Motors Corporation based in Seoul, Korea.
KMMG is located on 2,200 acres in West Point, Georgia.
KMMG began mass production on Nov. 16, 2009.
KMMG is home to the 2013 Sorento CUV and the 2013 Optima mid-size sedan, two of the brand’s top selling models.
Beginning in 2012, the plant has the ability to build 360,000 vehicles annually from U.S. and globally-sourced parts.
source_title: Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia provides $400,000 in support to the Troup Development Authority - Kia Georgia

facts: Kia committed a $1 million gift to the University of Georgia College of Engineering and the Georgia Network for Electric Mobility (GNEM).

The $1 million commitment provides endowed support for undergraduate scholarships, graduate research fellowships, community engagement programs, and experiential learning opportunities related to e-mobility for students in the College of Engineering.
Supported experiential learning opportunities include co-ops, internships, and capstone projects.
Kia's gift supports the Georgia Network for Electric Mobility (GNEM), which was established by the University of Georgia in early 2023.
GNEM focuses on enhancing research, education, and outreach opportunities in e-mobility across the state of Georgia.
GNEM brings together industry, academia, government, and communities to aid workforce development, economic development, and research.
GNEM takes special care to involve rural and underserved communities and small businesses in its initiatives.

As a founding partner of GNEM, Kia will provide support in perpetuity for educational pursuits, research productivity, and experiential learning efforts for students interested in electric mobility careers.
Kia established a state-of-the-art production facility in West Point, Georgia in 2009.
Since the facility opened, Kia Georgia has helped create more than 14,000 jobs.
The Kia Georgia facility in West Point has produced more than 4.2 million vehicles.
One of the vehicles soon to be produced at the West Point facility will be Kia's all-electric vehicle, the EV9.
Kia served as a premier sponsor of the University of Georgia's third annual Electric Mobility Summit.

The Electric Mobility Summit took place on April 23-24 and focused on electric mobility adoption in Georgia, educational programs to support industry growth, and the future of electric mobility in the state and beyond.
Kia's GNEM support aligns with UGA's land-grant mission to serve all populations within Georgia.
source_title: Kia commits $1M to e-mobility initiative at UGA - Give to UGA

facts: Kia announced on Thursday that it will assemble the latest model of its electric EV6 crossover at its Georgia factory.
This marks the second battery-powered model slated for Peach State production after the plant underwent a $217 million upgrade to incorporate EV manufacturing.
The EV6 is a two-row crossover.
The EV6 will roll down assembly lines next to the Korean automaker’s three-row electric EV9 SUV at its factory in West Point, roughly 80 miles southwest of downtown Atlanta.
Gasoline-powered models, including the brand’s Telluride and Sorento fleets, are also built at the factory.
The factory opened in 2009.

Gov. Brian Kemp said in a news release: “Today’s announcement by Kia is just the latest testament to how beneficial our decades-long partnership has been, both for this great job creator and the people of Georgia.”

facts: Kia Georgia, Inc. is the first manufacturing site in North America for Kia Motors Corporation based in Seoul, Korea.
Kia Georgia has an annual production capacity of 350,000 units.
Kia Georgia is located on 2,200 acres in West Point, Georgia.
Kia Georgia began mass production on Nov. 16, 2009.
Kia Georgia manufactures the Telluride SUV.
Kia Georgia manufactures the Sorento SUV.
Kia Georgia manufactures the Sportage SUV.
Kia Georgia manufactures the all-electric EV9.
Kia Georgia manufactures the all-electric EV6.
Kia's guiding principles include providing high-quality, high-value vehicles at prices well below the competition.
Kia backs its vehicles with a 10-year/100,000-mile Warranty Program and a customer-first policy.
Safety is always a priority for Kia from concept to crash test.
Kia ensures vehicle safety through a rigorous system of collision tests and simulations.
Kia maintains a thorough customer-oriented quality control system that assesses customer needs and demands to enhance the production process.

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) and Kia America celebrated the start of production for the Kia Sportage Hybrid in Ellabell, GA.
The Kia Sportage Hybrid production marks three major milestones for the facility: the first Kia model, the first hybrid electric vehicle (HEV), and the third vehicle to be produced at the site.
The Kia Sportage Hybrid now joins HMGMA’s lineup alongside award-winning Hyundai IONIQ 5 and IONIQ 9.
Production flexibility was built into HMGMA’s original design, enabling seamless integration of different powertrains with minimal modifications.
The plant has the ability to produce up to 10 different vehicles.

Georgia Governor Brian Kemp stated that mutual success is the direct result of a proven partnership approach creating long-lasting relationships between Kia America, HMG’s Metaplant, the local community, and hardworking Georgians.
Kia North America and Kia America President and CEO Sean Yoon said the event marks a very important moment for Kia in Georgia and for customers across the US.
Sean Yoon noted that Metaplant in Savannah is Kia’s second major investment in Georgia and testifies to confidence in the state’s future as an automotive powerhouse.
Sean Yoon added that Metaplant will continue the strong momentum the Sportage Hybrid has achieved since being updated last year to quickly become one of Kia’s highest volume models.

HMGMA President and CEO Tony Heo stated that through the dedication of Meta Pros and a flexible manufacturing system, they successfully prepared to produce their first hybrid vehicle and first Kia model.
Tony Heo said today represents an important next step in building the future of mobility through world-class manufacturing in Georgia.
The event expands Kia America’s nearly two-decade commitment to produce award-winning vehicles in the state of Georgia.
Kia’s West Point factory currently assembles the all-new 2nd generation Telluride, all-electric EV9 and EV6, and internal combustion powered versions of the Sportage and Sorento SUVs.
The celebration was attended by approximately 200 Meta Pros who shared in the milestone after extensive preparation for the new powertrain.
Many team members traveled to Kia facilities in West Point, Georgia and South Korea to learn best practices for Sportage Hybrid production.
Kia’s West Point facility currently employs more than 3,200 team members.
HMGMA adds nearly 2,000 Meta Pros, with a very high percentage coming from the state of Georgia.

Distinguished guests at the celebration included Georgia Governor Brian Kemp, Kia North America and Kia America President and CEO Seungkyu (Sean) Yoon, and Kia Georgia President and CEO Stuart Countess.
During the ceremony, the first Kia Sportage Hybrid assembled at HMGMA debuted on an Autonomous Mobile Robot (AMR).
Gov. Kemp rode in the vehicle as it was presented to highlight the innovation and technology embedded throughout the Metaplant.
The vehicle was signed by special guests following the reveal.
The addition of the Kia Sportage Hybrid expands HMGMA’s production portfolio across Hyundai Motor Group’s three brands: Kia, Hyundai, and Genesis.
HMGMA continues to ramp up to a production goal of up to 500,000 vehicles annually.
Future model plans for the facility are not being released at this time.

facts: Kia is expanding its only manufacturing plant in the US to make room for its first electric three-row SUV.
The Kia EV9 will be the automaker’s first EV assembled in North America.
Assembly of the EV9 will begin at Kia's West Point, Georgia, plant next year.
Kia announced an over $200 million expansion Wednesday.
The expansion is creating nearly 200 additional jobs in the area.
The EV9 will be the fifth model assembled at Kia's Georgia facility but the first all-electric vehicle to join the lineup.
Sean Yoon, president and CEO of Kia North America, said the “EV9 has the potential to be another ‘game changer’ for Kia.”
Sean Yoon added that the EV9 will be the most sophisticated vehicle Kia has ever built, a standout in the EV market and on the road, and assembled in West Point, Georgia.
The EV9 is the largest electric car from Kia so far and its first three-row electric SUV.
Kia says the EV9 is reshaping the company with a “bold vision for the new era of electrification.”

The EV9 includes some of the latest in-vehicle technology, including Kia’s most advanced Highway Driving Pilot, OTA updates, onboard power generator functionality (V2L), and ultra-wideband-based Digital Key.

Kia claims the EV9 offers “true SUV capabilities,” including 7.8-inch ground clearance, up to 81.9 cubic feet of cargo space behind the first row, up to 5,000 lbs towing capacity, and available torque-vectoring dual-motor AWD.
The EV9 is based on the Hyundai Motor Group’s 800-volt E-GMP platform (the same one used to power the IONIQ 5, IONIQ 6, and Kia EV6).
The EV9 includes fast-charging capabilities, designed to go from 10% to 80% charge (adding 148 miles of driving range) in under 25 minutes.
The EV9 will be available in two powertrain options.
The standard powertrain is a 76.1 kWh battery (fourth-generation) with a 215 hp (160 kW) motor.
A 99.8 kWh battery pack will be offered with a single motor, targeting 300 miles range, or a dual-motor 379 hp torque-vectoring AWD configuration with 516 lb-ft of torque.
Kia is expected to begin assembling the EV9 at its West Point, Georgia, plant starting in the second quarter of 2024.
The automaker began delivering the electric SUV to customers in South Korea last month, gathering 13,000 preorders in its first eight days on the market.

Although Kia has yet to release prices for the US market, it is expected that, based on the first European prices, the EV9 will start around $65,000 to $70,000 for the larger battery pack and around $60,000 for the standard model.
source_title: Kia Expands Georgia Plant to Produce Electric EV9 SUV With $200M Investment - World-Energy

The introduction of the EV9 is part of a broader $200 million expansion project.

SeungKyu (Sean) Yoon, President and CEO of Kia North America and Kia America, attended the milestone event.
James Watson, Vice President of Production at Kia Georgia, attended the milestone event.

SeungKyu (Sean) Yoon stated that the EV9 launch is an example of Kia’s commitment to the U.S. market and Georgia.
Through Plan S, Kia aims to be a leader in the e-mobility sector for years to come.
The EV9 has won accolades including World Car of the Year, World EV of the Year, and North American Utility Vehicle of the Year.
Stuart Countess is the President and CEO of Kia Georgia.
Stuart Countess credited the Kia Georgia team for their dedication and ability to turn challenges into opportunities.
The first Kia vehicle was assembled at the plant on November 16, 2009.
Kia Georgia, Inc. is Kia Corporation’s first manufacturing site in North America.

The facility has an annual production capacity of 350,000 units.

The facility began mass production on November 16, 2009.
The facility produces the Telluride SUV.
The facility produces the Sorento SUV, excluding HEV and PHEV models.
The facility produces the Sportage SUV, excluding HEV and PHEV models.
The facility produces the all-electric EV9 SUV.
The expansion allows Kia Georgia to assemble both electric vehicles and traditional products on the same assembly line.
The expansion increases the plant’s flexibility and capacity.

Total planned investments in Georgia’s e-mobility and battery ecosystem topped $27.3 billion over the five-year period from 2018 to 2023.
Georgia is historically a top destination for job seekers looking to reduce living expenses.
The state works with employers to identify opportunities to attract and train the local labor pool.
Georgia Quick Start is helping the state meet the demands of the clean energy boom.

CNBC ranked Georgia as the No. 1 state for infrastructure in the U.S. in 2023.
Georgia serves as a global gateway to the U.S.
The state has two deepwater ports, with one ranked among the fastest growing and the other a top port for automotive Ro/Ro in the country.
The busiest airport in the world, Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport, is located near the center of the state.
Infrastructure assets are connected to cities across the state via a seamless network of interstates, highways, and rail lines.
Reliable energy grids are diversifying Georgia’s energy mix through renewable options, including solar energy and Plant Vogtle.

facts: Kia America announced a $150,000 donation to the American Red Cross for Hurricane Milton recovery efforts.
This follows a previous $250,000 contribution from Kia America and Kia Georgia to the Red Cross for Hurricane Helene relief.
Kia America launched a customer assistance program for customers in Florida, Georgia, South Carolina, and North Carolina who need to replace vehicles damaged beyond repair by either hurricane.
Eligible customers with an insurance claim for total vehicle loss due to either storm will receive $500 toward the purchase or lease of a new Kia.
The $500 customer assistance is valid for purchases or leases made between October 4, 2024, and January 2, 2025.
SeungKyu (Sean) Yoon serves as President and CEO of Kia North America and Kia America.
Hurricanes Helene and Milton caused catastrophic damage across Georgia, Florida, South Carolina, North Carolina, and Tennessee.
Kia's donated funds will provide vital resources such as food, water, shelter, and emotional support to impacted individuals.
Kia team members are raising internal funds to further support Red Cross relief efforts.
Alicia Doherty serves as Regional Executive for the American Red Cross of Georgia.
The donations are part of Kia's "Accelerate the Good" charitable program, which includes national and local initiatives.
The "Accelerate the Good" program has provided more than $30 million to those in need since 2019.

Program funds support causes including childhood cancer treatment, ending childhood hunger, youth homelessness shelter, natural disaster relief, military family support, animal sheltering, and environmental cleanup.
Kia America is headquartered in Irvine, California.
Kia was recognized as one of TIME's World's Most Sustainable Companies of 2024.
Kia serves as the "Official Automotive Partner" of the NBA and WNBA.
Kia sells gasoline, hybrid, plug-in hybrid, and electric vehicles through a U.S. dealer network of over 775 locations.
Several Kia cars and SUVs are proudly assembled in America using U.S. and globally sourced parts.
The 2025 EV9 all-electric three-row SUV is among the models assembled in the United States.
The 2025 Sportage, Sorento (excluding HEV/PHEV variants), and Telluride are also assembled in the United States.
The $500 vehicle replacement support offer excludes the 2024 K5, 2024 Sorento Hybrid, and 2024 Sorento PHEV.

Eligibility for the $500 offer requires proof of residency in FL, GA, NC, SC, or TN, along with proof of a valid insurance claim indicating total loss due to Helene or Milton on or after September 26, 2024.
The $500 offer may be combined with cash incentives from Kia America and finance or lease offers from Kia Finance America, though not all incentive programs are compatible.
Customers must take delivery of the new vehicle from retailer stock by January 2, 2025.

facts: Fiscal Year 2025 spans July 1, 2024 to June 30, 2025.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce team secured 423 new locations and expansions during Fiscal Year 2025.
These efforts resulted in a record $26.3 billion in total capital investment.
Approximately 23,200 projected new private sector jobs were created alongside the investments.
Approximately three-quarters of total investment occurred outside the 10-county Atlanta region.

The state utilizes the Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) program to help communities complete environmental reviews, infrastructure planning, and engineering analyses in advance of project selection.
GRAD preparation minimizes delays and cost risks by allowing businesses to move smoothly from site selection to construction.
The state-backed Rural Site Development Initiative (RSDI) provides grant funding to communities for site studies, land grading, infrastructure improvements, and GRAD certification preparation.
Recent RSDI grants have improved access roads and expanded utilities in previously untapped regions to attract new business.
Georgia leverages Quick Start, a customized workforce training program, alongside technical colleges and universities to prepare employees for industry-specific requirements.
Quick Start tailors curriculum to accelerate employee readiness across advanced manufacturing, logistics, technology, and corporate services.
Companies consistently cite access to skilled talent and flexible training as key factors in choosing or expanding operations in Georgia.
The state offers access to one of the nation’s busiest airports, major port facilities including the Port of Savannah and the Port of Brunswick, and a robust interstate and rail system.
Ongoing infrastructure investments include highway expansions and freight/rail improvements to support supply chain efficiency and global trade.
Strategic planning in Georgia’s energy sector focuses on stability, low costs, and reliable power to support energy-intensive growth.

Key industrial sectors reinforced by the state's economic strategy include automotive, aerospace, food processing, advanced manufacturing, technology, life sciences, logistics, and corporate services.
The state actively builds supplier networks and sustains industry clusters to strengthen its competitive advantage in dynamic global markets.
Industry experts consistently rank Georgia highly for business climate and competitiveness due to its workforce development, site readiness, infrastructure, and economic planning.
As the state enters 2026, it continues investing in site readiness, workforce development, infrastructure, and energy to position companies for scaling operations.
