facts: The Volvo Car Charleston Plant, also known as Volvo Car US Operations (VCCH), is located in Ridgeville, South Carolina, United States.
Production at the facility commenced in 2018 and continues to the present.
It serves as the first Volvo Car plant established in the United States.
The operational scale of the plant is manufacturing.
Current production includes the EX90 and Polestar 3 models.
The facility previously produced SPA-based S60 models.
Vehicles manufactured at this plant serve worldwide markets.
source_title: List of Volvo Car production plants - Wikipedia

facts: Volvo’s manufacturing plant is located just outside Charleston, South Carolina.
The facility is Volvo’s first manufacturing plant in the US.
The factory exclusively produces the fully electric EX90 and the Polestar 3.
The factory opened in 2015, with Volvo investing $1.3 billion in the plant over the last decade.
Volvo announced plans to add capacity for production of the Volvo XC60 at the Charleston plant.
A “next generation hybrid” vehicle will be added to production by 2030.
The plant was built with an initial capacity plan of 150,000 vehicles per year.
Production of the XC60 will make use of existing floor space in the factory.
In the first 8 months of 2025, Volvo sold 27,000 XC60s in the US market.
Producing vehicles for the domestic market in South Carolina optimizes Volvo’s logistics footprint, lowers logistics costs, reduces emissions, and repurposes existing factory space.
Volvo did not share the specific model name, planned investment amount, or expected number of jobs for the next-generation hybrid.
Production of the next-generation hybrid will be online by 2030.
Volvo CEO Håkan Samuelsson stated the next-generation hybrid would be a range-extended electric vehicle.
The hybrid vehicle will behave like an electric car with very fast acceleration, agility, and quiet operation, but will have an onboard charger to extend its range.

The powertrain relies solely on the electric motor for propulsion until the initial battery charge is depleted, making it more of an electric car with a backup engine than a combustion car with batteries.
Volvo stated the new model is designed to meet specific US market demands in line with its increased focus on ensuring each region has needed products.

The vehicle will run as a true zero-emission vehicle until the initial battery charge is depleted, helping customers familiarize themselves with EVs and reinforcing Volvo's commitment to zero-emission vehicles despite federal government undermining of EVs.
The electric-first design makes it easier to ditch the range extender in the future.
Volvo used the event to highlight updates to the 2026 Volvo EX90, including an upgrade to 800-volt battery architecture and an updated core computer.
Volvo will upgrade the main Nvidia Orin-based system-on-chip processor to Nvidia’s latest Drive ATX platform.

Moving to 800-volt architecture enables faster charging, quicker 0-60 times, allows use of less copper in the high-voltage system, and enables compatibility with increasing numbers of 800-volt and 1000-volt public fast chargers.
The EX90 is getting two processing upgrades for 2026: upgrading to Nvidia’s latest Drive ATX SoC and adding a second, redundant chip.
The compute upgrade results in more than doubling in compute power, with an expected processing capacity of 500 TOPS.
Volvo Chief Technology and Engineering Officer Anders Bell explained the extra compute enables a modular, vertical ADAS stack.

New ADAS features enabled by the upgrade include emergency stop assist (monitors driver attention and safely pulls over after alerts) and an automatic e-call feature to contact authorities if the driver doesn't respond.
Volvo is teasing the big reveal of its next fully electric model, the EX60, coming on January 21, 2026.
There is a possibility of a completely new modular architecture being introduced alongside the EX60.
source_title: Volvo Expands Operations at its Charleston Plant and Updates the 2026 EX90 — CleanTechnica Field Trip - CleanTechnica

facts: Volvo Car USA donated a fully electric XC40 Recharge vehicle to the FDNY to advance post-accident safety training involving battery electric vehicles (BEVs).
The donation will help FDNY update training procedures and real-life protocols for safely extricating passengers from battery-powered vehicles.

Anders Gustafsson, Senior Vice President of Volvo Car Corporation and President and CEO of Volvo Car USA, and Eric Miller, Vice President of the Northeast Region for Volvo Car USA, handed over the keys to FDNY representatives and members of Manhattan’s Rescue Company 1 at Volvo Cars Manhattan.

Eric Miller stated that as the automotive industry speeds toward an electric future, first responders will experience changes in how they interact with these vehicles, and the donated XC40 Recharge will help ready the FDNY for this future.

Fire Commissioner Daniel A. Nigro noted that with the growing number of electric vehicles on the road, it is critical for FDNY members to train on extrications, fires, and other potential incidents involving them, thanking the FDNY Foundation and Volvo Cars for the support.

The donated XC40 Recharge includes first responder safety features such as a dedicated battery safety cage, independently sealed and cooled battery modules to prevent damage and leaks, brightly colored high voltage wiring to prevent unintentional contact, and an easily accessible deactivation switch to shut down all high voltage power.
source_title: Volvo Car USA provides XC40 Recharge to the New York City Fire Department to advance post-collision safety training on battery electric vehicles - The EV Report

facts: Volvo Trucks North America is headquartered in Greensboro, North Carolina.
The company delivers its Uptime Services commitment through a network of nearly 400 authorized dealers across North America.
A 24/7 Volvo Trucks Uptime Center supports the dealer network.
Every Volvo truck is assembled at the Volvo Trucks New River Valley manufacturing facility in Dublin, Virginia.
The Dublin, Virginia manufacturing facility meets the ISO 9001 standard for quality.
The Dublin, Virginia manufacturing facility meets the ISO 14001 standard for environmental care.
The Dublin, Virginia manufacturing facility holds a dual ISO 50001/Superior Energy Performance certification at the platinum level for sustained excellence in energy management.
Volvo Trucks North America provides complete transport solutions offering a full range of diesel, alternative-fuel, and all-electric vehicles.
Volvo Trucks North America is part of the Volvo Trucks global organization.
Volvo Trucks supplies medium- and heavy-duty trucks to professional customers via a global dealer network with 2,200 service points in about 130 countries.
Volvo trucks are assembled in 12 countries across the globe.
Approximately 120,000 Volvo trucks were delivered worldwide in 2025.
Volvo Trucks is part of the Volvo Group, one of the world’s leading manufacturers of trucks, buses, construction equipment, and marine and industrial engines.
The Volvo Group also provides complete solutions for financing and service.
Volvo Trucks’ work is based on the core values of quality, safety, and environmental care.
source_title: Volvo Trucks North America, headquartered in Greensboro, North Carolina, is one of the leading heavy-duty truck manufacturers in North America. Its Uptime Services commitment is delivered by a network of nearly 400 authorized dealers across North America and the 24/7 Volvo Trucks Uptime Center. Every Volvo truck is assembled in the Volvo Trucks New River Valley manufacturing facility in Dublin, Virginia, which meets the internationally recognized ISO 9001 standard for quality, 14001 standard for environmental care and holds a dual ISO 50001/Superior Energy Performance certification at the platinum level, indicating a sustained excellence in energy management. Volvo Trucks North America provides complete transport solutions for its customers, offering a full range of diesel, alternative-fuel and all-electric vehicles, and is part of the Volvo Trucks global organization.

facts: Volvo Cars received U.S. government approval to continue selling vehicles with Chinese "connected technology" in the country.
Volvo Cars is majority owned by China's Geely Holding.
In January 2025, President Joe Biden's administration finalized rules effectively barring nearly all Chinese cars and trucks from the U.S. market.
The January 2025 rules were part of a crackdown on vehicle software and hardware from China.
source_title: Volvo is approved to import 'connected car' tech vehicles to the US

facts: Volvo Cars is rolling out a sweeping strategy in the United States designed to blunt the impact of steep import duties imposed under the previous administration’s tariff measures.
Volvo decided to shift assembly of its XC60 crossover to its Ridgeville, South Carolina plant by late 2026.
The XC60 is a mild-hybrid and plug-in hybrid SUV that currently accounts for more than a third of Volvo’s U.S. sales volume.
Moving XC60 manufacturing stateside allows Volvo to avoid the 27.5 percent tariff on European-made vehicles and steeper levies on models sourced from China.
The Ridgeville plant currently produces the Polestar 3 EV and the EX90 electric SUV, and adding the XC60 will double its throughput.
Doubling plant throughput will spread fixed costs over higher volumes and improve overall factory economics.
Volvo is granting its U.S. operations greater decision-making power to tailor production schedules, supply-chain partnerships, and model configurations to local market preferences.
Engineering and core design for Volvo vehicles will remain anchored in Sweden while the U.S. arm gains latitude to align incentives, launch region-specific features, and expedite plant investments.
Volvo is phasing out its S60, S90, V60, and V90 sedans and wagons in America to concentrate resources on crossovers and electrified powertrains.
Higher-margin vehicles like the XC60 and XC90 deliver more profitability per unit and cater to prevailing buyer tastes as SUVs represent roughly 70 percent of new-vehicle sales in the U.S.
Volvo is ramping up its hybrid offerings, including mild-hybrid technology on the XC60 for incremental fuel-efficiency gains without high infrastructure demands.
Plug-in hybrid variants offer extended electric-only driving ranges to appeal to eco-minded consumers concerned about emissions.
Emphasizing hybrids allows Volvo to sidestep punitive 100 percent tariffs on Chinese-made EVs and avoid steep capital outlays required for dedicated U.S. battery production.
The company’s modular SPA3 platform underpins this transition, enabling shared architectures across multiple models including Polestar siblings and next-generation SUVs.
The SPA3 platform slashes development complexity and trims production costs by an estimated 15 to 20 percent.
CEO Håkan Samuelsson has publicly urged the European Union to eliminate its 10 percent tariff on U.S.-made cars as a reciprocal step to balance transatlantic commerce.
Volvo announced a one-time $1.2 billion non-cash impairment charge in the second quarter, largely attributable to EV launch delays and tariff pressures on two electric models.

The writedown prompted a targeted workforce reduction of approximately 15 percent of its office staff, equating to roughly 3,000 positions, aimed at preserving cash flow and funding critical plant enhancements in South Carolina.
Volvo reported better-than-expected second-quarter results where operating profit, though down from the previous year, topped analyst forecasts and sent shares up by double digits.
Management attributes this financial resilience to disciplined cost control, favorable mix shifts toward higher-margin hybrids, and early benefits of localizing key models.
Domestic assembly of the XC60 will insulate American buyers from sudden price spikes tied to global political disputes while maintaining stable pricing.
A steady slate of locally produced SUVs will simplify inventory planning for dealers and reduce logistical complexity by eliminating ocean freight schedules and customs delays.

Enhanced support for Volvo’s U.S. branch includes more direct input into product roadmaps, potentially accelerating rollout of market-first options like advanced driver-assist packages and regionally tailored infotainment systems.
Industry observers believe Volvo’s blueprint may set a template for other import brands grappling with tariff risks.
Ford and General Motors are expanding North American assembly of key models to insulate against potential trade escalations.
Japanese and Korean automakers, long reliant on cross-border supply chains, are likewise exploring regional production clusters.
Volvo’s strategy underscores a broader industry shift from pure globalization toward nimble regionalization by aligning production footprints with consumption hubs.
source_title: Volvo Reconfigures U.S. Operations with XC60 Production and Model Overhaul to Circumvent Tariffs

facts: Volvo Cars is primarily owned by China's Geely Holding.
Volvo Cars received U.S. government approval to continue vehicle sales despite a new regulatory landscape restricting Chinese vehicular products.
The new regulatory measures aim to limit Chinese software and hardware influence in the automotive industry and are effective from early 2026.
Due to its Chinese ownership, Volvo Car USA underwent a specific authorization process with the Department of Commerce, granting it the ability to maintain and grow its U.S. market share.
U.S. lawmakers are considering further tightening these policies.
Sales figures for 2025 showed a slight decline, but the company remains optimistic.
Volvo maintains its U.S. market presence despite new rules from the Biden administration.
Volvo plans to expand production in South Carolina to boost domestic manufacturing capacity.
Volvo plans to initiate U.S. production of a new hybrid model by the decade's end, ensuring compliance and market adaptation.
Future local production of the XC60 SUV in South Carolina is part of Volvo's broader strategy aligning with its hybrid and electric vehicle goals.
source_title: Volvo Cars Secures U.S. Approval Amid Chinese Vehicle Ban | Business

facts: Volvo Cars announced plans to begin producing a new hybrid model in the United States by the end of this decade.
The new hybrid model will be designed specifically for the U.S. market to help boost capacity utilization at Volvo's South Carolina plant.
Volvo Cars CEO Hakan Samuelsson stated that it will take time until America is fully electric.
Volvo's CEO described the new larger hybrid model as primarily an electric car with a backup combustion engine that kicks in when the battery is flat.
Automakers worldwide are adjusting production strategies to contend with President Trump's duties on foreign cars, part of a wider policy to boost U.S. manufacturing.
Volvo previously announced in April that it would produce more vehicles in the U.S.
In July, Volvo stated plans to start producing its XC60 mid-size SUV at the South Carolina plant from late 2026.
The South Carolina factory currently builds only Volvo's fully electric EX90 SUV and the Polestar 3.
Production data shows these current models take up only a small fraction of the site's annual capacity of 150,000 vehicles.
The announcement coincided with Volvo's 70th anniversary in the United States.
Volvo previously aimed to phase out all non-electric models by 2030 but reversed course last year to keep hybrids in its lineup.

Luis Rezende, president of Volvo Cars' Americas operation, stated that adding another model to production will take a big step toward realizing the full potential of local manufacturing investments and workforce.
A trade deal struck in July with the European Union cut U.S. tariffs on European cars to 15%.
The EU trade agreement has yet to take effect, leaving vehicles subject to a 27.5% levy.
CEO Hakan Samuelsson said the 10% duties eliminated on auto imports from the U.S. into Europe will make selling its U.S.-produced EX90 vehicle abroad more attractive.
Samuelsson noted that with the 10% tariff reduction, Volvo has the possibility to grow its operations in Europe.
source_title: Volvo Cars to produce new US model amid tariff pressure

facts: Volvo has ambitious plans to become a fully electric automaker by 2030.
For the 2026 model year, Volvo is making adaptive cruise control standard across its entire lineup.
Volvo’s next-generation infotainment tech is undergirded by Qualcomm Technologies’ Snapdragon Cockpit Platform.
The new computing platform provides processing power more than twice as fast as prior tech and generates graphics up to 10 times faster.
Volvo notes that making its screens more responsive decreases distraction and creates safer drivers.
The latest version of Volvo Car UX will be standard across the Volvo lineup for 2026.
The new interface features reconfigured menus that keep more functions visible and accessible as the driver navigates the vehicle’s system.
Volvo will distribute the next-generation UX software to vehicles equipped with Google Built-In via an over-the-air update later this year.
Volvo will offer the 2026 XC40 with the Black Edition styling package.
The Black Edition package blacks out the wheels, exterior trim, and badging.
Volvo is expanding the Black Edition package’s availability to Crystal White, Vapour Grey, and Denim Blue for both the XC40 and XC60.
source_title: Volvo Announces Tech Updates for Full 2026 Lineup | Cars.com

facts: Volvo Cars said it will begin producing a new hybrid model in the United States by the end of this decade as the Swedish auto maker continues to adjust to President Donald Trump’s tariff policies.
The new model will be designed for the U.S. market and help Volvo boost capacity utilisation at its South Carolina plant.
Automakers worldwide are scrambling to rethink their production strategies to contend with President Trump’s duties on foreign cars, part of his wider policy to boost U.S. manufacturing.
Volvo Cars’ CEO said in April the company would produce more vehicles in the U.S.
The group announced in July that it plans to start producing its popular XC60 mid-size SUV at the South Carolina plant from late 2026.
The factory currently builds only Volvo’s fully electric EX90 SUV and the Polestar 3.
Production data shows that these models take up only a small fraction of the site’s annual capacity of 150,000 vehicles.
News of the new hybrid model coincided with Volvo’s 70th anniversary in the United States.
Volvo, long positioned as an EV trailblazer with the aim of phasing out all non-electric models by 2030, last year reversed course and said hybrids would remain part of its lineup.

Luis Rezende, president of Volvo Cars’ Americas operation, stated that adding another model into production will take a big step toward realising the full potential of local manufacturing investments and workforce.
A trade deal struck in July with the European Union cut U.S. tariffs on European cars to 15%, but the agreement has yet to take effect.
Vehicles continue to face a 27.5% levy while awaiting the new tariff agreement's implementation.
source_title: Volvo Cars to produce new US model amid tariff pressure

facts: Volvo Group North America will lay off 550 to 800 employees, a company spokesperson confirmed on Friday, April 18.
The layoffs will occur at plants in Maryland, Pennsylvania, and Virginia.
The Swedish automaker cited the need to adjust to worsening economic conditions.
A spokesperson stated that heavy-duty truck orders continue to be negatively affected by market uncertainty about freight rates and demand, possible regulatory changes, and the impact of tariffs.
Between 250 and 350 employees will lose their jobs at Mack Trucks Lehigh Valley Operations in Macungie, PA.
The Macungie, PA facility employs about 3,050 workers.
Another 250 to 350 jobs are being cut at Volvo Trucks New River Valley Operations in Dublin, VA.
The Dublin, VA plant has a workforce of around 3,400.
A smaller round of 50 to 100 layoffs is hitting Volvo Group Powertrain Operations in Hagerstown, MD.
About 1,800 people work at the Hagerstown, MD facility.
Volvo's layoffs come amid rising fears about the state of auto manufacturing under Trump's sweeping tariffs.
Trump has imposed 25% duties on imported cars, light trucks, aluminum, and steel, along with a 145% tariff on Chinese goods.
Vehicle data company Cox Automotive warned that panic buying has dramatically lowered the US supply of new and used vehicles.
New-vehicle stock fell to 2.69 million units as of Tuesday, April 1, down 10.2% from the start of March.
Volvo said the layoffs were fueled by fewer purchases of its cars and trucks.
A spokesperson stated: "We regret having to take this action, but we need to align production with reduced demand for our vehicles."
Other automakers have cut North American jobs since Trump's tariff announcement.
Stellantis has temporarily laid off about 900 US workers while halting production at some Canadian and Mexican plants.
General Motors said about 200 temporary layoffs have happened at an all-electric vehicle plant in Detroit to align with market dynamics.
Another GM plant in Ontario will pause manufacturing, with a union leader saying there will be an indefinite layoff of nearly 500 workers once production resumes.
Volvo's website states it employs more than 19,600 workers in North America.

links.context: Follow Daily Voice for free news updates

source_title: Volvo Laying Off 550-800 Employees At Plants In MD, PA, VA | Daily Voice

facts: Volvo Trucks aims to reclaim lost ground and capture more market share in North America as the global truck market pulls out of the post-Covid hangover.
By 2025, commercial trucks in the U.S. have an average age of around 10.2 years.
The average age for regional haul trucks is 6.8 years, while linehaul units are about 6.4 years old.
Demand forecasts for 2025 suggest around 212,000 units will be delivered in North America, which is below the five-year order average of 285,000 units.
The market downturn may stem from tariffs, emissions regulations, and a cautious approach from operators regarding fleet growth.
Volvo Trucks noted that the North American market might struggle until 2026 due to ongoing decreases in freight activity and new tariffs affecting costs.
Regulatory uncertainties around emissions standards are making fleet owners hesitant to invest in new trucks.
Volvo’s focus on innovation includes electrification and self-driving technology to gain market share as these trends become prominent.
Volvo holds a 19.6% market share in Greater Europe.
The company has made significant investments totaling US$2 billion in the new VNL model and US$400 million in production facilities.
In North America, the Class 8 truck market is primarily dominated by Daimler Trucks (Freightliner), Paccar (Kenworth and Peterbilt), Navistar (International), and Volvo Trucks.
In 2024, Volvo Trucks achieved a 10.5% market share in North America, an increase from 10.2% the previous year.
The new VNL model promotes fuel efficiency and performance.
In 2024, Volvo reported a 6% increase in truck orders attributed to strong demand in vocational trucks.
Volvo experienced a 5% decrease in deliveries in 2024 due to transitioning to new models.
Volvo’s heavy-duty market share is currently 7.2%.
In global markets, Volvo Trucks achieved a 17.9% market share in the European heavy-duty sector (16 tonnes and above) in 2024.
Volvo secured a 23.7% market share in Brazil in 2024.
Economic factors like tariffs and regulatory compliance related to emissions are creating apprehensive purchasing behaviors among operators.
The electrification trend is vital, with Volvo leading in electric heavy-duty truck deliveries.
By October 2025, Volvo reported over 5,700 electric truck deliveries across 50 countries.
In North America, Volvo holds a 30% market share in the electric truck segment.
Freightliner held a 38.4% market share in North America in 2025.
Peterbilt held a 14.8% market share in North America in 2025.
Kenworth held a 14.3% market share in North America in 2025.
International (Navistar) held an 11.6% market share in North America in 2025.
Mack held a 7.7% market share in North America in 2025.
Western Star held a 5.4% market share in North America in 2025.
DAF Trucks held a 14.4% market share in Greater Europe in 2025.
Mercedes held an 18% market share in Greater Europe in 2025.
MAN held a 14% market share in Greater Europe in 2025.
Iveco held a 13% market share in Greater Europe in 2025.
Scania held a 12% market share in Greater Europe in 2025.
Renault held a 9% market share in Greater Europe in 2025.
In April 2024, Volvo Group announced plans to build a new heavy-duty truck manufacturing plant in Monterrey, Mexico.
The Monterrey plant is expected to be operational by 2026 and will focus on producing heavy-duty conventional vehicles for Volvo and Mack brands.
The Monterrey plant will be approximately 1.7 million square feet in size.
The facility will include comprehensive assembly capabilities, such as cab body-in-white production and painting.
Volvo Trucks completed a six-year, $400 million investment to upgrade its NRV plant in Virginia.
The NRV plant upgrade was undertaken to prepare the facility for the production of the new Volvo VNL model.
Enhancements at the NRV plant include expanding the industrial footprint and installing state-of-the-art equipment aimed at improving plant efficiency and product quality.
The Monterrey plant will provide additional capacity to support growth plans for Volvo Trucks and Mack Trucks in the U.S. and Canadian markets.
The expansion is expected to support Mack truck sales in Mexico and Latin America.
The strategic location of the Monterrey plant offers logistical efficiencies for supporting sales to the southwestern and western regions of the U.S., Mexico, and Latin America.
Magnus Koeck serves as Vice President of Strategy, Marketing, and Brand Management at Volvo Trucks North America.
Magnus Koeck stated, “The tide will turn. The question is, when the tide will turn,” indicating cautious optimism for future growth.

Magnus Koeck noted, “We aspire to grow a lot here in North America during the next business cycle,” underscoring Volvo’s commitment to enhancing market share with innovative products like the new VNL model.

Regarding sustainability, Koeck stated, “Achieving a 10% improvement in fuel efficiency across the North American trucking industry would have a significant impact on decarbonization efforts, equivalent to removing 30,000 new battery-electric trucks from the road.”
Koeck pointed out pre-buy effects expected around upcoming emissions regulations, stating, “Signs of this pre-buy activity are already emerging,” as operators adapt ahead of increased truck costs.
Volvo anticipates deliveries of 265,000 units in 2025, a downward revision indicative of economic uncertainties and fluctuating freight prices.
The U.S. and Canada are grappling with a freight market recession characterized by reduced volumes and prices.
Laid-off workers in various Volvo facilities underscore the consequences of declining market sentiment and high tariffs on imported components.
New emissions standards set to take effect in 2027 will impact the market and lead to price increases as manufacturers adapt product lines.
Historic trends suggest customers may engage in pre-buying activities to avoid higher prices, resulting in short-term demand fluctuations.
Volvo’s focus on electric heavy-duty trucks has enabled it to maintain a 30% market share in this segment despite broader market challenges.
Projected deliveries for 2024 are 250,000 units with an average freight price of $3,500 and a market growth rate of -4.0%.
Projected deliveries for 2025 are 265,000 units with an average freight price of $3,200 and a market growth rate of 1.2%.
Projected deliveries for 2026 are 280,000 units with an average freight price of $3,800 and a market growth rate of 3.0%.
Projected deliveries for 2027 are 300,000 units with an average freight price of $4,000 and a market growth rate of 4.5%.
Projected deliveries for 2028 are 320,000 units with an average freight price of $4,200 and a market growth rate of 5.0%.
source_title: Inside Volvo Trucks’ $2 Billion Investment: Strategies for Market Recovery – McGrath Trucks

facts: Volvo Group has lowered its 2025 sales outlook in North America to 265,000 trucks.
The reduction amounts to a cut of 10,000 units from the previous forecast.
The company cites new tariffs and weak demand as reasons for the downward revision.
The revised outlook was reported during the company's Q3 earnings this month.
President and CEO Martin Lundstedt stated that customers are in "wait-and-see mode" due to high uncertainty from current market conditions.
Lundstedt noted during the Oct. 17 earnings call that turmoil from uncertainty in North America is temporarily burdensome but manageable short-term.
Lundstedt emphasized that the company has built a strong platform for the future in North America.
Volvo Group faces mounting challenges in one of its largest markets due to trade tensions and a freight slowdown.
A new 25% tariff on imports of heavy-duty and medium-duty trucks and their associated parts begins Nov. 1.
The company incurred 500 million SEK in tariff-related costs in Q3 alone, according to CFO Mats Backman.
Lundstedt stated that Volvo Group has a "100% U.S. footprint for North American trucks."
The company is preparing for potential tariff agreements between the U.S. and Mexico.
Lundstedt said the company anticipates a starting point of around 10,000 units with the ability to move north if needed.
The company has a "structural undercapacity in North America for normal markets and also for upmarkets."
Lundstedt stated that adding capacity for North and South American markets makes Volvo and Mack necessary.
In the U.S., Volvo Trucks operates an assembly plant in Virginia.
The company maintains additional manufacturing facilities in Maryland and Virginia.
Volvo Trucks operates seven parts distribution centers in the U.S.
Sister brand Mack Truck shares Volvo's powertrain assembly facility in Maryland.
Mack Truck also has its own plant in Pennsylvania.
The Pennsylvania plant recently began manufacturing the Mack Pioneer, the brand's first long-haul truck in decades.
It is unlikely Volvo will expand production capacity due to the ongoing freight recession and weak demand.
The company expects new tariffs to further slow orders into 2026.
In Q3, Volvo Group's total truck orders increased by 10% year-over-year.
Deliveries fell by 20% year-over-year in Q3.
Sales of heavy-duty trucks during the first nine months of 2025 were down 10% compared to the same period last year.
source_title: Volvo Group cuts North America forecast by 10K trucks

facts: Volvo Group announced a $41 million investment in North Carolina’s Piedmont Triad.
The investment is designated to purchase and expand the building housing its U.S. Uptime Center.
The expanded facility will now also serve as the new global headquarters for Volvo Financial Services (VFS).
The expansion is located about two miles from VFS’ currently leased location.
The project will bring about 360 VFS colleagues onto Volvo Group North America’s campus.
Announcements were made during a groundbreaking ceremony by Martin Weissburg, Volvo Group North America chairman and Mack Trucks president.
Marcio Pedroso, Volvo Financial Services president at VGNA’s Uptime Center, also participated in the groundbreaking ceremony.
Martin Weissburg stated the Uptime Center is a strategic site for keeping customers and their vehicles on the road, delivering goods, transporting people, and servicing communities.
Weissburg noted the purchase and expansion reinforces commitment to the Piedmont Triad communities and welcomes VFS to the campus.
Marcio Pedroso stated moving VFS allows all Volvo Group entities in the Piedmont to be located on one campus.
Pedroso added co-location will enable deeper colleague relationships and help retain highly skilled, world-class jobs supporting the local economy.
The investment includes acquisition of the Uptime Center building, which opened in 2014 and was previously leased by the company.
The investment includes a 62,000 square-foot (5,800 square meters) expansion to house VFS’ global headquarters.
At project completion, the facility’s new three-story footprint will be about 185,000 square feet (17,150 square meters).
The expanded building will be connected by a glass atrium and two upper-level walkways.

VFS is the captive finance arm of the Volvo Group, providing financial services for customers of Mack Trucks, Volvo Trucks, Volvo Construction Equipment, Nova buses, Prevost coaches, and Volvo buses in North America and globally.
VFS operates in 50 countries worldwide with approximately 1,700 colleagues.
The Uptime Center site straddles the cities of Greensboro and High Point.
The site houses resources to ensure customers’ commercial trucks keep moving, with people and systems dedicated to real-time vehicle issue monitoring and response.
The site helps dealers troubleshoot difficult cases and find necessary repair parts.
The site includes connectivity services for over-the-air monitoring and software updates for optimized vehicle performance.
The site also houses service market logistics, IT, training facilities, and finance and accounting operations.
The Volvo Group currently employs around 600 people at the Uptime Center.
At project completion expected in the first quarter of 2023, Volvo Financial Services will relocate approximately 360 jobs to its new headquarters.
source_title: Volvo Group Invests in Expanded U.S. Uptime Center, Co-Location of Volvo Financial Services Headquarters | Rental Equipment Register

facts: Over the last few years, Swedish automaker Volvo has been investing more in electric vehicles and hybrid cars.
This past year alone, Volvo released the updated XC90 plug-in hybrid SUV and the 2025 EX30 twin motor EV.
Volvo is broadening its footprint in the United States to skirt stringent import tariffs.
Volvo announced it would invest heavily into its shared factory with Polestar in Ridgeville, South Carolina, expanding the facility's size and scope.
A large portion of the Ridgeville, South Carolina expansion will focus on manufacturing a brand-new American-made hybrid vehicle.
Production of the new American-made hybrid vehicle is projected to begin sometime before 2030.
Volvo has not divulged details or shown a prototype for the upcoming hybrid vehicle.
Volvo states that hybrid technology demand in the U.S. currently exceeds electric vehicle demand.
Hakan Samuelsson, chief executive of Volvo Cars, stated that electrification has not picked up as fast as anticipated a few years ago.
Hakan Samuelsson stated that Volvo believes it will need hybrid cars longer.
Volvo aims to increase its overall U.S. sales by about 60% in the next five years.
The targeted sales increase represents an additional 125,000 units compared to last year's sales figures.
Expanding the scale and operations of the South Carolina manufacturing plant will help Volvo recoup losses from import tariffs instituted this year.
Imports of Volvo vehicles from Sweden are currently subject to a 15% levy.
The 15% tariff rate could change as new deals are finalized with the United States government.
source_title: Volvo Targets US Market With Next-Gen Made-In-America Hybrid

entity_name: North American EV & Hybrid Market Trends
canonical_name: North American EV & Hybrid Market Analysis
facts: Hybrid vehicles now hold 14.5% market share, surpassing BEVs at 5.1%, reflecting consumer preference for fuel economy without full EV commitment.
OEMs are recalibrating investments, with over $234 billion spent since 2020 but recent trends favoring hybrids and ICE platforms.
Automakers are expanding battery use beyond vehicles into energy storage markets, leveraging growing US electricity demand and aiming to optimize battery capacity amid slower EV growth.
Changes to EV incentives, infrastructure funding and regulatory frameworks represent a near complete reversal in direction for full battery EV adoption momentum.
Hybrid vehicles now account for 14.5% of US market share, while BEVs have fallen to 5.1% after peaking at 7.8% in 2024.
Electrified vehicles as a whole, including hybrids, plug-in hybrids and BEVs, represent around 20% of the market, leaving internal combustion engine (ICE) vehicles with an 80% share.
Consumers are looking for better fuel economy without having to commit to EV charging.
Hybrid is the only propulsion type that has gained sales momentum compared to last year, while all other propulsion types are down in sales.
Vehicles priced below $60,000 are more likely to substitute steel for aluminium for fenders and hoods due in part to advances in high-strength steels.
US light vehicle sales reached around 16 million units in 2025, with forecasts for 2026 converging at approximately 15.7 million units, a modest decline of 300,000 vehicles.
Industry experts are continuing to lower full battery EV adoption forecasts, with the current projection of 16% market share by 2030 potentially overstated.
Since 2020, automakers have announced more than $234 billion in investments across North America, with 77% directed toward the United States, 13% to Canada and 9% to Mexico.
Honda halted its North American EV expansion, stating that EV business viability is not currently assured.
Honda cancelled the launch and development of several EV models planned for the region while pivoting toward hybrids and prioritizing stable earnings from the ICE business.
The US Department of Commerce slapped Honda with a 13.07% weighted-average dumping margin on hot-rolled coil in April.
Uncertainty surrounding the renegotiation of the USMCA is adding complexity, with market participants expecting a more fragmented trade environment where tariffs play a larger role.
Free trade is expected to be replaced by tariffs, according to Oscar del Cueto, president of American Chamber of Commerce Mexico and Canadian Pacific Kansas City Mexico.
Local production remains a meaningful initiative from the perspective of tariff measures, according to Honda’s leadership.
Ford launched a dedicated energy storage business to repurpose excess battery capacity and diversify revenue streams amid slowing EV demand growth.
US electricity demand is rising due to data centers, industrial activity and renewable energy expansion, accelerating the need for battery storage solutions.
Batteries are increasingly viewed not as vehicle-specific components but as flexible assets within a broader energy ecosystem.

Strategic priorities for OEMs include balancing powertrains, managing demand uncertainty, optimizing battery utilization through energy storage, and navigating trade dynamics with tariffs and localization requirements.
source_title: North American EV demand slows as hybrids gain momentum and automotive OEM strategies reset  - Fastmarkets

facts: Volvo Trucks North America is headquartered in Greensboro, North Carolina.
Every Volvo truck is assembled at the Volvo Trucks New River Valley manufacturing facility in Dublin, Virginia.
The Dublin, Virginia manufacturing facility meets the internationally recognized ISO 9001 standard for quality.
The Dublin, Virginia manufacturing facility meets the ISO 14001 standard for environmental care.
The Dublin, Virginia facility holds a dual ISO 50001/Superior Energy Performance certification at the platinum level for sustained excellence in energy management.
Volvo Trucks North America offers a full range of diesel, alternative-fuel, and all-electric vehicles as part of its complete transport solutions.
Peter Voorhoeve serves as president of Volvo Trucks North America.
The new Volvo VNL is designed specifically for North America and is the most connected truck Volvo has ever built.
Volvo Connect is the fleet management platform that brings vehicle insights, remote diagnostics, fuel economy reports, and over-the-air software updates together in one portal.
Uptime services use wireless connectivity to track vehicle health in real time, spot potential issues early, schedule maintenance, and install over-the-air software updates while a truck is parked.
Productivity services include fleet planning systems, fuel and safety reports, and driver support features.
The Volvo My Truck app allows drivers to check key vehicle information such as fluid levels and light status, and set cab climate control via phone or tablet before starting a shift.
Uptime Services are delivered by a network of nearly 400 authorized dealers across North America and the 24/7 Volvo Trucks Uptime Center.
Customer support is provided via a global dealer network with 2,200 service points in about 130 countries.
Volvo trucks are assembled in 12 countries across the globe.
In 2025, approximately 120,000 Volvo trucks were delivered worldwide.
Volvo Trucks is part of the Volvo Group, which also provides complete solutions for financing and service.
source_title: Volvo Trucks surpasses 1 million connected trucks worldwide, including 218,000 in North America

facts: Volvo Group North America agreed to pay $130 million to the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration for alleged failures regarding vehicle recalls.
The civil penalty is described by the government as "one of the largest-ever penalties for violations of the Vehicle Safety Act."
NHTSA's investigation allegedly found that Volvo failed to properly report death and injury incidents.
Safety regulators allege Volvo failed to recall vehicles in a timely fashion.
Regulators also allege Volvo failed to properly notify owners about safety recalls.
An independent third-party auditor will monitor Volvo as the automaker meets regularly with NHTSA about potential vehicle safety problems.
The auditor's job requires monitoring Volvo for possible Safety Act regulations violations.
As part of the consent order, employees will receive appropriate training about complying with federal safety regulations.
The consent order requires the company to make an upfront payment of $65 million.
Volvo must spend an additional $20 million on a specific performance obligation to create a safety data analytics infrastructure.
The agreement includes an additional $45 million deferred penalty that may become payable under specified circumstances.
The consent order is scheduled to have a three-year term unless NHTSA determines possible problems that could extend the order to five years.
source_title: Volvo Group North America Recall Failures to Cost $130 Million | CarComplaints.com

facts: Swedish truckmaker Volvo expects trucking demand in North America to remain under pressure into next year.
Weak freight activity and U.S. President Donald Trump’s tariffs are weighing on the North American truck market.
The Gothenburg-based Volvo Group, which also manufactures construction equipment and engines, has lowered its forecast for the North American truck market while keeping its European view unchanged.
Volvo Group expects heavy truck deliveries of around 265,000 in North America this year, which is 10,000 fewer than previously envisioned.
Volvo Group expects to deliver some 290,000 vehicles in Europe.
Volvo Group's shares fell 8% by 0935 GMT, touching their lowest price in six months.
Volvo stated that the North American long-haul freight market remains in recession with lower freight volumes and prices.
Customers are holding back orders amid uncertainty over new emissions rules and tariffs.
CEO Martin Lundstedt told Reuters that transport buyers are in a wait-and-see mode as uncertainty clouds market conditions.
Volvo expects European truck demand to hold up better than North America's because it is largely replacement-driven and supported by stable fleet utilization.
Equity analysts from Jefferies see a potential pickup later in 2026, as normalized inventory levels and back-end-loaded demand could support a gradual recovery.
Volvo Group's adjusted operating profit fell around 17% to 11.7 billion Swedish crowns ($1.2 billion) in the third quarter.
The third-quarter adjusted operating profit matched the average forecast in an LSEG poll of analysts.
President Trump's move to slap a 25% tariff on imported heavy trucks has deepened trade uncertainty across North America.
The USMCA trade agreement still shields most manufacturers from fresh duties despite the new tariffs.
Volvo Group is best positioned among European truckmakers to weather the trade barriers because it builds all trucks destined for the U.S. market domestically, along with most of its components.
Higher costs on imported parts may still affect Volvo Group's operations.
Volvo Group runs 16 manufacturing and remanufacturing sites across the U.S., Canada, and Mexico.
Volvo Group makes vehicles under brands such as Mack Trucks and Renault as well as its own name.
source_title: Volvo Group expects North America weakness through 2026 amid freight slump – Wall Street Observer

facts: Volvo may turn more of its assembly line in the New River Valley over to robots in the coming years.
Nearly 3,000 humans still do most of the complex jobs at the Volvo plant in Dublin.
Magnus Koeck is vice president of strategy and marketing at Volvo trucks North America.
Koeck stated that maybe in 15 years they can do everything automated, but they still have lots of manual labor for the final assembly.
A few years ago, Volvo built a second plant in Dublin.
Inside the second Dublin plant, 170 robots do simpler tasks like painting truck components and attaching doors to cabs.
Volvo spent $500 million building the second plant.
Engineers sit inside part of the facility monitoring the robots’ progress.
Mehdi Ahmadian is an expert in automotive engineering at Virginia Tech.
Ahmadian stated that the industry is replacing physical labor with intellectual labor.
Ahmadian expects to see an acceleration in automation in the auto manufacturing industry in the coming decades due to advancing artificial intelligence.
Ahmadian advises young people to get real-world experiences and training in AI technology before graduating college.
Companies like Volvo can help current workers learn new skills needed inside a more automated workplace.
source_title: Inside Volvo's New River Valley plant, where automated robots are increasingly used to build trucks | WVTF

facts: Wabash National is an American diversified industrial manufacturing company and one of North America's largest producers of semi-trailers and truck bodies.
The company specializes in the design and production of dry freight vans, refrigerated vans, platform trailers, liquid tank trailers, truck bodies, and composite products.

Previously sold products under Wabash National, Transcraft, Benson, Walker Transport, Brenner Tank, Bulk Tank International, and Supreme brands before consolidating under the Wabash brand name in 2022.
The company operates a number of Parts & Services centers throughout the United States.
Total revenue was $1.95 billion USD in 2024.

Founded in 1985 by Jerry Ehrlich as president, Ronald Klimara as vice president of finance, and William Hoover as vice president of sales, joined by a small group of Monon employees, with Rodney Ehrlich serving as head of engineering.
Headquarters are located in Lafayette, Indiana, where the company started out making trailers for Sears.
Early customers included Heartland Express, Dart Transit, Federal Express, Swift Transportation, Triple Crown Services, and Schneider National.
By 1986, the company had $70 million in sales from 15,000 trailers.
By 1990, Wabash had $170 million in sales.
Wabash became a publicly traded company on the NYSE under ticker WNC in 1991.
In 1992, it launched Wabash National Financial Corporation, a subsidiary that allowed customers to finance the purchases of Wabash trailers.
From 1992 to 1994, Schneider was its biggest customer, accounting for one-sixth of its sales.
By 1995, it reported $500 million in revenue and employed 3,400 people.
The company acquired the US manufacturing and sales distribution business of Fruehauf Trailer in 1997.
It also acquired Cloud Corporation, based in Harrison, Arkansas, and Cloud Oak Flooring Company of Sheridan, Arkansas, to produce laminated hardwood flooring for its trucks.
The wood products business was consolidated to create Wabash Wood Products, based in Harrison.
In 2000, the company acquired the Breadner Group of Kitchener, Ontario to expand its retail network into Canada.
Following a peak of 5,000 employees in the 1990s, the company had 3,000 personnel at the beginning of 2001.
A slowdown in orders resulted in a major restructuring and layoffs; in March 2001, 500 jobs were eliminated as part of a cost-cutting initiative.
Additional layoffs cut the workforce down to 2,000 before another 480 roles were eliminated in January 2002.
A sudden increase in orders resulted in the company rehiring these employees and hiring an additional 100 just six weeks later.
In 2003, Wabash sold off its trailer leasing and rental, as well as its wholesale aftermarket parts distribution businesses to Aurora Trailer Holdings.
The company hired over 400 employees in 2004 to meet growing production needs.
Wabash acquired Transcraft Corporation for $71 million in 2006.
It acquired certain assets from Benson International in 2008, including a 180,000-square-foot manufacturing facility in Cadiz, Kentucky.
In 2008, it also formed a purchasing consortium with Kentucky Trailer, Utilimaster, Federal Signal Corporation, and VT Specialized Vehicles.
During the Great Recession, orders diminished; at the end of 2008, Wabash announced an extended holiday shutdown where 800 jobs would be permanently eliminated.
By February 2009, cost reduction actions had eliminated more than 25% of the workforce since 2007.
The company temporarily reduced salaries, cut the standard paid work week, and introduced a voluntary furlough program to manage overhead costs.
Eventually, 40% of the salaried workforce and nearly 70% of hourly employees were laid off.
Wabash was able to stay afloat when it received $35 million in financing from Lincolnshire Management.
On May 8, 2012, Wabash National completed the acquisition of Walker Group Holdings for $376 million.
The deal gave Wabash control of the Walker, Brenner, Bulk, TST, Progress, Garsite, and Extract brand names.

In 2013, Wabash spent $15 million to acquire assets from Beall Corp's tank and trailer business, including equipment, inventory, product designs, intellectual property, and a Portland, Oregon manufacturing facility.
On September 27, 2017, Wabash National completed the acquisition of Supreme Industries, Inc.
In January 2019, Wabash announced it had sold off its Aviation and Truck Equipment division.
President and COO Brent Yeagy took over as company CEO in June.
In February 2022, the company announced it would rebrand under one simplified name: Wabash, eliminating the Benson, Brenner, Bulk Tank, Supreme, Transcraft, and Walker sub-brands.
Company trucks were rebranded starting in July 2022.
In February 2025, Wabash acquired TrailerHawk.ai and established its trailer-as-a-service offerings.

Product lines include dry freight vans, refrigerated vans, converter dollies, platform trailers, liquid tank trailers, intermodal equipment, engineered products, composite products, and high-quality laminated oak flooring used extensively in dry van trailers, truck bodies, and containers.
Wabash's initial innovation was the aluminum plate trailer, which reduced wasted space, water damage claims, and repair costs compared to competitors using aluminum-covered plywood.
In 1987, the company manufactured the RoadRailer, a trailer capable of being used on roads and railway tracks, for Thrall Car Manufacturing.
Wabash purchased the RoadRailer rights in 1991 and became the exclusive manufacturer.
In 1992, it began licensing the production of RoadRailers for international markets, starting with Australia and soon licensing to six firms in 19 countries.
Wabash began manufacturing refrigerated van trailers in 1995.
The following year, the company introduced its DuraPlate composite panel technology for semi-trailers, later adapted for other industrial and commercial uses.
The company produced its 200,000th DuraPlate trailer in 2004 and 500,000th in 2013.
DuraPlate HD, for heavy duty hauls, was introduced in 2003.
By 2008, Wabash established a new division dedicated to further applications for DuraPlate.
In 2009, Wabash National expanded into portable storage container manufacturing and introduced DuraPlate AeroSkirt, which focused on increased aerodynamics for better fuel economy.
In 2011, Wabash National introduced the industry's first dry freight van with a 35,000-pound floor rating, allowing heavy cargo typically hauled on a flatbed trailer to be transported in a dry van.
In 2015, Wabash introduced its first line of dry- and refrigerated-truck bodies, with production based in Lafayette, Indiana.
In 2022, Wabash debuted its trailer as a service offering, a subscription-based solution that provides guaranteed uptime, managed care, maintenance, and telematics.
It also established the Wabash Parts distribution network, a consolidation of the company's entire aftermarket portfolio behind a new e-commerce website.
Additionally the Acutherm sub-brand was developed to offer intelligent thermal management.
In 2023, Wabash established a joint venture with Fernweh Group to expand its e-commerce offerings.
Soon after, the Wabash Marketplace digital platform was launched to bring the company's offerings together into one ecosystem.
Wabash National is headquartered in Lafayette, Indiana, which is also the site of its main production facility.
Additional manufacturing plants are located in Harrison, Arkansas; Kansas City, Kansas; Portland, Oregon; New Lisbon, Wisconsin; Fond du Lac, Wisconsin; and San Jose Iturbide, Guanajuato, Mexico.
By 1986, the company was able to purchase a more suitable facility in Lafayette for $2.5 million.
A second Lafayette facility was acquired in 1994, tripling its capacity.

In 2001, the business underwent a restructuring that resulted in the closure of assembly plants in Fort Madison, Iowa, and Scott County, Tennessee, as well as a parts distribution facility in Montebello, California.
Retail distribution sites in Little Rock, Tampa, and Fresno were also shut down during the 2001 restructuring.
In 2010, the company closed its Anna, Illinois facility and consolidated its Transcraft operations in Cadiz, Kentucky.
The company opened a manufacturing facility in Little Falls, Minnesota in 2017.
Starting in 2018, Wabash began opening up fitting facilities under the Supreme name, starting in Griffin, Georgia and Cleburne, Texas.
A third Supreme fitting location in Tampa, Florida opened in 2019.
In 2023, Wabash converted its Lafayette facility to manufacture dry van trailers.
By 2025, the company had service facilities in California, Florida, Georgia, Ohio, Pennsylvania, and Texas.
That year (2025), it opened additional locations in Gary, Indiana and Atlanta, Georgia.
Another location was opened in Phoenix in 2026.
In 2026, the company announced the closure of its manufacturing facilities in Goshen, Indiana and Little Falls, Minnesota.
The company operates a network of dealerships in North America called Wabash National Trailer Centers.
source_title: Wabash National - Wikipedia

facts: Wabash National Corporation announced a significant expansion of its network in the Southeast.
The company is strengthening customer access and service in the greater Atlanta region through three strategic growth moves.
One strategic move involves opening a new Parts & Service center in the Atlanta area.
A second strategic move involves expanding its dealer relationship with Fleetco.
A third strategic move involves adding CS Truck and Trailer to its Preferred Partner Network.
Dave Hill serves as vice president of Parts & Services at Wabash.
The expansions are designed to put parts, service, and support closer to customers operating across Georgia, Alabama, South Carolina, and Tennessee.
The new Atlanta-area Parts and Services center is strategically located at the heart of the Southeast’s trucking corridor.
The center provides faster access to parts, service, and truck body upfitting.
It will offer Wabash’s Ready-to-Mount (RTM) truck bodies for dry freight and platform applications with rapid turnaround times for in-stock chassis.
The Atlanta site joins existing Wabash service centers in California, Florida, Ohio, Pennsylvania, and Texas.
Additional service center locations are on the way.
Doors at the new Atlanta center opened September 18, 2025.
Fleetco, a long-standing Wabash dealer, will now represent Wabash in the greater Atlanta market with trailer sales.
This expansion broadens Wabash’s dealer footprint in the Southeast and gives customers additional access to new and used equipment through a trusted partner.
Bill Tirrill serves as president at Fleetco Inc.
Fleetco has more than 14 years of experience working closely with Wabash.
The partnership allows Fleetco to expand its sales force across the region and provide immediate access to new and used trailers.
Wabash added three CS Truck and Trailer locations to its Preferred Partner Network in Georgia.
This addition brings expert service, maintenance, and Wabash Genuine Parts to more customers through authorized third-party dealerships.
The Preferred Partner Network model is central to Wabash’s strategy of making high-quality parts more accessible through a seamless national network.
Expanding the dealer and partner network is critical to delivering the speed and service customers expect as the customer base grows.
Wabash combines physical and digital technologies to deliver innovative, end-to-end solutions that optimize supply chains across transportation, logistics, and infrastructure markets.
The company is headquartered in Lafayette, Indiana.

Wabash designs, manufactures, and services an extensive range of products supporting first-to-final mile operations, including dry and refrigerated trailers and truck bodies, platform trailers, tank trailers, and structural composites.

Through the Wabash Marketplace and Wabash Parts, customers gain access to a nationwide parts and service network, Trailers as a Service (TaaS)℠, and advanced tools designed to streamline operations and drive growth.
source_title: Wabash National Corporation - Wabash Expands Southeast Footprint to Strengthen Customer Access and Service

facts: Management identified Q1 2026 as the cyclical low point, characterized by uneven order patterns and cautious customer capital decisions across the freight market.
Performance was primarily driven by lower-than-planned production volumes, which pressured operating efficiency and led to negative gross margins.
The company is executing a strategic pivot toward parts, services, and digital enablement to reduce cyclicality and build recurring revenue streams.
A 19% sequential increase in backlog to $837 million is cited as a historic high for a first quarter, signaling improving visibility into a broader market recovery.
Strategic capacity expansion at the Lafayette South plant provides the capability to produce 10,000 incremental trailers compared to previous upcycles without immediate large-scale hiring.
The truck body segment is experiencing a delayed downturn compared to dry vans, with recovery now expected to trail the trailer market by six to nine months.
Guidance for Q2 2026 assumes sequential revenue improvement to $380 million–$400 million, though adjusted EPS is expected to remain negative.
Management anticipates a constructive 2027 as spot rates, contract rates, and capacity contraction align to drive replacement demand and fleet expansion.
The company expects to achieve positive adjusted EBITDA for the full year 2026 as operational improvements and volume recovery progress through subsequent quarters.
Strategic targets include capturing over 25% of dry van market share during the first half of the upcoming growth cycle through digital differentiation and manufacturing efficiency.
New upfit site openings in Chicago, Atlanta, and Phoenix are projected to generate $10 million to $20 million in incremental revenue per site with gross margins approaching 20% at peak utilization.
Recognized $3 million in costs during Q1 related to the previously announced idling of facilities in Little Falls and Goshen to rationalize fixed costs.
Management expects meaningful relief and pricing stability in late 2026 and 2027 resulting from Section 232 tariffs and pending antidumping/countervailing duty rulings.
The company intends to address its existing ABL facility before September 2026 to maintain financial flexibility and liquidity ahead of the facility turning current.
Development of a repositioned refrigerated van product continues with low-level capital purchases for long lead-time items to support future cycle participation.
Management confirmed they have complete visibility on the backlog required to meet the Q2 revenue guidance range.
The 19% sequential growth in backlog provides a foundation for the expected quarter-over-quarter improvement.
Recovery in truck bodies is heavily dependent on discretionary spending and the housing market, which drives demand for rental fleets and vocational units.
Management is specifically monitoring discretionary spending and the housing market for recovery, noting that current movement in manufacturing must continue and hold to support a 2027 rebound.
Current installed capacity and existing shifts are sufficient to meet initial demand increases without significant immediate hiring.
Additional hiring will be deferred until the latter half of 2027 when the company expects to add shifts to meet accelerating demand.
source_title: Wabash National Corporation Q1 2026 Earnings Call Summary

facts: Wabash National Corp. is based in Lafayette.
Kevin Page has been promoted to senior vice president of customer value creation at Wabash National Corp.
Kevin Page most recently served as senior vice president and group president of Diversified Products Group and Final Mile Products.
Kevin Page joined Wabash National Corp. in 2017.
Prior to Wabash National, Kevin Page was interim president of Truck Accessories Group LLC from 2015 to 2016.
From 2012 to 2015, Kevin Page served as vice president of sales, marketing and business development at Truck Accessories Group LLC.
Kevin Page served as president of Universal Trailer Cargo Group from 2008 to 2012.
Kevin Page had a long career at Utilimaster Corp., serving in various sales roles including vice president of sales and marketing.
Dustin Smith has been named senior vice president of global operations at Wabash National Corp.
Dustin Smith previously served as senior vice president and group president of commercial trailer products at Wabash National.
Dustin Smith joined Wabash National in 2007.

At Wabash National, Dustin Smith held positions including director of finance of commercial trailer products, vice president of manufacturing – commercial trailer products, and senior vice president and general manager – commercial trailer products.
Prior to Wabash National, Dustin Smith held various positions at Ford Motor Co. across product development and manufacturing divisions from 2000 to 2007.
source_title: Wabash National Promotes Two – Inside INdiana Business

facts: Wabash National Corp. is ramping down traditional refrigerated capacity and converting floor space to add 20% more dry vans by 2023, counting on freight demand lasting well beyond the current peak.
CEO Brent Yeagy stated on the company's Q2 call that demand for dry van production has exceeded physical capacity.
Building dry vans to address demand spikes in 2018 and 2019 required a lot of overtime and weekend work, yet customers still wanted more than Wabash could manufacture.
The capacity expansion adds 10,000 dry vans, representing a 5% increase in industry capacity but 20% more dry vans than Wabash currently produces.
CEO Brent Yeagy noted that 10 years of continued growth in overall trailer demand makes it time for Wabash National to capitalize on this profitable opportunity.
Wabash will use more lightweight molded structural composite (MSC) technology across its portfolio, including dry and refrigerated vans.
Additional MSC for refrigerated van assembly capacity will follow in coming quarters, Yeagy said.
Yeagy stated that conventional reefer design is the past and the company is all-in on commercializing the future, including working on zero-emission, solar-powered trailer refrigeration units.
The company has about 100 zero-emission, solar-powered trailer refrigeration units in production with eNow.
West Lafayette, Indiana-based Wabash is reorienting its business to focus on first-to-last-mile trailer products and exiting businesses that do not fit that plan.
The company sold the Extract Technology business, part of its Diversified Products unit, to Dietrich Engineering Consultants at the end of the second quarter.
Extract provides containment and aseptic systems for the pharmaceutical, health care, biotech, and chemical markets and was part of Wabash's 2012 acquisition of Walker Group Holdings.
Extract accounted for $22 million in Wabash revenue in 2020.
Yeagy stated that although Extract is an excellent business, the strategy is now squarely focused on the transportation, logistics and distribution industries.
Wabash previously divested a fuel tank trailer business and sold its last remaining Wabash branch location.
Wabash sold Garsite, an aviation refueling business, in 2019.
CFO Michael Pettit stated that non-core asset sales have raised a total of approximately $40 million and structured the portfolio to align with the growth strategy.
Higher retail sales and low business inventories prompted increased trailer production in Q2 when Wabash delivered 11,590 units.
Rising commodity costs and supply chain disruptions drove higher costs.
Yeagy said the company is having difficult but necessary conversations with customers about recovering cost increases throughout its backlog, which at $1.3 billion is 77% higher than a year ago.

Pettit noted that a shortage of workers and unplanned downtime by chassis manufacturers because of a global shortage of semiconductors required adjusting capacity lower, a situation expected to last the rest of the year.
Order books for 2021 have been closed for months and the 2022 order book is not yet fully open.
Second-quarter revenue was $449 million for a gross margin of 12.4% of sales, Pettit said.
Operating earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization was $35 million or 7.8% of sales, comparable to pre-pandemic margins.
Net income was $4.3 million or 24 cents per diluted share.
During the quarter, Wabash spent $30 million to reduce debt, $22 million to repurchase shares, $7 million for capital projects and $4 million to fund its quarterly dividend.
source_title: Wabash National Bets Big On Dry Vans, Increases Capacity By 20%

facts: Wabash National Corp. is expanding its Greater Lafayette presence with a new space in Purdue University’s Discovery Park District.
The company will open a 2,000-square-foot space in the Convergence Center for Innovation and Collaboration.

Dave Teeter, senior director of talent and organizational effectiveness for Wabash National, stated, “We are excited to expand our partnership with Purdue by opening this new space to enrich our collaboration with students, faculty and other Discovery Park businesses.”
Teeter noted that Purdue offers extraordinary talent that will help the company continue to grow in Greater Lafayette.
The new campus space will be used to engage with the Purdue community on co-sponsored projects, student outreach, career development, research, and innovation.
Wabash National joins a growing list of businesses opening spaces in Discovery Park District, which also will be home to novel 5G technologies.

The district is described as a 400-acre launchpad where businesses of all sizes can access a business-minded university, global talent pipelines, and advanced research and development facilities to solve business problems.
Wabash National specializes in the design and production of engineered solutions for the transportation, logistics, and distribution industries.

The company designs and manufactures dry freight and refrigerated trailers, platform trailers, tank trailers, dry and refrigerated truck bodies, structural composite panels and products, trailer aerodynamic solutions, and specialty food grade and pharmaceutical equipment.

Greg Deason, Purdue Research Foundation’s senior vice president for alliances and placemaking, stated that Wabash National joining the district opens new opportunities for impact with students, faculty, and other members of the Purdue innovation ecosystem.
Wabash National Corporation is publicly traded on the NYSE under the ticker symbol WNC.

The company’s products are sold under brand names including Wabash National, Benson, Brenner Tank, Bulk Tank International, DuraPlate, Extract Technology, Supreme, Transcraft, Walker Engineered Products, and Walker Transport.
source_title: Wabash National set to open collaboration space in Purdue’s Discovery Park District - Purdue University News

facts: Wabash National Corporation has announced a 10-year agreement with Hydro, a leader in the global aluminum and energy industry.

Under the agreement, Hydro will be a key supplier for Wabash National’s demand for dry van trailers, refrigerated trailers with molded structural composite (MSC) technology, flatbed trailers, and truck bodies through 2031.
The agreement also covers requirements of new products Wabash National may release through 2031.
The enhanced supply aligns with Wabash National’s recent announcement of additional dry van capacity set to come online at the beginning of 2023.
Future capacity for MSC Technology™ products is also planned.
Richard Mansilla, Vice President, Global Supply Chain at Wabash National, stated they selected Hydro as a strategic supplier partner due to shared values and vision for the industry.
The partnership aims to address aluminum supply needs in the near term to aid production increases of dry van trailers, refrigerated trailers, and truck bodies with MSC Technology.
In the longer term, Wabash National plans to work more closely with Hydro on innovation and footprint optimization.
Hydro has supplied Wabash National with aluminum extrusions for over 12 years prior to this agreement.
Hydro is a three-time Wabash National supplier award winner and was named Supplier of the Year in 2020.
Jerrod Hoeft, Hydro’s VP National Accounts, Transportation, expressed excitement about working more closely on sustainability and innovation.

Wabash National designs and manufactures dry freight and refrigerated trailers, platform trailers, tank trailers, dry and refrigerated truck bodies, structural composite panels and products, trailer aerodynamic solutions, and specialty food grade processing equipment.

Wabash National's innovative products are sold under brand names including Wabash National®, Benson®, Brenner® Tank, Bulk Tank International, DuraPlate®, Supreme®, Transcraft®, Walker Engineered Products, and Walker Transport.

The Meritor Tire Inflation System has been selected as the preferred automatic tire inflation system.
The company’s air disc brakes are optional in all Wabash configurations.
Meritor Trailing-Arm (MTA) suspension is optional in all Wabash configurations.
Hubs are optional in all Wabash configurations.
Drums and slack adjusters are optional in all Wabash configurations.
MTec6™ large-diameter axle is optional in all Wabash configurations.

The company announced a strategic supply agreement with The Manitowoc Co. Inc., a provider of lifting equipment, to deliver multiple drivetrain products, including planetary axles, to the manufacturer.
Meritor products will be featured on several crane types.
