facts: West Central region of Georgia is experiencing economic movement, sustainability projects, and initiatives propelling it forward.
Rusty Warner serves as executive director of the Sumter County Development Authority.
Americus Solar broke ground on the first phase of a $750 million project expected to become the largest solar farm east of the Mississippi River.
The three-phase Americus Solar project will produce nearly 1,850 megawatts of energy.
NanoPV opened a $36 million solar panel factory in the Ted Baldwin Business and Technology Park, located northeast of downtown Americus.
NanoPV's new factory will bring more than 500 jobs to the Ted Baldwin Business and Technology Park.
NanoPV utilizes patented technology for high-energy efficiency, fully certified solar panels expected to be 99% recyclable.
New Jersey-based NanoPV already has preorders from Asia and Africa.
NanoPV joined Sumter County in 2021.
Georgia ranks 10th nationally in solar installation with 3,260.4 megawatts, enough to power nearly 416,000 homes, according to the Solar Energy Industries Association.
South Georgia Technical College's alternative energy program and the state’s Quick Start program help Sumter County meet specialized employment needs for the solar industry.
In January, Canadian-based crypto mining operations company Quarry Dynamics (QDI) announced a move to Sumter County.
QDI's move represents a reportedly $10 million investment for a 40,500-square-foot Bitcoin mining facility capable of providing up to 19 megawatts of power using renewable energy initiatives.
Hardee Fresh announced plans to invest $42 million in a new 350,000-square-foot facility later this year (2022).
The Hardee Fresh facility will employ at least 84 people in the production of indoor, organic, pesticide-free, USDA-certified vegetables.
The Sumter County facility will be known as Americus Fresh.
Muscogee County is one of the top 10 most populous counties in Georgia.
Uptown Columbus reports more than 30 major projects totaling nearly $275 million completed, underway, or announced since 2013.
Ed Wolverton serves as president and CEO at Uptown Columbus and Columbus Business Improvement District.
A $26 million project in 2013 transformed an industrial river focused on textile power generation into one adding outdoor recreation as a new economic driver for Uptown.
The Chattahoochee River flow changes will host the 2022 World Cup and the 2023 World Championships in freestyle kayaking, only the second time these events are held in the United States.
Three new hotel openings occurred in Uptown Columbus in 2021, almost doubling the hotel inventory.
City Mills is a $12 million transformation of a historic riverside mill into a 64-room luxury boutique hotel adding a riverfront lawn, event space, and restaurant opening in spring 2022.
A Hampton Inn was under construction in Uptown Columbus at the time of publication.

In October 2020, the Environmental Protection Agency awarded a $300,000 Brownfield Assessment Grant to the City of Columbus for community engagement, site assessments, and cleanup planning for the Mill District.
The Mill District cleanup project leveraged approximately $26.2 million in public and private investment for redevelopment.
About 42 acres of the Mill District became ready for reuse according to Terracon.
Terracon is a consulting engineering firm that worked with the City of Columbus on the Mill District cleanup and served as the project's environmental consultant.
Jerald Mitchell serves as CEO of the Greater Columbus Georgia Chamber of Commerce.
Highside Market is an adaptive reuse mixed-use development by The Cotton Companies in Columbus opening this summer (2022).
Highside Market will offer 20,000 square feet of retail, 20,000 square feet of restaurants, 15,000 square feet of office space, event and green space, and outdoor lounge areas.
Two empty buildings in Greenville, Meriwether County’s seat, found new life in 2021, translating into approximately 35 to 40 jobs this year.
Fellers Holdings bought and renovated the two Greenville buildings for custom cabinet production and distribution.
Jane Fryer serves as executive director of the Meriwether County Development Authority.
Remington Firearms will relocate its global base of operations to LaGrange, marking the 30th headquarters move to Georgia in the last five years.
America’s oldest firearms manufacturer will invest $100 million and create 856 jobs over a five-year period in Troup County.
The Remington Firearms relocation includes opening a new advanced manufacturing operation and a world-class research and development center.
Scott Malone serves as president of the Development Authority of LaGrange.
Meriwether Park is an industrial park located in northern Meriwether County that has become a popular destination for automobile suppliers.
Companies at Meriwether Park manufacture door frames, electronic steering, brake pads, and many other parts for General Motors, Ford, Honda, Tesla, Kia, and other production plants.
A significant portion of the companies located in Meriwether Park are based in South Korea.
KB AutoSys Manufacturing will add 180 jobs to the existing 800 Korean automotive industry jobs with its first U.S. advanced manufacturing facility at Meriwether Park.

Global brake pad and lining supplier KB AutoSys is infusing $38 million of investment in Meriwether County via 108,000 square feet of new construction scheduled for completion in the second half of 2022.
KIA Georgia’s production facility is located in West Point, spanning both Troup and Harris counties, and accounts for almost 40% of the automaker’s sales in the United States.
Meghan Duke serves as economic development director for the city of West Point.
Hyundai TRANSYS Georgia Seating System is infusing $9 million into Troup County with an expansion creating at least 150 more jobs in West Point.
Glovis Georgia is building a 300,000-square-foot light assembly facility to support its logistics operations.
The Glovis Georgia project involves a $34 million capital investment that will create 70 new jobs in Troup County this year.
Carolyn McKinley heads both Meriwether County’s chamber of commerce and tourism, and co-chairs the “Get Reading” campaign leveraging arts to promote literacy.

Dan Gilbert has over 40 years in the whitewater rafting business, served as a venue advisor for the 1996 Olympics, brought the kayaking World Cup and World Championships to Columbus, and his two sons have joined the family business Whitewater Express.
source_title: West Central: Enduring Action, Economic Edge - Georgia Trend Magazine

facts: Grand opening of Haering Precision USA LP in Lavonia, Georgia.

Inauguration events kick off Friday, November 04 with Future Day for local high school students and culminate Saturday, November 05 with official inauguration ceremony and community event from 11am to 6pm.
Over 60 years ago, Anton Häring founded the precision engineering plant in Bubsheim, Germany, starting with no more than 2 automatic cut-off machines in his parents' barn.
The company currently employs about 4,400 people at 5 locations on 4 continents.
The American location is a major milestone for the Häring Group.
President Dr.-Ing. Jürgen Häring explains the company's future plans and looks forward to the Grand Opening ceremony.
Around 400 regional students will participate in the Future Day school trip.
Students compete in a team challenge for a trip to Six Flags Theme Park, answer company questions, and collect points at stations.
The company aims to be remembered by young recruits as an attractive employer and highlight new opportunities.
The company cooperates with schools and universities, providing dual training and apprenticeships where students split time between classroom and company.
A highlight is the Global Rotational Training Program with headquarters in Germany for international experience.
Modern workplace design with state-of-the-art machinery is cited as an important criterion for a solid career start.
Events allow students and community to tour the manufacturing facility premises with infopoints spread across the site.
Community Event activities include a human foosball table, go-kart course, and international food market featuring products from all five continents where Häring has locations.
Guests can win prizes by visiting infopoints, answering quiz questions, or participating in a social media photo challenge.
President Dr.-Ing. Jürgen Häring states the ambition to put the production plant independently in the hands of American management personnel.
The company needs committed local individuals with roots in the region or beyond for professional challenges.
Miriam Häring, President of the Häring Group, explains the recruitment strategy and training programs.
source_title: News | Anton Häring KG

facts: Haering Precision USA LP received a High-Demand Apprenticeship Program Award.
The award underscores the company's commitment to workforce development and highlights the success of its partnership with North Georgia Technical College.
The High-Demand Apprenticeship Program Award supports businesses that are actively investing in upskilling workers.
These programs address workforce shortages in high-demand industries such as manufacturing through hands-on training and technical education.
Haering Precision USA LP's CNC Machinist Apprenticeship Program combines classroom instruction at North Georgia Technical College with practical, on-the-job training at its Lavonia facility.
The integrated apprenticeship approach ensures trainees acquire both technical expertise and real-world skills needed to excel in precision manufacturing.

Dustin Chambers, Commercial Plant Manager in Lavonia, stated the recognition reflects the company's dedication to developing the next generation of skilled professionals in precision manufacturing and showcases the vital role of partnerships with educational institutions like North Georgia Technical College.

Tim Bennett, Academy Manager in Lavonia, highlighted that such programs equip apprentices with the skills and knowledge they need to thrive in high-demand careers while meeting the needs of local industries.
The award strengthens the company's resolve to continue addressing workforce challenges, enhancing Georgia’s talent pipeline, and contributing to the economic growth of the region.
source_title: News | Anton Häring KG

facts: Haering Precision USA LP hosted its inaugural Skills Invitational in collaboration with North Georgia Technical College.

The event aimed to ignite interest in machining and metals jobs among high school students while providing them with a platform to explore diverse career opportunities within the manufacturing industry.
Participants from local high schools teamed up with senior precision machining students from North Georgia Tech in a proam style contest.
The contest engaged participants in challenges designed to test their technical skills, creativity, and teamwork.
Students faced a series of hands-on challenges carefully crafted to simulate real-world scenarios encountered in precision machining and manufacturing throughout the day-long event.
Challenges included interpreting technical drawings and programming CNC machines.
Judges evaluated teams based on criteria such as accuracy, efficiency, innovation, and collaborative problem-solving.
Winners received various prizes, including tools and paid internship opportunities at Haering Precision USA LP's US facility.
Franklin County High School senior Logan Turner took home first place in the contest.
The event recognized contributions from participating high schools, students, faculty, and industry partners from Franklin, Hart, Stephens, and Union counties.
Haering Precision USA LP plans to expand opportunities for students to explore and pursue careers in precision machining and manufacturing following the success of the inaugural event.
source_title: News | Anton Häring KG

Shipment counts from No 36 Jingling were: 0 in 2014-2019, 1 in 2020-2021, and 0 in 2022-2025.
source_title: HEXPOL COMPOUNDING supplier list

facts: HEXPOL has signed an agreement to acquire 80 percent of the shares in Piedmont Resin Supply, LLC from the founder Mr. Matt Griffith and Mr. Paul Daniel.
Piedmont was founded in 2013 and has grown to become one of the largest independent nylon compounders in the US.
The company is an industry leader in technical nylon compounds on the US market.
Piedmont services a variety of customers within automotive, transportation and the furniture industries.
Piedmont operates a well invested manufacturing facility in Cartersville, GA.
The Cartersville, GA facility has some 60 employees and further capacity to grow.
The company has a turnover of some 60 MUSD and a profitability on a similar level to the HEXPOL Group.

Founders Matt Griffith and Paul Daniel stated that the merger is a perfect fit between two successful companies with similar cultures and future ambitions, bringing a portfolio of superior nylon compounds and strong market presence.
Jan Wikström, President HEXPOL Thermoplastic Compounding, stated that the acquisition adds new capabilities, application knowhow and a new customer base to HEXPOL Thermoplastic Compounding in the US.

Klas Dahlberg, President and CEO, HEXPOL Group, stated that Piedmont has a diverse product portfolio and is a leading independent nylon compounder, and with this acquisition they broaden the capabilities of the HEXPOL Group on the US market.
The acquisition price amounts to 86 MUSD on a cash and debt free basis.
The acquisition is funded by a combination of bank facilities and cash.
The closing took place on October 31, 2024.
Pursuant to the agreement, HEXPOL has an option to acquire the remaining shares in Piedmont.
The founders have an option to sell their remaining shares to HEXPOL.
source_title: HEXPOL acquires 80 percent of the shares in Piedmont Resin Supply LLC, a leading US Nylon compounder - Inderes

canonical_name: Top 7 Rubber Compound Manufacturers Reshaping the Industry Dynamics
facts: Rubber products are prevalently used across construction, footwear, and automotive.
Business Research Insights estimates the market for rubber compound to reach USD 9,545.6 million by 2026.
Phoenix Compounding Technology GmbH is a Germany-based company incorporated in 2001 and headquartered in Hamburg.
Phoenix Compounding Technology GmbH deals in synthetic rubbers, rubber compounds, testing systems, and inquiry compounds.
The company claims it has more than 2,000 formulas for rubber compounds and uses state-of-the-art production methods and quality controls.
It reportedly manufactures products on 27 mixing lines in Germany, China, Hungary, Mexico, and the U.S.
Cooper Standard was founded in 1960 and is a leading materials science and manufacturing expert headquartered in Michigan, U.S.
The company employs around 25,000 people globally and functions in 21 countries.
Cooper Standard develops components, systems, and materials for a host of diverse markets.

Cooper Standard has manufacturing locations in Surgoinsville, Tennessee; New Lexington, Ohio; and Ontario, Canada, among 107 manufacturing locations globally and 32 other manufacturing locations in North America.
American Phoenix Inc. is a privately-held company established in 1992 that serves customers in the U.S.
American Phoenix offers rubber mixing services, including a supply of runner compounds, and provides pre-weighted chemical packaging services for the rubber sector.
The company has more than 400 employees with seven companies in the American Phoenix corporate family.
HEXPOL Compounding is a Sweden-based company reportedly established in 1893 and is one of the leading suppliers in manufacturing and developing high-quality advanced polymer compounds.
In July 2019, Hexpol announced buying Preferred Compounding in a USD 232 million deal.
Preferred Compounding has six factories, one in Mexico and five in the U.S.
TSRC was established in 1973 in Kaohsiung, Taiwan, by founder Mr. Glyn T.H. Ing.
The Taiwan-headquartered company claims to be a leader in synthetic rubber and Styrenic Block Copolymer (SBC).
TSRC is bolstering its business to boost commercial teams and production bases in the U.S., India, Vietnam, Thailand, and China (Nantong in Jiangsu and Song-Jiang in Shanghai).
Dongguan New Orient Technology was founded in 2004 and manufactures and distributes plastic and rubber products.
The China-based company produces high-temperature silicone rubbers, sealed silicone rubbers, mixing silicone rubbers, and accessory materials.
The company’s annual production capacity of raw rubber is around 30,000 tons.
Shin-Etsu Chemical is a chemical manufacturer headquartered in Tokyo, Japan, established in 1926.
Shin-Etsu Chemical has manufacturing bases in 19 countries with 93 overseas sales bases.
The company has upped investments in the Internet of Things (IoT), Artificial Intelligence (AI), energy conservation, and renewable energy.
HNBR, a hydrogenated version of NBR (nitrile) rubber, is sought-after in industrial, automotive, food, pharma, oil & gas industries, and medical applications.
Major upsides for HNBR include high mechanical strength, tremendous media resistance, enhanced wear resistance, and tremendous heat & oxidation stability.
source_title: Top 7 Rubber Compound Manufacturers Reshaping the Industry Dynamics

facts: Tracy Garrison appointed as new Chief Executive Officer of GEON Performance Solutions.
Appointment effective January 2020.
Mr. Garrison will assume the role held by interim CEO David Mezzanotte, a Director of SK Capital Partners.
Jack Norris, Managing Director of SK Capital, stated that Tracy Garrison has a record of driving growth at businesses he has led.
Most recently, Mr. Garrison served as President and Chief Executive Officer of Hexpol Compounding Americas.
Hexpol Compounding Americas operates 20 factories in the United States and Mexico.
Hexpol Compounding Americas employs more than 2000 associates.
Previously, Mr. Garrison spent five years at Rhein Chemie Corporation leading their commercial organization as Vice President of Sales and Marketing.
Mr. Garrison held a Business Development Manager role at MA Hanna.
Mr. Garrison holds a B.S. in Chemistry from the University of Tennessee Martin.
Mr. Garrison began his career at Colonial Rubber Works.
Following Mr. Garrison's start, Mr. Mezzanotte will remain closely involved to support a successful transition and continue serving as a member of the Company’s Board of Directors.
GEON Performance Solutions is a global leader in the formulation, development, and manufacture of performance polymer solutions.
GEON combines highly adaptable vinyl and polyolefin polymer technologies with a full-service manufacturing business.
GEON serves markets including appliances, building & construction, electronics, healthcare, transportation, wire & cable, and more.
GEON Performance Solutions has 1,100 global associates.
GEON Performance Solutions operates 12 world class manufacturing plants.
GEON Performance Solutions headquarters is located in a western suburb of Cleveland, Ohio.
GEON Performance Solutions is a portfolio company of SK Capital Partners.
Hexpol Compounding Americas is the leading subsidiary of publicly-traded Hexpol AB with strong global market positions in rubber and advanced polymer compounding.
source_title: GEON® Performance Solutions Announces New Chief Executive Officer

facts: Last week, Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Hyundai Industrial Co., Ltd., a global auto parts supplier, will create 100 new jobs and invest more than $24 million in a new manufacturing facility near Newnan in Coweta County.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that partners like Kia and Hyundai are attracting a large network of suppliers, creating jobs and opportunity for hardworking Georgians across the entire state.
Suppliers have now committed to bringing over 5,300 jobs to Georgia, mostly to communities outside of large urban areas, since last year's Hyundai Metaplant announcement.
Hyundai Industrial was established in 1978 and is headquartered in Ulsan, Korea.
The company specializes in manufacturing car seats, arm rests, and head rests.

Mr. Woosuck Kim, Team Leader at HDI America Inc., stated that Hyundai Industrial has more than four decades of experience providing comfortable and safe experiences for drivers and passengers with some of the world’s biggest automotive brands.

Hyundai Industrial expressed excitement to be part of the e-mobility transition happening in Georgia and look forward to working with Coweta County to create mutually beneficial growth for the community and future employees.
Hyundai Industrial has purchased an existing building at 83 Amlajack Way in Coweta County.
The purchased building will be retrofitted to produce arm and head rests for OEMs, including Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
Hyundai Industrial will focus on filling manufacturing production roles.
Job openings will be posted on Indeed or an online portal when available.
Coweta County Board of Commissioners Chairman John Reidelbach welcomed Hyundai Industrial to Coweta County, noting it joins the area's thriving manufacturing industries.
The new facility will have close proximity to I-85, making transport to nearby automobile plants effortless.
Director of Korean Investment Yoonie Kim represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project was completed in partnership with the Coweta County Development Authority and Georgia EMC.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson noted that with Kia located in West Georgia and Hyundai on the eastern coast, suppliers like Hyundai Industrial can locate almost anywhere in the state and remain central to at least two major manufacturers.
Commissioner Wilson highlighted that this relationship between Coweta County and Hyundai Industrial is a classic example of Georgia’s partnership approach to economic development.
Georgia has positioned itself as a leader in developing and harnessing emerging technologies for the evolving automotive and mobility industry.
Since 2020, Georgia has created more than $25.1 billion in investments and 29,000 jobs by pursuing the entire electric vehicle supply chain.
In fiscal year 2023, job creation in Georgia's automotive industry increased by 324 percent compared to fiscal year 2021.
Suppliers attracted by Georgia’s mobility original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) resulted in over $2 billion in investment across the state in FY23.
source_title: Hyundai, Kia supplier to invest in manufacturing facility in Coweta  - Grice Connect

canonical_name: Hyundai-LG Energy Solution Joint Venture Battery Plant
facts: A chartered plane is set to leave South Korea on Wednesday to retrieve hundreds of South Korean nationals detained in an immigration raid on a Hyundai facility in Georgia.

About 300 South Korean nationals were among the 475 people detained Thursday in a raid by Homeland Security Investigations, Immigration and Customs Enforcement, and other federal officials on a construction site in Ellabell, Georgia.
The South Korean companies Hyundai and LG Energy Solution are jointly building a battery plant at the raided construction site.
The incident has strained relations between the U.S. and South Korea.
South Korea has pledged hundreds of billions in U.S. investment as part of tariff negotiations.
Three Japanese nationals were also among those detained, according to the government of Japan.
U.S. officials detained workers from Guatemala, Colombia, Chile, Mexico, Ecuador, and Venezuela at the site, according to Migrant Equity Southeast.
South Korean President Lee Jae Myung met with President Trump at the White House just weeks before the raid and expressed a profound sense of responsibility for the safety of South Koreans.
President Lee stated his government will move quickly through close consultations with the U.S. to advance reasonable institutional improvements so similar cases do not occur again.
A Korean Air spokesperson confirmed a chartered plane would leave for Atlanta on Wednesday to facilitate the return of detained South Korean nationals.
Seoul and Washington are conducting detailed conversations to arrange for the detained South Korean nationals to voluntarily return home as soon as possible.
South Korean Foreign Ministry spokesperson Lee Jae-woong stated the government is making its utmost effort to enable detainees to leave the U.S. by Wednesday local time.
Because of changes in time zone, the chartered plane will land in the U.S. the same day it leaves South Korea.
The South Korean foreign ministry said it was continuing to work with U.S. officials to address visa difficulties faced by Korean businesspeople.

Before the raid, the South Korean foreign ministry held dozens of meetings with members of the U.S. House and Senate, congressional staff, and officials from the U.S. Embassy in Seoul, the federal government, and academia regarding visa issues.

The South Korean foreign ministry emphasized that visas are essential for the short-term stay of Korean professionals needed for the initial operation of factories and for training local staff during U.S. expansion.

Sarah Park, president of the Korean American Coalition, stated that the Hyundai-LG plant required highly specialized subcontractors and technicians to move the project forward, but visas were not granted at the scale required.
Park noted that technicians entered the U.S. on short-term visitor visas to meet urgent needs, highlighting a failure to align immigrant policy with economic promises.
U.S. officials stated the detainees were working unlawfully and the raid resulted from a monthslong criminal investigation.
Hyundai, South Korea’s biggest automaker, stated that none of the detained individuals were its direct employees.
LG Energy Solution reported that 47 of its employees and about 250 workers from partner companies were detained at the site.
LG Energy Solution instructed its employees to suspend most U.S. business trips following the detention.
LG Energy Solution stated it is making active efforts to resolve visa issues to ensure smooth overseas assignments for its employees and partner companies.
LG Energy Solution clarified that its workers were at the factory temporarily to offer training and other support for construction, and that the site has been shut down since their detention.
Atlanta-based attorney Charles Kuck stated he is representing two detained South Korean nationals who entered the U.S. legally under the Visa Waiver Program, which allows stays of up to 90 days.
Kuck noted that agreeing to a voluntary departure would suggest a violation of the law and wants ICE to release the detainees with no charges to preserve their future visa eligibility.
Kuck is also representing three other detained individuals on B-1 visas who work in after-sales and service installation, stating they were doing exactly what the B-1 visa authorizes.
Kuck criticized Homeland Security Investigations for not doing proper homework before arresting everyone at the site.
source_title: South Korea readies plane to bring home workers detained in Hyundai immigration raid

facts: Georgia spent years wooing Hyundai, a foreign carmaker.
A Hyundai electric-car factory is located just outside Ellabell, Georgia.
On September 4th, federal agents arrested 475 foreign workers at a Hyundai electric-car factory just outside Ellabell, Georgia.
Over 400 agents fanned out across the plant, ordering workers to present their papers or be carted off.
Most of the people detained were South Korean nationals with the wrong kind of visas.
Homeland Security declared it the biggest worksite raid ever conducted in the department’s two-decade history.
source_title: The ICE raid at Hyundai was a massive own goal

facts: TSMC warned that first-quarter revenue could fall by up to 5% due to softer demand from a slowing global economy.
Annual capital expenditure for 2023 will be reduced to $32-36 billion, down from $36.3 billion in 2022.
Fourth-quarter profit increased by 78%, highlighting a sharp slowdown in the global technology sector grappling with inflation and rising interest rates.
CEO C.C. Wei forecasts the semiconductor cycle to bottom sometime in first half 2023 and sees a recovery in second half 2023.
The economic rebound is expected to be boosted by new product launches such as artificial intelligence-enabled goods.
Revenue is expected to fall by a mid- to high-single-digit percentage in the first half of 2023.
First-quarter revenue is expected to be in the $16.7 billion to $17.5 billion range, down from $17.57 billion a year ago.
TSMC produces some of the most advanced chips for high-end customers like Apple Inc.
The current quarter is likely to mark the company's first sales decline in four years.

Chief Financial Officer Wendell Huang stated that the fourth quarter was dampened by end-market demand softness and customers' inventory adjustment, adding that such conditions would continue into the first quarter.
TSMC is backed by billionaire Warren Buffett's investment conglomerate Berkshire Hathaway Inc.

The company has repeatedly stated that it will benefit from a mega-trend of demand for high-performance computing chips for 5G networks and data centers, as well as increased use of chips in gadgets and vehicles.
TSMC announced plans to expand production outside of Taiwan as various governments offer incentives to boost chip manufacturing in their respective countries.
At least one-fifth of its 28 nanometre (nm) and more advanced node capacity could be deployed overseas within five years or more.
TSMC began construction on a second chip factory in Arizona late last year, which will begin production in 2026 using advanced 3 nm technology.
The total investment for the Arizona project in the United States is $40 billion.
CEO Wei stated that TSMC was considering building a second fab in Japan.
TSMC was also considering building a speciality fab focused on the auto industry in Europe.
The company expects the auto chip shortage to be relaxed quickly.
TSMC reported a record net profit of T$295.9 billion ($9.72 billion) from October to December, up from T$166.2 billion the previous year.
Fourth-quarter revenue increased 26.7% to $19.93 billion, exceeding the previous forecast range of $19.9 billion to $20.7 billion.
The share price of TSMC fell 27.1% in 2022 but is up 8.5% this year, giving the company a market value of $412.78 billion.
source_title: Weakening Chip Demand Prompts TSMC To Reduce 2023 Capex Even After A Record Q4

canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Company and General Motors Strategic Collaboration
facts: Hyundai Motor Company and General Motors confirmed plans to co-develop five new vehicles under their ongoing strategic collaboration.
The collaboration includes a jointly developed electric commercial van aimed at the North American market, expected to launch as early as 2028.
The remaining four co-developed vehicles will feature either internal combustion or hybrid drivetrains.
The jointly developed electric commercial van will be manufactured in the U.S. as early as 2028.
Hyundai will take the lead on the development of the co-developed vehicles.
GM refers to the jointly developed electric van as a smaller sibling to Chevrolet’s BrightDrop vans.
In April, GM paused production of Chevrolet’s BrightDrop vans at its Canadian plant for six month due to weak demand.

The collaboration will yield four flexible-platform vehicles for Central and South American markets, including a compact SUV, a car and pickup, and a mid-size pickup, capable of supporting internal combustion or hybrid powertrains.
The four flexible-platform vehicles for Central and South American markets are scheduled to reach market in 2028, with development efforts currently in progress.
Hyundai and GM will share platforms for all five co-developed vehicles while developing unique interiors and exteriors consistent with their respective brands.
Hyundai and GM expect to surpass combined annual sales of 800,000 units once full-scale production is achieved.
The two manufacturers are aligning operations across their supply chains and sustainability initiatives.
Joint sourcing programs are planned across North and South America for materials, transport, logistics, and complex systems.
The companies will explore using low-carbon emissions steel to support decarbonized manufacturing processes.
The announcement follows a framework agreement signed between GM and Hyundai in September 2024.
At the time of the September 2024 framework agreement, GM and Hyundai indicated potential collaboration on development and production, as well as joint procurement of battery materials and steel.
José Muñoz, President and CEO of Hyundai Motor Company, stated that the strategic collaboration will help deliver value and choice to customers across multiple vehicle segments and markets.
Muñoz noted that combined scale in North and South America will help efficiently provide customers with beautifully designed, high-quality, safety-focused vehicles with appreciated technology.
Shilpan Amin, GM Senior Vice President and Global Chief Procurement and Supply Chain Officer, highlighted the market relevance of the upcoming models.
Amin stated that partnering together will allow GM and Hyundai to bring more choice to customers faster and at a lower cost.
Amin noted that the first co-developed vehicles demonstrate how GM and Hyundai will leverage complementary strengths and combined scale.
source_title: Hyundai and GM to launch jointly-developed EV in 2028 - electrive.com

facts: The Hyundai Assembly Plant is located in Ramgram, Nawalparasi.
In 2024, Laxmi Motor Corporation (LMC), a subsidiary of Laxmi Group and the authorized distributor for Hyundai, inaugurated the nation’s first sophisticated passenger car assembly plant.
In just two years, the facility has rolled out nearly 2,000 vehicles.
The plant currently focuses on producing two of Nepal’s most popular SUVs: the Hyundai Venue and the Hyundai Creta.
Demand for the Creta has outpaced the Venue, with production numbers for the Creta nearly doubling.
By reducing import duties on fully built units (CBU), the Hyundai Venue price in Nepal dropped by approximately 400,000 NPR during its initial local launch phase.
The plant utilizes semi-robotic equipment and adheres to the global quality standards of Hyundai Motor Company (HMC), Korea.
The plant is operated by a 100% Nepalese workforce.
Engineers and technicians, previously limited to sales and service roles, are now experts in precision quality assurance and process engineering.
By assembling vehicles locally, Nepal is retaining internal resources and reducing the trade deficit.
The success of Hyundai paved the way for potential “backward integration” involving the future production of local components like batteries, tires, and seats.
As of March 2026, the “Made in Nepal” variants offer competitive pricing compared to their imported counterparts.
The Hyundai Venue E (1.2L Petrol) variant is priced from 40.96 Lakh NPR.
The Hyundai Venue SX variant is priced from 53.56 Lakh NPR.
The Hyundai Creta 1.5 E MT (Local) variant is priced from 55.56 Lakh NPR.
The Hyundai Creta 1.5 SX(O) IVT (Local) variant is priced from 80.56 Lakh NPR.
With the government’s commitment to industrialization and the private sector’s proven capability, Nepal is positioning itself as a budding hub for automobile assembly in South Asia.
Hyundai continues to expand its local lineup with rumors of electric vehicle (EV) assembly on the horizon.
source_title: Hyundai’s Industrial Revolution Nepal From Importing Local

facts: Hyundai Motor Group is announcing a significant investment of USD 21 billion in the United States from 2025 to 2028.
The commitment reflects the Group’s strategic focus on expanding its manufacturing capabilities, advancing future technologies, and enhancing energy infrastructure in America.
This latest U.S. investment builds on the Group’s existing allocation of approximately USD 20.5 billion since entering the U.S. market in 1986.
Hyundai Motor Group stated it is deepening its partnership with the United States, reinforcing a shared vision for American industrial leadership, and expanding operations and the American workforce.

To reinforce production capabilities, the Group will invest a total of USD 9 billion to establish an annual U.S. production capacity of 1.2 million vehicles across Hyundai Motor, Kia, and Genesis brands.
The Group plans to invest in improving its production facilities, including Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama and Kia Autoland Georgia.

A total of USD 6 billion will be allocated to increase the localization rate of automotive components, including core EV parts like battery packs, to form an auto cluster following facility expansions.
The same USD 6 billion allocation covers strengthening Group logistics for robust supply chains and investing in U.S. steel production.
Hyundai Steel, the Group’s steel affiliate, will construct an Electric Arc Furnace (EAF) steel mill in Louisiana capable of producing 2.7 million tons of steel annually.
The Louisiana steel facility will produce low-carbon steel sheets using abundant U.S. steel scrap to enhance agility and flexibility against external uncertainties.

The Group will invest USD 6 billion to drive innovation and expand strategic partnerships with U.S. companies in autonomous driving, robotics, artificial intelligence (AI), and advanced air mobility (AAM).
Key initiatives include collaborating with Boston Dynamics to expand the U.S. ecosystem for robotics components and establish a mass-production system.
The Group is partnering with NVIDIA to accelerate AI solutions development for future mobility, including autonomous driving and robotics.
R&D will be advanced with Supernal, the Group’s U.S. affiliate for AAM business, to commercialize an eVTOL vehicle by 2028.
Hyundai Motor Company will supply robotaxis to Waymo as part of a strategic partnership and co-develop autonomous driving services with Aptiv.
The Group plans to invest in potential startups through venture capital and other funding mechanisms to support U.S. startups specializing in mobility, robotics, and AI.
As part of the USD 6 billion commitment, the Group will invest in energy infrastructure projects to secure new business opportunities and contribute to sustainable energy generation development.
Energy initiatives include strategic cooperation between Hyundai Engineering & Construction and Holtec International on Small Modular Reactor (SMR) technology.
The Group plans to establish infrastructure to bolster the use of renewable energy.
The Group is investing in the IONNA EV charging alliance to expand infrastructure.
Through these investments, the Group anticipates creating 14,000 new direct full-time jobs in the U.S. by 2028.
The overall economic impact is expected to generate more than 100,000 direct and indirect job opportunities across related industries.

The Group plans to hold its Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America Grand Opening celebration in Georgia later this week, marking the completion of the largest economic development project in Georgia’s history, just two and a half years after breaking ground.

HMGMA exemplifies HMG’s dedication to driving economic growth, furthering technological advancements, and delivering sustainable solutions to the U.S. market, creating or supporting over 570,000 jobs nationwide.
source_title: Hyundai to Invest $21B in US, Build Steel Plant to Supply Georgia Auto Plants - Rome CEO

facts: State environmental permits were issued for four Bulloch County wells expected to supply up to 6.6 million gallons of water for Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America and related development.
The Georgia Environmental Protection Division (EPD) approved the state environmental permits on Oct. 7.
Opponents have 30 days to file an administrative appeal against the state permits, with a deadline of Wednesday, Nov. 6.

The Ogeechee Riverkeeper organization (ORK) has threatened legal action over a separate federal environmental permit for the 2,500-acre electric vehicle and battery manufacturing site along Interstate 16 in Bryan County.
ORK stated it is "working on a number of potential avenues" related to the state's approval of the wells.
ORK spokeswoman Meaghan Gerard stated that the organization is actively evaluating the details of the permits and EPD's comment responses while considering next steps.
ORK claims that EPD and the State of Georgia cannot continue to allow industry to monopolize limited, pristine groundwater.
Attorney Don Stark stated that the ORK Board has authorized exploring all legal avenues for a state permit appeal, while acknowledging the associated time restrictions.
Georgia EPD issued the well permits one week after production commenced at the 16-million-square-foot facility.
Hyundai states the facility will eventually use up to 4 million gallons of water piped across the county line from the wells when manufacturing hits its peak of 300,000 vehicles per year.

Bryan and Bulloch counties were each granted two permits to withdraw water from the Floridan Aquifer, which covers 100,000 square miles beneath Florida and parts of Georgia, Alabama, and South Carolina.
The wells are located in Bulloch County because Bryan County has state restrictions on pumping to prevent saltwater intrusion into the aquifer near Hilton Head Island.
EPD states there is no risk of saltwater intrusion into the Floridan Aquifer in Bulloch County.
Professor Ryan Rowberry of Georgia State University College of Law stated that an administrative appeal of the state permits serves as a precursor to potential legal challenges.
Rowberry explained that the appeal process aims to determine if opponents and the agency can reach an agreement to avoid litigation.
The administrative appeal process is designed as a negotiation-style exercise to find common ground between opponents and the agency.
Rowberry noted that the administrative appeal process is typically faster, more flexible, and less costly than litigation.
EPD could decline to modify the permits, which might lead to a court challenge.
To avoid litigation, EPD could potentially agree to reduce the legal water withdrawal limits and the number of wells, or add conditions to the existing permits.
Experts state that the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers (USACE) could set its own extraction limits while revisiting a federal environmental permit for the $7.6 billion Hyundai project.
USACE announced in August that it is launching a reevaluation of a key federal permit in response to ORK's threatened legal challenge.
USACE noted that state and local development officials omitted the site's projected water use from their federal Clean Water Act permit application.
USACE later learned of the Hyundai complex's water demands through EPD's draft permits for the four wells.
ORK informed USACE in June that it intended to challenge the October 2022 approval of a permit that facilitated the project.
ORK accused USACE of not completing required steps and overlooking water supply concerns during the site's permitting process.
The Clean Water Act requires USACE to collect pertinent information and analyze permit requests impacting or disturbing "waters of the U.S.," including wetlands.
ORK challenged USACE's finding that it was reasonable to assume the Bryan County water supply was adequate and would not require water withdrawals or an EPD permit.

Applicants Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority Secretary Trip Tollison and Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson knew the project's water needs months before the permit approval.
The Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority stated it has provided USACE with details on projected well withdrawals and their impact on the aquifer.

As a condition for the state permits, EPD requires Bryan and Bulloch counties to create a fund to address potential significant impacts to private wells within a 5-mile radius of the new withdrawal sites near the Interstate 16-Georgia Highway 119 interchange.
EPD estimates the Floridan Aquifer depth will drop by up to 19 feet near the new wells, with private wells potentially declining by up to 15 feet within the withdrawal's "cone of influence."
EPD identified 13 drinking-water wells and three agricultural wells drawing from the aquifer within the mitigation zone, where the aquifer level is projected to drop between 8 and 13 feet.
Professor Rowberry noted that mitigation funds mandated by agencies like EPD are typically well-intentioned but often poorly administered.
Rowberry stated, "After we've mucked something up, we usually don't know how to mitigate it very well."
Stricter limits on well withdrawals are significant because no short-term alternative water supply is currently available for the facility.
EPD's well permits include a provision requiring Bryan and Bulloch counties to develop plans to replace aquifer withdrawals with alternative water sources within 25 years.
The alternative water supply plans are due within six months of EPD's permit approval.
ORK criticizes EPD for not mandating a faster transition away from aquifer withdrawals, noting that Savannah is already decreasing its aquifer reliance in favor of the Savannah River.

ORK spokeswoman Meaghan Gerard stated that area residents are being forced to rely more on treated surface water for drinking, while agricultural producers face water supply uncertainties and higher production costs.
Gerard criticized the 25-year timeline provided by EPD to find an alternative water supply as inadequate and insufficient.

State and local economic development officials negotiating Hyundai's Bryan County agreement are considering Savannah's Industrial and Domestic Water Treatment Plant as a future alternative water source.
A consultant informed the SEDA Board of Directors that connecting to the alternative water source would likely cost around $1 billion.

Trip Tollison, president and CEO of SEDA and secretary of the Joint Development Authority, stated that securing funding, acquiring rights-of-way, and designing/installing the alternative water system will take up to 25 years.
source_title: Appeal among 'potential avenues' in challenging Georgia permits for Hyundai wells

facts: US Department of Homeland Security officials detained hundreds of people, including alleged undocumented South Korean workers, during a raid on a Hyundai-LG battery plant being built in Georgia.
475 people were detained in the raid, with the majority from South Korea.
The immigration component of the raid came as part of a larger, months-long investigation into labour practices at the site.
South Korea’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs demanded the rights of its investors and citizens to be respected following the raid.
Foreign Ministry spokesperson Lee Jaewoong stated: “The business activities of our investors and the rights of our nationals must not be unjustly infringed in the process of US law enforcement.”
The episode highlights the disruptive effect President Donald Trump’s immigration crackdown is having on his efforts to attract foreign investment.
The Hyundai-LG plant represents the biggest foreign investment in the state of Georgia.
Since Trump returned to power in January, US Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) has been bolstered by record funding and new latitude to conduct raids.
President Trump wants to deport “the worst of the worst” criminals, but ICE figures show a rise in the detainment of non-criminals.

Trump shifted his administration’s approach to workplace raids, which business leaders warn could undermine industries relying heavily on undocumented and seasonal workers not easily replaced with domestic labour.
Pew Research Center cited preliminary Census Bureau data saying the US labour force lost more than 1.2 million immigrants from January through July of this year.

White House spokeswoman Abigail Jackson framed the raid as part of a wider enforcement campaign against undocumented foreign workers, stating: “Any foreign workers brought in for specific projects must enter the United States legally and with proper work authorisations.”
Jackson added that President Trump will continue enforcing federal immigration laws while delivering on his promise to make the US the best place to do business.
Steven Schrank, special agent in charge of Homeland Security investigations in Atlanta, said the operation “underscores our commitment to jobs for Georgians and Americans.”
Schrank described the raid as the “largest single-site enforcement operation” in the department’s history.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp touted the manufacturing site as the largest economic development project in the state’s history.
Hyundai Motor Group, South Korea’s biggest automaker, began manufacturing electric vehicles (EVs) a year ago at the $7.6bn plant.
The plant currently employs about 1,200 people.
Hyundai partnered with LG Energy Solution to build an adjacent battery plant, which is slated to open next year.
It remained unclear how many detainees were held for lacking proper documentation versus arrested for other violations.
Al Jazeera reporter Mike Hanna noted there was no clarity on what exactly would happen to those arrested during the operation.
A South Korean government official told Reuters that many of the detainees were being held at an ICE detention facility.

South Korea’s top diplomat Lee announced plans to dispatch diplomats from its embassy in Washington and consulate in Atlanta to the site, and to form an on-site response team centred on the local mission.
In July, Seoul pledged $350bn in US investment to ease tariff threats from Trump.
The US president hosted South Korean President Lee Jae Myung at the White House in August.
source_title: South Korea objects as US detains 475 at Hyundai plant raid in Georgia | Donald Trump News | Al Jazeera

facts: More than 300 South Koreans — 307 men and 10 women — were detained in the Sept. 4 raid on a Hyundai plant in Georgia.
One detainee decided to remain in the U.S. rather than agree to “voluntary departure.”
The 316 others will be on the flight to Seoul, along with 14 employees of South Korean companies from other Asian countries: 10 from China, three from Japan and one from Indonesia.
Workers were released from the Folkston ICE Processing Center in rural Georgia at 2:18 a.m. ET.
They were being driven several hours to Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport where a plane sent by the South Korean government is waiting for them.
The Korean Air plane is scheduled to leave Atlanta at noon ET and arrive in Seoul on Friday around 4 p.m. local time (3 a.m. ET).
South Korean officials originally hoped the plane would leave Atlanta as early as Wednesday afternoon local time, shortly after it arrived from Seoul.
The foreign ministry said its departure was likely to be delayed “due to circumstances on the U.S. side,” and an airport spokesperson later said the charter flight had been canceled.
At a meeting with Secretary of State Marco Rubio in Washington on Wednesday, South Korean Foreign Minister Cho Hyun learned that Trump had ordered the suspension of the repatriation process.

The suspension was to discuss whether detained nationals — all skilled workers helping set up an electric vehicle battery plant — should remain in the U.S. to continue their work rather than be sent home.

Cho told Rubio that it would be better for them to return home first and then re-enter the U.S. “since these individuals were already in shock and are exhausted,” a South Korean government official said.
The U.S. agreed to repatriate the South Koreans and Trump instructed they be transported to the airport without physical restraints such as handcuffs, departing from standard U.S. practice.

Images of South Koreans being shackled at the wrists and ankles have caused outrage in South Korea, a key U.S. ally that has pledged hundreds of billions in U.S. investment as part of tariff negotiations.
The raid came weeks after South Korean President Lee Jae Myung reinforced that commitment at a meeting with Trump at the White House.

A South Korean government official stated that Trump’s consideration stems from deep bonds formed between leaders at the successful South Korea-U.S. summit and their consistent position on the detention issue.
Lee said all but one of the 317 detained South Koreans had elected to fly home.
Lee noted at a news conference in Seoul marking his 100th day in office that each person was allowed to choose whether to go or stay.
The raid has raised concerns about potential impact on U.S.-South Korea relations, though Lee said his government had “not considered it to that depth yet.”

Lee warned the incident could make South Korean companies more hesitant to invest in the U.S., where they have long sent employees temporarily for training or assistance where U.S. expertise is lacking.
Lee stated that if restrictions continue, companies will worry about proceeding with setting up factories in the U.S. due to endless difficulties.
The South Korean nationals were among the 475 people federal and immigration agents arrested in the raid on a construction site in the town of Ellabell.
Hyundai and LG Energy Solution are jointly building an electric vehicle battery plant at the construction site.
U.S. officials described the raid as the largest single-site enforcement operation in the history of Homeland Security Investigations, a division of Immigration and Customs Enforcement.
No criminal charges have been filed against the detainees.
Of the detained South Koreans, 47 were employed by LG Energy Solution and the rest were hired by subcontractors.
Hyundai, South Korea’s largest automaker, said none of its direct employees were detained.

Most detained South Koreans were engineers or worked in after-sales services and installation, according to Atlanta-based immigration attorney Charles Kuck, who is representing at least seven of them.
The workers were in the U.S. under various visa programs that foreign nationals have long used to do business amid U.S. bureaucratic delays and murky regulations.
Sarah Park, president of the Korean American Coalition, said detained workers should not be blamed for challenges companies face securing proper U.S. visas for key facility staff.
James S. Kim, chairman of the American Chamber of Commerce in Korea, urged Americans to be sympathetic and work with South Korea to mitigate the incident and move forward.
Federal authorities executed a judicial search warrant at the Hyundai-LG plant regarding a criminal investigation into allegations of unlawful employment practices.
The warrant authorized agents to seize employment records, immigration documents, and ownership/management records related to the construction site.
Authorities were looking for four specific individuals, but the reasons for their interest remain under seal.
On Sunday, U.S. and South Korean government officials reached a deal to release workers from immigration custody and return them home.
Consulate officials met with detainees at the Folkston ICE Processing Center over the weekend, according to Meredyth Yoon, litigation director at Asian Americans Advancing Justice-Atlanta.
Asian Americans Advancing Justice-Atlanta has been assisting detainees and their families seeking legal help.
As part of the deal, most South Korean nationals agreed to “voluntary departure,” avoiding formal deportation orders.
About 175 remaining detainees from Guatemala, Colombia, Chile, Mexico, Ecuador and Venezuela remain in immigration detention.
source_title: What we know about the South Korean workers arrested in the Hyundai raid in Georgia

facts: The state of Georgia and local governments are on track to give $2.1 billion in tax breaks and other incentives to Hyundai Motor Group.
The South Korean automaker and a partner announced last month that it will invest an additional $2 billion at an electric vehicle complex it's building in Georgia.
Associated Press calculations show projected incentives will rise by more than $290 million from the $1.8 billion deal originally announced last year.
Only $2.75 million of that represents additional cash from the state.
The rest will come from increases in tax breaks.
The deal calls for Hyundai and battery maker LG Energy Solution to invest $7.6 billion in the Georgia plant.
The deal requires hiring 8,500 workers by the end of 2031.
This is up from the original job projection of 8,100 at the sprawling electric vehicle and battery complex being built in Ellabell, west of Savannah.
It's the largest economic development deal in Georgia history.
It comes with the largest incentive package.
State leaders say benefits to Georgia outweigh the incentives.
Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson said Hyundai is projected to have a direct payroll of $4.7 billion over the next 10 years.
The company has promised to pay workers a yearly average of $58,105, plus benefits.

Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that as they work together to deliver a state-of-the-art facility in Bryan County that will provide well-paying jobs to Georgians, Hyundai Motor Group will give back to the region by investing in schools, families, and communities.
Local officials released property tax projections to the AP on Tuesday.
The Hyundai package has already been described as the largest subsidy package a U.S. state has ever promised a single automotive plant.
That's according to Good Jobs First, a group skeptical of subsidies to private companies.
Local governments have agreed to abate property taxes on the assembly plant through 2048.
Local governments have agreed to abate property taxes on the battery plant through 2049.
During that time, Hyundai is projected to pay $523 million in taxes, while saving $669 million.
The state projects it will waive an additional $81.8 million in sales taxes on construction materials and machinery.
This brings Hyundai's savings from those exemptions to more than $478 million.
The company is also projected to receive an additional $10.5 million in state income tax credits, at $5,250 per job over five years, because of the increase in jobs.
That would bring Hyundai's state income tax savings to $223 million.
If Hyundai didn't owe that much state corporate income tax, Georgia would instead give the company personal income taxes collected from Hyundai workers.
The state will spend an additional $2.75 million to help fund construction, machinery and equipment.
This boosts the total funding for construction, machinery and equipment to nearly $53 million.
State and local governments will spend more than $112 million to buy and prepare 2,913 acres (1,179 hectares) for the plant.
State and local governments will spend $175 million on water and sewer facilities.
The state will spend $210 million on road construction and improvements.
The state will spend more than $153 million to recruit and train workers.
The deal requires Hyundai to pay back a portion of the incentives if the company falls below 80% of promised investment or employment.
Hyundai plans to start EV production in 2025.
Initially, Hyundai will make 300,000 vehicles per year.
Production may possibly expand to 500,000 a year.
Since the company announced its first U.S. plant solely dedicated to electric vehicles, suppliers have pledged to invest $2.2 billion.
Suppliers have also pledged to hire 5,300 people.
The announcements are part of an electric vehicle and battery land rush across the United States.
Under the U.S. Inflation Reduction Act, EVs must be assembled in North America to qualify for a full $7,500 EV tax credit.
A certain percentage of battery parts and minerals must come from North America or a U.S. free trade partner to qualify for the full $7,500 EV tax credit.
source_title: Higher investment means Hyundai could get $2.1 billion in aid to make electric cars in Georgia | Georgia Public Broadcasting

facts: Hyundai Mobis will build a second electric vehicle plant in Georgia.
Governor Brian Kemp announced the investment on Wednesday.
Kemp’s office said that Hyundai is investing $926 million in an Electric Vehicle Power Electric system facility in Bryan County.
The new facility will eventually employ 1,500 people.

Governor Brian Kemp stated that celebrating the groundbreaking of Hyundai’s new electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facility in Bryan County would unleash transformational job creation and investment in that entire region of the state.
Governor Kemp noted they announced a second supplier in just two weeks to locate in that area, highlighting the growing list of other job creators that will soon follow.
Construction on the new facility is expected to begin in January 2023.
Production at the new facility will start sometime in 2024.
In October, Hyundai Motor Group broke ground on its $5.5 billion EV manufacturing factory in Bryan County.
The $5.5 billion factory is the first in the U.S. for Hyundai.
Euisun Chung, executive chairman of Hyundai Motor Group, said that the manufacturing plant would be the envy of the industry.
The first factory is expected to open in 2025.
The first factory will employ 8,100 workers.
The first factory will produce up to 300,000 EVs per year.

Governor Kemp stated that record jobs and investment coming to all parts of the state, along with award-winning workforce development programs and infrastructure, are pushing the Peach State’s economy to new heights.
Governor Kemp said their partnership with Hyundai Motor Group and the groundbreaking of this innovative facility exemplifies unprecedented success in improving lives and livelihoods.
Governor Kemp expressed a long-term commitment to continuing the partnership and seeing its impact on both the community and the state as a whole.

facts: The announcement was made in Seoul on March 14th, 2024.
Hyundai Mobis announced the development of an “Integrated Front Face Module” that maintains traditional grille design while reducing air resistance and securing design diversity.
The module incorporates an aerodynamic system that reduces air resistance and improves electric consumption efficiency (ECE).
The integrated module features a LiDAR sensor that protrudes outward only during driving for design and sensor protection.
The module includes an automatic charger retrieval feature that retrieves the charger after charging is completed.
A system automatically opens and closes parts like the grille and hood to reduce air resistance during high-speed driving and improve ECE.
This combined aerodynamic system extends the electric vehicle’s range by approximately 20 km.
The technology allows for the intake of external air and exhaust of air after heat exchange, improving battery cooling efficiency and controlling airflow.
Hyundai Mobis differentiated itself by maintaining a traditional grille design while improving aerodynamic performance, unlike typical streamlined EV designs that eliminate grilles.
Integrating design elements such as LED lighting has enhanced marketability for the module.
The technology is particularly beneficial for SUV and CUV electric vehicles where lowering vehicle height is challenging.
Technologies prevent external contamination and impact on sensors by hiding the LiDAR inside the grille when not in use.
A semi-automatic charging system initiates upon plugging in the charger, then automatically retrieves the charger and closes the charging area’s cap/cover after complete charging.
This automatic charging feature reduces user inconvenience and offers better cost-effectiveness than charging robots.
Hyundai Mobis is reinventing itself as a ‘Mobility Solution Provider’ focusing on future mobility core technologies including electrification, autonomous driving, and IVI.
The company achieved USD 9.22 billion in orders from global automakers last year.
Hyundai Mobis is intensifying efforts to penetrate foreign markets and expand its order book as part of its long-term growth strategy.
source_title: Newsroom | Hyundai Mobis

facts: Employees recruited as TN visa holders by Total Employee Solution Support LLC, SPJ Connect Inc., or GB2G Inc. (d/b/a Allswell) were assigned to work at Mobis Alabama LLC d/b/a Hyundai Mobis or Kia Georgia Inc. at their West Point, Georgia locations.

Kia Georgia Inc., Mobis Alabama LLC d/b/a Hyundai Mobis, GB2G Inc. d/b/a Allswell, SPJ Connect Inc., and Youngjin Lee agreed to pay $11.5 million to settle a class action lawsuit alleging they misrepresented job terms and violated state and federal statutes affecting TN visa workers at their Georgia facilities.
The class includes approximately 614 individuals, including the named plaintiffs.
Class members are divided into four groups based on days worked: Group 1 (1-60 days), Group 2 (61-120 days), Group 3 (121-400 days), and Group 4 (more than 400 days).
Under the FLSA settlement, each eligible class member receives $30 per week worked at Hyundai Mobis or Kia Georgia between Aug. 11, 2019, and the present.
The average class member worked 54 weeks, resulting in an average payment of $1,672.61.
Half of the FLSA settlement payment is reported as W-2 wages with tax withholding, and half is reported as 1099 liquidated damages with no withholding.
Group 1 class members receive $4,112.50 for working 1-60 days under the Rule 23 settlement.
Group 2 class members receive $8,000 for working 61-120 days under the Rule 23 settlement.
Group 3 class members receive $16,800 for working 121-400 days under the Rule 23 settlement, minus $2,500 if assigned after Dec. 31, 2023.
Group 4 class members receive $11,100 for working more than 400 days under the Rule 23 settlement, minus $2,500 for 100 members assigned after Dec. 31, 2023.
All Rule 23 settlement payments are reported as IRS Form 1099 income.
The defendants denied all wrongdoing but agreed to settle to avoid the uncertainty and expense of continued litigation.

The class action lawsuit alleged that Kia Georgia Inc., Mobis Alabama LLC d/b/a Hyundai Mobis, GB2G Inc. d/b/a Allswell, SPJ Connect Inc., and Youngjin Lee misrepresented job terms and wages to TN visa holders recruited to work at their West Point, Georgia facilities.
Plaintiffs claimed the defendants violated federal and Georgia RICO statutes and federal wage laws under the Fair Labor Standards Act.
The $11,500,000 settlement fund includes $3,450,000 allocated for attorneys' fees and costs, with the remainder going to eligible class members.
Settlement administration costs are not specified in the agreement.
Class members must submit a settlement distribution form online or via mail/email to the settlement administrator.
The settlement administrator is Atticus Administration, located at P.O. Box 64053, Saint Paul, MN 55164.
A fairness hearing is scheduled for May 29, 2026.
The deadline to submit a request for exclusion from the class is May 14, 2026.
Payouts will be distributed on or before the later of 60 days after the final settlement date or 60 days after receipt of the settlement distribution form.
Payout options include interbank electronic transfer (U.S. bank account preferred), wire transfer for international recipients, and paper checks sent via FedEx, UPS, or U.S. First Class Mail.
Class members must provide name, address, date of birth, gender, Social Security number, residency status, bank/payment details, Medicare beneficiary status, signature, and date to file a claim.
source_title: Kia Georgia, Hyundai Mobis $11.5M TN Visa Settlement

facts: Hyundai Transys is a global automotive parts manufacturer and a subsidiary of the Hyundai Motor Group.
Hyundai Transys specializes in chassis systems, drivetrain components, and automotive seating systems.
Hyundai Transys operates a Georgia-based seating production facility located in Montgomery County near Atlanta.
The Georgia facility supports just-in-time manufacturing logistics by supplying completed seat assemblies directly to final assembly lines.
The facility supplies factory-installed seats for vehicles assembled at the Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama (HMMA) plant in Montgomery.
There is a high probability that original seats for Hyundai Sonata, Santa Fe, or Palisade models built in Alabama were produced by Hyundai Transys at its Georgia plant.
The domestic seating supplier reduces shipping time and cost between parts suppliers and final assembly plants.
Factory-integrated seats meet strict OEM specifications for fit, durability, and safety compliance.
The facility ensures trim-level consistency across models with leather, heated, ventilated, or power-adjustable features.
Hyundai Transys does not sell seats directly to consumers but supplies automakers.
Engineers collaborate with Hyundai Transys early in the development cycle to integrate seating into overall interior architecture.

Seating integration decisions include frame structure and mounting points, adjustment mechanisms (manual, electric, memory functions), material selection (fabric, synthetic leather, genuine leather), advanced features like lumbar support, heating, ventilation, and massage, and compatibility with airbag systems and seatbelt pre-tensioners.
Differences in wiring harnesses, bracket alignment, and electronic controls between trims can prevent direct seat swaps without modification.
Power seat motors may use different pin configurations, heated seat modules may require ECU reprogramming, and folding mechanisms in rear seats may vary between trims.
Replacement seats are available through Hyundai dealerships or OEM parts distributors using the vehicle's VIN to match factory-original specifications exactly.
Factory-recycled programs operated by Hyundai Motor Group provide another sourcing channel for replacement seats.
Used and refurbished OEM seats are available through platforms like eBay, Car-Part.com, and regional auto recyclers.
Some salvage yards specialize in HMMA-built vehicles, increasing the likelihood that recovered seats originated from the same Hyundai Transys Georgia line.

Aftermarket companies like Katzkin and ShearComfort offer custom-fit seat covers or reupholstery solutions designed specifically for Hyundai models whose original seats were built under Hyundai Transys specifications.
Not all Hyundai seats sold in the U.S. come from the Georgia plant; other seats come from facilities in Mexico, South Korea, or third-party suppliers depending on the model and production schedule.
Some Elantra and Kona models built outside the U.S. may have seats produced elsewhere.
All Hyundai Transys plants adhere to the same global quality standards, meaning location does not imply superiority.
Post-2020 supply chain constraints increased lead times for OEM replacement seats, with some dealers reporting waits of 6–8 weeks for backordered items.
Production prioritizes new vehicle output over spare parts, which explains why certain seat configurations take longer to source.

The Ergo Motion Driver’s Seat, available on select Palisade and Genesis models, uses adjustable lateral support and dynamic cushion extension to reduce fatigue, with actuators and control logic integrated at the factory level by Hyundai Transys.

The Relaxation Comfort Seat, found in higher-trim SUVs, simulates a zero-gravity position by adjusting seatback angle and leg support, requiring firmware coordination between the seat module and infotainment system.
Ventilated and heated seats are standard or optional on SEL, Limited, and Calligraphy trims, with cooling fans and heating elements embedded during initial assembly.
Retrofitting advanced seating features often requires dealership-level programming for software integration even if hardware can be installed.
New OEM seats purchased through dealers typically carry a 12-month/unlimited-mile warranty.
Seat origin cannot be determined from the VIN alone; customers must contact Hyundai customer service with their VIN for component sourcing details or consult technical service bulletins.
source_title: What Is the Hyundai Transys Georgia Seating System?

facts: The facility is located in West Point, GA and operates in the Transportation Equipment sector under NAICS code 336360.
Between 2019 and 2023, the site reported cumulative toxic releases of 538 lbs across two distinct chemicals to the EPA Toxic Release Inventory (TRI).
Both reported chemicals are classified as carcinogens, representing 100% of the facility's chemical profile.
The primary release pathway for all reported chemicals is off-site transfers, accounting for 100% of total releases.
Reported toxic release totals decreased by 100% between 2019 and 2023, dropping from 538 lbs to 0 lbs.
In 2019, the facility reported 538 lbs of toxic releases across 2 chemicals; from 2020 through 2023, it reported 0 lbs annually while still disclosing 2 chemicals each year.
The specific chemicals reported are Toluene-2,4-diisocyanate (538 lbs total across reporting years) and Toluene-2,6-diisocyanate (0 lbs total across reporting years).
The facility files annual Form R disclosures to the EPA under the Emergency Planning and Community Right-to-Know Act (EPCRA) Section 313.
TRI release quantities are self-reported estimates that include releases to air, water, land, and off-site transfers for disposal, rather than measured stack emissions.
Inclusion in the EPA TRI database documents the facility's annual reporting record under federal community right-to-know requirements and does not establish a health risk to nearby residents.

facts: Norfolk Southern originates more automotive traffic than any other Class I Railroad.
The company has the most extensive intermodal network in the eastern U.S.
Norfolk Southern helps its customers avoid approximately 15 million tons of yearly carbon emissions by shipping via rail.

The company serves a majority of the country’s population and manufacturing base, with connections to every major container port on the Atlantic coast as well as major ports in the Gulf of Mexico and Great Lakes.
Norfolk Southern is targeting a sub-60% operating ratio in three to four years to close the margin gap with peers.
The company's plan includes capturing 400 basis points of productivity savings and upcycle improvement.
In 2024, the plan targets delivering 100-150 basis points of operating ratio improvement year-over-year.
There is a line of sight to 400-450 basis points of improvement in the second half of 2024 compared to the prior year period.
CEO Alan Shaw was appointed in 2022 to leverage Precision Scheduled Railroading (PSR) to deliver top-tier revenue and earnings growth with industry-competitive margins.
COO John Orr joined the company four weeks ago as of April 2024 and has improved Merchandise velocity by 8% and terminal dwell by 8%.

Within the next 60 to 90 days, the company expects to reduce terminal dwell in two major yards by 30%, reduce overtime by 20%, reduce recrew rate by 20%, and increase on-time connections system wide by 10%.
Following the East Palestine incident, Norfolk Southern reduced its mainline accident rate by 38% year-over-year in 2023, achieving the lowest rate since 1999.
The company increased train speed by 22% and decreased terminal dwell by 11% since Shaw became CEO.

In 2022, the company delivered record revenues, closed the margin gap to Class I peers with an operating ratio in the low 60s, and achieved the second highest five-year total shareholder returns among Class I peers.

facts: The Trump Administration’s investments will help save Americans in Georgia and Alabama over $7 billion in electricity costs.

On day one, President Trump declared an energy emergency after the energy subtraction policies of the Biden administration drove up electricity prices by nearly 30 percent – 13 times faster than the previous seven years.
Without a dramatic reversal in energy policy, the U.S. energy grid was on a dangerous path with blackouts projected to increase by 100 times in 2030.
The Energy Department is reversing course and lowering electricity costs for American families by adding reliable power generation.

On February 25, 2026, the Energy Department’s Office of Energy Dominance Financing (EDF) announced it had issued two loans delivering over $7 billion in electricity cost savings for Georgians and Alabamans.
The $26.5 billion loan package is made possible by President Trump’s Working Families’ Tax Cuts and will be provided to two wholly owned subsidiaries of Southern Company.
This represents the largest loan package ever issued by the Energy Department.
Real and immediate electricity savings are being provided to Georgia and Alabama ratepayers, with multilayer rate freezes already approved and in effect in both states.
The loans are estimated to reduce Southern Company’s interest expenses by over $300 million per year, allowing for greater savings to American families and businesses.
The loans will support over 200 individual energy projects in Georgia and Alabama.
Through these projects, 16.7 gigawatts (GW) of reliable power will be added or upgraded to the grid, enough power to support approximately 15 million homes.
The projects will provide $7.3 billion in estimated customer savings for millions of households and businesses.

The projects will provide 5.3 GW of new gas generation and nearly 500 megawatts (MW) in gas capacity upgrades, including three new natural gas turbines totaling 1.3 GW at the natural gas-fueled Yates Power Plant, set to be online by the end of 2027.
The Bowen Power Plant, McIntosh Power Plant, and Wansley Power Plant will also add generation set to be online by the end of 2030.
The projects will provide 6.3 GW in nuclear license renewals and capacity upgrades.
The projects will provide 1 GW in hydropower modernization and grid enhancement projects.
The projects will support approximately 1,300 miles of transmission and grid enhancement projects.
The projects will create and sustain thousands of jobs.
source_title: FACT SHEET: The Energy Department is Lowering Electricity Costs in Georgia and Alabama | Department of Energy
