source_title: Safety Staffing in Georgia | 3P Safety Staffing

facts: Two Korean companies are in a trade secrets dispute that threatens a massive factory being built in Georgia.
The two companies argued their positions to the White House last week.
SK Innovation told officials that President Joe Biden should overturn a recent ruling.
A recent ruling could halt SK Innovation's planned production of batteries for electric vehicles.
SK Innovation is building a $2.6 billion plant near Commerce, Georgia.
These details were presented by SK Innovation in a company presentation.
source_title: White House hears dispute threatening VW battery supplier | Chattanooga Times Free Press

facts: Norway’s Freyr is a publicly traded company with Norwegian roots.
Freyr is set to build a nearly $2.6 billion battery plant in Newnan, Georgia.
The plant aims to meet an anticipated spike in demand for energy-storage systems in North America.
The company plans to spend $1.7 billion on the first phase of its “Giga America” factory.
Later expansions could include solar parks with storage modules and materials processing, bringing the total investment to $2.57 billion.
The factory will focus first on battery energy storage solutions, which are seen as key to the growth of wind and solar energy in the United States.
Executives say Freyr is receiving consistent inquiries from electric vehicle manufacturers.
Freyr has committed to hiring 723 people in Coweta County.
The announcement follows the Hyundai EV and battery plant groundbreaking near Savannah, SK Battery’s success in Commerce, and a planned Rivian truck and SUV facility in Covington.
The company touted “robust incentives” from the state and Coweta County without outlining details in its news release.
Freyr CEO Tom Jensen stated at a Friday launch event that people would be the company’s most vital resource.
Jensen invited local firms to partner with Freyr as it moves into the engineering phase for the plant.

Mr. Jensen said in a livestream: “We are a partnership-based company,” and emphasized strong, local connections, enabling the community, hiring locals and contractors, and strengthening the company’s core.
John Daviston, chair of the Coweta County Industrial Development Authority, called Freyr a “perfect example on the leading edge of technology, innovation and caring about their people.”
Freyr picked Coweta County from five finalist sites in the U.S. after weeding out contenders from an initial pool of more than 150 locations.
The company waited to announce the location until the passage of the Inflation Reduction Act to qualify for production tax credits under the federal package.
Freyr will apply for U.S. Department of Energy funds including grants or loan assistance, perhaps under the bipartisan infrastructure bill.
A 368-acre site in Newnan’s Bridgeport Industrial Park has been purchased for the 34 gigawatt-hour plant.
The Georgia facility is one node in a broader global plan to build 200 gigawatt hours of capacity by 2030.
Freyr seeks to fulfill its mission of “accelerating the decarbonization of global transportation and energy storage systems.”
U.S. plans will be carried out through a $70 million joint venture with Koch Strategic Platforms.
The Koch conglomerate’s venture arm has invested $50 million, with Freyr contributing $20 million.
Freyr was founded in 2019 after Swedish battery company Northvolt proved that building massive factories close to clean energy sources could be commercially viable.
Mr. Jensen noted that the company tapped into Norway’s deep pool of energy-industry expertise and recruited top minds from 30 countries.
Freyr is technically domiciled in Luxembourg but keeps most intellectual capital in Norway, where it is building its initial Giga Arctic factory in Mo i Rana with strong government support.
A single test production line called the Customer Qualification Plant will go into operation just after Christmas 2022.
Processes refined at the test line will be replicated on eight production lines planned for Giga Arctic, which will serve as a model for the Georgia facility with similar size and design.
Freyr is not yet generating sales, posting only a paper gain of $4.7 million in Q2 after a loss of more than $33 million in Q1 of 2022.
The company has $488 million of cash on hand and a $1.6 billion market capitalization after going public via a SPAC merger with Alussa Energy in 2021.
By 2028, with both planned gigafactories built, Freyr projects generating $1.6 billion in free cash flow annually on $5.4 billion in revenues.
Freyr has secured agreements with massive global companies to secure financing, raw materials, and eventual purchase orders.
Taiwan-based Aleees is planning to build a lithium-iron phosphate plant in the Nordic region, from which Freyr will purchase supplies and license technology.
Freyr’s operating premise is that conventional lithium-ion batteries and renewable energy technologies don’t do enough or aren’t building quickly enough to solve the climate crisis.
Mr. Jensen explained that if batteries are charged or produced with non-renewable energy, it just moves the point of emission rather than solving it.
Without ample storage, intermittent solar and wind power production can waste energy and create mismatches between supply and demand.
Mr. Jensen stated that to stay on track with climate commitments by 2030, including electrifying transportation, the U.S. and Europe need a 34-fold increase in energy storage capacity.
He said the opportunity is enormous for coming to the United States as it advances its energy transition.
Freyr uses what it calls “the Norway advantage,” leveraging nearly all clean, cheap hydro power and huge offshore wind investments to make battery cells cleanly at an economically viable scale.
The company established a technology office in Boston where it is licensing and scaling 24M semi-solid battery tech developed at MIT.
Tom Rosseland, honorary consul for Sweden and Norway in Atlanta, praised Norway’s strides in electric mobility and energy storage markets.
Mr. Rosseland stated that Norway is a leader in electrifying passenger transportation infrastructure and expanding that knowhow to port operations and other sectors.
He expressed looking forward to seeing Georgia and Freyr expand the ESS and EV markets for Georgia and North America.
Mr. Jensen noted the Inflation Reduction Act should make energy cheaper by running on wind and sun.
Freyr is named after a Norse god representing prosperity, sunshine, peace and virility.
Mr. Jensen remarked during his speech: “Here we are giving birth to our first gigafactory in the United States.”
source_title: Norway’s Freyr to Build $2.6B Battery Plant in Newnan  - Global Atlanta

facts: President Joe Biden campaigned on electric vehicles and green jobs, and Georgia voters elected him to the White House while handing the state’s two Senate seats and Congress control to Democrats.
A U.S. International Trade Commission ruling threatens to shut down a giant electric vehicle battery factory set to employ upward of 2,600 workers in the suburbs north of Athens, Georgia.
Georgia’s Republican lawmakers have emerged as the project’s most vocal defenders.
President Biden has just 19 days left to veto trade authorities’ ruling.
The situation is enormously important for jobs in Georgia and the administration’s plans to electrify the nation’s automobiles over the next decade.
Last month, the ITC ruled in favor of South Korean battery giant LG Chem, finding rival SK Innovation guilty in a trade secrets case.
The ruling bans SK Innovation from importing what it needs to run its planned Georgia factory for the next decade.
There has been no sign from the White House that it will block the decision.
SK Innovation has hired consultants to draft a plan to shut down its $2.6 billion plant in Georgia.
Peach State officials are scrambling to save one of the largest economic development projects in the state’s history.
Pat Wilson, the GOP commissioner of Georgia’s Department of Economic Development, said: “There’s nothing partisan about the jobs of the future.”
Georgia’s Republican leaders have been very public in their call for Biden to act to save the Commerce, Georgia, plant.
Democrats have been largely silent on the issue; none of the six Democrats representing Georgia in the House of Representatives or the Georgia Democratic Party responded to requests for comment.
Five emails to Democratic Sen. Jon Ossoff’s office went unanswered, and former gubernatorial candidate Stacey Abrams declined an interview request.

Sen. Raphael Warnock (D-Ga.) declined an interview but a spokesman provided a link to a March 4 confirmation hearing video where Warnock pressed Polly Trottenberg, Biden’s pick for the No. 2 job at the Department of Transportation, on whether the agency would provide President Biden an analysis of the ITC ruling’s effect on U.S. battery supplies.
During the hearing, Warnock said: “It’s not a good position for U.S. automakers if they’re only relying on almost one cell supplier.”

Warnock stated during the hearing (which took place a month after the ruling): “An adverse ruling by the International Trade Commission threatens seriously the future of that project, whether it’ll happen at all,” and noted it would be “one of the largest economic investments in Georgia’s history and a severe punch in the gut, if you will, for folks who are counting on those jobs, not to mention President Biden’s own goals.”
Warnock faces reelection in two years amid mounting pressure from the right.

Conservative Atlanta-based pundit Erick Erickson called the ITC’s decision “a bad day for all of us here in Georgia” and compared the potential job loss to construction workers laid off after Biden blocked the Keystone XL oil pipeline.

Erickson wrote that Biden should want to salvage the project because Georgia is a state where Warnock and Ossoff were elected, warning that if they lack political clout to reverse the decision, it will contribute to the loss of over 2,000 jobs in Georgia ahead of the 2022 elections.

Carol Browner, former Clinton-era EPA administrator, served as an expert witness for SK Innovation and testified before the ITC that eliminating its Georgia plant put the U.S. electric vehicle supply chain at risk.

Former Obama-era deputy attorney general Sally Yates told The New York Times’ Dealbook that the case could “seriously undermine critical progress on the climate crisis” and risked “killing jobs and setting back the Georgia economy.”

Civil rights leader and former UN ambassador Andrew Young warned in an opinion piece that the ITC ruling “imperils thousands of direct and indirect Georgia jobs and postpones the future of the new era auto industry” over what he called a technicality.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp (R) repeatedly urged Biden to “support thousands of hardworking Georgians and their communities.”

On March 12, Kemp sent a letter to Biden asking him to “exercise the authority granted to you under law to disapprove the ITC ruling on grounds that it is contrary to the public interest and will seriously jeopardize your administration’s environmental and economic goals.”

Kemp wrote: “Simply put: the livelihoods of thousands of Georgians are now in your hands,” and added, “This critical opportunity requires us to work together for the good of my state and our country.”

GOP Rep. Buddy Carter hoped Biden was helping broker an agreement between LG Chem and SK Innovation, stating: “We don’t want to lose those jobs in Georgia and we don’t want to lose those jobs in the electric vehicle industry,” and expressed hope that the president and his staff are engaged in trying to broker a deal.

White House U.S. Trade Representative spokesman Adam Hodge stated the administration is “currently reviewing the policy implications of the ITC determination” and will complete the review within the 60-day deadline outlined by federal law.
The dispute started in 2019 when LG’s battery subsidiary, LG Energy Solutions, lodged a complaint with the ITC.
The underlying dispute began in 2017 when SK Innovation poached engineers in Seoul, prompting LG to file lawsuits accusing them of violating noncompete contracts.
Around two years later, as SK Innovation announced plans to open its massive Georgia factory to supply Ford Motor Company and Volkswagen with batteries, LG turned to the ITC.
LG filed a separate lawsuit in federal courts in Delaware for monetary damages for alleged intellectual property theft, which is pending the conclusion of the ITC case.
The ITC’s guilty verdicts typically carry trade bans that restrict what and whether a convicted defendant can import.
SK Innovation denied stealing trade secrets but argued that South Korean laws do not require companies to retain the same documents as U.S. statutes.
On Feb. 10, the ITC found SK Innovation guilty of destroying documents allegedly detailing what it learned from its LG hires.

The commission ruled unanimously to ban SK Innovation from importing raw materials for the next 10 years, which is the period LG said it would take for a competitor to develop batteries independently.
The ITC granted a carve-out allowing SK Innovation to build batteries for the next four years for Ford’s electric F-150 pickup truck and two years for Volkswagen’s Crossover series.
The commission determined this timeframe should give automakers enough time to find alternative sources or for SK Innovation to reach a settlement with LG.
Tesla is currently the largest battery maker in the U.S., but its Nevada Gigafactory only produces packs for its own vehicles.
LG operates several projects across the Midwest that, when completed, will produce roughly 30 gigawatt-hours per year and provide batteries to General Motors and other automakers.
SK Innovation’s Georgia plant would add another 22 gigawatt-hours of annual capacity.
The rest of the battery market is made up of much smaller companies.

LG sent a letter on March 10 to Warnock vowing to “do whatever we can to help the people and workers of Georgia,” hinting it was prepared to make investments in the state to offset the closure of SK Innovation’s factory, according to The Atlanta Journal-Constitution.
David Callahan, a lawyer for LG, stated the company is preparing to invest $4.5 billion in the U.S., resulting in at least two more factories, one of which could be in Georgia.
SK Innovation stated that LG is “using the ITC to try to muscle SK out of the US EV battery market, period.”

A SK Innovation spokesperson said: “SK has always been open to good-faith settlement talks with LG,” but noted that “LG continues to be inflexible and unreasonable in its demands. It’s clear that LG doesn’t want competition.”

James Frith, an analyst at BloombergNEF, stated that allowing LG to dominate the battery market would pose significant risks to producing electric vehicles at the speed and affordability the Biden administration wants.
Frith noted that competition within the battery industry has been a key factor in driving down cell and pack prices over the past decade.
Frith warned: “It’s not a good position for U.S. automakers if they’re only relying on almost one cell supplier, because that cell supplier could set prices as it liked.”
Other manufacturers that could fill SK Innovation’s void include Japan’s Panasonic Corporation, South Korea’s Samsung, and China’s CATL or BYD.
Callahan denied that LG was seeking a monopoly and accused SK Innovation of bluffing about its willingness to shutter the plant to avoid a costly settlement.

Callahan said: “If SK Innovation intends to be in this hugely growing market in the long haul, which they have said they are, it would be suicide to leave Ford and Volkswagen in the lurch,” and added that threatening to walk away from the plant is an idle threat.
Callahan remarked: “They’re threatening Georgia. If I were Georgia, I would be mad. I understand for political reasons not everyone is going to acknowledge that.”
SK Innovation already started hiring hundreds of workers, and it could take years for another company to replace the plant or increase supply to levels needed to electrify the U.S. auto fleet.

Wilson stated: “We need batteries in order to keep up with the demand, this was not only an investment into Georgia but an investment into electric vehicles in the United States and a diversification of the supply chain outside of Asia,” and added, “We saw this as a win-win for the state and the country.”
source_title: The Georgia GOP Is Begging Biden To Act On Battery Plant, While Dems Stay Silent | HuffPost Latest News

facts: SK Battery America laid off 958 workers at its Commerce, Ga., plant.
The layoffs cut about 37 percent of the workforce as automakers scale back EV plans and federal incentives shift.
SK Battery America confirmed on March 7, 2026 that it laid off 958 workers at its Commerce, Georgia, battery manufacturing facility.
A Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification (WARN) was filed by human resources chief Chuck Moore.
The WARN states Friday was the last working day for those employees, who will be paid through May 6.
The plant opened in January 2022 as a $2.6 billion investment.
The plant will continue operating with about 1,600 employees.
The layoffs mark a sharp reversal for a plant that was part of a wave of U.S. battery investments tied to automakers’ initial push into electric vehicles.
SK has supplied batteries for the Ford F-150 Lightning and is also a supplier to Volkswagen.
Ford announced in December that it was canceling the fully electric version of the F-150 Lightning, contracting near-term demand for SK’s cells and modules.

Joe Guy Collier, SK Americas spokesperson, explained the move in a company statement: "To align operations to market conditions, SK Battery America has made the difficult decision to reduce our workforce."
Joe Guy Collier added: "We appreciate the contributions of employees and will be working with those affected during this transition."
Joe Guy Collier stated: "SK Battery America remains committed to Georgia and to building a robust U.S. supply chain for advanced battery manufacturing."
Joe Guy Collier noted: "We are pursuing a range of future customers, including the Battery Electric Storage System arena."
The cuts follow a broader retreat in U.S. electric vehicle policy and consumer incentives, which has reshaped demand expectations for automakers and their suppliers.

The Trump administration has moved to scale back federal tax incentives for EV buyers and manufacturers and to loosen emissions rules, reversing a policy environment that earlier encouraged rapid expansion of domestic battery capacity.
Georgia Senator Jon Ossoff blamed the administration directly, stating: "Let’s be clear: these were battery manufacturing jobs and now they’re gone."
Jon Ossoff stated: "As predicted, Trump’s war on electric vehicles is hurting Georgia’s economy. We were booming and building new plants. Now Georgians are losing their jobs."
The Commerce layoffs expose the risk of oversized capital commitments when vehicle electrification plans shift.
SK and automaker partners had previously funneled billions into U.S. battery capacity, with one report citing a combined $11.4 billion investment with Ford in joint battery plants.
SK and Ford ended their joint venture in December, leaving standalone cell makers facing underutilized lines and elevated fixed costs.
The reduction removes a substantial share of good-paying manufacturing jobs in Jackson County, roughly 70 miles northeast of Atlanta.
SK said it will work with affected employees during the transition, but details on severance, benefits continuation, or recall rights were not disclosed in the WARN.
SK’s stated intent to seek customers in the Battery Electric Storage System market signals an attempt to pivot toward stationary storage demand to fill idle capacity.

The Commerce action underscores a broader inflection in the U.S. EV industrial strategy: without sustained policy incentives and stable automaker commitments, factory-scale build-out of battery capacity risks becoming a stranded-asset problem rather than a durable reshoring success.
source_title: SK Battery America cuts 958 jobs at $2.6 billion Georgia plant amid EV pullback | Prism News

facts: Georgia is at the epicenter of the nation’s electric vehicle revolution, and its system of higher education is working to drive the industry forward.

Georgia’s college and university systems are prioritizing research and development and gathering top researchers and players together as a prime incubator for collaboration and innovation to support the quickly evolving EV field.
In April, the University of Georgia hosted more than 150 people for a one-day Electric Mobility Summit.
The summit was held to learn more about the current state of electric mobility and how educational institutions can support the expansion of the EV industry.
The summit connected researchers with key stakeholders including government officials, economic development professionals, and leaders from companies like Kia, Rivian, and SK Battery America.
source_title: Georgia Universities: Providing Connections and Advancing Electric Mobility R&D

facts: Nissan Motor Corp. and SK On inked a battery agreement to bolster the automaker’s electric vehicle production in North America.
Under the $661 million deal, SK On will supply Nissan with roughly 100 GWh of high-nickel batteries from 2028 to 2033.
Nissan will use the U.S.-made batteries for EV manufacturing at its assembly plant in Canton, Mississippi.
The deal supports Nissan’s push to grow its U.S. EV manufacturing capacity.
Nissan is focusing on finding new partnerships to help bring vehicles to market faster.
Nissan previously invested $500 million toward EV production at the Canton plant.
In 2024, Nissan announced plans to launch 16 new EVs over the next three years, some of which will be assembled in Canton starting in 2028.
The partnership marks SK On’s first supply agreement with a Japan-based automaker.
SK On, a South Korea-based subsidiary of SK Group, has also collaborated with Ford on EV battery material production.
SK On currently operates two battery plants in the U.S., with four additional plants under construction with partners.
SK On’s U.S. production capacity is expected to surpass 180 GWh once all of its plants are fully operational.
source_title: Nissan, SK On announce $661M EV battery supply deal | Supply Chain Dive

facts: SK Battery America exceeded its hiring goal of 2,600 employees two years ahead of schedule for its two lithium-ion battery manufacturing facilities in Commerce, Georgia.
Gov. Brian P. Kemp announced that the company met its hiring goal at the end of 2022.
SK Battery America is expanding its workforce to approximately 3,000 employees over the next year to meet growing electric vehicle demand.
Gov. Brian P. Kemp stated that Georgia is seeing unprecedented growth and its citizens are benefiting from it.
Gov. Brian P. Kemp praised SK Battery America for going above and beyond in delivering on its commitments.
Gov. Brian P. Kemp noted that SK Battery America was one of the first companies to put Georgia on the e-mobility map.
SK Battery America announced its first project in Georgia in 2018 with an initial planned investment of $1.6 billion.
Since 2019, SK Battery America has invested $2.6 billion to build two battery manufacturing facilities in Jackson County.
The facilities will supply batteries for electric vehicles including the Ford F-150 Lightning and the Volkswagen ID.4.
SK Battery America started mass production at its Georgia facilities in early 2022.
SK Battery America CEO Timothy Jeong credited the company's partnership with the State of Georgia for meeting its employment goal ahead of schedule.
SK Battery America CEO Timothy Jeong stated that Georgia's Quick Start program helped the company attract and train workers at a speed and scale difficult for other companies to achieve alone.
SK Battery America CEO Timothy Jeong stated that more than 300,000 new electric vehicles a year will be on American roads because of the company's work in Georgia.
SK Battery America has worked with Georgia Quick Start, the state's top-ranked job training program through the Technical College System of Georgia, since announcing its first project in 2018.

In July 2022, SK Battery America announced a partnership with the Georgia National Guard’s Work for Warriors Georgia program to focus on hiring veterans, servicemembers, retirees, reservists, and their families.
SK Battery America has held numerous career fairs, visited schools, and held community events in recent months to attract talent.
SK Battery America has consistently improved wages, benefits, and its overall work environment to attract a high-quality workforce.

Jackson County Board of Commissioners Chairman Tom Crow expressed appreciation for SK Battery America's commitment to providing high-quality jobs and its substantial financial investment in the county.
Jackson County Chairman Tom Crow stated that the county looks forward to the continued benefits of SK Battery America's operation and building a long-term relationship.
SK Battery America is currently hiring for roles in production, quality, utility, logistics, ERT, maintenance, and other areas.

Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that SK Battery America has relentlessly pursued hiring Georgians for high-skilled, high-paying jobs and has become an incredible partner to the state.
Commissioner Pat Wilson applauded SK Battery America for its commitment to the state, its adopted community, and the people of Georgia.

Commissioner Pat Wilson expressed being consistently impressed with SK Battery America's dedication, front-line role in developing Georgia's electric mobility ecosystem, and participation in opportunities.
source_title: SK Battery America exceeds hiring goal ahead of schedule at Commerce, GA plant - On Common Ground News - 24/7 local news

facts: SK on’s Georgia battery plant is now operating at full capacity, four years after it began production.
The facility is located in Commerce, Georgia.
The site operates as a high-security facility with strict external access controls.
The facility was unveiled to the media for the first time during a recent visit by Maeil Business Newspaper.
Robotic arms and machines assemble components, from manufacturing battery materials such as cathodes and anodes to the stacking process of battery cell assembly.
Locally hired workers who completed systematic technical training programs monitor real-time production data and output via large screens installed along each production line.
The monitors display a yield rate of around 95 percent, reflecting virtually defect-free production.
SKBA is advancing its smart factory initiative by integrating artificial intelligence (AI) inspection systems into its battery manufacturing and quality control processes.
Plants 1 and 2 are running at full capacity.
The facility produces batteries sufficient for approximately 300,000 electric vehicles annually, equivalent to 22 GWh.
The company overcame numerous challenges since the start of commercial operations in 2022 to reach full operational capacity within four years.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, the automaker’s electric vehicle production hub in Georgia, is beginning operations earlier in 2025.
SKBA is experiencing accelerated growth as a key battery supplier due to Hyundai's upcoming operations.
SKBA Chief Executive Officer Yoonsang Chaey emphasized the company’s focus on process optimization and workforce training to achieve high productivity.
CEO Yoonsang Chaey stated, “We worked hard to achieve top-level productivity by fine-tuning the compatibility between equipment and workers.”

CEO Yoonsang Chaey added, “The yield rate represents field competitiveness built on technology, people, and countless trials, making this achievement even more meaningful given the challenges of U.S. manufacturing.”
source_title: SK on’s Georgia battery plant runs at full capacity - 매일경제 영문뉴스 펄스(Pulse)

facts: Volkswagen's Chattanooga electric vehicle assembly plant will use batteries produced at a planned $1.67 billion factory in northeast Georgia.
SK Innovation, a South Korean company, broke ground on Tuesday on the factory to make lithium-ion batteries for electric and hybrid vehicles.
source_title: Volkswagen Chattanooga will source electric vehicle batteries from new Georgia factory | Chattanooga Times Free Press

facts: SK On, the battery subsidiary of SK Innovation, has laid off more than one-third of its employees at a U.S. battery factory.
The large-scale workforce reduction is interpreted as a response to the slowing growth of the U.S. electric vehicle (EV) market.
The EV market slowdown was exacerbated by the Trump administration’s abolition of EV subsidies.
SK Battery America, the U.S. subsidiary of SK On, announced via email on the 6th (local time) that it laid off 958 workers (37%) out of 2,566 employees at its Georgia State factory.
The layoffs account for roughly one-third of the total workforce at the facility.
The last working day for the affected employees was on the 6th.
Affected employees are expected to receive salaries until May 6th, when their employment contracts expire.
The factory was built in January 2022 in Georgia State with an investment of $2.6 billion (approximately 3.85 trillion Korean won).
The facility has supplied batteries for the F-150 Lightning, Ford’s flagship electric pickup truck.
Ford decided to halt production of the F-150 Lightning model at the end of last year.
Ford also suspended development of electric commercial vans and trucks at the end of last year.
The Trump administration fully abolished $7,500-per-vehicle EV subsidies that had been implemented since October of last year.
A source from SK Battery America told AP and FOX, “We made the difficult decision to reduce our workforce to align operations with market conditions.”
The same source added, “We will continue to strive to build a robust domestic supply chain for advanced battery manufacturing in Georgia State.”
source_title: SK On Cuts One-Third of U.S. Battery Workforce Amid EV Slowdown

entity_name: Hyundai-LG Energy Solution Georgia Battery Plant
canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution
facts: ICE detained 450 illegal migrants at a construction site in Georgia on September 4.
The sweep included hundreds of Korean nationals who likely misused B-1/B-2 business tourist visas to take jobs from American construction workers.
More than 300 Koreans were arrested during the sweep, according to Korean news sites.
A giant Korean-owned battery factory is being built at the Ellabell, Georgia construction site.
The battery factory is being constructed for Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution Ltd.
More than 1,400 people are currently working at the Ellabell construction site.
South Korea’s government filed a sharp complaint regarding the detention sweep because of the Korean-owned battery factory under construction.
Lee Jae-woong, a ministry spokesperson for South Korea, stated that the economic activities of their companies investing in the U.S. and the rights of their nationals must not be unfairly violated.
Lee Jae-woong conveyed concern and regret through the U.S. Embassy in Seoul on the day of the press briefing.
Immigration expert Jay Palmer labeled Hyundai a "repeat offender" for allegedly using staffing companies to legally shield executives from liability when flying migrants to job sites.
Palmer noted that other companies like Mercedes, Kia, and Tesla also use staffing companies to avoid responsibility for workers' immigration status via master service agreements.
Palmer explained the corporate outsourcing strategy is driven by economics: hiring foreign workers for $5 to $8 an hour without insurance versus American workers for $20 to $30 an hour plus benefits.
This corporate labor strategy clashes with President Donald Trump’s push to hire blue-collar Americans for skilled trades.
Palmer warned that the Georgia raid will cost Hyundai a significant amount of money due to construction delays.
In 2020, Fox News reported on the widespread use of B-1/B-2 visas by Korean workers at another battery factory for SK Battery America in Georgia.
David Cagle of Georgia Local Union 72 complained that Georgians were excluded from applying for construction jobs at the SKI battery plant.
Cagle stated that about 500 local people were out of work and available to build the plant, but instead Koreans were hired.
Cagle clarified that much of the construction work was "generic stuff," including electrical work, ductwork, pipework, pipe fitting, and pipe welding.
The recent ICE detentions were conducted with support from the FBI, ATF, and the Georgia State Patrol.
In July, South Korea pledged $350 billion in U.S. investment to lower President Donald Trump’s threatened tariffs on its products, which were ultimately set at 15%.
In March, Hyundai announced it would invest $21 billion in U.S. onshoring from 2025 to 2028.
Hyundai later increased its stated investment commitment to $26 billion.
Hyundai's investment initiatives include a new $5.8 billion steel plant in Louisiana, expanded U.S. auto production capacity, and a state-of-the-art robotics facility.
These Hyundai initiatives are expected to create about 25,000 new direct jobs in the U.S. over the next four years.

Breitbart News has closely covered the illegal use of B-1 visas to facilitate the inflow of airport migrants from India, Mexico, and Europe into American trucking, technology, and construction industries.
Much of this labor travel activity is quietly backed by foreign governments that extract U.S. dollars in taxes and remittances via workers who enter legally but work illegally.
Trump’s deputies have begun efforts to curb the misuse of B-1 visas, which are intended for tourists and visiting business executives.
source_title: Reports: ICE Detains 300 Korean Migrants at Georgia Construction Site

facts: SK Battery America Inc. laid off nearly 1,000 workers at a manufacturing plant northeast of Atlanta on Friday.
The layoffs occurred amid automakers’ changing electrification plans and uncertain consumer demand for EVs.
Friday marked the last working day for 958 plant employees, about 37% of its workforce.
Impacted workers will be paid through May 6.
The plant will continue to employ about 1,600 workers.
SK opened the $2.6 billion battery plant in Commerce in January 2022.
The Korean company notably supplied the Ford F-150 Lightning electric pickup.
Ford announced plans to cancel the fully electric version of the truck in December.

The U.S. electric vehicle market is at a standstill amid the Trump administration steering federal support away from electrification in favor of more lax automotive emissions policies and a broader agenda supporting the oil and gas industries.
SK Americas spokesperson Joe Guy Collier said in a statement that the workforce reduction was made to align operations to market conditions.
SK and Ford had together previously invested $11.4 billion in joint battery plants in the U.S.
The battery maker ended the joint venture in December.

SK has invested significantly in Jackson County in Georgia in recent years as automakers shored up plans to spend billions to develop and build EVs and the federal government under former President Joe Biden supported efforts to build out a domestic EV supply chain.
It had also announced in June 2020 plans to pour $940 million to expand its battery manufacturing presence in Atlanta.
At the time, Gov. Brian Kemp’s office said the expansion would create 600 jobs.
SK and Hyundai are still jointly building a $5 billion battery factory near Cartersville, northwest of Atlanta.
The state of Georgia has attracted other massive EV manufacturing investments, Rivian’s $5 billion factory and Hyundai’s own $7.6 billion factory complex among them.
source_title: Electric vehicle battery maker lays off nearly 1,000 in Georgia

facts: Through the third quarter of fiscal year 2022 (July 1, 2021 - March 31, 2022), job creation and investments resulting from economic development projects have already surpassed fiscal year 2021 year-end totals.
251 project locations supported by GDEcD’s Global Commerce team will result in the creation of more than 35,400 new jobs and $12.9 billion in investment for the state.
These totals account for a 48% increase in jobs compared to three-quarters through the previous fiscal year and for a 6% increase in jobs compared to the prior year’s overall total.
There was a recorded increase of 54% in total investment compared to the same time period last year.
Nearly 78% of both projects and investments were outside the 10-county metro Atlanta area, and over half of the jobs created were outside the 10-county metro Atlanta area.
Key industry sectors include automotive, advanced manufacturing, logistics and distribution, software and technology, and food processing.
Georgia’s electric vehicle (EV) and sustainability ecosystems have grown tremendously over the past year.
Georgia is continuing to recruit the entire EV supply chain to ensure an effective, efficient, and affordable environment for EV production.
Rivian plans to develop a $5 billion campus at Stanton Springs North and an electric vehicle service center in Atlanta that will also provide training on EVs.

SK Battery America has partnered with American lithium-ion battery recycling and engineered materials startup Ascend Elements to recycle both cell and module lithium-ion battery manufacturing scrap from SK’s Jackson County facility.
Ascend announced its first commercial-scale battery recycling plant in Covington in January.

Aurubis, one of the leading recyclers of copper, precious metals, and non-ferrous materials worldwide, will invest $340 million in a ‘first-of-its-kind in the U.S.’ state-of-the-art recycling and secondary smelting facility for multi-metal recycling in Augusta.
Aspen Aerogels recently announced plans to locate their new 500,000-square-foot advanced manufacturing facility in Statesboro.
The company will produce PyroThin materials, which provide thermal management, mechanical stability, and fire protection properties to EV battery systems, at their new home in the state.
Recycling operations and partnerships like these are vital for the future of electric mobility.
source_title: More about Gov. Kemp: New Georgia Economic Development Records Set for Jobs, Investment in Just Nine Months - EIN Presswire

facts: Rivian chose a site near Covington for its new $5 billion assembly plant and battery factory.
Rivian plans to employ 7,500 workers at its factory.
State officials have said Rivian's workforce could grow to 10,000.
Gov. Brian Kemp called the Rivian project “the largest single economic development project ever in this state’s history.”
Hyundai announced in May an investment of $5.5 billion for its own EV and battery plant in Bryan County, near Savannah.
Hyundai's project will create 8,100 new jobs.
Numerous EV-related projects and initiatives have been announced over the past couple years positioning Georgia as a nexus of the growing EV industry.
Related infrastructure companies and suppliers are investing billions of dollars more in the state.
Participant communities are benefiting from direct and secondary investment from EV-related plant construction, tax-base growth, and ancillary economic benefits.
A boost in electricity sales is a likely benefit for MEAG Power Participants as EVs begin to predominate.
SK Battery America is building two plants on a massive site along I-85 in Commerce.
The $2.6 billion SK Battery America project was announced in 2018.
SK Battery America's plants will produce batteries for the Ford F-150 Lightning pickup truck and the Volkswagen ID.4.
Plant one at the SK Battery America site was slated to begin commercial operation early this year.
Plant two at the SK Battery America site is set to begin production in the first quarter of 2023.
Approximately 2,600 jobs will be created at the SK Battery America plants by the end of 2023.
Rivian's new plant near Covington is only its second production plant.
Rivian plans to build a carbon-conscious campus with the plant eventually employing more than 7,500 workers.
Once ramped up, the Rivian facility will be capable of producing up to 400,000 vehicles per year.
Construction on the Rivian facility is expected to begin in the summer of this year.
Start of production at the Rivian facility is slated for 2024.
Rivian's site considerations included logistics, environmental impact, renewable energy production, availability and quality of talent, and a good fit with company culture.
Kia chose West Point for its first and only U.S. production plant 15 years ago.
Kia's investment delivered 2,700 jobs from Kia and thousands more from suppliers that also located in the state.
Kia is part of the Hyundai Motor Group.
Kia says it will have 11 new electric vehicles by 2026.
Kia hopes that by 2030, EVs, hybrids, and plug-in hybrids will comprise 40 percent of all its sales.
The West Point plant was touted to be in the running to build an Apple EV before that plan was scuttled last year.
Rivian’s new plant will produce pickups and SUVs, as well as Amazon delivery vans.
Blue Bird manufactures school buses in Fort Valley.
Blue Bird expects to scale up eBus production capacity to 4,000 vehicles a year by 2024 to meet demand.
Blue Bird expects eBus demand to increase in part based on the Bipartisan Infrastructure Bill.
The Bipartisan Infrastructure Bill allocates $5 billion in funding for electric and low-emission school buses.
The University of Georgia has the largest fleet of electric buses on any college campus in the country.
The UGA fleet consists of 33 Proterra E2 buses made in Greenville, S.C.
The UGA electric bus fleet is part of the university's strategic plan to advance sustainability at its Athens campus.
In July, Georgia launched a statewide initiative to strengthen its position as a national leader in the electric mobility industry.
The Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance (EMIA) is led by the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD).
EMIA includes partners from the private and public sectors ranging from electric utilities, government, industry, education, nonprofits, and other stakeholders.
EMIA is focused on advancing Georgia’s role in developing the electric mobility industry and its supply chain.
ECG’s Daryl Ingram, senior vice president & chief external officer, is an advisor to the EMIA board.
GDEcD launched a new website for the initiative.
The state legislature passed the Joint Study Committee on the Electrification of Transportation.
The committee plan covers the rollout of EV charging stations.
The study committee plan should be signed by Gov. Kemp soon.
The U.S. Departments of Transportation and Energy announced the availability of nearly $5 billion under the new National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure (NEVI) formula program on Feb. 10.
NEVI was established by the infrastructure law to build a national charging network.
NEVI will distribute the money over five years to help states create a network of EV charging stations, especially along the Interstate Highway System.

The American Public Power Association (APPA), the National Rural Electric Cooperative Association, the Joint Office, and the Edison Electric Institute (EEI) are working together to support EV charging plan development efforts.
Each organization has designated individuals to be electric power industry liaisons to the Joint Office to help ensure utility engagement and facilitate appropriate connections.
APPA's designated contact for EV charging liaison duties is Patricia Taylor, Senior Manager, Regulatory Policy and Business Programs.
APPA has produced a presentation called Navigating the Electric Vehicle Market.

In late 2021, the National Electric Highway Coalition (NEHC), comprising more than 50 power companies mostly investor-owned, announced plans to install EV fast-charging infrastructure along major U.S. highways by the end of 2023.
The EEI is leading the NEHC effort.
According to the U.S. Department of Energy’s Vehicle Technologies Office, sales of light-duty EVs in the United States rose 85% last year.
Sales of plug-in hybrid vehicles grew 138%.
The EEI estimates that about 22 million EVs will be on U.S. roads in 2030.
Meeting the projected EV growth will require more than 100,000 fast chargers.

links.context: GDEcD launched website for EMIA initiative
Joint Study Committee on Electrification of Transportation plan

source_title: Georgia Poised to Become EV Nexus – MEAG Power

facts: Solvay SA operates a unit facility in Augusta, Ga.
A quality control lab technician at the Augusta facility filed race and age discrimination claims.
A Magistrate Judge ruled that the technician's race and age discrimination claims do not merit resolution by a jury.
The judge's ruling was based on the plaintiff lacking evidence to refute the employer's explanation that a 30-year-old White woman was the most qualified applicant for the position.
source_title: Solvay Worker Should Be Denied Race, Age Bias Trial, Judge Says

facts: Stryten Energy is an innovative energy storage solutions provider serving the transportation, motive and essential power industries.
The company manufactures high-quality advanced lead, lithium, and vanadium batteries and intelligent chargers.
Stryten offers cloud-based software for smart fleet design decisions.
It is the only energy storage solutions provider in North America who can offer advanced lead, lithium and vanadium solutions to solve complex energy storage challenges.
The company maintains a manufacturing, service and distribution network throughout North America.
Stryten Energy has a growing team of over 2,500 employees.

Sister-company Motrex LLC provides personnel for professional services roles including information technology, finance and accounting, logistics, EH&S, project management, quality and human resources.
The company maintains locations in Alpharetta, Georgia.

Additional Stryten Energy locations include Queretaro, Mexico; Mississauga, Ontario; Bridgeville, Pennsylvania; Charlotte, North Carolina; Ruckersville, Virginia; Columbus, Indiana; Fort Smith, Arkansas; Kansas City, Kansas; Kansas City, Missouri; Lakeland, Florida; Lampeter, Pennsylvania; Littleton, Colorado; Lombard, Illinois; Manchester, Iowa; Minneapolis, Minnesota; Milwaukee, Wisconsin; Niagara Falls, New York; Attleboro, Massachusetts; Salina, Kansas; Edmonton, Alberta; Moncton, New Brunswick; Ottawa, Ontario; and Corona, California.
In September 2023, Stryten Energy was awarded a five-year contract to provide backup battery systems for U.S. Navy Submarines, supplying high-quality, top-performing battery cells and components.
Stryten Energy entered the long-duration energy storage market by purchasing the assets of Storion Energy Inc., a technology innovator of advanced vanadium redox flow battery technology.
Vanadium redox flow batteries are emerging as a solution for long-duration power applications and are a preferred option for large-scale energy storage.

The company's leadership team includes Tim Vargo, Mike Judd, Petar Oklobdzija, Dan Autey, Scott Childers, Lacey Chitwood, Melissa Floyd, Jeremy Furr, Matt Gould, Wendy Henderson, Bob Holmes, Rodger Meyer, Bill Nonnamaker, Bob Penman, Alvin Shao, Jully Sieglaff, and Brian Woodworth.
source_title: About Us. Who is Stryten Energy?

facts: Georgia Tech and Stryten Energy are partnering to give new life to lead batteries, a technology with more than 160 years of history.
Stryten Energy is headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia, with manufacturing facilities located in Kansas.
A new lead battery energy storage system (BESS) was unveiled last week and installed on Georgia Tech’s Atlanta campus.
The BESS will serve as an experimentation site for advanced research on medium-duration energy storage solutions and applications.
The installation is designed to complement Georgia Tech’s existing clean energy offerings, which include a previously-installed solar array and a new electric vehicle (EV) charging testbed.
The lead BESS will enable bi-directional EV charging and load shifting of peak solar power generation.
The system will provide backup power and support the school’s microgrid and grid-interactive efficient buildings program.
The project creates a real-time, real-world testbed for evaluating lead battery performance in advanced energy applications within institutional environments.
Lead batteries offer advantages over lithium-ion devices, including a proven safety record, recyclability, and domestic availability of materials.
Scott Childers, vice president of essential power at Stryten Energy, stated that at comparable deployment costs, lead is ideal for smaller, behind-the-meter applications.
Growing U.S. policy focus on building secure supply chains is making lead batteries increasingly appealing.
The lead battery systems are 100% domestically built.
Stryten Energy has institutionalized a 98%-plus closed-loop recycling process for its lead systems.

Researchers from Georgia Tech’s Carbon Neutral Energy Solutions Laboratory and Strategic Energy Institute (SEI) will collect and analyze data on the system's performance, lifecycle impact, and grid interaction.
source_title: Georgia Tech, Stryten Energy tap lead battery innovation - pv magazine USA

facts: Snapping Shoals EMC and Stryten Energy LLC commemorated the installation of its advanced vanadium redox flow battery (VRFB) at Snapping Shoals EMC's headquarters on August 2.
The project represents the first VRFB energy storage system manufactured and installed in the state of Georgia.

SSEMC's partnership with Stryten Energy will focus on demonstrating the VRFB’s energy storage capabilities, as well as evaluating additional use cases for the cutting-edge long-duration energy storage technology.
The VRFB System can store and provide clean energy to Snapping Shoals EMC customers for up to 20 years.
Shaun Mock serves as President and Chief Executive Officer of Snapping Shoals EMC.
Snapping Shoals EMC provides service to more than 100,000 residential and business members.
Mike Judd serves as Chief Executive Officer and President of Stryten Energy.
Stryten Energy’s VRFB offers industry-leading power density with a versatile, modular platform that is independently scalable in power and capacity.
An unlimited cycle life, with proper maintenance, enables the VRFB system to operate for more than 20 years without the electrolyte losing energy storage capacity.

The technology is uniquely suited for applications that require medium- to long-duration energy storage of six or more hours to help ensure grid stability and facilitate increased utilization of renewables for businesses and consumers across the U.S.
The Department of Energy reports that Georgia has more than 3.6 gigawatts (GW) of solar, wind, and storage capacity.
There is almost 1.1 GW of additional planned clean energy capacity in the works in the state, which will power more than 155,000 homes.
source_title: SSEMC, Stryten Install Georgia's 1st Vanadium Redox Flow Battery

facts: Stryten Energy has been named a 2026 Silver Military Friendly Employer in its annual list.
The designation recognizes companies that create sustainable and meaningful career paths, community outreach, brand enthusiasm and enduring partnerships that benefit the military community.
Stryten Energy hires military members across every level of the company, from the plant floor to the executive management team.
Many military employees at Stryten Energy have progressed through the company via multiple promotions.
Military veterans make up over seven percent of Stryten’s workforce.
Mike Judd serves as President and Chief Executive Officer at Stryten Energy.
Mike Judd stated that Stryten Energy values the service and sacrifice of military veterans and offers opportunities for them to use skills gained in the military to build fulfilling careers.
Mike Judd expressed pride in the recognition, noting it reaffirms the company's commitment to creating a veteran-friendly culture and hiring veterans.
Institutions earning the Military Friendly Employer designation are evaluated using public data sources and responses from a proprietary survey.
Over 1,200 companies participated in the Military Friendly survey.
Methodology, criteria, and weightings for the evaluation were determined by Military Friendly in consultation with the Military Friendly Advisory Council.

Final ratings combine an organization’s survey score with assessments of its ability to meet thresholds for Recruitment, New Hire Retention, Employee Turnover, and Promotion & Advancement of veterans and military employees.
Kayla Lopez serves as Vice President of Memberships at Military Friendly.
Kayla Lopez stated that earning the designation reflects deep-rooted values and strategic foresight, building pathways for lasting impact rather than performative commitment.
Stryten Energy will be showcased in the 2026 Military Friendly Employers Guide in the Winter issue of G.I. Jobs magazine.
Stryten Energy will also be featured on the Military Friendly website.
Stryten Energy currently has job openings for military members across numerous U.S. locations.
Stryten Energy maintains a dedicated section on its corporate website for military and veteran job seekers.
Stryten Energy participates in events supporting the military and veterans throughout the year.

Stryten Energy is a proud sponsor of Rivers of Recovery, a national nonprofit that uses outdoor-based activities to treat symptoms of post-traumatic stress, depression and anxiety in combat veterans and active-duty military.
Through Operation Gratitude, Stryten Energy employees support active military and their families by making Battalion Buddies and hygiene kits.
Stryten Energy employees hand fill more than 200 Battalion Buddy bears each year for distribution to children of deployed service members.
Stryten Energy employees also assemble hygiene care kits sent to troops deployed overseas.
Stryten Energy is a sponsor for Wreaths Across America, an organization that honors veterans by placing wreaths on their graves during the holidays.
This year, Stryten Energy sponsored 1,000 wreaths to be placed on veterans’ graves at Green Lawn Cemetery & Mausoleum in Roswell, Georgia.
Stryten Energy employees assisted with the placement of the wreaths at the Roswell, Georgia location.

links.context: Military Friendly Employers Guide / Military Friendly website

source_title: North American Clean Energy - Stryten Energy Named a 2026 Military Friendly Employer

facts: Stryten Energy LLC has announced a major plan to expand its domestic manufacturing capacity to 24 gigawatts.
The expansion aims to boost American energy security by increasing battery production across its 11 U.S. plants.
These facilities currently employ over 2,500 workers.
The company's facilities manufacture batteries for applications including military, government, data centers, grid storage, automotive, and material handling.
The expansion investment is backed by advanced manufacturing tax credits to help accelerate growth and strengthen the domestic industrial sector.
Stryten utilizes advanced lead, lithium, and vanadium redox flow battery technologies.
Manufacturing plants in Kansas, Georgia, Iowa, Virginia, and Arkansas will expand production.
The plant expansions are expected to create hundreds of high-paying jobs and boost local economies.
Stryten operates a vertically integrated supply chain to ensure a steady U.S. supply of battery components.
Plants in Pennsylvania, New York, Indiana, Missouri, and Wisconsin produce critical materials for battery manufacturing.
The company is investing in recycling plastic from spent batteries to support sustainability and the circular economy.
Recycled materials are used for new batteries in automobiles, forklifts, and locomotives.
Stryten has opened a lithium battery assembly plant in Georgia to meet military and industrial needs using its proprietary lithium module technology.
Stryten is collaborating with Nevada-based Dragonfly Energy to develop advanced lithium batteries for the automotive aftermarket and battery distributors.

Mike Judd, President and Chief Executive Officer of Stryten Energy, stated that the company is committed to growing domestic energy storage capacity to serve U.S. customers and partners, emphasizing that American energy security relies on domestic companies manufacturing batteries for supply chains and critical infrastructure backup power.
Mike Judd noted that increasing gigawatt capacity across the company's footprint will support growing energy demand and achieve national energy resilience goals.
Stryten recently launched Storion Energy LLC through its affiliate Stryten Critical E-Storage LLC in partnership with Largo Inc. and Largo Clean Energy Corp.
Storion Energy aims to help U.S. battery manufacturers access cost-effective electrolytes for vanadium redox flow batteries (VRFB) to support long-duration energy storage (LDES) solutions.
The expansion strengthens U.S. energy security, creates jobs, and supports sustainable battery production for the future.
source_title: Stryten Energy To Scale Up U.S. Energy Storage With Massive 10 GW Manufacturing Capacity - SolarQuarter

facts: Stryten Energy expands its involvement in the Rebelle Rally as a new Gold Partner for 2026.
Stryten Energy has been involved with the Rebelle Rally for several years through team entries and stage sponsorship prior to becoming an official partner.
Stryten Energy is an energy storage solutions company specializing in battery technologies that support critical industries, including transportation, military, and industrial applications.
In 2024 and 2025, Stryten Energy supported employees Jully Hirt and Amanda Elve by equipping their 2022 Jeep Gladiator with Enhanced Flooded Battery (EFB) technology.
The EFB battery is designed for durability and reliability to withstand high heat and sustained demands.
Mike Judd serves as the CEO and President of Stryten Energy.
In 2026, two employee teams will compete in the Rebelle Rally, with additional teams already training for the 2027 event.
Stryten Energy operates under five core values: Care, Challenge, Commit, Create, and Change.
Stryten Energy is headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia.
Stryten Energy partners with recognized companies to meet the growing demand for reliable and sustainable energy storage capacity.
Stryten Energy powers applications ranging from submarines to subcompacts, microgrids, warehouses, distribution centers, cars, trains, and trucks.
Stryten Energy's stored energy technologies include advanced lead, lithium, and vanadium redox flow batteries, intelligent chargers, and energy performance management software.
Stryten Energy is backed by more than a century of expertise in the industry.
source_title: Rebelle Rally Welcomes Stryten Energy as Official Gold Partner | The AI Journal

facts: Stryten Energy supports the transition to clean energy with sustainable end-to-end storage and management tools for the material handling industry.
The company showcased an extended line of GNB Industrial Power branded energy storage solutions and tools for the material handling industry at MODEX 2022.

Stryten Energy's advanced lead and lithium batteries combined with its intelligent chargers and energy performance management software provide an end-to-end solution to help support the transition to sustainable energy models.

The LiFTFORCE LPX 2.0 lithium battery delivers faster charging, greater efficiency and increased cycle life in a maintenance-free package, offering lower operational costs compared to traditional power sources.
The Tubular LMX advanced lead battery allows up to 90 days between watering intervals for increased uptime and reduced maintenance costs.
The new Fury X-7 charger is part of the 208-240 volt line.
The Fury X-7 charger is available in a 2-bay (7-15 kW) and 4-bay (7-30 kW) configuration to handle fleet demands cost-effectively.
The Fury X-7 chargers can be configured for standard, opportunity or fast charging and include built-in Bluetooth and Wi-Fi for integration with GNB Cloud.
GNB Cloud is an IoT-enabled decision support software suite that makes it easy to design and deploy web-connected battery and charger solutions.
Bret Aker, Vice President of Software Solutions at Stryten Energy, will present at a seminar on Monday, March 28 from 12 p.m. to 12:45 p.m. in Theater F.
Andrea Vasquez, Fleet Energy Data Specialist at Stryten Energy, will present at the same seminar.
The seminar session is titled 'You Have 10,000 Batteries Talking to the Web… What Now? Discover How Advanced IoT Software Can Make Your Life Easier.'
Stryten Energy provides a broad range of energy storage solutions across the Essential Power, Motive Power, Transportation, Military and Government sectors.
Stryten Energy is headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia.
The company partners with recognized companies to meet the growing demand for reliable and sustainable energy storage capacity.
Stryten Energy's stored energy technologies power applications including submarines, subcompacts, microgrids, warehouses, distribution centers, cars, trains and trucks.

facts: Largo Clean Energy Corp. and Stryten Critical E-Storage, LLC finalized the formation of Storion Energy LLC as a joint venture.
The transaction to establish Storion Energy closed effectively on Friday, January 31, 2025.
Storion focuses on removing barriers to entry for battery manufacturers by providing domestically sourced, price-competitive components for the flow battery industry, including vanadium electrolyte.
Each of Largo Clean Energy Corp. and Stryten holds a 50% equity interest in Storion Energy.
Stryten has paid US$1 million in cash directly to Largo and will contribute US$6 million over time to fund Storion’s operations.
Storion’s board composition will be generally proportional to ownership, with Stryten holding one additional seat.
Largo amended its Safekeeping and Supply Agreements with LPV and assigned its Safekeeping Agreement to Storion.
Largo and Storion entered into a separate supply agreement granting Storion a right of first offer for vanadium products.
Storion intends to establish a fully integrated, domestic vertical supply chain for utility-scale vanadium redox flow battery (VRFB) long-duration energy storage (LDES) solutions in the U.S.
VRFB technology is described as a safe and reliable option for Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) that need to provide energy storage of four or more hours.
Storion has locations in Alpharetta, Georgia and Wilmington, Massachusetts.
Stryten Energy is headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia, and provides energy storage solutions across Essential Power, Motive Power, Transportation, Military, and Government sectors.
Stryten’s stored energy technologies include advanced lead, lithium and vanadium redox flow batteries, intelligent chargers and energy performance management software.

Francesco D’Alessio, President of Largo Clean Energy Corp., stated that the transaction closing represents a transformational step for Largo to integrate into the fast-growing vanadium flow battery market.
Through Storion, Largo is establishing a fully integrated vanadium electrolyte supply chain in North America to address key challenges for vanadium flow battery adoption.
Largo Inc. is one of the world’s largest primary vanadium producers, sourcing high-quality vanadium and ilmenite from its Maracás Menchen Mine in Brazil.
Largo maintains a 50% ownership stake in Storion Energy to strategically invest in the clean energy storage sector.
LPV aims to actively supply vanadium to end users of vanadium flow batteries to advance renewable energy integration in long duration storage.

The partnership leverages Largo’s high-quality vanadium supply, LPV’s innovative electrolyte leasing model, and Stryten’s U.S.-based manufacturing expertise to enhance grid stability and energy security.
source_title: Largo and Stryten Energy Finalize Formation of Storion Energy to Strengthen U.S. Energy Resilience and Address Long Duration Energy Storage Needs | Nasdaq

facts: Storion Energy LLC intends to become a leading U.S.-based manufacturer of vanadium electrolyte.
Storion plans to develop additional vanadium flow battery solutions to satisfy projected demand from the long duration energy storage (LDES) sector.
Definitive agreements were signed on December 19, 2024, between Largo Clean Energy Corp. and Stryten Critical E-Storage, LLC to establish Storion Energy LLC.
Francesco D’Alessio serves as Chief Commercial Officer of Largo and President of LCE.
The transaction aims to maximize the value of Largo’s vanadium products and leverage Storion’s patented purification process to accelerate vanadium electrolyte manufacturing.
The partnership is anticipated to increase demand for vanadium from Largo Physical Vanadium Corp. (LPV).

Storion will be positioned as a U.S. domestic leader for vanadium electrolyte production and supply, with future plans to develop competitively positioned vanadium flow battery components for the long-duration energy storage market.
Storion will share product and industry knowledge with Stryten Energy LLC, a leading U.S.-based battery manufacturer.
Significant synergies are anticipated by leveraging complementary assets and integrating top-tier talent from both Largo and Stryten.
Upon closing, LCE and Stryten will each contribute certain vanadium flow battery-related assets and liabilities to Storion.
Upon closing, Stryten will pay US$1 million in cash directly to Largo.
Upon closing, Stryten will contribute to Storion over time a total of US$6 million in cash to fund operations.
LCE and Stryten will each hold a 50% equity interest in Storion upon closing, with customary pre-emption rights and anti-dilution protections.
Board representation will be proportional to ownership, with Stryten holding one additional seat while ownership interests remain similar.
At closing, Largo will amend its Safekeeping and Supply Agreements with LPV to adjust commercial terms and assign the Safekeeping Agreement to Storion.
At closing, Largo and Storion will enter into a separate supply agreement giving Storion a right of first offer to purchase vanadium products.
Gallatin Capital LLC acted as financial advisor to Largo, and Linklaters LLP acted as legal counsel for the transaction.
Storion intends to remove barriers to entry for battery manufacturers by providing domestically sourced, price-competitive electrolyte for vanadium redox flow batteries (VRFB) used in LDES.
Storion will maintain locations in Wilmington, Massachusetts and Alpharetta, Georgia.
The JV is expected to support scalable domestic electrolyte production to establish a fully integrated vertical supply chain for utility-scale VRFB LDES solutions.
VRFB technology provides long-duration energy storage greater than four hours to ensure grid stability and facilitate increased utilization of renewables for businesses and consumers.
Stryten Energy is headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia, and partners with major companies to meet demand for reliable and sustainable energy storage capacity.
Stryten’s stored energy technologies include advanced lead, lithium and vanadium redox flow batteries, intelligent chargers, and energy performance management software.
Largo Physical Vanadium (LPV) aims to provide direct exposure to physical vanadium, a non-degrading and fully recyclable metal essential for steel, aerospace, and energy storage industries.
LPV’s strategy includes actively supplying vanadium to end users of vanadium flow batteries to advance renewable energy integration in long duration storage.
Largo produces critical materials from its Maracás Menchen Mine in Brazil and is one of the world’s largest primary vanadium producers.
Largo holds a 50% ownership stake in Storion Energy as part of its strategic investment in the clean energy storage sector.

Forward-looking statements assume collaborative party performance and management execution, with risks including transaction completion failure, inability to realize benefits, and capital raising challenges.
source_title: Largo and Stryten Energy to Form Storion Energy for the Manufacture of Vanadium Electrolyte and Battery Components for Long Duration Energy Storage - Junior Mining Network

facts: South Korean companies are reconsidering their US$54 billion investment blitz to build electric vehicle (EV) battery plants in the US.
The reconsideration is driven by concerns that president-elect Donald Trump could undo tax credits for EVs.
Some Korean companies have slowed or hit the pause button on ongoing construction of some plants due to concerns about reduced EV demand and potential policy changes under Trump's second term.
Posco Future M, which makes cathodes for General Motors, delayed completion of its plant in Quebec due to “local conditions” per a September filing.
Kenny Kim, CEO at Seoul-based SNE Research (a firm focusing on Korean battery makers), stated that many companies are anxious about how much Trump would slash government incentives for the EV market.
Trump has long criticized President Joe Biden’s efforts to subsidise EVs through the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA).
The incoming administration is looking to slash fuel-efficiency requirements.
According to a Reuters report last month, the incoming administration could eliminate the key US$7,500 consumer-tax credit.
Ending hundreds of billions of dollars in subsidies, tax credits, and incentives would threaten tens of thousands of US jobs.
Ending these incentives would undo years of work shifting the global EV supply chain away from China.
Cutting incentives could hit the earnings of Korean firms, which are key US partners in reducing reliance on Chinese suppliers.
Korean firms are already suffering from weaker demand for EVs and falling battery prices.

Byeonghoon Kim, CEO at Ecopro Materials (a supplier of precursor materials for EV batteries used by Ford Motor and General Motors), said: “We are paying attention to every single word from Trump” about EVs.
Byeonghoon Kim added: “We have considered the Inflation Reduction Act as a very important issue so far... If there’s any change in the policy, we may have to change our strategy too.”
The Biden administration last week offered US$7.5 billion to help finance a joint venture between Samsung SDI and Stellantis to create cell manufacturing plants in Indiana.
The Trump transition team quickly called the US$7.5 billion offer into question.
Vivek Ramaswamy, nominated co-chair of Trump’s Department of Government Efficiency, said on X that the department would scrutinise the facility.
South Korea’s three largest battery makers – Samsung SDI, LG Energy Solution, and SK On – have announced 15 battery plants in the US.
This represents the most aggressive investment drive of any of the world’s three major battery hubs: China, Japan, and South Korea.
Half of the Korean plants were announced after the IRA was signed in 2022.
The announced plants promise to create more than 20,000 jobs in total.
The plants are located largely in the “battery belt”, which stretches from Michigan through Ohio and Kentucky to Georgia.
Park Tae-sung, vice-chairman at the Korea Battery Industry Association, stated: “South Korea has contributed to creating jobs and investments in the Rust Belt.”
Park Tae-sung noted that having Korean battery suppliers is beneficial for the US to win competition against China-dominated supply chains for EVs.
The Korea Battery Industry Association is in talks with US authorities to lobby to keep the credits.
EV batteries accounted for about half of all foreign direct investment (FDI) and reshoring from overseas in the US between 2021 and the first quarter of 2024, according to Reshoring Initiative data.
Korean FDI and reshoring in the US created 20,360 jobs in North America in 2023, more than any other country.
Prices of lithium, a key mineral tied to EV battery selling prices, plunged nearly 90% from their highs in 2022 due to slower-than-expected EV adoption.
LG Energy Solution, a key partner of GM, booked about one trillion won (S$943 million) of IRA credits in its accounts so far this year but analysts expect a net loss for fiscal year 2023.
SK On, the partner of Ford, received about 211 billion won in tax credits from the US during the first three quarters, yet still reported an operating loss during the period.
Korean companies worry Trump might allow Chinese battery companies to enter the US market.
CATL said it will consider building a US plant if Trump opens the door, according to Reuters.
The IRA has blocked investments from China by asking carmakers to gradually reduce sourcing critical battery minerals from “foreign entities of concern”.
Park Chulwan, professor in the car engineering department at Seojeong University, warned: “China’s entry to the US would be a disaster for Korea. Chinese battery firms would offer much lower prices.”
Some industry players are optimistic Trump will not cut credits given their plants are mostly located in Republican-governed states.

Kitae Kim, CEO at SungEel Recycling Park Indiana (a battery recycler under construction in Whitestown, Indiana), said: “I don’t think there is a high chance they will reduce the benefits in the Inflation Reduction Act.”
The state of Georgia is home to four SK On plants.

Pat Wilson, Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner, stated in an email that Georgia will help Korean companies “maximise their ability to efficiently reach consumers across the country”.

Pat Wilson added: “The US market is still the most important consumer market in the world. Korean companies knew that prior to the Biden Administration, and that fact will not change with the new administration.”
source_title: Trump EV scepticism threatens US$54 billion in South Korean investments - The Business Times

facts: A Korean recycler of lithium-ion batteries is the latest company plugging into the growing electric-vehicle value chain being constructed in Georgia largely with foundational links partly provided by other foreign-owned firms.
SungEel HiTech will build a $37 million factory in Toccoa, located in Stephens County in the northeastern part of the state.
SungEel Recycling Park Georgia LLC, the local subsidiary, plans to hire 104 people at the plant.
The plant will recover cobalt, nickel, lithium and other metals.
SungEel takes secondary batteries from phones, power tools and other vehicles.

SungEel puts them through a proprietary process to create a battery powder that can be further separated into materials prized by cathode manufacturers and battery producers like fellow Korean investor SK On.
SK On has a massive plant just down Interstate 85 in Commerce.
SungEel is the latest participant in a growing group of companies aiming to create a closed-loop supply chain for battery production in the state.
Lawmakers hope the growing group will lead to greater resiliency and a cleaner environment.
Recycling tends to be less energy intensive and emits less carbon than mining the primary materials.
Germany’s Aurubis and Japan’s Denkai both are putting hundreds of millions of dollars into copper plants in Augusta.
Ascend Elements has a separate lithium-ion recycling facility in Covington.
The SungEel deal was announced just after the passage last week of the Inflation Reduction Act.
Under the Inflation Reduction Act, electric vehicles must be made in North America and their batteries produced with metals sourced from the region to qualify for subsidies.
One goal is to wean the U.S. off its dependence on Chinese-made EV batteries.

Georgia is “cultivating a vertically integrated supply chain that will help companies increase efficiencies by reducing the reliance on imported materials,” according to the Georgia Department of Economic Development.

“SungEel HiTech’s entry into Georgia is the last piece of the puzzle to build a sustainable ecosystem of Georgia’s electric vehicle supply chain,” said Suk Jae Yim, representative of SungEel Recycling Park Georgia.
The SungEel project took eight months to come to fruition, said Brian Akin, Chairman of the Stephens County Development Authority.
The company picked a Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development, or GRAD, site, one of many prepared for fast-track development around the state.

“The investments that we have made in infrastructure, workforce development, and education were all significant factors in the selection process, and we couldn’t be any happier with their decision to expand here,” Mr. Akin said.
SungEel HiTech operates plants in South Korea, China, Malaysia, India, and Hungary.
New facilities are being built in Poland and Germany.
The Georgia plant should be on line by 2024.
source_title: Korean Battery Recycler Is Latest in Parade of Metals Firms Investing in Georgia - Global Atlanta

facts: SungEel Hitech has a recycling capacity of 50,000 tonnes.

The company offers an extensive portfolio of wrinkle finishes that provide superior protection and enhance the aesthetic appeal of surfaces.
TCI Powder Coatings focuses on customer satisfaction and eco-friendly practices.
The company's dedication to research and development ensures clients receive the latest advancements in the powder coating industry.
Wintoly Coatings' wrinkle powder coatings are widely used in automotive parts, furniture, electrical enclosures, machinery, lighting, and architectural applications.
NIC manufactures coatings with applications in the automotive industry, technology, aerospace, and sporting goods.
Axalta's advanced wrinkle powder coatings are engineered to conceal surface imperfections while providing excellent mechanical and chemical resistance for industrial uses.
TIGER Coatings serves architectural, furniture, industrial equipment, HVAC, and general manufacturing applications worldwide with its wrinkle powder coating portfolio.
Wrinkle powder coating is a textured finish that creates a unique matte, wrinkled surface when cured.
It is widely used on metal substrates to hide surface imperfections while providing a durable, anti-glare, and scratch-resistant coating.
The coating is popular in industrial machinery, electrical enclosures, automotive parts, metal furniture, power tools, hardware, and architectural metal applications.
Typical curing requirements for wrinkle powder coatings are 160°C–200°C for 15–30 minutes.
Best application results require proper surface preparation, electrostatic spray, and a film thickness of 60–120 microns.
