facts: The Greater Hall Chamber of Commerce presented its 2018 Industry of the Year Awards at the 10th Annual Industry Appreciation Luncheon on November 13, 2018.

As reported by the Gainesville-Hall County Economic Development Council, 2018 year-to-date announcements in Gainesville-Hall County included 17 new and expanded businesses with 400 new jobs and $200 million in capital investment.
Since 2013, 150 new and expanded businesses in Gainesville-Hall County have announced 5,400 jobs and $1 billion in capital investment.
ALBAform, Inc. employs 98 people and manufactures precision metal components, primarily wire frames for seating for the automotive industry.
In the last 12 months prior to November 2018, ALBAform grew its customer base by 17% and added eight robotic machines.
ALBAform created new jobs in the last 12 months and offers employees company paid benefits, Paid Time Off, Tuition Assistance, and an attendance recognition program.
ABB's local manufacturing facility in Flowery Branch produces over 60 highly custom engineered electric motors per day.
ABB has been employing Work Based Learning High School students since 2015 as well as college interns in the fields of design and manufacturing engineering.
ABB is actively engaged with the STEM program at Flowery Branch High School.
Kubota Manufacturing of America employs over 1,500 local area residents in Gainesville.
Kubota employs up to 30 high school students each year in Work Based Learning and 15 College Internships.
Kubota partnered with the University of North Georgia to offer a 12 month management development course for new managers.
Kubota recently made a $1 million dollar donation to Lanier Technical College.
Wilheit Packaging participated in a partnership with Southern Waste & Recycling, Kubota Manufacturing of America, and the Hall County Recycling Center.
The Wilheit Packaging partnership diverted 1,500 tons of fiber and 160 tons of plastic from the landfill in nine months.
Lanier Technical College sponsored the Greater Hall Chamber’s 2018 Industry of the Year Awards program.
Jan Vintrlik serves as President of ALBAform, Inc.
Monika Vintrlikova serves as Vice President of ALBAform, Inc.
Alex Villalobos serves as Production Manager at ALBAform, Inc.
Mike Holman is the Plant Manager at ABB's Flowery Branch facility.
source_title: 2018 Industry of the Year Awards - Greater Hall Chamber of Commerce

facts: An $800 million project with Anovion Technologies will be on a 1.5 million square foot factory.
The facility will create over 400 jobs for residents of Decatur County.
Specifically, 412 jobs will be created with the groundbreaking.
Anovion is an American-owned, climate-tech-driven company that broke ground in Bainbridge, Georgia.
The project aims to push Georgia to be the number one state for the path of e-mobility.
Georgia Senator Jon Ossoff stated that manufacturing incentives and infrastructure policy are bringing jobs and investments to the state of Georgia.
U.S. Congressman Sanford Bishop said the project will contribute to American energy independence, lower costs for families and businesses, and create jobs in Southwest Georgia.
Anovion is an upstream supplier to battery makers that produce lithium-ion batteries for vehicles.
The company manufactures materials that go into those lithium-ion batteries.
Those batteries are used in everyday items like cellphones, cars, and medical devices like pacemakers.
Anovion is bringing the capability of making synthetic graphite back to the United States.
Most synthetic graphite production currently happens in Asia and China.
Chairman and Lead Investor Chip Dunn stated that Bainbridge's location provides access to ports like Savannah or Panama City (Florida), or possibly the Bainbridge port itself.
The facility requires significant power, and Georgia Power is developing big new infrastructure for them.
Anovion's average wage is $63,000 a year.
Executive Director of the Developmental Authority Rick McCaskill identified keeping young people in Bainbridge after graduation as a main local problem.
The new jobs are hoped to serve as an incentive to keep residents in the city.
The technical and high-quality jobs will cover roles from warehouse to office, to manufacturing to supervision.
Anovion plans to provide scholarships for deserving students from the area to attend Southern Regional Technical College.
President of Southern Regional Technical College Jim Glass commented on the educational doors opening for local students.
City Manager of Bainbridge Chris Harvey agreed that graduates will have jobs to come home to after high school, technical college, or college education.
Bainbridge is considered an ideal location for the facility due to its great location and potential to help reduce a carbon footprint while increasing Georgia's clean air manufacturing capacity.
State leaders describe this as one of the fastest-growing projects in Georgia.
The grand opening is planned for 2025.
source_title: New manufacturing facility breaks ground in Bainbridge

facts: Anovion Technologies is one of the first U.S. suppliers of synthetic graphite anode materials for lithium-ion batteries used in electric vehicles.
Gov. Brian Kemp announced the Anovion project last May (2023).
The facility will be a 1.5 million-square-foot greenfield building located in the Downrange Industrial Park.
Production at the Anovion site is set to begin in the first quarter of 2025.
The project is expected to create 400 jobs.
The investment was initially announced as $800 million but has since grown to a record $1.2 billion.

Rick McCaskill, executive director of the Development Authority of Bainbridge/Decatur County, notes that Anovion's supply role ties into the Rivian electric vehicle manufacturing plant near Social Circle, Georgia, which is slated to begin production in 2026.
The city added infrastructure to an undeveloped section of the Downrange Industrial Park specifically for Anovion, including a water tower, sewer lines, and natural gas services.

Mayor Edward Reynolds states that the state helped fund these industrial park infrastructure projects, while federal money and support from U.S. Rep. Sanford Bishop assisted with the sewer treatment plant and water tower.
Bainbridge-Decatur County Chamber of Commerce President Karen Tobin focuses on workforce development to provide a qualified workforce for incoming manufacturing companies like Anovion.
The chamber partners with Southern Regional Technical College and Abraham Baldwin Agricultural College on youth programs in Decatur County high schools to support local talent pipelines.
Local companies are willing to foot the bill to certify students through dual enrollment programs if they are not pursuing a four-year university degree.
Rural Zone Tax Incentives for rehabilitation and job creation were implemented between 2018 and 2022, acting as a "game changer" for attracting industry and downtown growth.
Between October 2022 and September 2023, $6.2 million in private investments went toward rehabilitating nine buildings downtown, strengthening the local business ecosystem.
The city completed a $28 million wastewater treatment plant renovation last fall and another $30 million in water and sewer upgrades to support industrial and population growth.
A second Transportation Special Purpose Local Option Sales Tax (TSPLOST) allotment will fund complete street resurfacing and sidewalk repairs to improve infrastructure for workers and businesses.
Decatur County experienced a 14% population increase per the last census, making it one of the fastest-growing communities in Southwest Georgia, which impacts local workforce availability.
Many skilled workers currently live in Decatur County but commute to Tallahassee, Thomasville, Dothan, or Albany for work; retaining them locally is a key focus for economic development leaders.
source_title: Bainbridge | Decatur County: Working in Synergy - Georgia Trend Magazine

facts: At the 2023 Paris Air Show in France on Tuesday, Pratt & Whitney announced a $206 million expansion to its Columbus campus, promising an additional 400 jobs.

Since the mid-1980s, the Connecticut-based company has used Georgia’s second-largest city, which hugs the Alabama border, as its home base for manufacturing critical components for its military and commercial engines.
Airbus is a major aerospace customer of Pratt & Whitney and supplies passenger jets to Atlanta-based Delta Air Lines and carriers around the world.

Gov. Brian Kemp attended the Paris Air Show where multiple Georgia-related announcements were made and touted the investment in a news release as a sign that the state’s sky-high business ambitions are being rewarded.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated: “From the flights that go through the world’s most utilized airport to the engines in the very airplanes, the Peach State is crucial to the movement of goods and products.”
Gov. Brian Kemp added: “Pratt & Whitney’s decision to expand will only further that reputation.”

Georgia is home to Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport, the world’s busiest airport, and more than 800 aerospace facilities, including several operated by Lockheed Martin and private jet-maker Gulfstream.

Last year, Georgia factories shipped $9.2 billion of aerospace products across the globe, making the state the fifth-largest exporter in the United States, according to the Georgia Department of Economic Development.

Pratt & Whitney has an existing employee base of roughly 2,000 in Columbus, where workers maintain hundreds of engines and produce compressor airfoils — the metal blades seen whirring in a jet turbine that produce thrust.
The expansion is expected to be completed by 2028 and will include 81,000 square feet of new engine maintenance space and new machinery.
The new capacity will allow the company to annually overhaul 400 Geared Turbofan (GTF) engines.
Shane Eddy, president of Pratt & Whitney, stated: “Our Columbus business has grown from a small manufacturing facility to a large, state-of-the-art manufacturing and overhaul center.”
Shane Eddy added: “This investment will help ensure that we have the infrastructure, machinery and equipment upgrades in place to provide the best products and services to our customers worldwide.”
Pratt & Whitney will likely qualify for job creation tax credits and worker training through Georgia Quick Start.
A Georgia Department of Economic Development spokesperson said negotiations involving discretionary incentives, such as grants, remain active.
The expansion comes on the heels of a new engine repair shop by Delta TechOps, which services Pratt & Whitney’s GTF engines.
Delta TechOps’ 155,000-square-foot facility houses more than 100 mechanics who repair engines used across about 150 airlines.
Archer Aviation announced progress on its forthcoming 100-acre campus in Covington, east of Atlanta, at the Paris Air Show.
The California-based company plans to build its first mass production facility for electric vertical takeoff and landing (eVTOL) aircraft for taxi service.
The factory is a partnership with European auto brand Stallantis and is on track to open in mid-2024.

Archer’s founder and CEO, Adam Goldstein, stated: “High-volume manufacturing is critical to ensuring we can meet this goal and joining forces with one of the leading mobility companies in the world is helping us realize the once-in-a-generation opportunity we have to redefine urban transportation.”
Qcells announced a solar panels manufacturing project in Dalton and Cartersville, promising 2,500 jobs and $2.5 billion in investment.
Anovion Technologies announced a synthetic graphite manufacturing project in Bainbridge, promising 400-plus jobs and $800 million in investment.

More than a half-dozen Hyundai suppliers are located in several areas of coastal Georgia for the future Hyundai Motor Group EV factory, collectively promising more than 2,600 jobs and $2 billion in investment.
Anderson Corp. announced an energy-efficient window and door manufacturing project in Henry County, promising 900 jobs and $420 million in investment.
Admares announced a manufactured housing and commercial buildings project in Waycross, promising 1,400 jobs and $750 million in investment.
Sam’s Club announced a distribution center project in Lithia Springs, promising 600 jobs and $142 million investment.
Meissner announced a pharmaceutical manufacturing and research and development project in Athens, promising 1,700-plus jobs and $250 million in investment.
source_title: Jet Engine Maker Announces Big Georgia Expansion at Paris Air Show | Aviation Pros

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp and the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) announced that Georgia earned the No. 1 spot in Site Selection’s annual Prosperity Cup rankings for successful investment recruitment in 2023.
The Prosperity Cup ranking criteria include the total values of new and expanded facilities in the state, business-climate attractiveness, and workforce readiness.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that the award adds to a decade of evidence that Georgia's business environment is working for job creators.
Georgia provides access to global markets through logistics assets including Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport and some of the fastest-growing ports in the nation.
In 2023, Georgia attracted 318 new and expanded facilities to secure its No. 1 ranking.
The Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) project represents an expanded scope and is the largest project in state history.
HMGMA announced an additional 400 new jobs and $2 billion in investment in 2023, bringing its total investment to $7.59 billion.
The HMGMA project will net 8,500 new jobs for Georgians.
Anovion Technologies selected southwest Georgia in May for an $800 million, 400-job synthetic graphite manufacturing facility.
Anovion Technologies broke ground on its synthetic graphite manufacturing facility in southwest Georgia in June of last year (2023).
Meissner Corporation plans to create more than 1,700 new jobs at a new campus in northeast Georgia that will more than double the company’s U.S. manufacturing footprint.

Site Selection’s Mark Arend stated that the Prosperity Cup recognizes state-level economic development competitiveness and that Georgia consistently delivers location attributes required by capital investors.
Following HMGMA's groundbreaking in October 2022, Georgia welcomed more than 10 Hyundai and automotive suppliers to middle Georgia and beyond in 2023.
These Hyundai and automotive suppliers will ultimately create over 4,300 new jobs and bring $1.2 billion in combined investment.
Kia’s facility in West Point announced an expansion during 2023.
Toyota’s campus in Pendergrass announced an expansion during 2023.
The Kia and Toyota expansions created a combined 440 new jobs and $286 million in investment.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Georgia's economic development team is collaborative, working with sister state agencies, utility partners, and local economic developers to match companies with communities.
The top Prosperity Cup spot reaffirms the effectiveness of Georgia's decades-long partnerships and consistent pro-business policies in intentional economic development.

links.context: Full article on Georgia’s selection for this recognition or more information on the 2024 Prosperity Cup

source_title: Georgia Scores Top Spot in Competitive Economic Rankings - Valdosta CEO

entity_name: Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) May 2023 Investment Announcements
canonical_name: Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) May 2023 Investment Announcements
facts: During the month of May, the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) announced that three companies are investing a combined total of $1.59 billion to establish new business operations in Georgia.
On May 15, GDEcD announced that Anovion Technologies will build a new manufacturing facility in Bainbridge.
Anovion Technologies supplies premium synthetic graphite anode materials for lithium-ion batteries.
The Anovion project is expected to create more than 400 jobs and over $800 million in investment in Decatur County.
Governor Kemp announced the Anovion facility, noting it boosts Georgia's future as an e-mobility capital.
Eric Stopka, CEO of Anovion, stated the company is thrilled to be opening its first large-scale expansion manufacturing facility in Georgia.
Stopka cited the state’s strong technical workforce and top-tier schools to help advance research partnerships and contribute to a highly skilled talent pipeline for climate technology jobs.
The week after the Anovion announcement, GDEcD revealed that Hanon Systems will create 160 new jobs and invest more than $40 million in a new manufacturing facility in Bulloch County.
Hanon Systems is a global provider of automotive thermal and energy management solutions.
The Hanon facility will focus on assembling automotive thermal solutions.
Hanon Systems is a supplier to Hyundai Motor Group.
Hanon's newly-announced operations are expected to begin in May 2024, according to GDEcD.

Min Sung, President and CEO of Hanon Systems, stated the company is pleased to contribute to the local economy of Bulloch County through job creation and thanked the State of Georgia and agency cooperation.
The third May investment project announced is a $750 million investment from ADMARES to construct its first U.S. manufacturing facility in Ware County.
ADMARES specializes in industrialized manufacturing of buildings and homes.
ADMARES plans to construct a 2.5-million-square-foot build-to-suit manufacturing facility in Waycross to specialize in producing buildings for the housing sector.
Mikael Hedberg, Founder and CEO of ADMARES, stated Waycross is an ideal location due to easy access to major highways and extensive rail connections.
Hedberg noted that proximity to the Port of Brunswick offers a competitive advantage for global trade.

links.context: Learn more about Anovion Technologies investment
Learn more about Hanon Systems investment

source_title: Three Investment Projects Coming to Georgia Totaling $1.59 Billion Announced Last Month - Tennessee Star

entity_name: U.S. Electric Vehicle Supply Chain & Policy Landscape
canonical_name: U.S. Electric Vehicle Supply Chain & Policy Landscape
facts: Jay Turner, a professor of environmental studies at Wellesley College and author of Charged, tallied 48 new EV supply chain projects representing a combined $50.3 billion in investment as of late May.
The tally increased by at least $3.8 billion over the previous 30 days.
General Motors and Samsung SDI announced plans to invest more than $3 billion in a battery factory since late April.
Anovion, a maker of graphite for lithium-ion batteries, unveiled plans for an $800 million production facility in Georgia.
Tesla began construction of a $375 million lithium refining plant in Texas expected to supply enough material for the lithium-ion batteries in about 1 million EVs per year by 2025.
The U.S. has seen 331 gigawatt-hours of new planned battery production capacity announced since the Inflation Reduction Act became law.
The U.S. is on track to produce 910 gigawatt-hours’ worth of batteries each year by 2030.
This capacity is projected to supply 5.3 million EVs per year in the U.S. by 2030, up from 1.6 million per year currently.
Jay Turner described the investments as "enormous, unprecedented" and noted the U.S. has never seen this scale of investment before.
The Biden administration has set a goal of EVs making up half of all new light-duty vehicle sales by 2030.
The Inflation Reduction Act boosted ambitions with tax credits and incentives for domestic manufacturing of EVs, batteries, critical materials, and for consumers/companies buying them.

Automakers including BMW, Ford, General Motors, Hyundai, Mercedes, Nissan, Stellantis, Volkswagen, and Chinese companies plan to invest a collective $1.2 trillion through 2030 in EVs and batteries globally, according to an October 2022 Reuters analysis.
Atlas Public Policy forecasted $860 billion invested by 2030, with nearly a quarter ($210 billion) going to the U.S., more than any other country.
China overwhelmingly dominates much of the EV supply chain, from mineral refining to cathode/anode production, battery cells, and EV manufacturing.
U.S. EV and battery capacity may expand roughly twentyfold over the next decade but starts from a minuscule share of global supply.
The debate centers on the Inflation Reduction Act’s 30D tax credits of up to $7,500 for purchasing plug-in or fuel-cell EVs.
Eligibility depends on complex rules regarding battery and material sourcing from the U.S. or free-trade partner countries.
A future implementation stage could begin as early as next year, making tax credits unavailable for EVs containing materials/products from "foreign entities of concern," including China.
U.S. Senator Joe Manchin played a pivotal role in crafting the tax credit and enabling the Inflation Reduction Act's passage.
Manchin wants strict limits on foreign battery components to push manufacturers to set up shop in the U.S.
Manchin threatened to sue the federal government over the Treasury Department's April rollout of relaxed guidance on domestic and free-trade-partner content requirements.
Rules require at least half of the value of battery components to be produced or assembled in North America to qualify for $3,750 of the credit.
40 percent of the value of critical minerals must be sourced from the U.S. or a free-trade-agreement country to qualify for the other half.
These requirements will rise by 10 percentage points per year.
Rules effective since the start of the year require EVs to undergo final assembly in North America to qualify for the tax credit.
The final assembly rule disqualifies EVs from Hyundai and Kia, which are assembled in South Korea, prompting them to plan moving some production to the U.S.
Domestic-content rules disqualify EV models from Audi, BMW, and Nissan, and limit others like the Ford Mustang Mach-E to partial credits of $3,750.

The full $7,500 credit is available for the Tesla Model Y and about a dozen other models including the Cadillac Lyriq, Ford F-150 Lightning, Volkswagen ID.4, Chevy Bolt, Blazer, Equinox, and Silverado.
Eligible models currently make up more than 90 percent of the U.S. EV market, according to Albert Gore, executive director of the Zero Emission Transportation Association (ZETA).
ZETA represents automakers, battery manufacturers, mining companies, charging equipment makers, utilities, and other companies invested in a growing domestic EV market.
Albert Gore noted it will take time for North America’s EV supply chains to develop sufficient capacity to serve increasingly stringent sourcing requirements.

Gore stated the Inflation Reduction Act tries to accomplish three overlapping goals: making EVs plentiful/affordable, securing the supply chain by onshoring capacity from vehicles to batteries and minerals, and moving the supply chain away from China toward free-trade agreement countries.
The Biden administration announced a late March trade deal with Japan that could allow Japanese EV battery minerals to qualify under 30D tax-credit rules.
Industry observers expect similar efforts with the European Union and other allies in the coming months.
The pot of money for manufacturers via tax credits is many times larger than the $7,500 consumer credit.
Gore identified the 45X production tax credits as more powerful for encouraging domestic production of cathodes and battery components.
45X credits include up to $45 per kilowatt-hour for domestically produced batteries and 10 percent of production cost for cathode/anode materials and critical minerals.
These credits were designed to close the cost gap between U.S.-made batteries and Chinese batteries, and help achieve EV cost parity with internal-combustion vehicles.
Atlas Public Policy tallied at least $83 billion in loans, grants, and tax credits from the IRA and 2021 infrastructure law for the domestic EV industry.
This includes $8.1 billion of grants and at least $25 billion of loans specifically for low- or zero-emissions vehicle production.
The U.S. Department of Energy has $7 billion in grant money from the infrastructure law to directly fund battery and critical-mineral supply chains.
The first round of DOE grants in October awarded $2.8 billion to 20 companies, expected to leverage more than $9 billion in private investment.
Some U.S. EV supply chain companies fear lax rules may open loopholes for imported products that will stifle U.S. competitiveness.
6K, a U.S.-based company, won a $107 million DOE grant in October to develop plasma-based technology for refining minerals into cathode-grade materials.
Thanh Nguyen, 6K’s senior vice president of deployment, stated they must leapfrog Chinese technology because copying China is impossible on cost.

Current Treasury rules allow 30D tax credits for EVs with batteries containing cathodes made in China or non-free-trade partners, as long as at least 50 percent of the completed product's value comes from U.S. or free-trade partner minerals/materials.
Mary Cronin, 6K’s senior vice president of government affairs, warned this may undermine efforts to get U.S. battery manufacturers to use domestically sourced cathode material.
Cronin called current supply-side incentives "a good start" but "a drop in the bucket."

CelLink CEO Kevin Coakley highlighted challenges for his company's next-generation battery-pack interconnection circuits, which reduce about 70 percent of weight and 90 percent of space compared to traditional systems.
CelLink won a conditional $362 million loan commitment from DOE’s Loan Programs Office to build its first large-scale U.S. manufacturing site in Texas.
CelLink has already integrated its technology into battery packs used by at least one major EV maker with about 2.5 million vehicles on the road.

Coakley noted that specifying a domestic value for this part of a battery could help his technology gain traction, but current Treasury guidance appears to discount the innovation and creates ambiguity/loopholes.
Gore acknowledged policymakers face a chicken-and-egg problem in crafting industrial policy that accommodates current lack of U.S. battery-materials capacity while encouraging domestic production.
South Korea announced it would make $5.3 billion in government loans and guarantees to South Korean companies to expand production in the United States.
Republicans in Congress joined Manchin in attacking Biden administration EV supply-chain policies, claiming they benefit Chinese companies at the expense of U.S. competitors.

DOE canceled a $200 million grant to Texas-based battery manufacturer Microvast following Republican attacks based on its inclusion in a 2022 SEC list of companies suspected of intellectual-property violations in China.
Ford’s joint venture with Chinese lithium-ion battery giant CATL to build batteries in Michigan has also come under scrutiny.
The Treasury Department has yet to issue guidance on barring products/materials passing through China at any stage from 30D tax credits.

Sam Jaffe, vice president of battery storage solutions at E Source, noted that barring such materials could immediately cut out a large proportion of currently eligible EVs and drive demand for non-Chinese sources.
Eric Stopka, CEO of Anovion (formed from Amsted Graphite Materials and Pyrotek’s battery materials division), won a $117 million DOE grant in October to support graphite production for EV batteries.
Anovion's Georgia facility will be capable of producing 40,000 metric tons of synthetic graphite per year starting in 2025, scaling up to 150,000 metric tons a year eventually.
It takes about 1,200 metric tons of graphite to make a gigawatt-hour’s worth of lithium-ion batteries.
North America will need 1.2 million metric tons per year to supply a forecasted annual battery manufacturing capacity of 1,000 gigawatt-hours by 2030.
China currently controls more than 90 percent of global graphite production capacity with virtually no production in North America.
Stopka said Anovion will rely on 45X tax credits to bolster cost-competitiveness over the coming decade.
Stopka emphasized the need to deploy capital and realize earnings without Chinese competitors undercutting prices to eliminate U.S. competition.

Stopka acknowledged the complexity of creating policies that both get people to buy EVs and support a North American supply base, worrying that yet-to-be-released Treasury rules on foreign entities may fail to clearly establish qualifying supply chains for 30D credits.
source_title: The US has big EV goals. Can the domestic EV industry… | Canary Media

facts: Apache Mills is widely recognized as the leading floor mat manufacturer worldwide.
Apache Mills employs over 800 associates in its manufacturing and distribution facilities located in Calhoun, Georgia USA.
Last year alone Apache Mills used in excess of 50,000,000 pounds of recycled tires in its production processes, keeping over 3 million tires out of landfills.
Apache Mills continually invests in new technologies revolving around the use of recycled materials.
Apache Mills products are proudly crafted in the USA.
Apache floor mats and matting products are sold through Home Matting Retailers and Commercial Matting Distributors all over the world.
Apache Mills has an estimated annual revenue of $94.4M per year.
Apache Mills has an estimated revenue per employee of $313,750.
Apache Mills has 301 employees, having grown its employee count by 21% last year.
source_title: Apache Mills: Revenue, Competitors, Alternatives

facts: Apache Mills was founded in 1970.
Apache Mills is one of the largest mat manufacturers in the United States.
The company operates four business units: Apache at Home, Apache Commercial, Apache Industry, and AMI.
Apache Mills designs and manufactures high-quality floor mats, rugs, and OEM materials for residential, commercial, and industrial markets.
The company's manufacturing facility is located in Calhoun, Georgia.
The manufacturing facility covers 750,000 square feet.
Apache Mills employs over 600 people.
Art Director Jeff Young has used Wasatch software for nearly 20 years.
Apache Mills uses Wasatch SoftRIP along with McDermid AutoType to produce film positives for screen printing on various mat materials.
The company utilizes a Roland Soljet PRO III XJ-640 printer for its production process.
Apache Mills provides solutions for all mat requirements across commercial, industrial, and residential product lines.
source_title: Apache Mills

facts: Ascend Elements operates a battery recycling facility located in Covington, GA, USA.
The facility is situated at coordinates -83.8440563, 33.61416667 within Congressional District GA13.
The project announcement date was 1/5/2022.
The target for starting production was set for 2022.
Commercial production officially started on 3/1/2023.
The facility was not announced post-Inflation Reduction Act.
Target annual production is 30000 tpa.
Current annual production is 3000 tpa.
Operating status is Operating.
Target jobs for the facility is 150.
Capital investment is $50 million.
No government subsidies are currently tracked for this site.
The sector is Batteries, with activity focused on Recycling and producing Recycled Materials.
source_title: The Big Green Machine |  Ascend Elements | Base 1 | Covington, GA

facts: Ascend Elements, a U.S.-based manufacturer of sustainable, engineered battery materials for electric vehicles, has raised $542 million in new equity investments.
The $542 million equity financing includes $460 million in Series D investments and $82 million in earlier investments this year.
The investment will help Ascend Elements realize its vision of commercially producing sustainable, engineered battery materials.
The sustainable lithium-ion battery materials will power electric vehicle batteries and hasten the global transition to zero carbon emissions.
Series D funding for Ascend Elements was led by Decarbonization Partners, Temasek of Singapore, and Qatar Investment Authority (QIA).

Other investors include Tenaska, Alliance Resource Partners, PULSE – CMA CGM Energy Fund, BHP Ventures, Fifth Wall, Hitachi Ventures, Mirae Asset, At One Ventures, Agave Partners, Alumni Ventures and other leading global investors.
The $542 million equity financing is one of the ten largest cleantech equity private placements in the United States in 2023.
The $542 million equity financing is one of the ten largest equity private placements in the United States this year.
The funding will facilitate construction of the Apex 1 facility in Hopkinsville, Kentucky.
The Apex 1 facility will be the first sustainable cathode precursor (pCAM) and cathode active material (CAM) manufacturing facility in North America.
CAM and pCAM are engineered materials manufactured to exact microstructure specifications for use in batteries for electric vehicles.

While most of the world’s pCAM and CAM are made in China from primary (mined) metals, U.S.-based Ascend Elements is commercializing an ultra-efficient method to manufacture sustainable pCAM and CAM from black mass.
Black mass is the traditional output of lithium-ion battery recycling facilities.
In the past year, Ascend Elements reached several significant milestones, including the signing of its first commercial scale pCAM contract.
In the past year, Ascend Elements reached the commencement of construction on its Apex 1 facility.
In the past year, Ascend Elements reached the receipt of two grants from the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE).
When finished, the one-million-square-foot facility will produce enough sustainable pCAM to power 750,000 electric vehicles (EVs) annually.
In October 2022, the U.S. Department of Energy awarded Ascend Elements two grants totaling $480 million as part of the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law (BIL).
source_title: Ascend Elements raises $542 Million to produce U.S.-engineered battery materials - Power Electronics News

facts: The startup Ascend Elements has outlasted several rival battery recycling firms that ran out of money.
It has operated a battery-disassembly plant near Atlanta since 2023.
It is building a facility in Kentucky to turn ground-up batteries into useful ingredients for new units.
The company filed for bankruptcy on April 9, with leadership citing “insurmountable” financial difficulties.
Ascend Elements faced financial headwinds as buyers of recycled battery materials pushed back their timelines.
The firm nixed plans to produce cathode active material after agreeing to give up a $164 million federal grant in 2025.
The company lost another $110 million from a separate federal grant.
President and CEO Linh Austin cited a “long history of fiscal and operational mismanagement” in a LinkedIn post.
Leadership promised to use Chapter 11 proceedings to “reset and move forward,” not give up.
The broader battery recycling industry is struggling to figure out what to do with old batteries piling up from the electric vehicle revolution.

Early in the Biden administration, venture capitalists funded lithium-ion recycling startups aiming to retrieve nearly 100% of useful material from spent batteries and sell it back into the supply chain, but these entrepreneurs struggled to achieve that performance at commercial scale.
The Trump administration killed electric vehicle incentives for consumers last year, prompting many U.S. automakers to lose their nerve and pivot away from assertive EV plans.
Faltering American demand for EVs and batteries has rippled through the supply chain.
Redwood Materials has been grinding up gigawatt-hours of used batteries at its campus outside Reno, Nevada, located down the road from a Tesla Gigafactory.
Redwood Materials confirmed it will expand its flagship used-battery installation that powers modular data centers.
Redwood Materials announced it will repurpose 100 Rivian packs to store energy for the EV company’s factory in Illinois.

Georgia Public Service Commission approved a BYONCE program allowing tech companies and major power customers to directly pay for solar, battery, and other clean energy projects to keep data center boom costs off households.
source_title: Battery recycling still isn’t easy. Just ask Ascend… | Canary Media

facts: Ascend Elements is a closed-loop battery materials solutions provider founded in 2015 and headquartered in Westborough, Massachusetts.
The company provides EV battery recycling and commercial-scale production of lithium-ion battery precursor (pCAM) and cathode active materials (CAM).
Ascend Elements produces new cathode materials from spent lithium-ion cells using a proprietary technology that enables lower costs, improved performance, and reduced emissions.
Ascend Elements announced it has raised $162 million in new equity investments to support building a new plant.
The proceeds will fund a 1-million square foot sustainable lithium-ion plant in Hopkinsville, Kentucky.
The new Hopkinsville project is named Apex 1 and is anticipated to open in early 2025.
Apex 1 will be North America’s first sustainable pCAM manufacturing facility.
The facility will produce sustainable pCAM and CAM for up to 750,000 electric vehicles per year.
The project is supported in part by the U.S. Department of Energy and the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law.

Ascend Elements CEO Mike O’Kronley thanked new and existing partners for investing in North America’s critical EV battery infrastructure and stated that recycling lithium-ion batteries and making new engineered battery materials with lower carbon emissions accelerates the global transition to zero carbon emissions.
Major investors in the new $162 million funding round include Just Climate, Clearvision Ventures, and IRONGREY.
The new equity financing brings the company’s 12-month funding total to $704 million.
This follows a $542 million funding round led by Decarbonization Partners, Temasek, and Qatar Investment Authority in September 2023.

Just Climate Managing Director Aruna Ramsamy stated that Ascend Elements' Hydro-to-Cathode technology transforms spent lithium-ion batteries into critical materials for new batteries and champions circularity in an industry poised to scale significantly.
The company's first pCAM facility is currently under construction in the United States.
source_title: Sustainable Battery Materials Provider Ascend Elements Raises $162 Million to Build new US Plant - ESG Today

facts: Ascend Elements has started Chapter 11 bankruptcy proceedings in the U.S.
Investors had sunk nearly $900 million into Ascend Elements prior to the filing.
CEO Linh Austin announced the decision in a LinkedIn post late Thursday night, stating the company faced “insurmountable” financial challenges.
The bankruptcy filing comes amid a softening market for electric vehicles in the U.S.
The Trump administration canceled a $316 million grant intended for an Ascend Elements facility under construction in Kentucky.
Of the canceled grant, $204 million had already been disbursed to Ascend Elements before the cancellation.
Ascend Elements was forced to seek additional capital to cover the shortfall left by the canceled grant.
Electric vehicle sales in the U.S. surged prior to the end of tax credits in September last year but have not yet recovered.
Analysts attribute the lack of recovery to customers pulling forward purchases to take advantage of expiring tax credits, which has heightened automakers' fears.
Several automakers have reduced their plans for new electric vehicles in the U.S. due to market conditions.
Volkswagen announced it is ending production of the ID.4 at its Chattanooga, Tennessee factory and shifting focus to the gas-powered Atlas.
Ascend Elements developed a process to extract valuable critical minerals from scrap and end-of-life batteries.
The company's process limits the number of steps required to transform shredded battery waste into precursor materials for new cathodes.
Ascend Elements was constructing a 1 million-square-foot facility in Kentucky that has faced lawsuits and delays.
Battery-related startups like Ascend Elements operate in a challenging and cutthroat industry with long automaker lead times and frequently changing specifications.
Chinese manufacturers currently dominate the battery materials market, driving down costs through steady and generous state support.
Competitor recycling startup Redwood Materials has pivoted to repurposing battery packs for grid-scale batteries capable of powering data centers.
The market for stationary energy storage has exploded in recent years, allowing Redwood Materials to generate near-term revenue while continuing its recycling operations.
source_title: Battery recycler Ascend Elements files for bankruptcy | TechCrunch

facts: Ascend Elements announced a series of year-end milestones that cap a transformational 2025 and position the company for accelerated growth through 2026 and beyond.
Ascend Elements stands alone as the proven, US- and EU-based answer to delivering circularity and security from core battery material recycling to engineered-component production at scale.
Linh Austin serves as President and CEO of Ascend Elements.
In May, Ascend Elements secured a $340M non-dilutive grant commitment from the Republic of Poland to support development of advanced pCAM and lithium processing capacity in Europe.
In September, Ascend Elements became America’s first commercial-scale producer of recycled lithium carbonate with greater than 99% purity.
Production of recycled lithium carbonate is currently located in Georgia.
Near-term expansion of production is planned for Poland.
The company plans to deliver more than 15,000 tons of recycled Li₂CO₃ annually by 2027.
This output directly addresses critical mineral shortages in the US and Europe.
In November, Ascend Elements executed a 15,000 metric ton take-or-pay lithium carbonate agreement with Trafigura.
The agreement assures multi-year revenue and reinforces market confidence in its lithium-first strategy.
The company executed a multi-year, nearly $1B supply contract with a leading global automaker.
This demonstrates clear OEM demand for certified, circular, domestic supply at scale.
Ascend is the only Western firm delivering full, commercial-scale vertical integration from recycling to cathode-active pCAM and battery-grade lithium carbonate today, not in pilot.
The company utilizes a patented Hydro-to-Cathode® Process that upgrades recycled feedstock straight into high-value components including Lithium Carbonate and pCAM.
This process cuts cost and steps while reducing CO₂e by up to 90% over mining-based approaches.
Commercial production of recycled lithium carbonate is already operational in the U.S.
Upon financial close expected in Q1/Q2 2026, Ascend will scale its Kentucky and Poland facilities.
The scaling aims to answer critical mineral supply shortages with low-carbon throughput for North America and Europe.
The company's growth plan will power robust revenue and EBITDA expansion through the decade while supporting national security, energy transition, and climate targets.

President and CEO Linh Austin stated that 2025 proved circularity works at commercial scale, establishing Ascend Elements as North America’s most advanced platform for integrated, domestic production of essential battery ingredients with industry-leading cost and carbon footprint reduction.

Austin noted that company momentum underscores global partner confidence in its advanced recovery and manufacturing technologies and its ability to supply high-quality materials with transparency and reliability.
Austin added that the company is building a new era of energy independence, industrial sustainability, and regional security for the US and EU by looping more rather than mining more.
Austin emphasized that Ascend Elements is setting the standard for how a resilient, low-carbon, and transparent battery economy is built.
source_title: North American Clean Energy - Ascend Elements Closes 2025 with Strong Business Momentum and Commercial Scale Production of Critical Minerals for Battery Production

canonical_name: February 2022 GEDA Monthly Meeting - EV-erywhere Panel on Electric Mobility Movement
facts: GEDA assembled a knowledgeable panel of experts to offer perspective on drivers for the electric mobility business and how communities can be ready to accommodate it, covering utility and workforce needs as well as infrastructure and charging stations.

Panelist Thomas Frey serves as Global Director of Communications for Ascend Elements, Inc., a vertically integrated lithium-ion battery recycling and engineered-materials company previously known as Battery Resourcers.
Thomas Frey organized the announcement of North America’s largest lithium-ion battery recycling facility to be opened in Covington, Ga.
Panelist Jeff Pratt is President of Green Power EMC, responsible for day-to-day management of a renewable energy portfolio that provides energy to 38 Electric Membership Corporations in Georgia.
Jeff Pratt leads efforts to source, evaluate, and contract for new renewable energy projects.
Georgia’s Electric Membership Corporations have about 1,200 MW of renewable energy in operation or under construction.
Green Power EMC provides education materials and programs on renewable energy that Georgia’s Cooperatives employ for the benefit of their end-use members.

Panelist Virginia Sengewald is a Project Manager on the Logistics, Energy, Agribusiness, and Food Processing (LEAF) industry team within the Global Commerce Division of the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD).
Virginia Sengewald assists companies throughout their statewide site selection process and recruits new jobs and investment to the State of Georgia.
Virginia Sengewald focuses on German-speaking projects in addition to her industry focus on renewable energy and the battery supply chain.
Moderator Matt Colvin is Director of Economic Development for the University of Georgia (UGA) and serves as a top advisor to university leadership on economic development matters.

Matt Colvin represents UGA with the Georgia Department of Economic Development and its key partners for business recruitment efforts, marketing the strengths of UGA’s talent and research pipeline across the state to potential companies looking to locate or expand operations in Georgia.
The event was held at the Georgia Tech Hotel & Conference Center located at 800 Spring Street Northwest Atlanta, GA 30308-1015.
Registration for the event closed at 4:00 PM on Friday, February 11, 2022.
The event agenda included registration from 10:45 AM to 12:00 PM, networking from 11:00 AM to 12:00 PM, and a lunch and program from 12:00 PM to 1:30 PM on February 14.
The event was sponsored by Matt Colvin (Director of Economic Development at the UGA Carl Vinson Institute of Government) and the UGA College of Engineering.
source_title: February 2022 GEDA Monthly Meeting - EV-erywhere - Georgia Economic Developers Association

facts: Battery Resourcers plans to invest $43 million to open its first commercial-scale battery recycling plant in Covington.
Gov. Brian Kemp announced the investment and facility opening plans on Wednesday.
The Covington facility is expected to generate 150 jobs in Newton County.
The facility will be the largest lithium-ion battery recycling facility of its kind in North America.
Battery Resourcers is headquartered in Worcester, Massachusetts.
The company was founded in 2015 with a mission to return 100% of battery active materials back into new batteries.
Battery Resourcers manufactures EV-grade, finished cathode active materials that meet or exceed performance requirements set by other industry-leading brands.
The Covington facility is scheduled to open in spring 2022.
Upon opening, the facility will be able to process 30,000 metric tons of discarded lithium-ion batteries and scrap annually.
The annual processing capacity is equivalent to 70,000 vehicle batteries per year.
The company's recycling process returns battery-grade lithium, cobalt, and nickel back into the battery supply chain.
Battery recycling is only one component of Battery Resourcers' technology and strategic expansion plans.
The company has engineered a process to turn recyclables back into critical battery materials, specifically nickel-manganese-cobalt cathodes.
The recovered nickel-manganese-cobalt cathode materials are sold back to battery manufacturers.
Major brand investors in Battery Resourcers include Jaguar Land Rover, Orbia, TDK, TRUMPF Group, Doral Energy, and Hitachi.
CEO and Director Michael O’Kronley stated the company is looking forward to bringing 150 tech jobs to the Covington area.
Automotive manufacturers currently have very few options for responsible and cost-effective disposal of discarded batteries and scrap.

The convenient U.S. location and company technology will provide a sustainable solution that minimizes the need for mining and returns valuable, battery-grade materials back into the lithium-ion supply chain.
Battery Resourcers will occupy an existing 154,000-square-foot facility located on Industrial Drive in Covington.
The company has already begun hiring for the new facility.
Newton County Industrial Development Authority Chairman Lanier Sims expressed pride in Battery Resourcers joining the area's mix of businesses.
Local officials stated the company's presence will help continue making the I-20 corridor a business and technology-focused hub.
Covington Mayor Steve Horton stated that Battery Resourcers is an outstanding addition to the community's business ecosystem and will complement many regional businesses in Georgia.
The facility is expected to be fully operational in July.
Project Manager Virginia Sengewald represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce division on the project.
The project was developed in partnership with the Newton County Industrial Development Authority, the Metro Atlanta Chamber, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia Power.
The announcement follows Rivian's recent plan to locate its second EV manufacturing facility at Stanton Springs, a multi-county industrial park near Social Circle.
Rivian is investing $5 billion and will create 7,500 jobs for its Georgia facility.
Construction of Rivian's plant is scheduled to start in summer 2022.
Once fully operational, Rivian expects to produce up to 400,000 vehicles per year at the site.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that battery recycling has become a vital part of the EV supply chain as the industry grows rapidly.
Cutting reliance on unstable global areas for battery materials is crucial for the future, according to Commissioner Wilson.
The timing of Battery Resourcers' announcement aligns well with Georgia's recent acquisition of Rivian's second EV manufacturing facility and campus.
source_title: Covington future home to continent’s largest lithium-ion battery recycling facility - The Covington News

facts: Ascend Elements operates primarily in the business-to-business (B2B) sector, focusing on large-scale manufacturers and original equipment manufacturers (OEMs).
The company's target market is centered around the electric vehicle (EV) and energy storage industries.
Primary customer segments include major automotive OEMs, battery cell producers, and providers of stationary energy storage systems.
Ascend Elements provides recycled battery materials and closed-loop recycling services to help customers meet sustainability goals, secure supply chains, and comply with evolving regulations.
The global lithium-ion battery market was valued at USD 57.1 billion in 2024.
The global lithium-ion battery market is projected to reach USD 302.2 billion by 2032, representing a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 23.1% from 2024 to 2032.
Ascend Elements has strategically shifted its focus from early-stage research and development partners to large-scale commercial agreements.
Customer purchasing decisions are heavily influenced by the quality and consistency of recycled materials, supply chain reliability, and the company's ability to scale with production demands.
The company addresses customer pain points such as volatile virgin material prices and ethical concerns surrounding traditional mining practices.
Ascend Elements utilizes a closed-loop process and Hydro-to-Cathode direct precursor synthesis technology to produce sustainable battery materials.
Customer feedback regarding accelerating EV adoption and the push for domestic battery production has significantly influenced product development.
The company invested in large-scale facilities like the Apex 1 plant in Hopkinsville, Kentucky, designed to produce sustainable cathode precursor (pCAM) and cathode active materials (CAM).
Ascend Elements' customer base geographic location is primarily focused on North America, reflecting demand for localized battery recycling and production capabilities.

The United States serves as a primary target market, significantly boosted by initiatives like the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA), which encourages domestic manufacturing and sourcing of EV battery components.
The Apex 1 facility in Kentucky is designed to supply North American automotive and battery manufacturers with essential materials.
Regulatory landscapes such as the European Union's Battery Regulation, which mandates recycled content, influence market strategies and localized partnerships.
Ascend Elements secured $542 million in equity and debt financing in 2023.
In 2024, Ascend Elements strategically acquired a former plastics recycling facility in Georgia to expand commercial-scale battery recycling operations.
Customer acquisition and retention strategies center around direct sales, strategic partnerships, long-term contracts, and collaborative development agreements.
The Hydro-to-Cathode process produces high-performance cathode precursor (pCAM) and cathode active materials (CAM) from recycled batteries.
Competitors in the sustainable battery solutions market include Li-Cycle, Redwood Materials, American Battery Technology Company, and Cirba Solutions.
source_title: What is Customer Demographics and Target Market of Ascend Elements Company? – businessmodelcanvastemplate.com

facts: Aspen Aerogels will invest a minimum of $325 million to build a factory in Bulloch County’s Southern Gateway Commerce Park south of Statesboro for production of aerogel thermal insulating material used in electric vehicle batteries.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp announced the investment on Thursday, February 18, 2022.
Company officials expect the plant to be in operation by late 2023.
The plant will create more than 250 advanced manufacturing jobs.
For that cost or possibly more, the company expects to build and equip a 500,000-square-foot facility.
The project greatly expands production capacity beyond that of Aspen Aerogels' existing plant in Rhode Island.
This appears to be the largest announced dollar-value private investment for any manufacturing facility in Bulloch County, Georgia, in its history.
Benjy Thompson, CEO of the Development Authority of Bulloch County (DABC), stated it is the largest single private investment as far as he knows going back nearly 30 years.
Previous major investments in Bulloch County include a Walmart Distribution Center and plants by Briggs & Stratton, Great Dane Trailers, and Viracon (recently replaced by Cardinal Glass Industries).
The Briggs & Stratton small engines plant was first announced in 1994, employed nearly 800 people a few years later, and had an initial investment of a little less than $100 million.
Public investment incentives will come from the state of Georgia, Bulloch County, and the city of Statesboro in exchange for job creation commitments.
The DABC board met at 3 p.m. on Thursday to approve local contributions to the incentives package.
The Bulloch County Board of Commissioners and Statesboro City Council held special meetings simultaneously at 4:30 p.m. and unanimously approved agreements for local incentive contributions.
Gov. Brian Kemp said Aspen Aerogels has put confidence in Georgia, its people, and the growing electric mobility sector, adding an American company to the state's rapidly expanding EV portfolio.
The DABC is providing a 90-acre site within the commerce park at the Interstate 16 and U.S. Highway 301 interchange free-of-charge to Aspen Aerogels.
Through a lease arrangement, the company will enjoy a 10-year, 100% abatement of county-government property tax on the factory and site.
The property tax abatement does not extend to the portion controlled by the Bulloch County Board of Education, so Aspen Aerogels will pay taxes supporting local public schools.
The city of Statesboro extended its water, sewer, and natural gas systems several miles earlier to serve the commerce park and county Tax Allocation District.
The city will extend utility pipelines farther within the park to serve Aspen Aerogels.
The DABC is applying for a federal Employment Incentive Program grant for $750,000 to cover pipeline extension costs.
If the grant is not successful, the city will be responsible for up to $500,000, with Aspen Aerogels covering the remainder.

The site is currently outside city limits, but the City Council agreed that if annexed into Statesboro or its fire service district, the city will not impose property tax or fire fees within the 10-year lease term.
The company will pay standard rates for city utility services after connections are provided.
TravelCenters of America broke ground in November for a TA Express center, becoming the first business to start construction in the park after over 10 years of infrastructure spending.
The Aspen Aerogels plant will be the first actual industry in the park and will take up about half of the DABC’s remaining land at the site.
Board of Commissioners Chairman Roy Thompson said county officials are never eager to grant tax abatements but that they are necessary to secure new industries with higher-paying jobs.

Aspen Aerogels originally announced plans in 2015 to build a $70 million plant with a little over 100 jobs at the original Gateway Industrial Park for traditional aerogel products used to insulate petroleum industry pipelines.
Those 2015 plans changed after a dip in oil prices.
The company shifted to making new aerogel products under the registered trademark PyroThin to serve as thermal barriers in electric vehicle batteries.

Aspen Aerogels President and CEO Don Young cited access to efficient ports, a strong available workforce, and secure low-cost utilities and critical raw materials as reasons for choosing the location.

Young stated the company is increasingly being asked to provide broader solutions for improved battery performance and safety centered on PyroThin materials that provide thermal management, mechanical stability, and fire protection properties to EV battery systems.
Aspen Aerogels is publicly traded under symbol ASPN on the New York Stock Exchange.
The company's corporate headquarters are in Northborough, Massachusetts.
Aspen Aerogels currently operates a single production facility in East Providence, Rhode Island, which employs about 250 people.
Multiple online financial sites showed the corporation’s market capitalization as a little over $900 million as of Thursday.

DABC Vice Chair Billy Allen welcomed the company, citing the region's commitment to innovation in the electric mobility ecosystem and tremendous education resources including Georgia Southern University and Ogeechee Technical College.

Marie Gordon, communications director for the Georgia Department of Economic Development, said officials plan to finalize and sign state documents that week and will release details upon signing per state law.

Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson welcomed Aspen Aerogels' innovative solutions for the electric vehicle and energy storage markets and looked forward to Georgia employees collaborating with Aspen’s network of scientists and engineers.
Aspen plans to begin hiring in the second half of 2022 across skilled manufacturing operators, advanced process engineers, quality and maintenance technicians, and professional support roles.
source_title: Aspen Aerogels to build $325M plant in Bulloch’s commerce park at I-16 - Statesboro Herald

facts: Aspen Aerogels, Inc. (NYSE: ASPN) designs, develops, and manufactures innovative, high-performance materials used primarily in the energy industrial, sustainable insulation materials, and electric vehicle markets.
The company has developed and commercialized its proprietary line of PyroThin® aerogel thermal barriers for battery packs in electric vehicles.
Aspen is developing applications for its aerogel technology in the battery materials market and several other high-potential markets.
The company’s products replace traditional insulation in existing facilities during regular maintenance, upgrades, and capacity expansions.
Its aerogel products are increasingly being specified for use in new-build energy industrial facilities.

Aspen’s aerogel products promote the adoption of electric vehicles, enhance energy efficiency and asset resiliency, improve safety in green buildings, and support the availability of cleaner fuels in traditional energy markets.
The company’s technology is proven and protected with an installed base of over $1 billion of aerogel materials and a comprehensive global IP portfolio.
Built on two decades of R&D investment, its Aerogel Technology Platform™ holds the potential to solve emerging global challenges and contribute to a more sustainable future.
Per Aspen’s FY23 annual report, the company’s technologically advanced insulation products are targeted at the estimated $3.9 billion annual global market for energy industrial insulation materials.
During 2022, Aspen’s market share was approximately 3% of the estimated yearly global market.
According to the company’s Q2 FY24 presentation, strong demand for Energy Industrial products across all regions and applications is currently supply-constrained.

The company’s aerogel insulation is used by many of the world’s largest oil producers and the owners and operators of refineries, petrochemical plants, liquefied natural gas facilities, and power generating and distribution assets, such as ExxonMobil, Reliance Industries, PTT LNG, and Royal Dutch Shell.
Per Aspen Aerogels analysis, the serviceable addressable market of pouch and prismatic cell packs is $1.6B and is growing at 27% CAGR to $8.7B by 2030.

The company has entered into multi-year production contracts with General Motors to supply fabricated, multi-part thermal barriers for use in the battery system of its next-generation electric vehicles.
Aspen provides thermal barrier production parts to both General Motors and Toyota.
The company supplies thermal barrier prototype parts to several other U.S., European, and Asian manufacturers of electric vehicles, grid storage, and home battery systems.

The company recently received a letter of intent from the luxury brand of a German original equipment manufacturer (OEM) group where the thermal barrier parts are targeted for a battery platform intended for use across several of their models.
Aspen also received an order for approximately 1.5 million prototype parts for a commercial vehicle brand within the same German OEM group.
Historically, Aspen’s insulation product revenue grew from $17.2 million in 2008 to $124.8 million in 2022, representing a compound annual growth rate of 15%.
During this period, the company sold nearly $1.3 billion of its insulation products globally, representing an installed base of more than 400 million square feet of insulation.
In the near term, Aspen aims to build more than $550 million annual revenue capacity and deliver $200 million annual gross profit and $140 million Adjusted EBITDA.
For Q2 FY23, total revenue was $48.2 million, compared to $45.6 million in Q2 FY22.
During this time, the company delivered gross margins of 17%, a 20-percentage point improvement over the same period last year.
For Q2 FY23, Aspen achieved a record quarterly Energy Industrial gross profit margin of 27% and decreased YoY quarterly net loss by $8.6 million, a 36% reduction on 6% higher revenues.
The company ended the quarter with cash and equivalents of $134.3 million.
For FY22, Aspen reported total revenue of $180.4 million, compared to $121.6 million in FY21, an increase of 48% YoY.
During this time, Aspen generated $55.6 million of PyroThin® thermal barrier revenue (an increase of nearly nine times FY21 revenues) and $124.8 million of Energy Industrial revenues.

For FY23, Aspen has provided guidance of total revenue between $200 million and $250 million (an increase of between 11% to 39%, YoY), Adjusted EBITDA between $(55) million and $(45) million, and net loss between $85.0 million and $75.0 million.
The company expects CAPEX of up to $150 million to enable continued growth in 2024.

Aspen is subject to risks including annual net losses since inception and the possibility of never reaching profitability due to expenses related to business expansion such as research and development, sales and marketing, and general and administrative costs.

The company’s growth depends on substantial amounts of additional capital for expanding existing production lines or constructing new production lines or facilities, covering ongoing operating expenses, continuing development of its Aerogel Technology Platform, or introducing new product lines, with no guarantee of obtaining funds on favorable terms or on time.

Estimates regarding the market opportunity for Aspen’s products in the electric vehicle market and assumptions on financial targets and planned production capacity increases may prove inaccurate, potentially causing actual results to materially differ from targets and adversely affecting future profitability, cash flows, and stock price.
The company has a market capitalization of $557.45M with a current share price of 7.94 USD.
source_title: Aspen Aerogels: Gaining from Energy Industrial & EV markets

facts: Aspen Aerogels, Inc. won a conditional commitment for a proposed DOE loan worth up to $670.6 million.
The loan is part of the Advanced Technology Vehicles Manufacturing program.
The loan will finance the construction of Aspen’s second aerogel manufacturing facility in Register, Georgia.
Both the DOE and Aspen Aerogels must meet specific technical, legal, environmental, and financial conditions before finalizing the loan agreement and disbursing funds.
The Register Plant will manufacture PyroThin aerogel blankets, ultrathin thermal barriers designed to prevent thermal runaway in batteries.
The plant is projected to generate $1.2 billion to $1.6 billion in initial revenue.
The plant is projected to create up to 550 construction jobs and 255 permanent positions.
Donald R. Young, Aspen’s President and CEO, stated that PyroThin can enable increased battery safety and performance and solves an important and challenging problem.

Young noted the company is excited to further the DOE’s mission to onshore and re-shore domestic manufacturing technologies critical to meeting the federal goal of having half of all new vehicles sold in 2030 be zero-emissions vehicles.
Aspen announced preliminary results for the third quarter of 2024, expecting quarterly revenue of approximately $117 million compared to a consensus of $95.5 million.
The company expects a net loss of around $13 million for Q3 2024, which includes a one-time charge of $27.5 million related to the extinguishment of its convertible note on August 19, 2024.
Aspen sees adjusted EBITDA of approximately $25 million for the quarter.
As of September 30, the company held about $113 million in cash and cash equivalents.
Aspen plans to report its third-quarter results on November 7, 2024.
Following the announcement, Aspen Aerogels (ASPN) shares rose 13.1% to $25.38.
source_title: Why Aspen Aerogels Shares Are Seeing Blue Skies On Wednesday — TradingView News

facts: Aspen Aerogels, a technology company in sustainability and electrification solutions, closed on a $125 million term loan facility and $100 million capacity asset-based revolving credit facility with MidCap Financial.
MidCap Financial is a commercial finance company managed by Apollo Capital Management, focused on middle market transactions.
The collateral structure of the financing agreement explicitly excludes the assets of Aspen’s wholly-owned subsidiary, Aspen Aerogels Georgia.
The excluded collateral specifically covers the company’s planned second aerogel manufacturing facility in Statesboro, Georgia.
Proceeds from the transaction will be utilized for the full cash redemption of the company’s legacy convertible note and for general corporate purposes.
The term loan has a maturity date of Aug. 19, 2029.

Aspen Aerogels president and CEO Donald R. Young stated MidCap Financial is an outstanding partner for building a highly valuable and profitable business by leveraging the Aerogel Technology Platform into high-growth markets.
Young noted the transaction was made possible by Aspen’s dedicated focus on generating near-term profitability.
Aspen Aerogels CFO and treasurer Ricardo C. Rodriguez stated the combined $225 million debt facilities put the company on a path to further reduce its cost of capital.
Rodriguez added the financing provides access to additional liquidity to support profitable revenue growth.

The collateral structure is specifically optimized to fit into the anticipated structure of a previously disclosed potential debt option for accelerating the longer-term construction of the second aerogel manufacturing facility in Statesboro, Georgia.
Rodriguez stated eliminating the potential overhang of existing convertible debt near or above the conversion share price of approximately $29.94 is an added benefit that can enable value creation.
source_title: Aspen Closes on $125MM Term Loan Facility and $100MM Credit Facility with MidCap - ABF Journal

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Aspen Aerogels (NYSE: ASPN) plans to construct an advanced manufacturing facility at Bulloch County’s Southern Gateway Commerce Park.
Aspen intends to invest a minimum of $325 million in the project.
The company will create more than 250 advanced manufacturing jobs at its new site near Statesboro.
Production at the Georgia facility is expected to begin in late 2023.
Governor Kemp stated that Aspen Aerogels has put confidence in Georgia, its people, and the growing electric mobility sector in the state.
Combined with Aspen’s original East Providence, Rhode Island plant, the planned facility is designed to triple Aspen’s nameplate aerogel capacity.
The expansion supports Aspen’s thermal barrier expansion plans in the fast-growing electric vehicle market.
Aspen aims to capitalize on increased demand for its Aerogel Technology Platform™ as EV OEMs continue to adopt PyroThin® aerogel thermal barrier solutions.
Don Young, President and CEO of Aspen, noted the company is pleased to be in Georgia for the siting, development, and construction of its PyroThin materials plant.
The City of Statesboro and Bulloch County region offer Aspen access to efficient ports, a strong available workforce, secure low-cost utilities, and critical raw materials.

Aspen is increasingly being asked to provide broader solutions for improved battery performance and safety centered on PyroThin materials that provide thermal management, mechanical stability, and fire protection properties to EV battery systems.
Aspen plans to build a 500,000-square-foot advanced manufacturing facility situated on a 90-acre parcel in southeast Georgia.

The company plans to begin hiring personnel in the second half of 2022 across skilled manufacturing operators, advanced process engineers, quality and maintenance technicians, and professional support roles.
Billy Allen, Vice Chair of the Development Authority of Bulloch County, welcomed Aspen Aerogels to the Statesboro-Bulloch County community and region.
The Development Authority highlighted that Georgia Southern University and Ogeechee Technical College will provide critical education resources to help Aspen flourish.

Assistant Director Alyce Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce division on this project in partnership with the Development Authority of Bulloch County, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia EMC.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson welcomed Aspen Aerogels’ innovative, sustainable solutions for the electric vehicle and energy storage markets to Georgia.
The GDEcD looks forward to having Georgia employees collaborate with Aspen’s network of world-class scientists, engineers, and additional skilled workforce to solve mobility challenges.
Southern Gateway Commerce Park is a Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Certified park requiring due diligence and specific certification requirements prior to business visits.
Aspen Aerogels is a technology leader in sustainability whose aerogel technology enables customers to achieve objectives around resource efficiency, electrification, and sustainability.
PyroThin® thermal barrier products enable solutions to thermal runaway challenges within the electric vehicle and energy storage markets.
Aspen’s carbon aerogel program seeks to increase the performance of lithium-ion battery cells to enable EV manufacturers to extend driving range and reduce the cost of electric vehicles.
Aspen’s Spaceloft® sustainable building materials provide industry-leading energy efficiency and fire safety to building owners.
Aspen’s Cryogel® and Pyrogel® products are valued by the world's largest energy infrastructure companies.
Aspen is headquartered in Northborough, MA, and manufactures its products at its East Providence, R.I. facilities.
source_title: Gov. Kemp: Aspen Aerogels to Open Advanced Manufacturing Facility in Bulloch County - EIN Presswire

facts: Aspen Aerogels, Inc. received a conditional commitment from the U.S. Department of Energy ("DOE") for a proposed loan of up to $670.6 million under the Advanced Technology Vehicles Manufacturing loan program within DOE's Loan Programs Office ("LPO").
The loan is intended to finance the construction of Aspen's planned second aerogel manufacturing facility, called the Register Plant, in Register, Georgia.
Aspen Aerogels Georgia, LLC, a subsidiary of Aspen Aerogels, Inc., will be the borrower of the proposed loan.
The loan will be structured as project financing with repayment commencing upon project commissioning.
The interest rate for the loan will be the applicable U.S. Treasury Rate at the time of each cash draw.
The Register Plant is designed to manufacture PyroThin® aerogel blankets.
Aspen's PyroThin platform offers ultrathin and lightweight thermal barriers designed to help prevent thermal runaway propagation in batteries.
Thermal runaway is an event in which a battery cell overheats and triggers neighboring cells, possibly resulting in a sudden fire.
PyroThin is designed to offer a unique combination of thermal management, mechanical performance, and fire protection properties.
Aerogel thermal barriers have the potential to increase energy density of EV battery systems when compared to alternative thermal barrier products.
The Register Plant has an initial estimated revenue capacity of $1.2B - $1.6B, depending on product mix.
The project is expected to create up to 550 construction jobs.
The project is expected to create 255 permanent, full-time operations jobs.
Donald R. Young, Aspen's President and CEO, stated the proposed loan would fully fund the required CAPEX for the second aerogel manufacturing facility.
Thermal Barrier segment revenues increased to $232 million over the trailing twelve months ended June 30, 2024, representing a 277% year-over-year increase compared to the same period in 2023.
Aspen secured additional EV production contracts, bringing its total to six major OEMs in the United States and Europe.
Aspen won a prestigious Automotive News Pace Award.
The DOE and the Company must satisfy certain technical, legal, environmental, and financial conditions before entering into definitive financing documents and funding the loan.
Q3 2024 preliminary quarterly revenue was approximately $117 million.
Q3 2024 preliminary quarterly net loss was approximately $13 million, including a $27.5 million one-time charge from the extinguishment of a convertible note on August 19, 2024.
Q3 2024 preliminary quarterly Adjusted EBITDA was approximately $25 million.
Cash and cash equivalents were approximately $113 million as of September 30, 2024.
Aspen's Battery Materials initiative seeks to increase the performance of lithium-ion battery cells to enable EV manufacturers to extend driving range and reduce EV costs.
The company is headquartered in Northborough, Mass.
