facts: EV thermal barrier revenue dropped 25% in Q1 2025 as OEM production schedules reset and distributors cleared excess inventory.
Total company revenue fell 17% year-over-year in Q1 2025.
The EV thermal barrier segment contributed $48.9 million to Q1 2025 revenue, representing roughly 62% of total company revenue.
Gross margins in the EV segment dropped to 23%, missing a 35% target, due to suboptimal fixed cost absorption and strategic pricing actions.
Company-wide gross profit fell 35% year-over-year in Q1 2025.
Adjusted EBITDA for Q1 2025 was positive but modest at $4.9 million.
Working capital gains from a $31.9 million reduction in accounts receivable were offset by $13 million in CapEx.
The $13 million in CapEx was largely allocated to ongoing obligations in Georgia and equipment for future launches.
Strategic cost actions aim to reduce the revenue required for EBITDA breakeven by $90 million.
Fixed cost reductions have lowered the revenue threshold needed for positive operating income to $245 million.
Management aims to deliver at least $20 million of adjusted EBITDA on annualized revenues as low as $250 million.
Aspen Aerogels secured OEM awards with General Motors, Mercedes-Benz, and Volvo Truck for next-generation battery platforms.
The industry shift from pouch to prismatic cell batteries is structurally reducing EV thermal barrier content per vehicle from over $1,000 to $200-$250.
Management notes that the mix shift toward prismatic cells allows for better equipment utilization across multiple OEMs.
Energy industrial revenue grew 2% year-over-year to $29.8 million in Q1 2025 as distributors cleared excess inventory.
Aspen targets a full-year energy industrial revenue outcome similar to 2024's $145.9 million.
The company utilizes dual sourcing and regionalized production across its U.S. and Mexican operations, plus an external manufacturing facility (EMF), to mitigate tariff risks.
Most of Aspen's EV thermal barrier products are USMCA compliant and exempt from tariffs.
Targeted exemptions for energy industrial products cap raw material tariff exposure at under $4.5 million in 2025.
Aspen Aerogels is actively monetizing its Georgia facility, aiming to recoup cash quickly through equipment sales and potential site disposition.
Record quoting activity and new OEM engagements with Korean cell makers indicate a robust medium-term commercial pipeline.
Management expects additional OEM wins to begin impacting financial results meaningfully from 2027 onward.
European customers are currently supplied from Mexico to leverage cost advantages and minimize risk.
Aspen guided for Q2 2025 revenue of $70 to $80 million and adjusted EBITDA between breakeven and $7 million.
Full-year 2025 baseline expectations include at least $280 million in revenue and $20 million in adjusted EBITDA.
CapEx for the full year is tightly controlled, targeting less than $25 million (excluding Georgia demobilization costs).
Don Young serves as President and Chief Executive Officer of Aspen Aerogels.
Ricardo Rodriguez serves as Chief Financial Officer and Treasurer of Aspen Aerogels.
source_title: Aspen Aerogels (ASPN) Q1 2025: EV Thermal Barrier Revenue Drops 25% as Inventory Destocking Reshapes Outlook | EarningsIQ

facts: Aspen Aerogels develops and manufactures aerogel-based insulation and thermal-management products for energy industrial and electrification markets.

Company news covers segment trends in Energy Industrial and Thermal Barrier, customer demand for EV thermal barriers, and project activity tied to energy infrastructure and subsea pipeline applications.
Aspen Aerogels was named a 2025 General Motors Supplier of the Year as part of GM’s 34th annual awards.
The honor recognizes Aspen’s innovation and contribution to GM’s electric vehicle thermal management strategy and battery performance, underscoring a strong, ongoing technology partnership.
Aspen Aerogels began a staged restart of manufacturing at its East Providence, Rhode Island facility following an April 8 incident.
The restart follows comprehensive mechanical, operational, and safety reviews with local, state, and federal agencies.
The company plans a deliberate, phased ramp-up and expects restoring full capabilities will take time, emphasizing employee and community safety as its top priority.
Aspen Aerogels reported Q1 2026 revenue of $37.9M versus $78.7M a year earlier and a net loss of $23.7M.
The company received a $37.6M cash settlement from General Motors; quarter-end cash was $175.6M.
An explosion at the East Providence plant damaged production space; a staged restart is expected beginning in May.
Q2 2026 guidance: revenue $40M–$48M, net loss $14M–$20M, adjusted EBITDA $(4)M–$(10)M; FY 2026 capex $10M.
Aspen Aerogels will release first-quarter 2026 financial results before the market opens on Thursday, May 7, 2026, and will host a conference call at 8:30 a.m. ET that same day.
Executives Don Young (President & CEO) and Grant Thoele (CFO) will discuss results.
Aspen Aerogels provided an update on its East Providence, Rhode Island manufacturing facility after an explosion on April 8, 2026 that damaged production space and halted operations.
Preliminary reports indicate the blast originated in a high-temperature oven.
Eleven employees were transported for evaluation and released.
The facility remained offline as of April 21, 2026.
Aspen has initiated insurance claims and will provide a further update with its fiscal 2026 Q1 earnings in early May.
Aspen Aerogels confirmed an incident occurred the evening of April 8, 2026 at its East Providence, Rhode Island manufacturing facility.
The site is secure, the situation is under control, and all eleven employees transported for evaluation have been released.
The company said it is assessing impact, has opened a full investigation, and is cooperating with local authorities; further updates will be provided as warranted.
Aspen Aerogels will participate in the 38th Annual Roth Conference on Monday, March 23, 2026, at the Ritz Carlton in Laguna Niguel, CA.
Don Young, President & Chief Executive Officer, and Neal Baranosky, Senior Director, Head of Investor Relations & Corporate Strategy, will hold one‑on‑one investor meetings.
Aspen Aerogels reported sharply lower revenue and large losses for Q4 and FY2025, citing reduced EV-related demand and charges tied to reassessed capacity.
Total FY2025 revenue was $271.1M vs. $452.7M prior year; FY net loss was $389.6M including a $291.2M impairment.
Year-end cash was $158.6M and the company expects a $37.6M GM settlement in Q1 2026.

Aspen Aerogels will release fourth quarter and fiscal year 2025 results before market open on February 25, 2026 and will host a conference call that day at 8:30 a.m. ET with CEO Don Young and CFO Grant Thoele.
Aspen Aerogels announced on Dec. 17, 2025 that it entered into an amendment to its Credit, Security and Guaranty Agreement with MidCap Financial.
The amendment updates the company's financial covenant framework to provide additional flexibility and includes other technical changes intended to enhance Aspen's financial position.
Aspen's CFO said liquidity is trending ahead of expectations due to operational efficiencies, working capital improvements, and prudent capital spending.
The amendment will be filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission.

facts: Aspen Aerogels designs, develops, manufactures, and sells aerogel materials primarily for use in the energy industrial, sustainable insulation materials, and electric vehicle (EV) markets in the United States, Canada, Asia, Europe, and Latin America.
The company maintains a strategic focus on EV thermal barriers and U.S.-based manufacturing to align with regulatory trends, supporting future growth and market share expansion.
Aspen Aerogels holds strong positioning in the EV thermal barrier market with major OEMs like GM, Stellantis, and Daimler, alongside active new quoting and prototyping activities.

Domestic manufacturing capacity and flexible sourcing strategies cater to automaker preferences for U.S.-based supply and position Aspen as a preferred supplier amid intensifying regulations favoring energy efficiency and sustainability.
High customer concentration in the EV Thermal Barrier segment, particularly reliance on GM, poses a risk to future revenues and earnings if GM's EV production or market share stalls or declines.

Adjacent OEM programs, design-in activity, and new platform launches outside of current key customers (e.g., Daimler, Stellantis, ACC) have uncertain timelines or significant slippage due to shifting automaker plans and reaction to changing government incentives.
Ongoing policy and regulatory volatility in key markets (notably the U.S. EV market) create uncertainty for EV adoption rates and timing of OEM launches.

facts: The Aspen Aerogels project was announced in February of this year and has placed the Bulloch community in the EV ecosystem.
The Development Authority of Bulloch County and their partners, Bulloch County, and City of Statesboro offered Aspen Aerogels a 90-acre land gift at the Southern Gateway Commerce Park.
This will be the first manufacturing project to locate in the park.
Aspen Aerogels primary product mix has changed to become a supplier to the electric vehicle industry.
The Statesboro-Bulloch Plant 2 will manufacture insulation blankets to wrap battery compartments and prevent thermal runaway in EVs.
This deal has impact of over $10.9 million in net project benefits to county and school over 20-year timeline.
The deal includes a commitment of 250 jobs.
Nearly 200 economic developers from around the state attended the ceremony where GEDA recognized the Development Authority of Bulloch County for this achievement.

Eric McDonald, CEO of Haralson County Chamber of Commerce presented the award to Benjy Thompson, Chair of GEDA, and CEO of Development Authority of Bulloch County and other key leaders in the project.

GEDA recognizes recipients in four categories, including Deal of the Year Awards that recognize outstanding achievement in the location or expansion of new businesses in the state of Georgia that meet community economic development goals and involve significant community program support, such as financial or permitting assistance, workforce development, state financial support, etc.

facts: Aspen Aerogels abandoned a project consisting of a combined 320,000 square feet across two buildings in the Bruce Yawn Commerce Park in Bulloch County in February.
The planned Aspen Aerogels plant building was projected to be a $3.7 million investment.
The project was expected to create 250 jobs for Bulloch County residents.
Aspen Aerogels was intended to become the largest company ever located in Bulloch County.
Since February, local officials have been negotiating clawbacks with Aspen Aerogels to recover invested taxpayer funds after the company decided not to move in.
The city of Statesboro anticipates receiving a $741,000 refund this week.
The refunded $741,000 originated from a Georgia Department of Community Affairs grant intended as an incentive for hiring local Bulloch County employees.
Statesboro officials followed a Georgia Department of Community Affairs (DCA) agreement requiring Aspen Aerogels to repay a $750,000 grant after deciding not to move forward.
Statesboro City Manager Charles Penny stated that $500,000 in taxpayer funds will not be reimbursed until another company utilizes the infrastructure.
The unreimbursed $500,000 was originally allocated to fund water and sewer lines for the Aspen Aerogels plant.
City Manager Charles Penny described the unrecovered funds not as a loss but as a "temporary pause" in potential revenue, contingent on future industry utilization of the built infrastructure.
Benjy Thompson of the Bulloch Development Authority expressed confidence that another large company will lease the pre-built infrastructure once it becomes available.
The presence and success of multiple Hyundai suppliers located next door in the same commerce park are expected to attract a new tenant.

facts: Blue Bird Corporation is a leading manufacturer of school buses.
The company was founded by Albert Luce, Sr. and has spent the last century defining the modern yellow school bus.
The company has built more than 620,000 school buses over its lifetime.
About 180,000 of Blue Bird's manufactured school buses are still in operation today.
Blue Bird is the only remaining U.S.-owned and operated school bus manufacturer.
The company's headquarters is located in Macon, Georgia.
Blue Bird operates a manufacturing facility in Fort Valley, Georgia.
In 2016, Blue Bird became the first to market with electronic stability control for school buses to prevent rollovers and loss of control.
In 2025, Blue Bird made driver airbags standard across its school bus lineup.
In 2025, Blue Bird became the first to market with three-point seatbelts for school buses.
Blue Bird school buses feature high-resolution front and rear cameras.
Blue Bird school buses are equipped with LED headlights for improved visibility in early-morning and evening conditions.
Blue Bird school buses include illuminated signage and stop arms.
Blue Bird school buses utilize high-intensity exterior LED lighting to increase visibility to nearby drivers.
Blue Bird is developing low- and zero-emission vehicles to reduce environmental impact.
The company produced its first electric school bus in 1994.
The company produced its first propane-powered school bus in 2008.
More than 25,000 propane and electric school buses have been sold by Blue Bird to date.
Collectively, Blue Bird's low- and zero-emission buses reduce tens of thousands of metric tons of carbon dioxide annually.

facts: Workers at Blue Bird’s assembly plants and warehouse in Fort Valley, Ga., have approved their first union contract a year after voting to unionize.
The three-year contract is between Blue Bird Corp. and the United Steelworkers union.
The contract covers all 1,500 workers at the Fort Valley facilities.
All covered workers will receive at least a 12% raise, with some of the lowest-paid workers receiving raises of more than 40%.
Blue Bird will contribute to a retirement plan for workers, share profits, and improve health and safety under the new contract.
Blue Bird received $40 million in federal aid to build electric school buses.
The negotiations were closely watched by President Joe Biden’s administration due to the company's receipt of federal aid for electric school bus production.
Acting Labor Secretary Julie Su helped bring the contract negotiations to a successful agreement.
Blue Bird is one of three major school bus manufacturers in the United States, alongside Thomas Built Buses (a subsidiary of Daimler Truck AG) and IC Bus (a unit of Navistar International).
Blue Bird had previously urged employees to reject unionization last year but CEO Phil Horlock stated contract talks were “very collaborative” and expressed looking forward to a strong partnership.
The company stated that higher pay, benefits, and career development opportunities will help attract workers.
CEO Phil Horlock said the agreement provides positive outcomes for all parties and will bolster Blue Bird’s position as an employer of choice in the region.
Blue Bird is a publicly held company based in Macon with about 2,000 employees overall.
Blue Bird has long been the largest private employer in Peach County, Georgia.
The unionization vote was considered a notable win for organized labor in the traditionally unfriendly Deep South.

United Steelworkers District 9 Director Dan Flippo stated that federal investments must come with a seat at the table for workers and criticized corporations trying to run away from unions in the South.
The nationwide share of unionized workers dipped to 10% last year, with private sector workers at 6%, according to the federal Bureau of Labor Statistics.
Only 4.4% of Georgia workers are union members, and state business leaders have historically been hostile to unions.
Republican Gov. Brian Kemp pushed through a law this year barring companies taking state incentives from recognizing unions without a formal secret-ballot election.
source_title: Workers at Georgia school bus maker Blue Bird approve their first union contract | AP News

facts: Blue Bird Corporation (Nasdaq: BLBD) is the leading independent designer and manufacturer of school buses.
Blue Bird Corporation appointed Razvan Radulescu as Chief Financial Officer effective October 1, 2021.
Razvan Radulescu has over 20 years of financial and operating experience at Daimler Trucks in Finance, Controlling, Procurement, IT, and Compliance.
Most recently, Razvan Radulescu served as General Manager of Procurement for Daimler Trucks North America, responsible for all production and aftersales component purchases.
Prior to that role, Razvan Radulescu was CFO of Daimler’s Global Powertrain Truck Business Unit in Stuttgart, Germany.
From 2013 to 2017, Razvan Radulescu served as Controller for Freightliner Trucks inside Daimler Trucks North America.

Phil Horlock, Chief Executive Officer of Blue Bird Corporation, stated that Razvan is an accomplished global executive with extensive financial experience in the truck industry and will join the senior leadership team.
Razvan Radulescu will take over from interim CFO Phil Tighe on October 1, 2021, while Phil Tighe continues through the end of the year to facilitate a smooth transition.
Razvan Radulescu holds a Bachelor’s of Computer Science from the Academy of Economics Studies in Bucharest, Romania, and an MBA from Case Western Reserve University in Cleveland, OH.
Blue Bird has sold more than 570,000 buses since its formation in 1927 and has approximately 180,000 buses in operation today.
Blue Bird distinguishes itself by its singular focus on the design, engineering, manufacture, and sale of school buses and related parts.
Blue Bird is the only manufacturer of chassis and body production specifically designed for school bus applications.
Blue Bird is the market leader in alternative-fuel applications with a best-in-industry range of electric-, propane-, and compressed natural gas-powered school buses.
Blue Bird manufactures school buses at two facilities in Fort Valley, Georgia.
The Micro Bird joint venture operates a manufacturing facility in Drummondville, Quebec, Canada.
Service and after-market parts are distributed from Blue Bird’s parts distribution center located in Delaware, Ohio.

facts: Blue Bird Corporation announced a new product offering for buses ordered on or after May 1, 2017 with Allison transmissions: FuelSense 2.0 featuring DynActive Shifting.
FuelSense 2.0 is a software update to the previous FuelSense offering that allows fleets to optimize fuel economy and performance to their specific needs.
Mark Terry, chief commercial officer at Blue Bird Corporation, stated excitement over Allison's response to customer needs to help increase fuel economy and anticipated savings for districts.
FuelSense 2.0 uses DynActive Shifting to provide an infinitely variable combination of shift points and a learning algorithm to continuously find the ideal balance of fuel economy and performance.
The technology provides maximum value for applications with heavy start-stop needs, such as school buses.
Randy Kirk, senior vice president of product engineering at Allison, stated they tested FuelSense 2.0 with customers across various duty cycles, accumulating more than 3 million miles.
Testing showed fuel economy gains of up to 6 percent.
This improvement resulted in a potential vehicle savings of $300 to $900 annually based on the application, annual mileage and fuel price.
Three packages are available: FuelSense 2.0, FuelSense 2.0 Plus, and FuelSense 2.0 Max, all including DynActive Shifting.
FuelSense 2.0 Plus and Max include Neutral at Stop, while FuelSense 2.0 Max includes enhanced Acceleration Rate Management.
The Improved ‘Neutral at Stop’ feature lowers fuel consumption and emissions by reducing or eliminating the load on the engine when stopped.

Two versions of Neutral at Stop are available: Standard (partial/first-level) and Premium (full Neutral at Stop with low-speed coasting capability), both featuring a locked output at stop to prevent rollback.

Enhanced ‘Acceleration Rate Management’ mitigates aggressive driving by automatically controlling engine torque, providing more precision by limiting vehicle acceleration to a customized rate across five levels of control.

Heidi Schutte, vice president of marketing and sales for the Americas with Allison Transmission, stated Allison is constantly working towards improved fuel economy and that FuelSense 2.0 offers increased specification flexibility and delivers the best economic value to customers.
New Blue Bird buses with Allison Transmissions are now available with FuelSense 2.0 options.
Blue Bird Corporation is celebrating its 90th year in business as of the announcement date.

Blue Bird (Nasdaq: BLBD) is the leading independent designer and manufacturer of school buses, with more than 550,000 buses sold since its formation in 1927 and approximately 180,000 buses in operation today.
Blue Bird distinguishes itself by a singular focus on the design, engineering, manufacture and sale of school buses and related parts.
Blue Bird is the only manufacturer of chassis and body production specifically designed for school bus applications.
Blue Bird is recognized as an industry leader for school bus innovation, safety, product quality/reliability/durability, operating costs and drivability.
Blue Bird is the market leader in alternative fuel applications with its propane-powered and compressed natural gas-powered school buses.
Blue Bird manufactures school buses at two facilities in Fort Valley, Georgia.
Blue Bird's Micro Bird joint venture operates a manufacturing facility in Drummondville, Quebec, Canada.
Service and after-market parts are distributed from Blue Bird’s parts distribution center located in Delaware, Ohio.

Allison Transmission (NYSE: ALSN) is the world's largest manufacturer of fully automatic transmissions for medium- and heavy-duty commercial vehicles and a leader in hybrid-propulsion systems for city buses.
Allison transmissions are used in applications including refuse, construction, fire, distribution, bus, motorhomes, defense and energy.
Allison Transmission was founded in 1915, is headquartered in Indianapolis, Indiana, USA, and employs approximately 2,600 people worldwide.
Allison has a market presence in more than 80 countries with regional headquarters in the Netherlands, China and Brazil.
Allison has manufacturing facilities in the U.S., Hungary and India.
Allison has approximately 1,400 independent distributor and dealer locations worldwide.
source_title: Blue Bird Corporation has announced a new product offering

facts: The Georgia Center of Innovation hosted hundreds of attendees at the March 16th Georgia Logistics Summit in Macon.
The total sales impact of Georgia’s logistics industries jumped to $83.4 billion in 2021, equaling approximately 7% of the state’s total sales, an increase from $60.7 billion (or 6%) in 2018.
Georgia outpaced the nation in warehousing services growth at 92.8% growth since 2018.
In 2018, one out of every 14 Georgia jobs owed its existence to logistics; today, that number has grown to one in every nine Georgia jobs.

Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that long-term investments have made Georgia’s logistics networks a gateway to the entire United States, with connectivity and speed-to-market service leading to record-breaking economic investment and trade year after year.
The Summit featured two panels: “Inside the Mind – Awakening Your Talent Search” and a keynote panel, “A Vision for Supply Chain Digitalization in 2030.”
Panelists included business leaders from Amazon, RaceTrac, Blue Bird Corporation, and Accenture who shared experiences on how technology is shaping logistics and fulfillment operations.
Companies are focusing more on vertical integration through leading-edge technology and automation to streamline logistics sequences through the supply chain.
It was noted that 50% of existing repetitive tasks can be automated through existing technology.
Amazon highlighted its electric delivery van fleet order with American startup Rivian to accommodate increasing demand for “last mile” deliveries.
Sean Holloway, General Manager, Sav 3, at Amazon in Macon, stated that companies will become more focused on being good stewards and green technology moving into the future.
Macon Mayor Lester Miller and Irving Consumer Products highlighted the host city of Macon and factors making it a prime community for logistics success.

Georgia Department of Transportation Planning Director Jannine Miller provided updates on strategic investments to maintain and improve Georgia’s top-ranked interstate highway system, roads, and bridges.
GDOT provided a briefing on strategic investments in local projects to help trucks move more efficiently and updates on long-term projects such as the I-75/I-16 Interchange in Macon.

Center of Innovation Executive Director David Nuckolls stated that digitalization changes how business is done and the Center fills a critical need for Georgia businesses by showcasing industry best practices to make the logistics industry more competitive.

The event’s first panel focused on educating the needed workforce, with representatives from secondary and higher education, along with the Technical College System of Georgia, discussing aligning education with industry needs.
New career pathways in warehousing and distribution are preparing students for direct entry into the industry upon graduation, and in some cases while still in school.
The availability and skill of Georgia’s large population of veterans was discussed, including opportunities through the VECTR Center (Georgia Veterans Education Career Resource).

The VECTR Center provides unique, accelerated programs in high demand and strategic industries tailored to abbreviate the process of receiving post-secondary certificates and degrees by recognizing military training.
Panelists focused on developing students’ core skills, creating future leaders, and developing partnerships with local schools and industrial and economic development authorities.
Georgia is home to 85% of third-party logistics companies and is ranked No. 1 for infrastructure and access to global markets by Area Development.
The state is home to the busiest and most efficient airport in the world, Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport, and the fastest-growing seaport in the U.S. at the Port of Savannah.

Georgia offers a robust rail and highway infrastructure, with more rail miles than any other state in the Southeast at 5,000 miles of rail, and 1,200 miles of interstate highways including I-75, I-85, I-95, and I-20, plus 20,000 miles of federal and state highways.
Many world-leading logistics companies have operations or headquarters in Georgia including Amazon, The Coca-Cola Company, Delta Air Lines, The Home Depot, Norfolk Southern, and UPS.
Additional information on the 2022 Georgia Logistics Summit and next year’s plans is available through official channels.

links.context: 2022 Georgia Logistics Summit information and next year's plans

source_title: Georgia Center of Innovation Hosts 2022 Logistics Summit in Macon - The ATL Airport Chamber

facts: On Tuesday, April 4, Bonnell Aluminum announced the operation expansion of its TSLOTS line of aluminium structural framing system into the Southeastern region of the United States.
Bonnell is adding a new manufacturing and distribution site at Newnan, Georgia, to its existing facilities in Clearfield, Utah and Niles, Michigan.
The expansion will make the TSLOTS product line more easily accessible to customers in the Southeastern United States.
The TSLOTS aluminium framing system enables the creation of light, durable, versatile, and functional structures using T-slotted aluminium extrusions and connection hardware.

Randy Johnson, Senior Brand Manager for TSLOTS by Bonnell Aluminum, stated that the company has been successful in growing its TSLOTS product line by positioning manufacturing and distribution sites closer to where customers operate.
This expansion marks Bonnell's second major investment in TSLOTS in two years, following a successful expansion into the Midwest in 2021.
The capacity addition will increase the company's footprint across the U.S. and provide customers with reduced shipping costs and shorter lead times.
Bonnell Aluminum is a subsidiary of Tredegar Corporation.
The extrusions produced by Bonnell serve the building and construction industry, automotive and transportation, and machinery and equipment sectors.
Besides TSLOTS, Futura Transitions is another framing system product line offered by Bonnell Aluminum.

facts: A South Korean automotive supply company announced it would open a facility in Macon, according to a press release from the Macon-Bibb County Industrial Authority.
Boogook Industries will redevelop the old Brown and Williamson cigarette plant on Weaver Road near the Macon Downtown Airport in the industrial area of east Bibb.
The plant is expected to open in 2024.
The facility will create 80 full-time jobs in Macon.
Job fields will include management, manufacturing, assembly, and delivery.
Boogook committed to investing a total of $6.8 million into the project.
Boogook specializes in fluid containers for cars.
The company chose the Macon location because of the nearby Hyundai plant in Savannah and Kia plant in West Point.
This facility will be the first American plant for Boogook.
The former cigarette factory was one of the largest employers in Macon, with Brown and Williamson employing 3,000 workers at its peak in 1997.
Mayor Lester Miller stated that the location's access to interstates and highways makes it a prime spot for industries needing quick and efficient product movement.
Mayor Lester Miller thanked the Industrial Authority and welcomed Boogook Georgia as the newest member of Team Macon-Bibb.
Joong Suk Oh, Chief Executive Officer of Boogook Georgia, stated that leadership recognized an opportunity to support Korean automotive manufacturing partners more locally.
Joong Suk Oh noted that expanding operations to Macon will drive their ability to serve the Southeast U.S. and expressed anticipation for a successful partnership with the MBCIA.
The former cigarette plant closed roughly 16 years ago prior to this announcement.
The property was sold to Los Angeles-based Industrial Realty Group for redevelopment in 2021.
It remains unclear how Industrial Realty Group developed the property.
source_title: South Korean factory should bring 80 jobs to Macon. Here’s where, when it will open

Boogook is a global leader in automotive tube manufacturing that specializes in crafting automotive parts and materials crucial for fluid systems within vehicles.
Last year, Boogook Georgia unveiled plans to invest $6.8 million to bolster its operations in Macon-Bibb County.
The company also planned an additional $300,000 in leasehold improvements.
This significant foreign investment enhances the automobile supply market in the Southeast and solidifies the region’s reputation as a hub for automotive manufacturing.
Boogook Georgia is revitalizing a sprawling two-million-square-foot industrial building located at 2600 Weaver Road.

Stephen Adams expressed excitement about seeing this industry become a key player in future economic and job growth.

entity_name: Automotive Parts Antitrust Class Action Settlement
canonical_name: Re: Automotive Parts Antitrust Litigation, Case No. 12-MD-02311
facts: The settlement resolves antitrust allegations against automotive parts manufacturers who allegedly conspired together to artificially raise and fix the price of vehicle components.
The total auto parts class action settlement is valued at $1.2 billion, with this being round five of the distribution.

Round five of the settlement includes $3.152 million and resolves claims with Bosal Industries Georgia Inc., Bosal USA, Robert Bosch GmbH, Robert Bosch LLC, ZF TRW Automotive Holdings Corp, ZF Friedrichshafen AG and Lucas Automotive GmbH.
Automotive parts covered by round five are electronic braking systems, hydraulic braking systems and exhaust systems.
Rounds one to four of the settlement resolved claims from various manufacturers and covered parts such as air conditioning systems, brake hoses, bearings and more.
The automotive parts manufacturers have not admitted any wrongdoing but agreed to pay $1.2 billion to resolve these claims.
The conduct was allegedly the subject of the largest-ever antitrust investigation pursued by the Department of Justice’s Antitrust Division.

According to the lawsuit, the DOJ has charged more than 100 individuals and companies with criminal antitrust violations and levied more than $2.9 billion in criminal fines against various automotive parts manufacturers.

The settlement benefits individuals who purchased or leased an eligible new vehicle in the United States between 2002 and 2018 or who paid to replace one or more qualifying vehicle parts not for resale.
Class members can receive a cash payment based on the number of vehicles purchased, the amount paid for replacement parts and other factors.
Payments will be proportional based on these factors, though all class members will receive at least $100 for claims across all rounds of the settlement.
The deadline for exclusion and objection is Dec. 20, 2022.
The final approval hearing for the auto parts antitrust settlement is scheduled for Jan. 12, 2023.
In order to receive settlement benefits, class members must submit a valid claim form by Jan. 7, 2023.
Class members who submitted a claim during rounds one through four do not need to file another claim form.

Automotive parts manufacturers often produce the same or similar parts for countless vehicles under a range of brands, such as electronic braking systems manufactured by Robert Bosch being used in multiple vehicle brands.
source_title: Auto parts antitrust $3.2M class action settlement

facts: Howard Industries, Inc. is headquartered in Laurel, MS.
The company specializes in the design, manufacturing, and distribution of electric transformers.
Howard Industries has decades of experience and is a leading provider of high-quality transformers and comprehensive service capabilities.
The product portfolio includes dry-type transformers, oil-filled transformers, cast coil transformers, pad-mounted transformers, and custom-designed transformers.
These transformers are engineered to meet diverse power distribution and control needs across industries.
The company emphasizes customization, with skilled engineers and technicians collaborating with clients to develop tailored transformer solutions based on specific power requirements.
Howard Industries operates state-of-the-art manufacturing facilities equipped with cutting-edge equipment and machinery.
Rigorous quality control measures are applied throughout the manufacturing process, including premium material selection, stringent testing, and inspection procedures.
The company provides comprehensive service capabilities including installation assistance, preventive maintenance services, repairs, retrofits, and technical support.
Howard Industries serves a wide range of industries, including utilities, industrial, commercial, and renewable energy sectors.
The company maintains a commitment to quality, innovation, and customer-centricity to meet evolving power needs.
source_title: Howard Industries, Inc. | Electric Transformer

facts: Daesol Ausys plans to build a $72 million factory in West Point, Georgia.
The new plant will make luggage boards and covers for compartments in electric vehicles.
The company will hire more than 140 workers for the new facility.
Daesol Ausys supplies parts to Hyundai, Kia, and General Motors.
This will be the third factory in the same West Point industrial park for members of the company's corporate family.
Daehan Solution opened a million-dollar factory there in 2008 to make headliners and other sound insulation parts for the nearby Kia plant.
Daesol Material opened a $35 million factory there in 2017 to make automotive fiber padding.
Daehan Solution currently employs 350 full-time workers.
Daesol Material currently employs 50 workers.
Meghan Richardson, an economic developer for the city of West Point, provided employment figures for the existing factories.
Daehan Solution also has a plant near Montgomery, Alabama, which supplies the Hyundai assembly plant there.

The announcement follows a wave of electric vehicle suppliers announcing plants in Georgia after Hyundai said in 2022 that it would build a $5.5 billion plant to assemble electric vehicles and batteries in Ellabell, Georgia, near Savannah.
The Hyundai site in Ellabell could grow to 8,100 employees and is slated to begin producing vehicles in 2025.
Suppliers for the Hyundai project have pledged to invest nearly $2.2 billion and to hire 5,000 people.

Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp stated that the state's automotive industry employs over 50,000 hardworking Georgians and will continue to grow as e-mobility suppliers choose all corners of the state for the jobs of the future.
Daesol Ausys is scheduled to begin production in December 2024.
The company will pay workers $15 to $20 an hour.
The state will pay to train workers for the new facility.
The state gave $500,000 last year to help Harris County clear and grade the site and build a road.
Harris County matched the $500,000 state grant using sales tax collections.
The city of West Point and Harris County are granting eight years of property tax breaks worth a projected $5.7 million.
Daesol Ausys could qualify for $875,000 in state income tax credits, at $1,250 per job over five years.
To qualify for the $875,000 state income tax credits, workers must make at least $31,300 a year.
source_title: South Korean auto supplier plans $72 million plant in Georgia to build electric vehicle parts - Yahoo News UK

facts: Daimler AG will become Mercedes-Benz Group AG on February 1, 2022.
Following the successful stock market debut of Daimler Truck, the group's renewed focus is on the automotive business.
The world-famous brand Mercedes-Benz was created in 1926 when the predecessor companies of Carl Benz and Gottlieb Daimler were merged with the aim of revolutionizing automobile production.
The company's stock exchange symbol changes from »DAI« to »MBG», with no further changes for shareholders, and shares will remain listed on Germany’s blue-chip DAX stock index.
The renaming underlines a renewed strategic focus on building the most desirable cars in the world.
The company aims to take the lead in electric mobility and vehicle software.
Ola Källenius serves as Chairman of the Board of Management of Mercedes-Benz Group AG.
Daimler Mobility AG is adapting its brand to Mercedes-Benz Mobility AG, offering mobility services for passenger cars and vans in financing, leasing, and insurance.
The finance and mobility division enables customers to use vehicles flexibly via rental and subscription models, fleet management, and digital services for charging and payment.
The historic realignment was approved by shareholders with an overwhelming majority, completing a process that began last year.
On December 10, 2021, Daimler Truck Holding AG was launched as an independent company on the Frankfurt Stock Exchange.
The new Mercedes-Benz Group fully concentrates on its passenger car brands Mercedes-Benz, Mercedes-AMG, and Mercedes-Maybach as well as vans.
Mercedes-Benz Group holds a minority stake of 35% in Daimler Truck Holding AG, with around 5% held in pension assets.
source_title: Daimler embarks on a new era as Mercedes-Benz Group | Mercedes-Benz Group > Company > News

facts: The new electric trucks will pick up parts from various regional suppliers and deliver them to DTNA’s consolidation centre in Portland, Oregon.
The company already runs the ‘Electric Island’ there, a truck charging site that opened in 2021.
The eCascadia is also built in Portland.
Daimler’s US brand Freightliner launched the eCascadia in 2022.
The battery-electric truck is a Class 8 semitrailer tractor.
Customers can choose between three battery options for up to 230 miles or 370 kilometres.
Its most recent customer, Reyes Coca-Cola bottling, received 20 semis this week.
For becoming a client, DNTA says, deploying its trucks in the inbound logistics network demonstrated their potential within the company’s own fleet.

“By integrating electric trucks into our inbound logistics network, we aim to reduce our environmental impact and contribute to a more sustainable future that goes beyond truck development and manufacturing,” added Jeff Allen, Senior VP of operations and specialty vehicles of DTNA.
Daimler Truck North America has headquarters in Portland and comprises the Freightliner, Western Star, and Thomas Built Buses brands.
DNTA is a subsidiary of Daimler Truck Holding AG.
source_title: DNTA to rely on its own electric trucks in logistics - electrive.com

facts: Daimler Truck North America announced plans last month to invest $285 million to expand and upgrade subsidiary Detroit Diesel Corp.’s facility in Redford Township, Michigan.

The planned investment aims to modernize and enhance the existing plant to maintain its current production capacity while equipping it to manufacture electric powertrain components for medium- and heavy-duty electric trucks.
Matthew Pfaffenbach, VP of Daimler’s powertrain operations, provided details on the investment in an email to Manufacturing Dive.
The projected investment will create 436 jobs as well as retain 2,000 employees.
Construction is expected to begin in early 2025.
Daimler applied for approximately $31 million in state incentives for the Detroit Diesel project, which the Michigan Economic Development Corp. approved on Dec. 10.
The Detroit Diesel facility in Redford Township dates back to 1938 and now spans over 3 million square feet.
The facility produces engines, transmissions and axles for the Freightliner, Western Star and Thomas Built Buses vehicle platforms.
With the investment, the company plans to add approximately 110,000 square feet to the Detroit Diesel facility.
Daimler’s Detroit Diesel project includes establishing a research and development facility focused on ZEV battery prototyping and testing.
The company added that it may further invest in the Detroit Diesel facility as it establishes a ZEV manufacturing hub within its production network.
The project aims to strengthen Daimler’s position within the ZEV market, which it expects will rapidly grow through the decade’s end.
The truck maker’s latest plans align with its joint project with utility company DTE Energy, “Truck Stop of the Future,” which helps companies transition their fleets to electric commercial vehicles.
Diversifying the site’s product portfolio is critical to its long-term viability as the commercial truck industry evolves from traditional internal combustion engine products to ZEV solutions.

The upgrades aim to modernize and enhance the existing plant to maintain its current production capacity and innovation of ICE components, which remain crucial for many commercial trucking applications, while also supporting the growing demand for cleaner, more fuel-efficient engines and components.
Daimler entered the ZEV market in 2021 by introducing a battery electric product line, including the Detroit ePowertrain for the Freightliner eCascadia and Freightliner eM2.
Since entering the ZEV market, Daimler has continued to invest in the development and manufacturing of EV batteries and components to adapt to the rapidly evolving automotive market.
In May, Detroit Diesel expanded a facility in Hibbing, Minnesota focusing on BEV-specific remanufactured parts and high-voltage manufacturing components, such as batteries and inverters.
source_title: Daimler Truck to upgrade Michigan plant for traditional, zero-emission trucks | Trucking Dive

facts: Daimler Truck plans to lay off 573 employees in North Carolina.
The layoffs are due to struggles to grow North American sales amid uncertainty over the economy and U.S. trade policy.
Daimler Truck North America notified state officials in a July 11 letter about the planned layoffs.
The layoffs will affect mostly truck assemblers at a plant in Mount Holly.
Layoffs are scheduled to start on Friday.
The company cited a sustained reduction in orders and a diminished build rate as reasons for the layoffs.
Workers can be paid through Sept. 9 in lieu of notice.
The notification letter was sent to the North Carolina Department of Commerce.
The company stated the layoffs are expected to be temporary, but of an unknown duration.
A company spokesperson did not immediately respond to a request for additional information.
The layoff notification was required under a federal law intended to give workers advance notice to plan job transitions.
WRAL received the letter through a public records request.
Daimler Truck makes commercial vehicles under the Freightliner, Western Star, and Thomas Built Buses brands.
Many of these commercial vehicles are made in North Carolina.
The company’s North American sales were down 18% during the first half of 2025.
The sales decline was due in part to uncertainty over the U.S. economy.
The company had lowered its North American sales forecast in May.
The company expects to maintain a profit margin of 11% to 13% for the unit.
The company expected to sell between 260,000 and 290,000 units in North America this year.
This sales forecast is down from a previous forecast of between 280,000 and 320,000 units.
Daimler mainly sells its trucks to other businesses.
Customers have delayed purchases while assessing how U.S. trade policies affect their industries.
The company’s customers haul products including steel, produce, petroleum, and more.
These products could be affected by tariffs, executive orders, or provisions of President Donald Trump’s One Big Beautiful Bill Act.
John O’Leary, chief executive of Daimler Truck North America, stated that people are looking for certainty regarding tariffs or other economic policy.
O’Leary noted customers are in wait-and-see mode because their end customers generating freight moves and rates are also waiting for certainty.
Customers are currently on the sidelines waiting for signs of certainty and not buying trucks.
Trump has increased tariffs on nearly every other country in the world.
This tariff policy is expected to drive up prices for goods but supporters say it might lead to a resurgence in American manufacturing.
Critics argue tariffs don't make sense in the modern global economy.
Mike Pence, Trump’s former vice president, traveled to Raleigh in May to make the case against tariffs.
Pence stated that Americans will ultimately pay the cost of tariffs in higher prices, lower wages, and lost jobs.
Daimler Truck has been analyzing the potential impact of tariffs on truck manufacturing and sales.
O’Leary told equity analysts in North Carolina last week that there is still a lot of clarity that needs to be provided from the government regarding tariffs.
O’Leary added that tariffs would have an inflationary effect on the industry.
Added costs from tariffs would likely be pushed out to customers.
Daimler Truck North America executives have tried to dissuade the federal government from imposing tariffs affecting truck manufacturers.

Carsten Kirchholtes, DTNA’s general manager of procurement and supply chain, and Sean Waters, the company’s vice president of product integrity, wrote a May public comment letter to officials at the U.S. Department of Commerce’s Bureau of Industry and Security.
The bureau has been investigating import policies across a variety of sectors.
O’Leary told analysts last week that the company has the flexibility to shift some North American production from Mexico to the U.S. if needed.
O’Leary stated the company has game-planned different scenarios but it is still not clear, and he cannot wait for clarity.
The temporary layoffs in North Carolina come less than two months after Daimler Truck Financial Services USA announced plans to consolidate and relocate its headquarters to Charlotte.
The financial services relocation is expected to create up to 276 high-paying jobs in Mecklenburg County.
Commerce officials approved an incentives package of about $5.2 million for that expansion, including a jobs grant worth up to $4.2 million.
Daimler Truck Financial Services USA plans to invest more than $7.8 million to relocate its headquarters.
The financial services headquarters are currently split between offices in Redford Township, Michigan, and Fort Worth, Texas.
The new North Carolina headquarters is expected to house administration, human resources, and financial operations.
The factory layoffs are not expected to affect DTFS’s relocation plans, according to a spokesperson for the state Department of Commerce.
source_title: Daimler Truck laying off 573 at North Carolina plant as economic uncertainty slows sales :: WRAL.com

facts: DTNA will lay off around 2,000 employees at five sites as the weak and uncertain freight market continues to take a toll on truck manufacturers.

Employees at production facilities operated by the parent company of Class 8 truck brands Freightliner and Western Star in Mount Holly and Gastonia, N.C.; Detroit; Portland, Ore.; and Saltillo, Mexico, will be laid off.
A spokesman confirmed the layoffs in a statement released to Transport Topics on July 14.
DTNA cited a notable slowdown in new truck orders, particularly in its medium-duty, on-highway, and electric vehicle segments.
Each affected site will experience different impacts based on local business needs.
DTNA remains optimistic that market conditions will improve, enabling it to reassess operational needs and potentially reinstate affected roles.

The Portland and Mount Holly layoffs will take place on July 18, Gastonia employees will be laid off on July 25, and Detroit plant staff will be laid off on Sept. 18 per a spokesman update on July 15.

DTNA declined to provide details on how many jobs would be affected at each site, but Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification Act reports filed with the North Carolina Department of Commerce indicate 573 will be at Mount Holly and Gastonia.
The most up-to-date WARN notice filings for Michigan and Oregon did not have any record of the DTNA layoffs.
A majority of the head count reductions across the five sites are production-related positions.
DTNA hopes to recall employees as soon as market order activity picks back up.
DTNA currently has about 28,000 employees across North America.
Mount Holly produces Freightliner’s medium-duty models.
The Gastonia facility carries out stamping, metal fabrication and sub-assembly of cab and chassis parts.
The Detroit Manufacturing Plant in Redford Township, Mich., is the home of DTNA’s Detroit Diesel engine division.
In December, DTNA said more than 400 jobs were being added at the Detroit plant as part of a $285 million capital investment program.
The Detroit plant had 2,000 employees at that time in December.
The Portland plant builds Freightliner’s eCascadia and eM2 battery-electric trucks as well as Western Star’s X-Series lineup.
The Saltillo plant opened in 2009 and focuses on production of the Freightliner Cascadia, North America’s best-selling Class 8 on-highway tractor.
Portland, Ore.-based DTNA is the third truck maker to announce a head count reduction across North American production facilities in recent months.
Volvo Group axed 1,000 positions across its Volvo Truck North America and Mack Trucks divisions in the spring.
Traton Group’s International Motors unit cut 900 jobs at its heavy-duty truck manufacturing plant in Escobedo, Mexico, shelving the second shift at the assembly facility.
Both of DTNA’s peers blamed demand weakness for their respective job cuts.
DTNA's parent company cut its 2025 global and North American truck sales guidance in May as a result of the weak U.S. freight market and trade policy uncertainty.
Globally, Daimler Truck expects to sell between 430,000 and 460,000 trucks and buses in 2025, compared with prior expectations in a 460,000-480,000 range.
In North America, heavy-duty Class 8 sales are expected to be in a range of 260,000 to 290,000 vehicles, compared with previous expectations in a 280,000-320,000 trucks range.

DTNA is forecast to sell 155,000 to 175,000 trucks in 2025 across all classes, down from a 180,000 to 200,000 range previously, with Daimler Truck citing the Trump administration’s decision to introduce tariffs and reciprocal actions of its peers.

No job cuts had been implemented because of the tariffs, according to DTNA CEO John O’Leary during a media roundtable at the parent company’s Capital Market Day 2025 at the Cleveland Truck Plant on July 8.
O’Leary said some shifts had been trimmed due to shutdown days and weeks.
Cleveland Truck Plant is Freightliner’s largest U.S. manufacturing plant.
The United Auto Workers, 7,400 of whose members inked a four-year labor contract with DTNA in May 2024, was not immediately available for comment on the layoffs.
The union deal covers unionized staff at Cleveland, Mount Holly and Gastonia.
DTNA truck and bus sales fell 20% year over year to 38,580 units in the second quarter of 2025 from the year-ago period’s 48,246 total.
North American Class 8 truck orders in June slumped 36% year over year to 9,400 units and dived 28.8% from the 13,200 vehicles reported for May, according to ACT Research.
Sales and orders have failed to rebound as the freight market weakness has become ever more elongated, quashing hopes expressed at the start of the calendar year.
The Cleveland Truck Plant was chosen as the site for the CMD because of optimism about the prospects for North America, per Daimler Truck CEO Karin Radstrom.
Radstrom noted there is a lot more doubt about the economy, pre-buy, and everything, adding that markets are very uncertain and volatile.
Daimler Truck announced plans to ax around 5,000 jobs in Germany in the hours preceding the CMD.
The company also plans to double the size of its defense business by 2030 as military spending increases globally.
The German job cuts are part of a “Stronger 2030” strategic overhaul that includes a renewed focus on diesel powertrains.

One of the five pillars of the overhaul is termed “transform at the speed of right” and involves downshifting Daimler Truck’s battery-electric and hydrogen fuel cell truck development while accelerating diesel powertrain investment.

O’Leary stated that market maturity for zero-emissions vehicles is losing momentum in North America, prompting a reshaped capital allocation strategy to refocus on further developing internal combustion technology.
source_title: DTNA to Temporarily Lay Off 2,000 Production Plant Staff - TT

facts: Freightliner Trucks is an indispensable part of the North American haulage sector.
In weight classes 5 to 8, Freightliner trucks serve a wide range of truck applications.
Freightliner is the top-selling truck brand in North America in weight classes 6-8.
State-of-the-art technology, superb efficiency and optimum safety make Freightliner trucks a compelling proposition for customers.
Freightliner Trucks is a Daimler Truck North America brand with production locations in the United States and Mexico.
The Freightliner New Cascadia® is the flagship in the long-distance haulage segment.
The vocational segment portfolio includes a wide range of customizable vehicles for numerous applications.
Fleet managers and independent companies associate Freightliner with efficient, reliable vehicles used day-in, day-out even in grueling conditions.
With the eCascadia and eM2 models, Freightliner offers fully electric medium and heavy-duty trucks for the North American market.
The brand's product portfolio includes natural gas and hybrid models as further alternative drive technologies for specific customer requirements.
source_title: Freightliner | Daimler Truck

facts: Joanna Buttler serves as Head of the Global Autonomous Technology Group at Daimler Truck North America.
Joanna Buttler joined Grayson Brulte on The Road to Autonomy podcast to discuss the development of the autonomous-ready Freightliner Cascadia and Daimler Truck’s outlook for autonomous trucking.
Joanna Buttler stated that the company sees autonomy as one of its biggest opportunities.
Daimler Truck is focused on deploying long-haul autonomous trucks in the United States as part of a hub-to-hub strategy.
Daimler is developing the autonomous-ready Freightliner Cascadia truck platform.
Torc Robotics is developing the autonomous driving software for the platform.
At the Daimler Truck Capital Market Day 2023 in July, the company announced that autonomous trucks represent a strong strategic pillar.
The company projected that autonomous trucks have the potential to deliver €3 billion in revenue by 2030.
The official launch for Daimler’s autonomous trucks is scheduled for 2027.
Customers will purchase the autonomous-ready Freightliner Cascadia from Daimler Truck and an autonomous driving software subscription from Torc with flexible pricing models.
Daimler will coordinate the entire transaction to simplify the sales process.
Daimler will offer financing for the vehicles through Daimler Truck Financial.
Customers will be able to achieve higher utilization with autonomous trucks as they will operate 24/7 with limited downtime.
Higher utilization could lead to lower shipping costs and increased margins for fleets.
Daimler is developing a redundant autonomous-ready chassis to ensure the highest levels of safety and functionality.
The development process started with a list of 1,500 requirements jointly developed with Waymo and Torc to identify systems, features, and tasks required for autonomous driving.
Joanna Buttler stated that autonomy can and will bring great benefits for society.
source_title: Autonomous-Ready Truck Platform | The Road to Autonomy

facts: Rakesh Aneja, head of eMobility at DTNA, has been appointed to the newly founded Electric Vehicle Working Group (EVWG) by the U.S. Department of Energy and U.S. Department of Transportation.

The EVWG was established by the Joint Office of Energy and Transportation to provide vital recommendations to the U.S. Secretaries of Energy and Transportation for integrating light-, medium, and heavy-duty electric vehicles into the U.S. transportation and energy systems.
The EVWG collaborates closely with federal interagency working groups, especially on fleet conversion, to ensure effective coordination and insightful consultation.
The EVWG will regularly report on barriers and opportunities to scale up EV adoption to the U.S. Secretaries of Energy and Transportation and key congressional committees.
Under the guidance of the U.S. Secretaries of Energy and Transportation, the EVWG comprises 25 members, encompassing 6 federal stakeholders and 19 non-governmental stakeholders.
The non-governmental participants represent diverse backgrounds, experiences, and viewpoints across geographically diverse regions in the United States, spanning rural, urban, and suburban contexts.

Rakesh Aneja stated he is incredibly honored and enthusiastic to accept the invitation to serve as a Committee member of the U.S. Government’s Electric Vehicle Working Group, calling it a significant recognition of DTNA's strides in eMobility and underscoring their commitment to leading sustainable transportation.
DTNA has been a pioneer in commercial electric vehicle development.
DTNA’s portfolio of electric vehicles includes the Jouley, an all-electric bus from Thomas Built Bus, available since 2020.
DTNA’s portfolio includes the FCCC MT50e walk-in van chassis for final mile delivery, available since 2021.
DTNA’s portfolio includes the Class 8 Freightliner eCascadias, available since 2022.
DTNA’s portfolio includes the Freightliner eM2, set to debut later in 2023.
Beyond vehicle manufacturing, DTNA is proactively fostering the transition to zero-emission commercial vehicles, including infrastructure and holistic ecosystem initiatives.

DTNA established "Electric Island" in collaboration with Portland General Electric in 2021 as the first of its kind commercial vehicle charging site to drive the development, testing, and deployment of zero-emission commercial vehicles.

In partnership with NextEra Energy and Blackrock Alternatives, DTNA made substantial investments through the Greenlane joint venture launched in April 2023 to address the urgent need for a publicly available, nationwide electric charging infrastructure for commercial vehicles.
DTNA is the nation’s leading manufacturer of Class 6-8 commercial vehicles.
DTNA collaborates with federal and state regulatory agencies to bolster U.S. competitiveness and achieve shared goals of improved fleet efficiency and reduced greenhouse gas emissions.
DTNA remains steadfast in its long-term ambition to transition all new vehicle sales to exclusively carbon-neutral (in driving operation) by 2039.
DTNA emphasizes the importance of investing in clean energy and domestic clean energy industries to decarbonize commercial vehicles.

Daimler Truck North America LLC is headquartered in Portland, Oregon, and designs, engineers, manufactures and markets medium- and heavy-duty trucks, school buses, vehicle chassis and their associated technologies and components under the Freightliner, Western Star, Thomas Built Buses, Freightliner Custom Chassis Corp and Detroit brands.
Daimler Truck North America LLC is a subsidiary of Daimler Truck Holding AG (DTG), one of the world’s leading commercial vehicle manufacturers.

links.url: Daimler Truck North America Executive Joins U.S. Government’s Electric Vehicle Working Group | Daimler

source_title: Daimler Truck North America Executive Joins U.S. Government’s Electric Vehicle Working Group - School Transportation News

facts: Thomas Built Buses (TBB) is a leading manufacturer of school buses in North America and a division of Daimler Truck Specialty Vehicles (DTSV).
TBB is expanding its Type A Minotour production capacity at its legacy manufacturing plant located at 1408 Courtesy Road in High Point, N.C.

The expansion builds on TBB's legacy in High Point, which has been home to the company's original facility dating back to 1916 when it first produced streetcars before evolving to build Type C, Type D, and now Type A buses.
The project reflects TBB's streamlined production strategy following the relocation of Type D production to the Saf-T-Liner plant earlier this year.
Work on the Type A expansion began in late 2024 and is expected to be completed by 2026.
T.J. Reed, president and CEO of DTSV, stated that investing in the legacy facility honors more than a century of craftsmanship in High Point while continuing to modernize for the future.
The expansion will give teams the capacity and tools needed to build even more Type A Minotours for customers across North America.
The project includes a series of modernization efforts aimed at improving workflow, enhancing safety, and supporting long-term sustainability.
The expansion will increase production capacity by more than 50 percent.
Dedicated space for Minotour production has nearly tripled, now encompassing roughly 72,000 square feet of the building.

Jeff Allen, senior vice president of operations and specialty vehicles at Daimler Truck North America (DTNA), stated that investing in North Carolina strengthens operations while ensuring the company can serve more customers with greater efficiency, sustainability, and innovation.
Thomas Built Buses was founded in 1916 and is headquartered in High Point, North Carolina.

TBB is a subsidiary of Daimler Truck North America LLC, which designs, engineers, manufactures, and markets medium- and heavy-duty trucks, school buses, vehicle chassis, and associated technologies and components under the Freightliner, Western Star, Thomas Built Buses, Freightliner Custom Chassis Corp, and Detroit brands.
Thomas Built Buses and Freightliner Custom Chassis Corp together form Daimler Truck Specialty Vehicles.
Daimler Truck North America is a subsidiary of Daimler Truck, one of the world’s leading commercial vehicle manufacturers.
source_title: Thomas Built Buses Expands Type A Minotour Production Capacity at Plant in High Point - School Transportation News

facts: Daimler Truck North America is laying off 2,000 workers across five facilities in the U.S. and Mexico due to diminished market demand.
The layoffs are effective immediately and impact various operational roles, including those in manufacturing and logistics.
Medium-duty, on-highway, and electric vehicle segments are experiencing a notable slowdown in orders.
The layoffs are labeled as temporary, yet no definitive date for employee return has been established.
The announced layoffs start Friday and primarily impact two facilities near Charlotte along with production plants located in Detroit, Portland, and Saltillo, Mexico.
Each site will face unique challenges depending on its respective business needs, as outlined by the company’s officials.
A spokesperson for Daimler Truck North America stated that there has been a significant decrease in new truck orders, particularly within the medium-duty, on-highway, and electric vehicle segments.
Andrew Johnson emphasized that the decision to reduce the workforce across several facilities is essential to adapt to current market conditions.
Nearly 9,000 jobs have been slashed nationwide in the freight sector recently, underscoring collective industry difficulties.
In North Carolina, the layoffs will let go of 546 employees from a manufacturing facility in Mount Holly and an additional 27 from a components and logistics site in Gastonia.
The NC layoffs were notified through a Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification Act notice.
The impacted roles range widely from truck assemblers and painters to supervisory, material handling, and logistics positions.
Daimler Truck North America has approximately 29,200 employees listed on its website.
The company has a substantial presence across various manufacturing plants in Cleveland, Gastonia, High Point, Mount Holly, Gaffney, Redford, Saltillo, and Santiago in Mexico.
Daimler Truck North America is a subsidiary of Germany-based Daimler Truck AG.
The company boasts a portfolio of brands including Freightliner, Western Star, Mercedes-Benz, Fuso, BharatBenz, and Rizon.
Workforce reductions could lead to delays in shipments and services, making it difficult for businesses to maintain timely deliveries and efficient operations.
source_title: Daimler Truck Cuts Workforce by 2,000 Amidst Market Slowdown | Transportation News and Trends

facts: Waymo will soon begin on-highway testing of self-driving Freightliner Cascadia models equipped with redundant systems and components designed to eventually support unmanned operation.
Engineers at Daimler Truck North America modified the company’s flagship Cascadia model to create a Class 8 vehicle platform with backup systems necessary to ensure safety with no driver onboard.
The deployment of those vehicles in Waymo’s test fleet is the result of an ongoing collaboration between the technology developer and the truck manufacturer.
Daimler Truck North America and Waymo first announced their partnership in August 2020.
Boris Sofman, Waymo’s head of engineering for trucking, hailed the introduction of “Level 4-capable” Cascadias as an important milestone in the development of autonomous trucks.
During an Aug. 23 press briefing, Sofman stated they will put the first batch of these trucks into autonomous testing on public freeways in the upcoming weeks across Dallas and Phoenix.

Waymo’s test fleet is paving the way for Class 8 trucks capable of SAE Level 4 automated driving, where the vehicle can operate autonomously without relying on a human driver as a fallback under specific routes and conditions.
Jason DiGrande, engineering lead for Waymo’s OEM program, highlighted the collaboration between the engineering teams at Waymo and DTNA.
The partnership combines Waymo’s autonomous driving software and sensors with Daimler’s truck manufacturing expertise and product testing and validation.
Suman Narayanan, DTNA’s director of engineering, outlined a variety of systems added to the vehicle to support Level 4 operation.
The truck is equipped with a redundant braking system with two brake controllers and a redundant steering system with two servo motors.
It also features a redundant low-voltage communication network and high-speed CAN communication with redundancy.
Those systems are designed to enable an unmanned, fully autonomous truck to perform a “minimum risk maneuver” to bring the vehicle to a safe state in the event of a critical fault.
Additional safety and cybersecurity processes were built into the design.
Narayanan stated they are building the vehicle platform to seamlessly integrate into the Daimler Truck sales and service network, which is the backbone for their customers today.
The systems are being developed to be agnostic to the autonomous driving system and also to the propulsion system.
Separately from its partnership with Waymo, DTNA is working with its independent subsidiary Torc Robotics to develop Level 4 autonomous trucks.
Waymo owns and operates its own fleet of self-driving Cascadia trucks, which it is testing on public roads with safety drivers behind the wheel.

As Google’s sister company readies its technology for fully autonomous operation and commercial deployment in the years ahead, it envisions fleet customers purchasing trucks equipped with the Waymo Driver directly from DTNA.

As part of its ongoing testing and development efforts, Waymo has partnered with carriers such as J.B. Hunt and freight brokers C.H. Robinson and Uber Freight, along with a maintenance support agreement with Ryder System.
source_title: Daimler, Waymo Partner on Autonomous Test in Texas - TT

facts: Decostar Industries, Inc. is located at 1 Decoma Dr, Carrollton, GA 30117.
The company employs approximately 20+ people.
Decostar Industries, Inc. operates on a 24-hour schedule daily from Monday through Sunday.
Workers wear chemical resistant suits while operating loud machinery on the production floor.
Production processes involve conveyor belts that move metal down the line to be sprayed.
Temp hires face strict time policies and will be sent out for being 1 minute late from a break, losing the ability to get the job.
The orientation process is used to screen candidates to determine who is genuinely committed to working versus those seeking only a paycheck.

facts: German multinational liner shipping company Hapag-Lloyd will invest $18 million in its new North American headquarters.
The new North American headquarters will be located in Dunwoody, Georgia.
The project will create an additional 250 jobs in the metro Atlanta region.
Hapag-Lloyd has been moving people and cargo around the globe for over 150 years.
Logistics is considered an innate part of Georgia’s economic prowess, with the state’s economy connected to the movement of goods via sea, rail, air, and road.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson expressed excitement about bringing one of the most important companies in the logistics industry to Georgia.
Hapag-Lloyd is based in Hamburg, Germany, and operates a fleet of over 250 modern container ships with a total transport capacity of 1.8 million TEUs.
Hapag-Lloyd currently employs more than 500 Georgians across the state.
Once the North American headquarters project is complete, Hapag-Lloyd will employ more than 750 people in Georgia.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson met with company leadership in Hamburg during a November economic development mission to Germany.
Uffe Ostergaard, President of Hapag-Lloyd’s North American region, stated the new headquarters will bring the team together under one roof to better serve customers and expand career pathways.
The company will open its new 125,000-square-foot headquarters at Three Ravinia in Dunwoody by summer 2022.
Hapag-Lloyd will be hiring for careers in finance, human resources, IT services, and more.
Dunwoody Mayor Lynn Deutsch welcomed the company to Dunwoody and highlighted the longstanding partnership with the State of Georgia and DeKalb County for growing the Dunwoody Perimeter Market.
DeKalb County President Dorian DeBarr noted that DeKalb has a skilled workforce, is easily accessible to major highways and transportation hubs, and offers a business-friendly community.

GDEcD Director of Corporate Solutions and Cybersecurity in Global Commerce Kristi Brigman represented the Department on this project in partnership with GDEcD’s Europe office, Decide DeKalb, the Dunwoody Development Authority, and Georgia Power.
source_title: Multinational Liner Shipping Company Hapag-Lloyd Selects Georgia for North American Headquarters - Albany CEO

The 1,100-ton hydraulic press has a bed size of 84” by 120” with about 12” of cushion travel for large draws and SSDC capability to 1,000 tons.
Dekalb Tool & Die can accommodate high-volume and low-volume production programs.
The company has experience in multiple material types and grades, including mild steel, galvanized, pre-paint, high strength low alloy, stainless steel, aluminum, brass, and plastic.
The metal stamping process produces high volumes of identical metal components to exacting customer requirements across a wide range of industries.

facts: DENSO is investing nearly $1 billion in North America over the next four years.
The investment will result in more than 2,000 jobs across the region.

The investment will allow DENSO to better support its North American customers, as well as expand new business areas and localize products, many of which will help automakers meet upcoming fuel requirements.
More than $750 million will be invested in the United States alone.
More than 1,200 jobs will be created in the United States.
Terry Helgesen serves as senior vice president of industry relations at DENSO International America Inc.
DENSO announced plans to drastically localize products and critical tooling in the regions where it operates.
In the U.S., DENSO is looking at investing more than $750 million in Michigan, Tennessee, Iowa, California, and North Carolina.

The U.S. investment represents an increase in research and development, an expansion in existing production lines, the creation of new production lines, and the opening of a new assembly and warehouse facility to support heavy-duty customers.
DENSO is investing in making more of its own manufacturing equipment and dies within the states.
A building has been dedicated at DENSO Manufacturing Tennessee in Maryville, Tennessee, for proprietary machinery, serving as the regional headquarters for manufacturing machinery and dies.
DENSO plans to make all of its critical manufacturing equipment in North America in the future.
The company has sourced several million dollars of business to American machine shops, which will positively impact communities and create additional jobs.
Currently, DENSO employs more than 14,000 people at 28 consolidated companies and affiliates in North America.
Of these North American entities, 26 are manufacturing facilities located in the U.S., Canada, and Mexico.
In the U.S. alone, DENSO employs more than 10,000 people across California, Michigan, North Carolina, South Carolina, Tennessee, Kentucky, Georgia, Iowa, Ohio, and Arkansas.
