entity_name: LG Energy Solutions & Hyundai Battery Plant Joint Venture
canonical_name: LG Energy Solutions & Hyundai Motor Group Battery Cell Manufacturing Joint Venture
facts: LG Energy Solutions (LGES) and Hyundai teamed up to build a battery cell manufacturing plant in the United States.
Hyundai Motor Group and LGES signed a memorandum of understanding on May 26 in Seoul, Korea, for a battery cell manufacturing facility in Bryan County, Savannah, Georgia.
Each company holds a 50% stake in the joint venture for the U.S.-based battery plant.
The companies plan to invest over $4.3 billion in the battery facility.
The parties agreed to produce EV batteries so Hyundai could accelerate its electrification efforts in North America.
The battery plant would allow Hyundai electric vehicles (EV) to qualify for the Inflation Reduction Act’s (IRA) $7,500 tax credit.

Jaehoon Chang, President and CEO of Hyundai Motor Company, stated that Hyundai is focusing on electrification efforts to secure a leadership position in the global auto industry and will create a strong foundation to lead the global EV transition through establishing a new EV battery cell plant with LG Energy Solution.
Construction on the new battery facility is expected to start in the second half of 2023, with cell production slated to begin by the end of 2025.
Hyundai and LGES aim for an annual production capacity of 30 GWh at the battery facility in Georgia.
The 30 GWh annual capacity is enough to support the production of 300,000 EVs annually.
Hyundai Mobis will assemble battery packs using the cells from the plant in Georgia.
After assembly, the battery packs will go to U.S. manufacturing facilities producing Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis EV models.
Hyundai aims to become one of the top three global automakers by 2030.
The company is already preparing to achieve its goal by investing $18.1 billion in electric vehicle production.
The Asian automaker plans to increase its annual production run rate for EVs to 3.64 million globally by 2030.
Hyundai will launch 31 EV models from its brands, including Kia and Genesis, to reach its goal.
Most of Hyundai’s EV models will be available in all major markets.
source_title: LG Energy Solutions & Hyundai team up for battery plant in the U.S.

entity_name: Hyundai-LG EV Battery Plant Joint Venture
canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution Joint Venture for EV Battery Manufacturing in Georgia
facts: Korean firms Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution (LGES) have announced their collaboration to establish a state-of-the-art EV battery manufacturing plant in the United States.
The joint venture aims to take advantage of tax breaks offered by the administration for green initiatives.
The investment amount for the facility is $4.3 billion.
Both LGES and Hyundai Motor Group will each hold a 50 percent stake in the joint venture.
The facility will have a yearly production capacity of 30 GWh.
The plant's capacity is estimated to power 300,000 EVs in North America.

Jaehoon Chang, President and CEO of Hyundai Motor Company, stated the firms aim to create a strong foundation to lead the global EV transition through establishing the new EV battery cell plant with LG Energy Solution.
The battery manufacturing facility will be located in Bryan County, Savannah, Georgia.
The site is adjacent to Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, which was under construction at the time of the announcement.
To become eligible for tax rebates, manufacturers must fulfill new sourcing requirements concerning EV battery components and minerals.
Meeting IRA sourcing requirements enables EV buyers to avail tax credits of up to $7,500.
The regulations are part of President Joe Biden’s initiative to have 50% of new vehicle sales in the United States be EVs or plug-in hybrids by 2030.
The initiative is intended to lessen the country’s reliance on China for EV battery supply chains.
Construction of the new facility was planned to begin in the second half of 2023.
The joint venture scheduled production to start at the end of 2025.
This is the second JV concerning Hyundai and LG for battery manufacturing.
In 2021, both parties began building a facility in Indonesia.
Production at the Indonesian facility was anticipated to begin in the first half of 2024.
The battery cells produced from the Georgia plant will be assembled in Hyundai Mobis.
Assembled batteries would be shipped to the manufacturing facilities of Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis EV models.
The new facility will help create a stable supply of batteries in the region and allow the Group to respond fast to soaring EV demand in the U.S. market.
With this JV, LGES now has seven battery factories running or being built in the United States.
LGES is focusing the majority of its efforts on increasing output and hopes to hasten the U.S.’s transition to clean energy.
LGES and Hyundai Motor Group have long collaborated on the supply of EV batteries for vehicles including the Elantra Hybrid, Kona Electric, and IONIQ 6 dedicated EVs.
The Hyundai Elantra Hybrid debuted in 2009 as the first electric model produced by the Group.
The partnership is poised to help the brands further strengthen ties going forward with EV battery cooperation.
source_title: Hyundai and LG partner to build a $4.3 billion-EV battery plant in US

canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution Joint Venture Battery Plant in Georgia
facts: A major immigration raid occurred at Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution's (LGES) battery plant in Georgia.
The raid has exposed the labor and visa hurdles facing South Korean firms in the US.
The case raises fresh doubts over how sustainable Korean investment in...
source_title: Commentary: Hyundai-LGES Georgia plant raid threatens to unravel South Korea's US$350 billion EV bet

entity_name: Hyundai-LG Energy Solution Georgia EV Battery Plant
canonical_name: Hyundai-LG Energy Solution Georgia EV Battery Plant
facts: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution (LGES) are going to inject an additional $2 billion into their $4.3 billion US EV battery factory.
Carmaker Hyundai and lithium-ion battery maker LGES signed a memorandum of understanding at LGES’s headquarters in Seoul in May.
The two companies will 50/50 open their EV battery plant in Bryan County, Georgia, south of Savannah.
It will be adjacent to Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, currently under construction.
The annual production capacity of Hyundai and LG’s new battery plant will be 30 GWh.
The plant will be able to support the production of 300,000 EVs annually.
Hyundai Mobis will assemble battery packs using cells from the battery factory.
Hyundai Mobis will supply the assembled battery packs to Hyundai Motor Group’s US EV factory.
The US EV factory will produce Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis EV models.
The EV factory and the battery factory together represent more than $7.5 billion in investment in Georgia with this new $2 billion injection.
This boosts the number of new jobs to be created to 8,500 over the next eight years.
The Biden administration’s Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) offers a $7,500 tax credit for EVs that meet domestic manufacturing requirements, including batteries.
The IRA also offers incentives for companies to build manufacturing hubs for EVs, batteries, and clean energy.
Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis EV models currently don’t qualify if purchased because they don’t meet those domestic manufacturing requirements.
Hyundai and LGES decided to make this huge joint industrial investment in the US because their current EV models don’t qualify for the tax credit.
Battery production is expected to start at the end of 2025, at the earliest.
source_title: Hyundai and LG to spend an extra $2B on their US EV battery plant | Electrek

canonical_name: Hyundai-LG Energy Solution Georgia Battery Plant
facts: Work at Hyundai's U.S. facility in Georgia was paused after hundreds of workers were detained in a major raid by U.S. authorities.
The raid forced a pause in the construction of a car battery factory that is part of a major investment by the Korean automaker in the state.
The setback affects a project due to start operating at the end of the year.
The arrests threaten to roil the relationship between Washington and Seoul, a key ally and investor in the United States.
South Korea and the U.S. have been at odds over the details of a trade deal that includes $350 billion US of investments.
Just last month, South Korea pledged $150 billion in U.S. investments, including $26 billion US from Hyundai Motor, at a summit between the two countries.
South Korea's Foreign Ministry expressed concern about the impact on its nationals detained during the operation by U.S. immigration authorities.
A DHS agent stated that multiple U.S. agencies conducted a judicially authorized enforcement operation as they actively investigate unlawful employment practices.
Steven Schrank, special agent in charge of Homeland Security Investigations for Georgia, confirmed that arrests are being made.
The Atlanta office of the U.S. Justice Department agency ATF reported on X that up to 450 people had been detained.
A South Korean government official said detainees were being held at a United States Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) detention facility.
The Korea Economic Daily reported around 560 workers at the Hyundai Motor facility and LG Energy Solution had been detained, citing unidentified industry sources.
Some 300 of the detained individuals are South Korean nationals, according to local media reports.
A spokesperson for the Hyundai-GA battery company stated the company is cooperating fully and has paused construction work.
The facility is a joint venture between South Korean battery maker LGES and Hyundai Motor.
LG Energy Solution said the facility was due to start operations at the end of this year.
Hyundai Motor stated that its production of electric vehicles at the sprawling site was not affected by the raid.
LG Energy Solution issued a statement saying it is closely cooperating with the South Korean government and relevant authorities to ensure employee safety and secure swift release from detention.
LGES shares dropped 2.3 percent following the news.
In 2023, Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution announced a $4.3 billion venture to produce EV battery cells, with each company holding a 50 percent stake.
The plant will supply batteries for Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis EV models.
The battery factory is part of Hyundai's $12.6 billion investments in Georgia, which also includes the automaker's just-opened car factory.
The combined investment would be "the largest economic development project" in the state's history.
Social media video footage showed an HSI agent telling workers in yellow safety vests that they had a search warrant for the whole site and needed construction to cease immediately.
Under President Donald Trump, ICE has become the driving force of a sweeping crackdown on migrants, bolstered by record funding and new latitude to conduct raids.
Trump has stated he wants to deport "the worst of the worst" criminals, but ICE figures have shown a rise in non-criminals being picked up.
source_title: Work paused at Hyundai's U.S. site after hundreds of workers detained in raid | CBC News

canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution Georgia Battery Joint Venture
facts: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution (LGES) announced plans to increase their joint investment in a battery manufacturing facility located in Bryan County, Georgia by an additional $2 billion.
The joint venture aims to strengthen the production capabilities of electric vehicle (EV) batteries and create more job opportunities in the region.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp expressed his gratitude to Hyundai Motor Group and LGES for their investment in the state.
The announcement brings the companies’ total investment to $7.59 billion in the electric vehicle manufacturing facility and battery joint venture in the region.
The project will create 8,500 new jobs over the next eight years, according to the governor's press release.

José Muñoz, president and global chief operating officer of Hyundai Motor Company, stated that the incremental investment in Bryan County reflects their continued commitment to creating a more sustainable future powered by American workers.

David Kim, head of Advanced Automotive Battery Division at LGES, stated that the investment demonstrates their commitment to working with Hyundai Motor Group to lead electrification in the U.S. while strengthening the local economy by creating quality jobs.
Back in May, Hyundai Motor Group and LGES announced their joint venture plant in Georgia to supply EV batteries for the North American market.
At the time of the initial announcement, the companies stated they would invest $4.32 billion by 2028 to build a battery cell production plant to cover about 300,000 EVs per year.
source_title: Hyundai, LGES to spend $2 billion more on Georgia battery plant - 매일경제 영문뉴스 펄스(Pulse)

facts: JAC Products Inc. was founded in 1967 and is a leading designer and manufacturer of automotive roof racks and cargo management systems.
JAC Products supplies its products to Ford, Toyota, Honda, General Motors, Subaru, Renault, Hyundai, Chrysler, Kia Motors, Nissan, and Peugeot.
The company has offices around the world and manufacturing facilities in Michigan, Georgia, and Portugal.
JAC's engineering and manufacturing capabilities include plastic injection molding and steel rollform for base components.
JAC's Franklin, Georgia facility was originally set up as an aluminum extrusion plant in the mid-1960s and was taken over by JAC 20 years ago.
The extrusion lines at the Franklin, Georgia facility produce millions of pounds per year of 6XXX series aluminum.
JAC Products has about 1,500 employees in North America.
JAC's competitors include Germany-based WKW Automotive Group, DURA Automotive (with more than 21,000 employees in 15 countries), and China-based MINTH Group.
Engineering Manager Jeff Cavalier admits that JAC is relatively small compared to its competitors.
Cavalier states that JAC's competitive advantage lies in faster engineering turnaround and superior product quality due to its specific technologies.
JAC's manufacturing process involves stretching and forming metal to fit specific vehicle roof line profiles, rather than just CNC machining or cutting.
The tolerances for JAC's rail arrangements are defined by tenths and hundredths of a millimeter.
Cavalier emphasizes that manufacturing practices require repeatable processes, material management support, and timely delivery of quality parts to ensure customer satisfaction.
JAC's cargo management systems for the trucking industry include side rails, header bars, bed dividers, load bars, and lockable storage bins.
Cavalier describes JAC's innovations in racking systems for work trucks as top-notch and world-class.
JAC is broadening its expertise in manufacturing complex step rails.
JAC Products supports the automotive industry's shift from steel to aluminum substructures to reduce vehicle weight.
The industry's move toward robotic welding instead of bolting requires JAC to adjust the aluminum alloys it supplies.
JAC's product development group in Michigan stays closely involved with OEM engineers to support these alloy and manufacturing changes.
Cavalier credits JAC's Product Development team for forecasting market trends two, five, and ten years out to maintain OEM relationships.
JAC aims to be a key player in the OEM process by expanding its capabilities beyond roof racks.
JAC is integrating cameras and LED lighting systems directly into its roof racks.
Cavalier notes that JAC's roof racks can incorporate cameras for a 360-degree vehicle view and security systems.
JAC extensively tests its product coatings and sealings to withstand harsh car wash detergents and rough weather.
Cavalier notes that proximity to manufacturers in Michigan and the Southeast helps minimize freight costs and meet delivery needs.
The Franklin, Georgia facility's location helps JAC minimize freight costs for its customers.
JAC's future strategy focuses on diversifying its product line for OEMs and expanding global relationships beyond North America.
Cavalier identifies China, Central America, and South America as untapped markets with significant potential for JAC's growth.
JAC Products is currently on top in North America for roof racks and cargo management systems despite not being the largest globally.
JAC Products Inc. is headquartered in Saline, Michigan.

In 2013, Thumb Tool & Engineering joined forces with Mid-South Extrusion Die and Central Extrusion Die to position the Gemini Group as the second largest supplier of aluminum extrusion dies and tooling in North America.

facts: JAC Products is a Tier One automotive supplier located in Franklin, Georgia.
The facility puts 30-40 million holes into approximately 6 million pieces of extruded and formed aluminum each year.
The products made at this facility are used as roof rack rails on nearly every major automobile, mini-van, and truck brand.
JAC holds a majority share of the North American vehicle market for these roof rail components.
Mike Traylor serves as the tool room & die shop manager at JAC Products.
JAC operates hundreds of drilling stations in its Franklin factory, located on the banks of the Chattahoochee River in southeastern Georgia.
The company’s extrusion department processes millions of pounds of aluminum annually from 6061, 6063, and 6463 alloys.
Each dedicated production station produces an average of 100,000-200,000 left- and right-side rail sets per year for each specific automobile model.
Suhner Automation Division, based in Rome, Georgia, supplies flex shaft and direct motor-driven drilling units to JAC Products.
JAC has maintained a partnership with Suhner for nearly 20 years.
The flex shaft design removes drive motors from the cutting area, improving accessibility for operators and maintenance personnel.
Mike Traylor states that JAC achieves upwards of 60-70% greater output from its equipment since beginning to use Suhner drilling solutions.
Previous drilling equipment was prone to breakdowns and service problems, causing unacceptable delays in production during the industry's transition to just-in-time manufacturing.
Charles Stitcher is the regional marketing manager at Suhner who presented the company’s flex shaft drilling solutions to JAC.
The flex shaft design allows machine designers to position drilling mechanisms in various configurations and tighter proximities to meet required accuracies and secondary counterbore operations.
Mike Traylor has overseen nearly 500 machine builds at JAC and notes the adaptability of the flex shaft drill across a wide variety of applications.
For a Ford vehicle rail set, up to 11 drills and a cutoff operation are now performed simultaneously instead of sequentially by a single operator.
Output for one specific job increased from 1200 sets per day to 1200 sets per hour.
Various Suhner motor-mounted drills are utilized on dedicated machining operations, including a specially designed system for sawing.
Alberto Blanco is the Senior Launch Manager at JAC Products.
JAC must hold +/- 0.1-0.2mm tolerances on drilling and +/- 0.5mm on cutoff lengths to meet customer requirements.
Mike Traylor notes that Suhner rebuild kits containing o-rings and seals simplify maintenance for the virtually non-stop used drills.
Jeff Cavalier is the engineering & facilities manager at JAC Products.
The compact work area concept enabled by the equipment saves operator steps, representing a huge annual savings for the company without sacrificing safety.
SUHNER INDUSTRIAL PRODUCTS, CORP. is located at Hwy 411 S./Suhner Drive, P.O. Box 1234, Rome, GA 30162.
Lee Coleman works in the Automation Division at Suhner Industrial Products Corp.

links.context: Download link for text and pictures files

source_title: JAC Products Uses Flex Shaft Drills for Increased Roof Rail Production - Bernard and Company

facts: Nio outsources its vehicle production to JAC Motors.
Nio plans to double its annual production volume from JAC from 120,000 to 240,000 cars per year.
The production outsourcing contract between Nio and JAC was renewed for a term running from May 2021 through May 2024.
Under the agreement, Nio is responsible for developing and engineering the vehicles, quality management and assurance, and supply chain management.
JAC Motors handles parts assembly and operations management under the deal.
JAC receives payment from Nio for each car it manufactures.
JAC is entitled to use the regulatory credits generated from the production.
Nio covers the costs for the vehicles, manufacturing taxes, and the amortization and depreciation of the production machines.
Nio and JAC operate a joint venture named Jianglai Advanced Manufacturing Technology (Anhui) Co.
Nio holds a 49 percent ownership stake in the Jianglai Advanced Manufacturing Technology joint venture.
NeoPark is planned as a massive smart electric vehicle industry park that will include manufacturing and research & development activities.
When fully operational, NeoPark aims to deliver 1 million EVs and 100 GWh of capacity per year.
The target deadline for NeoPark reaching full operation is likely by 2024.
On May 6, Nio announced that Norway would be the first international market for its vehicles.
Nio vehicles will retain their battery pack swapping capability in the Norwegian market.
The continuation of the Nio-JAC partnership depends on vehicle demand in China and worldwide.
source_title: Nio Doubles Production Outsourcing From JAC: 240,000 Cars/Year

entity_name: Data Centers in Atlanta's South Suburbs
facts: Georgia is one of the states where companies are keen to build data centers, thanks to generous incentives and a strong fiber infrastructure, especially around Atlanta.
Many data centers are popping up in Atlanta suburbs, especially majority-Black ones on the south side.
Fayetteville, Georgia, located about 40 minutes south of downtown Atlanta, features large parcels of greenery and suburban homes suitable for development.
Diana Dietz has lived in Fayetteville since the '90s and now tracks data center growth in the area.
A construction site in Fayetteville has had miles of trees cleared for new facilities.
QTS is building a 600-acre data center campus in Fayetteville, Georgia.
Kecia Scott lives in Planters Ridge, a predominantly Black middle-class subdivision, and her house is now right across the street from the new data center.
Residents like Kecia Scott did not know about the data center project until after moving into their homes.
Residents worry about environmental impacts and the large amount of water and electricity needed to run data centers.
Georgia has at least a hundred data centers in operation, most located south of Atlanta.
Data centers are concentrated south of Atlanta because there is still more rural land available than in other parts of metro Atlanta.
Lynn McKee teaches real estate at Georgia State University and notes the land availability factor for data center placement.
Georgia Tech professor Ahmed Saeed states that Georgia is making it very attractive to build data centers here.
Lucrative tax incentives to the data center industry are supported by Georgia's Republican governor, Brian Kemp.
Governor Brian Kemp vetoed a bill last year that would have paused the tax exemptions for data centers.
Residents on the south side are starting to speak out about the impact of data centers in Black neighborhoods.
Some Republicans want more protections for communities, like state Senator Chuck Hufstetler.
State Senator Chuck Hufstetler stated: "You know, there are some people that want to give away the farm and others that don't want any growth. And I just want us to be smart about it."
State Senator Chuck Hufstetler's efforts to implement more community protections have stalled in the legislature.
Georgia Power reversed its efforts to close two coal-fired power plants, saying it now needs them to serve customers with large energy demands.
Diana Dietz noted regarding construction vehicles: "You can't go out without seeing several of those huge construction vehicles on the roads."
Kecia Scott stated about her neighborhood's transformation: "I have seen all of the what used to be cozy, quaint, wooded kind of area completely change."

Lynn McKee stated regarding data center placement: "People don't want these big data centers near their houses. But you go out and you go to South Atlanta or into rural Georgia, there's just land, land, land."

source_title: Tax incentives are drawing data centers to Atlanta's south suburbs, worrying residents : NPR

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced on Monday that Joon Georgia, Inc., an automotive parts manufacturer, will create 630 new jobs and invest $317 million in Bulloch County.
Plans for the company’s new manufacturing facility mark the first confirmed supplier for Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America in Bryan County.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that less than two weeks after the groundbreaking of Hyundai Motor Group’s innovative facility, the regional impact is already being seen.
Governor Brian Kemp noted that Joon Georgia will be the first of many companies drawn to Georgia to support the Metaplant, creating jobs and opportunity for generations of hardworking Georgians.
Joon Georgia is part of Ajin USA, which is a current supplier for multiple Hyundai Motor Group facilities.
The company produces a variety of automotive body parts and electronics for original equipment manufacturers.

Jung Ho Sea, CEO of Ajin USA, stated the company is excited to be one of the first Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America automotive suppliers to build an automotive parts manufacturing facility in Bulloch County and its first plant in Georgia.
Jung Ho Sea noted that Joon Georgia will create approximately 630 new jobs with competitive benefits in Bulloch County and the State of Georgia.

Jung Ho Sea expressed a commitment to be the best parts supplier for automotive and EV companies like Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, as well as the best employer and corporate citizen giving back to the community of Bulloch County and the City of Statesboro.
Joon Georgia’s new facility will be located at Bruce Yawn Commerce Park, previously known as the Southern Gateway Commerce Park, near Statesboro and in close proximity to the Port of Savannah.
With connections to the port, both Hyundai and Joon Georgia will be able to access global markets quickly and efficiently.
Operations at the Bruce Yawn Commerce Park are expected to begin in mid-2024.
The company will begin hiring for the new facility in mid-2023.
Billy Allen, Chair of the Development Authority of Bulloch County, stated the authority is honored and proud to welcome Joon Georgia to the Statesboro-Bulloch County community and region.
Billy Allen noted that the site in Bruce Yawn Commerce Park and proximity to the new Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America will serve the company well.
Billy Allen highlighted that education resources, including Georgia Southern University and Ogeechee Technical College, will provide critical workforce support to help Joon Georgia succeed.

Alyce Thornhill, Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy, represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project in partnership with the Development Authority of Bulloch County, Georgia Quick Start, Georgia Ports Authority, and Georgia EMC.
Workforce recruitment for the new facility will be supported by Quick Start, the top-ranked workforce training program in the nation.

By partnering with the Technical College System of Georgia, Quick Start helps assess workers, train new employees on unique processes, and develop customized job-specific training using the most current techniques and media.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Joon Georgia’s announcement is a landmark moment as Georgia drives its automotive industry into the future.
Pat Wilson noted that Hyundai Motor Group, its suppliers, and the Savannah area JDA are creating generational opportunities for communities in Georgia.
Pat Wilson emphasized that supporting the jobs of tomorrow is a team effort and regional collaboration ensures benefits are shared across county lines.
Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric mobility industry.
Since 2020, more than 30 EV-related projects have contributed over $13.3 billion in investment and announced more than 18,100 jobs in Georgia.
On October 25, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground in neighboring Bryan County on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility.
Non-affiliated suppliers connected to the Hyundai project are predicted to invest approximately $1 billion in other locations around the state.
source_title: First Hyundai Motor Group Supplier to Build Facility in Bulloch County - Athens CEO

facts: Kumho Tire U.S.A. announced the all-new Road Venture HT, an advanced all-season Highway-Terrain tire engineered for optimal performance across any road condition.
The Road Venture HT is purpose-built for light truck and SUV owners seeking a dependable everyday driving tire.
It offers enhanced wet and snow traction, extended tread-life mileage, and a smooth, quiet ride.
The Road Venture HT is EV-compatible.
Shawn Denlein serves as Kumho Tire U.S.A. president of sales and marketing.
The tire features an optimized structural design and a new generation polymer compound with High Dispersible Micro (HDM) Silica.
These materials contribute to superior wear resistance, providing a 10% improvement in tread life compared to its predecessor.
3D chamfers and integrated tie-bar noise reduction technology ensure a quiet and comfortable ride.
Reinforced polyester body plies strengthen sidewall stiffness and improve cornering stability.
The tire features a high-grip resin and four wide circumferential grooves for efficient water evacuation.
Wider lateral shoulder grooves, multi-angle sipes, and independent tread blocks with enhanced biting edges deliver superior traction and stability.
A zigzag tread design and waved 3D interlocking sipes deliver enhanced snow traction.
The tire earned Three-Peak Mountain Snowflake (3PMSF) certification for winter-rated capability.
It is engineered to handle the unique demands of EVs, including higher weight and increased torque.
Kumho Tire conducted independent testing at a third-party facility and benchmarked performance against leading brands in the category.
Testing showed the Road Venture HT outperformed competitors in ride comfort and wet handling, plus delivered superior results in dry and wet braking.
The tire will be available to dealers in May and consumers in June.
It comes in more than 40 sizes, ranging from 15-inch to 22-inch rim diameters.
It is backed by an industry-leading 75,000-mile limited treadwear warranty for P-metric sizes.
It includes a 50,000-mile limited treadwear warranty for LT-metric sizes.
A road hazard warranty and a 30-day satisfaction guarantee trial are included with the tire.
Kumho Tire is headquartered in Atlanta, Georgia.
Kumho Tire U.S.A. serves as the US sales, marketing, product development, and distribution arm of Kumho Tire Co. Inc.
Kumho Tire has more than 60 years of history of pioneering innovative approaches to developing tires.
source_title: Kumho Tire U.S.A. Announces Road Venture HT Highway-Terrain Tire - Traction News

facts: Kumho Tire in Macon will be expanding with a $21.8 million investment.
The expansion plans, announced Thursday, will add about 20,500 square feet to the facility.
A new Automated Production Unit (APU) will be brought in to help improve quality and efficiency.
The expansion means about 500,000 more tires can be produced annually.
The announcement was made at the 2021 Greater Macon Chamber of Commerce’s State of the Community address.
Mayor Lester Miller stated he is excited to see Kumho’s progress and growth, noting the company recognizes Macon as a great place to do business.
Mayor Lester Miller emphasized that economic development focuses on attracting new industries and supporting existing ones, calling it a great day for Macon-Bibb County.
The groundbreaking for the expansion is scheduled to happen in May.
Keith Lolley, Director of Human Resources of Kumho Tire Georgia, stated the company is thrilled about the expansion and called it great news for team members and the Macon-Bibb community.
source_title: Macon's Kumho Tire facility to expand with $21.8 million investment

facts: Kumho Tire USA Inc. officially opened its Macon plant on May 2, 2016, eight years after initially announcing plans for the facility.
The company invested $450 million in the new facility.
The plant has a production capacity of 4 million tires per year.
The grand opening ceremony was attended by Georgia Governor Nathan Deal and local leaders.
The facility is described as the largest industrial building to grace Macon since the 1970s.
Governor Nathan Deal stated he would express deep appreciation for Kumho’s continued investment in Georgia and praised the company's leadership vision that flourished over the last 10 years.
Gov. Deal highlighted the strong relationships between Kumho, the community, and the state as a model for the culture of collaboration they work to create.
Kumho Tire USA Inc. will supply tires to Kia Motors, Hyundai, and Chrysler for the original-equipment market (tires sold to auto makers for brand new vehicles).
The U.S. accounts for 22 percent of tires consumed globally.

Park Sam-gu, chairman of Kumho Asiana Group, stated that North America is home to one of the world’s largest auto and tire markets and that a production base there is crucial for expanding their presence to become a top tire manufacturer.
The project faced uncertainty after its initial 2008 announcement was derailed by the global recession of 2009.
Millions of dollars in state grants were used to prepare the site at Sofkee Industrial Park.
In December 2014, the Macon-Bibb County Industrial Development Authority approved a new bond issue of up to $475 million for the project, enabling it to move forward again.
The project was initially slated at $225 million, but delays allowed for new equipment that substantially increased the investment figure.
Automotive investment ignited by the Kia plant has made Korea one of Georgia’s largest source nations for foreign direct investment.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development maintains a trade and investment office in Seoul.
Governor Deal has made multiple visits to South Korea during his two terms and touted the “marriage of diverse and talented cultures” in Georgia and Korea at the event.
Kia Motors recently celebrated the completion of its 2 millionth vehicle in Georgia since opening in 2009.

Chris Carr, commissioner of the Georgia Department of Economic Development, stated that Kumho’s investment exemplifies how Georgia’s thriving automotive sector, including a robust network and equipped workforce, helps leading companies flourish.
The state-of-the-art plant sits on 130 acres with buildings occupying 861,000 square feet.
The facility is capable of producing one tire every 36 seconds.
source_title: Eight Years Later, Kumho Officially Opens Macon Plant  - Global Atlanta

facts: Automotive News published a special 100th anniversary edition chronicleing the evolution of the automotive industry over the last century and Georgia’s role in it.
The first hybrid 2027 Kia Tellurides will roll off the production line in West Point, Georgia.
Early roots (1909-1920s) saw White Star Automobile in Atlanta and Savannah Blowpipe (now Great Dane) launch Georgia’s automotive presence.

Genuine Parts Company was established in 1928 during Georgia's foundational automotive growth period.
Ford and General Motors invested in Georgia by 1947.
The former GM plant in Doraville was repurposed into Assembly Studios.
The Ford Hapeville facility transformed into the Porsche Experience Center Atlanta.
Kia Georgia, established in 2006, along with its suppliers, has created over 14,000 jobs.

Pirelli, Novelis, Honda Precision Parts, Kumho Tire USA, Toyo Tire, Cox Automotive, Porsche, Toyota Industries Corporation, and Mercedes-Benz have expanded their Georgia operations in the 21st century.

Recent investments in Georgia focus on hybrid and electric vehicles, DC-to-DC production, e-mobility innovation corridors including The Ray and Curiosity Lab, and the $12.6 billion Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
Misti Martin serves as Deputy Commissioner at the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD).
GDEcD connects automakers with communities, utilities, education partners, and industry leaders to accelerate long-term growth of integrated supply chains in a stable, business-friendly environment.
Georgia Quick Start and the Technical College System of Georgia provide customized workforce training for rapid, efficient scaling.
Facilities like Kia Georgia and Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America produce a mix of traditional electric and hybrid vehicles backed by a robust supplier network.
The Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) program ensures industrial sites are prepped and primed for major projects.
The Rural Site Development Initiative supports site readiness for industrial development in Georgia.
Hyundai’s $12.6 billion Metaplant America is a major project supported by Georgia's site readiness programs.
Automotive investments in Georgia spark job creation, charge educational partnerships, drive infrastructure growth and cross-cultural exchange, and shift local economies.

facts: Kyungshin America Corporation (KAC) is a global automotive parts supplier and the newest tenant in The Broe Group’s Savannah Gateway Industrial Hub.
The Savannah Gateway Industrial Hub (SGIH) is a 2,600-acre multimodal logistics park near Georgia’s Port of Savannah.
KAC is a division of the South Korean Kyungshin Corporation.
KAC’s new operation will import and distribute automotive components to Hyundai Motor Group’s new $7.5 billion electric vehicle plant in Savannah.
KAC will also supply the growing automotive manufacturing network throughout the southeastern United States.
Sean Fitzsimmons is Broe Real Estate Group Senior Vice President.
KAC’s new facility is strategically positioned with the Port of Savannah and the new Hyundai Metaplant, both within 15 miles.
The location leverages Effingham County’s strong workforce and education system.
KAC produces wire harnesses for automotive companies.
KAC is a certified Tier One supplier to Hyundai.
KAC’s new facility is 136,000 square feet.
KAC’s new facility is expected to open in February.
The move supports Kyungshin’s continued North American production expansion.
Brandt Herndon is Effingham County Industrial Development Authority CEO.
SGIH is a public-private partnership between the Effingham County Industrial Development Authority (ECIDA), Broe Real Estate Group, and OmniTRAX.
SGIH is located 12 miles from the Port of Savannah.
SGIH provides dual rail access to the Class I rail networks of CSX and Norfolk Southern.
source_title: Kyungshin America Picks Broe's Savannah Industrial Hub - Industry Today - Leader in Manufacturing & Industry News

facts: Lear Corporation secured a contract to supply seats for General Motors’ next-generation pickup trucks and SUVs.
Lear plans to open a highly automated “dark factory” near GM’s Orion Assembly plant.
The new seating operation will occupy 440,000 square feet in Auburn Hills.
The Auburn Hills facility is strategically positioned to support just-in-time delivery for Orion Assembly.
GM confirmed the Orion plant will begin building the next-generation Chevy Silverado 1500 and GMC Sierra 1500 in early 2027.
These trucks are currently built in Mexico and Canada, but GM is moving to build more USDM trucks in the U.S. to avoid tariff costs.
Production of the ICE-powered Cadillac Escalade is moving from Texas to Michigan.
The Cadillac Escalade move makes room for more Chevy Tahoe/Suburban and GMC Yukon/Yukon XL production at the Arlington Assembly plant.

Lear’s win represents a significant setback for competitor Magna International, which had previously announced a $100 million seating plant and 500 jobs to supply the now-canceled EV program at Orion.
The canceled Orion EV program left Magna with stranded capital, mirroring broader pain across the supply base as automakers recalibrate electrification timelines.
GM attributed over half of a recent $7 billion in EV-related charges to supplier contract cancellations and settlements.
Lear’s winning strategy relies on aggressive automation to make U.S.-based production cost-competitive.
Lear recently showcased a “lights out manufacturing” center in Rochester Hills, demonstrating fully automated production.
Lear executives position leveraging automation and digital strategy as essential for winning onshoring business.

Lear CFO Jason Cardew stated during the company’s October earnings call: “We see leveraging automation and our unique position with automation and our digital strategy as a way to earn higher returns on our seat business that we’re conquesting.”
The automated approach is expected to keep capital investment and job creation for the Auburn Hills facility lower than traditional operations.
Lear is expected to provide further details on the landmark GM contract during its fourth-quarter earnings conference call scheduled for February 4th.
source_title: GM Seat Supplier Lear To Open Dark Factory Near Orion Plant

facts: Lehigh Technologies is a leading specialty chemicals company that produces high-performance micronized rubber powders (MRP).
MRP is a sustainable raw material that reduces costs by up to 50%.
Lehigh's cryogenic turbo mill technology converts end-of-life rubber into MRP used in a wide range of markets.
Lehigh operates a state-of-the-art applications and development center where technical experts collaborate with customers to optimize products for each application.
Lehigh's Application & Development Center has comprehensive compounding, formulation and testing capabilities to help customers optimize products for individual needs.
Lehigh was recognized as a World Economic Forum Technology Pioneer in 2010.
Lehigh was recognized as a Bloomberg New Energy Pioneer in 2015.
Lehigh received the Sustainable Technology of the Year award by Tire Tech Expo in 2014.
Lehigh is headquartered in Tucker, Georgia.
Lehigh has a capacity of over 140MM pounds/year.
Lehigh's capacity is the largest known capacity in the industry.
Lehigh Technologies is privately held.

facts: Michelin North America, Inc. acquired Lehigh Technologies in an add-on acquisition deal dated September 1, 2017.
The seller for Lehigh Technologies was NGP Energy Technology Partners.

Michelin North America is a designer, manufacturer and seller of tires for every type of vehicle, including airplanes, automobiles, bicycles, earthmovers, farm equipment, heavy-duty trucks and motorcycles.
Michelin North America also publishes travel guides, hotel and restaurant guides, maps and road atlases.
Lehigh Technologies was founded in 1950 and operates in the Automotive sector.
NGP Energy Technology Partners is a private equity firm focused on the energy sector.
NGP Energy Technology Partners targets buyout and growth capital transactions in sub-sectors spanning alternative energy, energy efficiency, oilfield technology and power.
NGP Energy Technology Partners considers both minority and control investments and looks to invest $5 to $30 million per transaction.
NGP Energy Technology Partners is a subsidiary of NGP Energy Capital Management.
NGP Energy Technology Partners was formed in 2005 and is based in Washington DC with an additional office in New Orleans.

facts: Leaf Clean Energy Company (LON:LEAF) has decided to invest US$5 million in Tucker, Georgia-headquartered green materials company Lehigh Technologies.
Leaf led the investment round, joining other major investors in Lehigh.
Lehigh has the leading technology for addressing of end-of-life tires, a market worth billions of pounds per year.
Lehigh turns end-of-life tire materials and other post-industrial rubber into sustainable powders.
Lehigh’s micronized rubber powders are used in tires, consumer goods, building materials and other consumer and industrial applications.
Last year, the company’s powder was used to make more than 100 million tires.
Each pound of micronized rubber powder saves 0.8 gallons of oil and 10 kilowatt hours of electricity.
Clean Leaf added that the company’s products are becoming increasingly attractive to customers in view of rising rubber, polymer and energy prices.

Executive of Leaf Clean Energy Keogh stated: “Lehigh is a compelling investment because they address a major environmental problem with a product that has a great value proposition for their customers.”
Leaf's mission is to support companies that can achieve environmental sustainability with economically viable business models.
source_title: Leaf Clean Energy invests $5m in green materials group Lehigh Technologies | AIM:LEAF

facts: Lehigh Technologies is a green materials company that manufacturers micronized rubber powders made from end-of-life tires and other post-industrial rubber.
Lehigh's micronized rubber material is used in a wide range of consumer and industrial applications.
The company has an office located in Tucker, Georgia, USA.
Employees work from physical offices.

facts: Lehigh Technologies is a Michelin subsidiary that produces sustainable raw materials from end-of-life tires and post-industrial rubber.
Lehigh Technologies started production at its new Murillo el Fruto Micronized Rubber Power (MRP) Plant in Navarra, Spain.
The Murillo plant is the first facility for Lehigh Technologies outside of the United States.
The Murillo plant was constructed in partnership with Lehigh’s Spanish partner Hera Holding.
The Murillo plant incorporates Lehigh’s proprietary cryogenic turbo mill technology.
The Murillo plant has a production capacity of 10,000 metric tons of MRP per year.
The Murillo plant will produce Lehigh’s PolyDyne and MicroDyne range of MRP products.
Lehigh’s MRP products are currently used by customers in more than 18 countries worldwide.
Jason Stravinski serves as Lehigh’s VP of Operations.
Engineers from Lehigh U.S., Lehigh Spain, and Hera Holding collaborated with the Jex project team to complete the Murillo plant on time and on budget.
"I’m very proud of the international team that made this project successful," said Jason Stravinski, Lehigh’s VP of Operations.
Lehigh expects the Murillo plant to be a crucial component in meeting rising demand for green chemicals in Europe and beyond.
Construction of the Navarra plant was managed by Lehigh and executed by UK-based Jex Engineering.
The Navarra plant construction received support from the Dennis Group and Air Products.
The Navarra plant was commissioned at the end of June 2018.
The Navarra plant employs the exact same layout and equipment as Lehigh’s Tucker, Georgia plant in the U.S.
Using the same layout and equipment as the Tucker, Georgia plant ensures consistent quality and products specification across all locations.
"Demand for Lehigh’s products is increasing rapidly and this new plant comes at the perfect time to support the growth," said Lluís Molina, General Manager Lehigh Spain.
The Navarra plant will supply the tire, bitumen, coatings, construction, and polyurethane industries in Europe and the Middle East with Lehigh’s MRP products.
"The Murillo plant is the next step in Lehigh’s mission to commercialize MRP for customers in a wide range of performance driven markets."
source_title: Lehigh Technologies opens new Micronize Rubber Power plant in Spain

facts: German auto supplier Linde + Wiemann has concluded its partnership with Türkiye’s Opsan.

The Turkish Competition Authority approved the transfer of Linde + Wiemann’s shares in Linde Opsan Otomotiv to the Ersu family on Thursday.
The Ersu family’s KEB Metal Investment now holds full control of the company.
Opsan is one of Türkiye’s leading automotive suppliers, instrumental in producing sheet metal and welded assembly parts for the sector.
Opsan was established in 1963 in Istanbul.
Opsan initially focused on manufacturing pressure vessels and sheet metal products.
Opsan transitioned to the automotive sector in 1976, producing chrome-plated bumpers for Oyak-Renault.
In 2010, Linde + Wiemann partnered with the Ersu family to form Linde Opsan.
The joint venture launched mass production in 2012.
Linde Opsan supplies components for projects like the Ford Tourneo and Ford Transit.
Linde Opsan has facilities spanning 25,000 square meters.
Linde Opsan has a workforce of 475 employees.
Linde Opsan produces 130 million spot welds and 55 million fastener welds annually.
Linde Opsan has collaborated with prominent brands including Türkiye’s domestic electric vehicle (EV) Togg, Ford Otosan, TOFAS, Stellantis, and Magna.
Linde + Wiemann was founded in 1939 in Dillenburg, Germany.
Linde + Wiemann operates production facilities in Spain, the Czech Republic, Hungary, China, South Africa, and Türkiye.
Despite ending its partnership with Opsan, Linde + Wiemann continues to maintain a strong international presence.

From Greenville, the plant is accessible by taking exit 177 on I-85 North, turning left at the end of the exit, and turning left at the 4-way stop sign, with the plant visible on the left hand side.
source_title: Download area - Linde + Wiemann

canonical_name: Understanding Incentives in the Automotive Industry
facts: Economic development incentives play an integral role in influencing corporate location decisions for new automotive jobs and investment.
Incentives can take the form of cash grants; tax credits, exemptions or abatements; free or discounted land and buildings; and employment training assistance.
These incentives are able to materially offset large upfront investments required by production facilities, mitigate risks, and/or reduce projected operating costs.
State legislatures set aside large sums called “deal closing funds” or “governor’s funds” to attract new jobs and investment.
Stipulations such as pay-for-performance measures are commonplace for cash grant programs, requiring job creation and investment milestones to be achieved before payments are received.
Cash grant awards can range from several hundred thousand dollars to $10 million (or more) depending on the state and project.
The Texas Enterprise Fund (TEF) provides typical awards ranging from $5,000 to $10,000 per job based on jobs created, average annual wage, capital investment, and fund availability.
TEF requires a minimum of 25 new jobs for rural areas and at least 75 new jobs for urban areas.

In 2016, the TEF program attracted CGT US Limited to New Braunfels, Texas, awarding a $2 million grant after the company committed to reshoring manufacturing from China, creating 275 jobs and investing $80 million.
Toyota received a $40 million TEF award for its North American Headquarters relocation to Plano in 2014.
The Michigan Business Development Program (MBDP) is a discretionary cash grant program offering pay-per-performance awards approaching $10,000 per new job created.
MBDP requires at least 50 new jobs in developed counties and at least 25 new jobs in rural counties.
In 2016, Magna Seating Inc. received a $740,000 MBDP grant for its Highland Park manufacturing facility expansion after committing to creating 148 new jobs and investing $6 million.
While most states offer statutory sales and use tax exemptions on machinery and equipment, only a limited number offer discretionary exemptions or refunds on building or construction materials.
Forgiveness of sales tax on construction materials can save projects millions in construction costs during early cash-flow-sensitive phases.
Kentucky Enterprise Initiative Act (KEIA) offers a discretionary refund of 6% sales and use taxes paid on building and construction materials.
KEIA requires a minimum investment of $500,000 in building and construction expenditures and provides the incentive for up to seven years.

In 2016, MACA Plastics received a $33,000 KEIA award to renovate a 122,600 square foot facility in Maysville, KY, as part of a $7.9 million investment while committing to creating 156 new jobs supplying automotive components.
Real and personal property tax abatements typically extend for up to ten years at varying percentages and are approved at the local government level.

Some states require a public ownership model utilizing bond issuances where a government body leases property back to the company, with lease payments equivalent to a reduced property tax levy (FILOT/PILOT).

Dana Light Axle Products used Missouri’s “Chapter 100” program in 2016 to abate half of its personal property taxes for seven years, saving $1.4 million while committing to create 135 new jobs and invest $39 million at its Columbia, MO plant.
Illinois, Michigan, and Ohio do not levy property taxes on manufacturing personal property.

Some states offer discretionary tax incentives based on incremental employee payroll, allowing retention or refund of withholding taxes or offering refundable/non-refundable corporate income tax credits.
The Missouri Works Program offers a payroll benefit of up to 7% of new employee payroll for up to six years, combining withholding tax retention with transferable tax credits.

In 2014, Martinrea received a $3.2 million award from the Missouri Works Program for a new 275,000 square foot facility in Riverside, committing to invest $51.6 million and supply the nearby GM OEM plant.

Grants for public infrastructure improvements are widely used throughout the Midwest and Southeast, with state governments transferring funds to local governments for water/sewer, road, rail, and site preparation improvements.

The Mississippi Development Infrastructure Grant Program allows counties or municipalities to apply on behalf of companies for state funding based on job creation, investment, and infrastructure needs.

Tennessee’s FastTrack Infrastructure Development Program provided a $5 million grant to DENSO Manufacturing Inc. in 2015 for land acquisition and site prep as part of a $400 million expansion creating 500 new jobs in Maryville, TN.
Georgia and South Carolina provide non-refundable job creation tax credits that vary in amount by county.

In 2016, LINDE + WIEMANN received an estimated $3 million in Georgia Jobs Tax Credit for a new 63,500 square foot manufacturing facility and office in Hart County, investing $35 million and committing to creating 200 new jobs.
Indiana’s Hoosier Business Investment Tax Credit offers a non-refundable credit equal to ten percent of qualifying capital investment with a carry forward for up to nine years.
Non-refundable tax credits only have value if the company has sufficient tax liability against which to use them.
Many electric providers offer “economic development riders” establishing lower rates per kWh for qualified projects, typically ranging from two to five years.
Utility incentives often include minimum kW demand requirements (ranging from 10-25 kW to 1,000 kW, with most requiring 200 kW or more) and minimum job creation/investment targets.
Operations with high load factors may be eligible for additional savings on utility rates.
The Tennessee Valley Authority (TVA) can offer performance grants and low-interest loans for qualifying projects.
Additional utility incentives include energy efficiency programs, renewable energy programs, and renewable electric commodity options.
Georgia’s Quick Start program provides extremely flexible and customized workforce training to prospective workers, mitigating talent risk for manufacturing start-ups.
South Carolina’s readySC program similarly provides customized training to mitigate talent risk in the automotive industry.
Special legislation can be used to create customized incentive offers outside statutory menus for projects with significant job creation, capital investment, or emerging technology.

In 2014, Tesla announced a gigafactory near Sparks, Nevada, creating 6,500 jobs and investing $10 billion, secured by an estimated $1.3 billion incentive package requiring special state legislation including twenty-year sales tax exemptions, ten-year property/corporate tax reductions, and direct-to-consumer sales authorization.

Mississippi used a special legislative deal valuing $596 million to attract Continental Tire’s commercial vehicle tire plant in Clinton, securing 2,500 jobs and $1.45 billion in investment through grants, twenty-five-year reduced corporate income tax, property abatements, and payroll retention.
Realizable incentive value depends on flexibility, usability, scalability, and compatibility with other programs rather than just the total dollar amount awarded.
Legal prohibitions often exist against combining certain incentive programs for the same project.
Companies must maintain “but for” status until all incentive approvals are achieved, as moving forward too quickly risks ineligibility.
Timing of real estate acquisitions, equipment purchase orders, hiring, and public announcements is critical to maintaining eligibility for most incentive programs.

First article in the location economics series

facts: Magna International has over 60 years of experience manufacturing auto parts.
The company’s recent focus is on electric mobility solutions and AI.
Magna is a mobility manufacturer specializing in automotive components.
Magna International is headquartered in Aurora, Ontario, north of Toronto.
It is the largest automotive part supplier in North America.
It is the third-largest automotive part supplier in the world.
As a contract manufacturer for other OEMs, Magna has designed and produced countless components for nearly every major automaker on the planet.
They manufacture complete vehicles for other OEMs that currently includes some electric vehicles in low volume.
This includes New Entrant Fisker, for which Magna recently announced it will soon be producing an EV.
The company considers itself “a 64-year-old startup.”
Magna International applies its experience with big innovations to a startup mentality.
It has remained on the forefront of new technologies, thanks to its research and development departments.
In the electrification of vehicles especially, Magna is developing unique and scalable solutions that target issues current OEMs face in terms of efficiency and cost.
Magna International’s Executive Vice President of R&D is Sherif Marakby.
Sherif Marakby stated that Magna focuses on automotive and transportation trends to identify growth areas like electrification and driver assistance.
Marakby noted that no one knows exactly what the volumes will be or how big the market is going to be by segment.
Magna has taken a modular approach to its electric drivetrains to keep components adaptable to suit all customers.
This modular approach helps keep costs down, no matter whether the volume is for 5,000 vehicles or 50,000.
The modular approach allows Magna to work with OEM customers to provide the most suitable configuration of electric drive(s) that best fit the vehicle.
Whether it’s payload, towing capacity, or range, Magna’s components can be added or subtracted purposefully.
Magna recently announced its eBeam technology.
eBeam is an electric axle drive system that can be dropped into an existing vehicle.
Due to this design, eBeam can replace a traditional transmission and rear axle in a ladder frame chassis quite easily.
The existing brakes, suspension, and chassis itself can stay as is when using eBeam.
Ladder frame chassis is prevalent in most pickup trucks.
Magna International developed the eBeam with the goal of electrifying one of the toughest vehicles, the pickup truck.
Sherif Marakby explained that the pickup truck market is huge in the US but focused on attributes like towing and capabilities.
He argued that a pickup truck is probably the last thing you want to electrify because of those capabilities and battery electrics can get very expensive for a heavier vehicle.
By having a modular approach, having the capabilities that this drop-in replacement can provide, is important to the pickup truck customer.
Boat-towing, horse-hauling pickup truck owners remain some of the toughest customers to sway toward electric.
If automakers can deliver the same torque, payload, and towing capacity, the argument gets a little easier.
The eBeam is highly modular and can work in a lightweight truck, or a heavy-duty commercial vehicle, large battery, small battery, or even a hybrid.
Magna calls this its “building block approach,” allowing OEMs to configure multiple drivetrain pieces together depending on the purpose of the truck.
Since Magna International is a parts provider to nearly all major automakers, the eBeam can one day be implemented on different-sized chassis with different size batteries.
According to Marakby, the eBeam is still a few years away.
So far the interest level from OEMs for the eBeam has been “tremendous.”
He elaborated that it's a strong indication in the space that they’re a few years out, maybe less.

He noted it's a promising technology in a tough segment and while consumer change might be slow, providing a component that lends itself to an electric drive may start low volume but he’s convinced it’s going to take off.
As for electric mobility solutions, Magna recently announced a joint venture agreement with LG to expand its powertrain electrification.
Next, the biggest focus will be getting the highest efficiency and power density out of its products at the lowest cost.
In addition to electric powertrains, Magna is also quite fluent in autonomous driving and AI.
The company is one of the few in the world to bundle radar, LiDAR, cameras, and domain controllers.
It continues to steadily expand those capabilities.
Magna-made ADAS products can now be found on more than 250 different vehicle models.
AI is already part of their business in many ways, from employing it in manufacturing facilities for planned maintenance, to incorporating it in vehicle technologies.

Their connected eDrive systems leverage AI to predict and improve driving dynamics and efficiency, enabling the vehicle to predict changes in terrain and traffic situations and better manage battery usage for extended range.
They also use AI as they continue to grow their ADAS L1/2+ business.
In developing driver assistance and AI technologies, Magna International is providing OEMs all components of a vehicle, inside and out.
The company is using its vast knowledge of manufacturing and research to help other automakers electrify their future vehicles more quickly and easily.
source_title: Electric vehicle spotlight (EVS): Magna International | Electrek

facts: Magna is a 66-year-old Canadian firm and one of the largest automotive parts suppliers in the world.
The company builds everything from seats and mirrors to advanced driver-assistance systems for major car companies.
Magna supplies components to at least 59 global automakers, including BYD, Tesla, Hyundai, Ford, Volkswagen, and Xiaopeng.
The company has hand-built entire vehicles, including the Mercedes-Benz G-Wagen.
Magna operates 330 manufacturing and assembly plants spanning 28 countries.
The firm reports roughly $42 billion in annual sales.
Magna is currently embedding artificial intelligence across its global manufacturing footprint.

Sharath Reddy, the company's SVP of R&D, stated that AI is already embedded across multiple layers of Magna's supply chain and manufacturing operations, adding that "We don't view it as a stand-alone technology."
Magna's AI investments focus on five areas: product quality, equipment maintenance, factory safety, energy reduction, and output speed.
For customers, the company deploys an AI-powered vision inspection system that uses high-resolution scanners and machine learning to detect parts defects and irregularities in real time.
Sharath Reddy noted that the clearest payoff from AI comes from applications "closest to the physical operation" rather than through sweeping, end-to-end automation.
Magna uses AI systems that monitor vibration, temperature, and pressure to predict equipment failures before they happen, helping plants avoid costly downtime.
The company is deploying autonomous mobile robots to move heavy materials between workstations.
Magna uses machine learning to track energy use, water consumption, and industrial waste across facilities, flagging anomalies and identifying ways to reduce costs.
The company is working toward a "unified factory" where data, software, and automation systems are connected across factory-wide operations.
Reddy explained that the unified system's value shows up across scheduling, material flow, and decision-making rather than in a single metric, stating, "The value is real, but it's more distributed."
Magna uses AI to monitor global supply chain disruptions caused by factors like tariffs, trade tensions, material supply shortages, and uneven global EV demand.
Reddy stated that AI does not replace the fundamentals of supply chain management but instead acts as an "amplifier" for potential threats.
Regarding near-term impacts, Reddy said AI will provide better visibility and faster decision-making through earlier signals, stronger scenario modeling, and more coordinated response.
Magna's factories are becoming more software-defined step by step, with intelligence layered into the parts of the system where it can reliably deliver results.
source_title: A $42B Auto Giant You've Never Heard of Is Building AI Into Factories - Business Insider

facts: Magna International, Inc. operates a facility in Carrollton, Georgia.

The company's Magna Exteriors portfolio includes access systems such as liftgates, exterior trim, modular systems, front-end modules including fascia, active aerodynamic systems and other lightweight structural components for automotive, commercial truck and other industrial markets.
Magna provides materials development and design through manufacturing and assembly to help automakers create state-of-the-art vehicles globally.
The Carrollton facility posted a job opening for a WCM/Lean Manager, which has since expired.
The WCM/Lean Manager role coordinates and encourages all WCM activities and continuous improvement workshops.
The role manages Six Sigma and Lean projects to support Decostar's improvement initiatives and strategic goals.

The position provides strategic direction, leadership, support and decision making to ensure successful application of continuous improvement concepts, including Lean Enterprise, Six Sigma, Layouts and Material flow.
The role assists Decostar Managers by soliciting and reviewing functional area continuous improvement plans and activities to maintain and improve overall plant continuous improvement results.
The position facilitates management updates and communication on all WCM and activities.
The role supports manufacturing engineering resources.
The position manages Standardized Work Process and Kaizen events.
The role leads and participates in customer lean and MAFACT assessments.
The position ensures compliance with all safety, quality, environmental and BOS policies and procedures.
The role supports cost reduction and innovation idea programs.
Applicants require five years automotive experience; two years manufacturing experience; one year continuous work experience.

Candidates must have working knowledge of manufacturing processes and plant operations, project management experience involving responsibility for planning timing and milestones and fiscal control, and supervisory experience or position in which leadership skills have been demonstrated.
Applicants need experience and demonstrated performance in implementing lean manufacturing and Six Sigma improvements.
Candidates require excellent problem solving, computer, and math / statistics skills.
Applicants must have the ability to manage multiple priorities with moderate stress tolerance.
Candidates need the ability to translate customer requirements to Decostar's operational environment.
Applicants require the ability to conduct optimization analyses.
Candidates must possess excellent verbal and written communication skills.
A BS in Engineering or Engineering Discipline is required; a Certified Six Sigma Green Belt or higher is preferred.
The work environment consists of office and manufacturing settings using normal PPE and safety precautions, with travel using public modes of transportation.

Magna operates as an equal opportunity employer that hires on the basis of experience and qualifications regardless of color, ancestry, religion, gender, origin, sexual orientation, age, citizenship, marital status, disability or gender identity.
source_title: WCM/Lean Manager at Magna International, Inc. in Carrollton, Georgia | Job Openings for DisABLED Veterans

facts: Magna International Inc. continues to work closely with its customers to mitigate the impact of tariffs.
CEO Swamy Kotagiri told analysts during the second-quarter earnings call that the company has settled almost all of its 2025 net tariff exposure with major automakers.
The company will continue to pass on tariff costs to its customers.
Magna lowered its estimated annualized tariff-related cost projections for the year to $200 million, from a previous estimate of $250 million.
The company is continuing to increase its compliance with the Canada-U.S.-Mexico Agreement.
General Motors Inc. reported a 35 percent profit decline in its second quarter after taking a US$1.1 billion hit from tariffs.
General Motors expects its net tariff costs to be higher in the next quarter.
Magna's net income for the three months ending June 30 was US$379 million, or $1.35 per diluted share, up from US$313 million or $1.09 per share in the same quarter last year.
Adjusted diluted earnings worked out to $1.44 per share, up from $1.35 per share last year, beating the mean analyst estimate of $1.14 per share.
Magna raised its annual outlook despite lower vehicle production, crediting cost-saving efforts over the past months.
The revised outlook shows a dip in overall North American vehicle production but increased output in China.
Magna boosted its sales outlook by US$400 million to upwards of US$42 billion.
Magna reported quarterly sales totalled US$10.63 billion, down from US$10.96 billion in the same quarter last year.
Light vehicle production was down six percent in North America and two percent in Europe.
The company ended production of some programs.
source_title: Magna International raises outlook on back of cost-cutting measures - The Globe and Mail

facts: Magna International has announced the sale of its lighting and rooftop systems businesses.
The company disclosed this decision on Thursday, April 9, 2026, as part of a strategic move to streamline its operations and focus on other core areas.
The transaction involves divesting two segments that have been integral to Magna’s portfolio.
The lighting business specializes in advanced automotive lighting solutions.
The rooftop systems division focuses on manufacturing sunroofs and related components for vehicles.
Magna did not provide specific details about the buyer or financial terms of the deal at the time of the announcement.
This development marks a significant shift for Magna as it continues to evaluate its business structure and prioritize growth opportunities in other sectors.
Further updates regarding this transaction are expected in due course.
source_title: Magna International to Sell Lighting and Rooftop Systems Businesses in Strategic Shift - GeneOnline News

The facility is located in Woodstock, AL.
The facility covers 151,313 square feet.

The new plastic injection molding facility produces interior parts for Mercedes-Benz’s next generation SUVs.
The plant serves as a flagship U.S. operation for the company.
The plant is located just six miles from the Mercedes-Benz manufacturing campus in Vance, Alabama.
The plant employs more than 200 people.
