canonical_name: Giyani Metals High-Purity Manganese Sulphate Monohydrate (HPMSM) Production Facility
facts: South Africa invested R29.9 million in high-purity manganese sulphate monohydrate (HPMSM) production through the Industrial Development Corporation (IDC).
Funding supports Giyani Metals' demonstration facility in Johannesburg, which has successfully validated reagent consumption optimisation and crystalliser operation protocols.
Validation achievements enable progression toward definitive feasibility study completion by Q2 2026.
The total expanded IDC loan facility is R329.9 million, representing an 11x increase from initial funding levels.
Commercial application targets the K.Hill project in Botswana.
Battery-grade HPMSM typically demands minimum purity levels of 99.7-99.95% with iron contamination limits below 100 ppm.
Raw manganese ore trades at approximately $3.50-4.50 per tonne, while high-purity manganese sulphate monohydrate commands $2,500-3,500 per tonne.
Combined cobalt and nickel contamination must be below 50 ppm, with optimised particle size distribution of 5-15 micrometers.
Controlled crystalline structure is required for electrochemical stability, alongside consistent chemical composition across production batches.
The Johannesburg demonstration plant validated reagent consumption efficiency, minimising chemical inputs per unit of finished HPMSM whilst reducing production costs and environmental footprint.
Crystalliser operation parameters require precise temperature, pH, and residence time control for target specifications.
South Africa's government implemented a 150% tax deduction on qualifying EV production capital investments between 2026-2036.
Additional policy instruments include Automotive Production and Development Programme (APDP) amendments and Production Rebate Certificates for customs duty relief.
The National Critical Minerals Strategy is being developed with World Bank partnership, targeting employment creation for approximately 1.6 million workers.
Global lithium-ion battery production capacity reached approximately 2,000 GWh in 2023, with projections indicating expansion to 4,500+ GWh by 2030.
Global EV sales reached 13.6 million units in 2023 (14% of total vehicle sales), with projected sales of 35 million units annually by 2030.
Manganese demand from the battery sector is 200,000 tonnes annually in 2023, rising to 400,000+ tonnes by 2030.
Battery cathode chemistry evolution includes LiMn₂O₄ for high-temperature stability, NMC for higher energy density, and LMFP combining manganese with iron for cost advantages.
Iron contamination above 200 ppm reduces battery cycle life by 10-15%, while nickel and cobalt contamination creates preferential ionic migration pathways.
Calcium and magnesium contamination increases electrolyte consumption during cycling, and particle size inconsistency affects cathode powder manufacturing efficiency.
South Africa holds 4 billion tonnes of measured and indicated manganese ore reserves, representing 80% of global identified manganese ore resources.
Current South African manganese ore production reaches 6.2 million tonnes annually, representing 30% of global production.
High-grade ore availability typically contains 40-50% manganese content, reducing transportation costs and processing requirements.
Major automotive OEMs including Volkswagen, BMW, and Tesla have established supply chain sustainability targets favouring low-carbon material sources.
Direct investment channels include Giyani Metals equity exposure (TSX: EMM) and IDC co-investment opportunities in related projects.

South Africa's initial $1.9 million investment is modest compared to Indonesia's $15+ billion commitment to nickel processing, Chile's $2.6 billion for lithium extraction, and Australia's $1.2 billion for critical minerals processing.
Technical risks include process scaling challenges, maintaining purity standards at commercial volumes, and equipment reliability for specialised chemical processing operations.
Skilled workforce development presents ongoing challenges requiring technical expertise for sophisticated processing operations and quality consistency across batch-to-batch variation control.

Critical success factors include reliable electricity supply, transportation infrastructure, port capacity for international shipments, and regulatory compliance with international battery material standards.
Electricity supply reliability remains a concern requiring attention through renewable energy development or alternative supply arrangements.
Projected timeline includes definitive feasibility study completion in Q2 2026, commercial-scale facility construction in 2027-2028, and full commercial operations by 2029-2030.
According to CNBC Africa's analysis, South Africa's battery manufacturing opportunity extends beyond raw materials to encompass broader manufacturing capabilities.
Forbes Africa reports that whilst Africa's EV revolution shows promise, several challenges continue holding back broader continental adoption.
source_title: South Africa Invests in EV Battery Supply Chain Hub

entity_name: Georgia Training Center for Industrial Operations
canonical_name: Georgia Training Center for Industrial Operations
facts: In November 2024, Ogeechee Technical College President Lori Durden said the Georgia Training Center for Industrial Operations would not be just another addition to the college’s campus when it opens in the fall of 2026.
The facility's groundbreaking ceremony took place at the corner of A.J. Riggs Road and Highway 301 South.

Ogeechee Technical College President Lori Durden described the center as a gateway to advancement, a catalyst for community growth, a crucial role in preparing a skilled workforce for advanced industries, and a hub for career development across the region and state.

Last week, Ogeechee Technical College President Lori Durden was joined by Technical College System of Georgia Commissioner Greg Dozier, OTC faculty and staff, the college’s Foundation Board and members of the community in signing one of the last beams that will be placed in the Center.
OTC Vice President for Economic Development Jan Moore stated that the beam signing marks a key milestone in the completion of the new Georgia Training Center for Industrial Operations.
The facility is unique within the Technical College System of Georgia and will deliver state-of-the-art training in industrial systems and advanced automation, including robotics.
A painted white beam signed by attendees will be installed in the ceiling of the Training Center but remain visible from inside the building.
The 37,000-square-foot Training Center will provide training to employees of manufacturers within the region, as well as companies that use automation in their supply and warehousing facilities.
Once operational, the college’s noncredit training capacity will increase to 460,000 hours annually, tripling the college’s current output.

Most of the demand now and in the future for robotics training stems from when Hyundai broke ground on the 2,541-acre Bryan County Metaplant in October 2022 to manufacture electric and hybrid vehicles.
The Hyundai Bryan County Metaplant complex is projected to employ, eventually, 8,500 people on-site.

In 2024, Lori Durden stated that the vision for the Training Center was inspired by the sheer amount of demand for training in industrial systems and robotics to equip the region's workforce with necessary skills.
More than a dozen manufacturing plants under construction or in operation in southeast Georgia are directly related to the Metaplant, including three in Bulloch County.
Most of these related manufacturing plants use advanced forms of robotics in their manufacturing process.
Ajin USA, a supplier of metal auto body parts to Hyundai, opened its $317 million plant in July 2024 in the Bruce Yawn Commerce Park next to I-16.
Two other suppliers to the Metaplant opened in Bulloch County in 2025.
Hanon Systems is a maker of "automotive thermal and energy management" equipment, which includes air-conditioning components.
Ecoplastic America Corporation manufactures injection-molded plastic automotive body parts for Hyundai Motor Group vehicles.
source_title: Beam signing marks milestone for OTC robotics training center - Statesboro Herald

facts: SK On is investing 1.5 trillion won ($1.1 billion) to build a third battery factory in Seosan, South Chungcheong.
This investment represents the largest domestic investment ever for the Korean battery maker.

SK On signed a memorandum of understanding with the South Chungcheong Provincial Office and the Seosan city government to secure investment to construct a factory at a 44,125-square-meter (10.9-acre) site near its No. 1 and 2 facilities.
The latest investment will increase the company's production capacity in Seosan fourfold.
The third factory will be constructed by 2025 and will have a capacity of 14 gigawatt-hours (GWh) by the end of 2028.
This expansion will boost SK On's domestic production capacity to 20 GWh, enough to supply around 280,000 electric vehicles.

SK On CEO Jee Dong-seob stated that the latest investment is meaningful in strengthening the country's overall battery production capability and that SK On will endeavor to become a leader in the EV industry by contributing to a regional economy based on aggressive investments.
The third factory in Seosan will be a smart factory, which will raise production speed by 30 percent and increase accuracy.
Seosan is where SK On first started its battery production back in 2012.
The company's No. 1 plant initially had a 0.2 GWh capacity, which was expanded to the current 5 GWh through a series of expansions.
SK On's investment comes as Hyundai Motor prepares to break ground on its first domestic EV factory in Ulsan in September.
SK On is a major battery supplier to Hyundai Motor and is currently the fifth-largest battery maker globally with 89 GWh of global production capacity.
SK On is building four more plants in the United States and Europe, which will boost its capacity to 220 GWh by the end of 2025.
SK On aims to become the third-biggest market player by 2025.
source_title: SK On to invest $1.1B, quadruple domestic battery capacity

facts: LG Energy Solution Ltd. is set to secure $1.36 billion to expand US battery output capacity.
The company is joining the fundraising race among South Korean battery makers to increase overseas production bases.
LG Energy’s US unit decided on a rights offering of 788.1 billion won ($681 million).
Parent company LG Chem Ltd. announced the rights offering in a filing to a South Korean financial regulator.
LG Energy Solution Michigan Inc. plans to raise $681 million through loans.
The company is slated to use the proceeds as investment sources to meet the increasing demand for electric vehicle batteries and energy storage systems (ESSs).
LG Energy operates a battery plant with an annual capacity of 5 gigawatt hours (GWh) in Michigan, US.
The company is expected to increase battery output in North America to 150 GWh.
LG Energy plans to set up another 40 GWh-capacity factory.
The company is building two battery plants of 35 GWh capacity each through a joint venture with General Motors Co.
One of the GM JV battery plants will be located in Ohio.
The other of the GM JV battery plants will be located in Tennessee.
In October, LG Energy and Stellantis N.V. launched a 4 trillion won joint venture to build a 40-GWh capacity battery manufacturing facility in the US.
LG Energy is set to go public on South Korea’s main bourse around the end of January 2022.
The IPO aims to raise as much as 10 trillion won in capital.
Competitor SK On Co. already planned to raise about 3 trillion won in a pre-IPO share sale next year to fund its massive investment plans for overseas factories.

facts: Stuart Countess serves as president and CEO of Kia Georgia in West Point.
Drew Ferguson currently serves as senior vice president of governmental affairs for Hyundai Motor Group in Washington, D.C.
Kia’s West Point manufacturing facility recently produced its five millionth vehicle.
Kia launched the first hybrid electric vehicle built in the state of Georgia at its West Point facility.
Ferguson described Kia's West Point milestone as evidence of the generational impact of economic development decisions made nearly two decades ago.
Ferguson stated, 'Think about where this community was 20 years ago. Those were generational decisions that brought Kia here, and now we’re seeing the generational success that came from them.'

Ferguson noted that companies face significant uncertainty related to trade policy, immigration, and tax debates, particularly as negotiations continue with major U.S. trading partners such as Canada and Mexico.

Ferguson identified four priorities central to community success: access to quality jobs, safe housing, strong educational systems, and the freedom for individuals to build better futures for their families.
Ferguson highlighted artificial intelligence and digital infrastructure as among the most significant issues facing the nation’s future economy.
source_title: LaGrange-Troup Chamber hosts Early Bird Breakfast, hears fro...

facts: Vernon, Southern California had high lead levels principally caused by a major battery recycling plant decommissioned in 2015.
Soil samples at Vernon’s preschool taken in 2014 contained lead levels of 95 parts per million.
Cleanup efforts around Vernon are still ongoing more than 10 years after the contamination was discovered.
Lead pollution measured in Ogijo, Nigeria reached 1,900 parts per million, which is 20 times the amount recorded in Vernon’s soil.
Ogijo has more lead recycling factories than anywhere else in Africa.
Lead recycled in Ogijo from used car batteries is shipped to supply companies including Tesla, Ford, and General Motors.
More than half of the children tested in Ogijo registered blood lead levels that could cause lifelong brain damage.
All recycling factory workers in Ogijo had blood lead levels exceeding the World Health Organization’s threshold of five micrograms per deciliter for serious health risks.
The investigation was published in November 2025 as a series of articles, videos, and multimedia visuals.
Will Fitzgibbon served as the main reporter for The Examination on this investigation.
Initial data for the topic came from seven university academics throughout Africa who published a paper in 2017.
In September 2023, Fitzgibbon published a piece reviewing international studies and surveys warning of prevalent worldwide lead contamination.

In December 2023, Fitzgibbon published a story researched with The Museba Project, Ghana Business News, and Grist focusing on lead pollution caused by battery recycling in Ghana, Cameroon, and the Democratic Republic of Congo.
The team collaborated with The New York Times and local African outlets including Premium Times, Joy FM, Pambazuko, and Truth Reporting Post.
US manufacturers have known about lead pollution problems for years but have avoided commitments to using lead certified as safely produced.
Automakers’ environmental policies aimed at reducing emissions do not include reducing pollution from lead.
Environmentally responsible battery recyclers in Africa can be pushed out of the business by less scrupulous and cheaper competitors.
The team used financial resources from The Examination to access trade databases tracing exports from Nigeria, Togo, South Korea, Pennsylvania, and New Jersey.
Trade data showed a single lead smelter in Ogijo received discarded batteries from abroad, extracted the lead, and shipped it through Trafigura to the port of Baltimore.
From Baltimore, the recycled metal traveled to East Penn Manufacturing in Lyon Station, Pennsylvania.
East Penn Manufacturing supplies batteries for carmakers including Tesla, General Motors, and Ford.
East Penn Manufacturing is one of the world’s largest battery producers.
After the investigation, East Penn Manufacturing stated it was unaware of lead pollution from its Nigerian suppliers and ended all imports from them.
The Examination contracted SRADev (Sustainable Research and Action for Environmental Development), a Nigerian nonprofit research group recommended by experts.
Through SRADev-hosted events, 70 people in the Ogijo community volunteered to have their blood tested.
SRADev distributed nasal filters to detect lead in the air released by the lead recycler’s smelting process.
The University of Ibadan collected and analyzed soil samples near factories.
Olamide Agunbiade is a regional health official quoted by The Examination regarding the study's necessity.
Impact producer Funmibi Ogunlesi and impact editor Miriam Wells created a WhatsApp group to keep Ogijo community members informed of developments.
Steve Myers served as the story’s editor for The Examination.
Nigerian authorities started cataloging the health and environmental damage caused by the factories and promised to close some down.
source_title: Tracing Lead Pollution in Nigeria to the Global Auto Supply Chain – Global Investigative Journalism Network

facts: Energy storage manufacturers are building domestic supply chains and experimenting with new materials to bring about the future of clean energy.
Nearly 200 countries gathered at the U.N. Climate Summit and signed a pact specifically urging the world to move away from fossil fuel production and focus more on clean energy sources.
The domestic lead recycling supply chain is already considered a success.
The recycling rate of lead batteries in the U.S. is nearly 100%.
A new lead battery produced in the U.S. typically contains more than 80% recycled raw materials.
Domestic lead deposits are available to easily source the small percentage of new materials needed for battery manufacturing.
Some lead batteries are still sent outside of North America for processing and remanufacturing.
Exporting batteries for recycling and importing finished batteries from foreign manufacturers creates unnecessary costs.
Concerns exist about how battery materials are handled once they leave U.S. shores.
U.S. battery manufacturers and recyclers follow some of the most comprehensive worker safety and environmental controls in the world.
These standards protect people in the plants, as well as the local community and the environment.
Domestic battery manufacturers and recyclers ensure essential batteries are readily available to keep supply chains running and people on the move.
The domestic battery manufacturing and recycling industries contribute nearly $33 billion to the U.S. economy.
These vital industries support more than 120,000 American jobs.
These industries strengthen domestic energy security and resilience.
In 2024, policymakers should work to ensure that the lead battery industry remains competitive with foreign suppliers.
Economically recycling and extracting critical minerals for many battery chemistries continues to be a challenge.
With the majority of lithium batteries produced abroad, the U.S. is vulnerable to supply chain disruptions for this technology.
2024 will be the year for lithium recyclers to build and scale domestic facilities and prove their capabilities.
As electric vehicle lithium batteries begin to reach end of life, recycling facilities will need to be open.
Lithium recyclers will be responsible for ensuring the U.S. has enough capacity to handle the wave domestically.
Vanadium redox flow batteries (VRFB) hold significant promise for a clean energy-driven future.
VRFBs offer a near-infinite lifecycle with proper maintenance.
The VRFB electrolyte can last 20 years or more before it begins to lose storage capacity.
Because the electrolyte can be recycled, VRFB is a sustainable solution for long-duration energy storage needs.
The domestic lead battery supply chain's expertise in producing large amounts of electrolyte can serve as a model for scaling up vanadium electrolyte production.
Manufacturers can lay the groundwork in 2024 to enable a robust VRFB supply chain.
VRFB manufacturers are making progress on sourcing both raw materials and the electrolyte.
Countries of concern have been a major source of vanadium in the past.
Efforts are underway to increase output from mines in South America and Australia to meet the growing demand for vanadium.
Friendly mining sources can scale up production in parallel as the VRFB market grows.
2024 will likely see groundbreakings for electrolyte manufacturing facilities centrally located in the U.S.
Localizing production where VRFB manufacturing is concentrated will help reduce shipping costs.
2024 will see efforts to establish production of storage system components, such as microchips and power electronics, and battery assembly plants in North America.
Clean energy initiatives saw a groundswell of support in prior years.

Legislation such as the Chips and Science Act, the Inflation Reduction Act, and the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act made it financially attractive to build clean energy capture technology and standalone energy storage systems.
In 2024, battery manufacturers will need to engage with the Department of Energy to take advantage of incentives for projects supporting domestic energy supply chain and manufacturing industries.
2024 will be a year of collaboration between manufacturers and suppliers at an operational level.
Companies have learned there is a meaningful competitive advantage in partnering with domestic suppliers to prepare for roadblocks.
Shorter supply chains mean shorter lead times before products hit the shelves.
More transparent partnerships can help customers ensure they have the supply needed to successfully run their operations.
Organizations can prepare for supply chain disruptions and more effectively meet each other’s needs by sharing data early.
For lead batteries, manufacturers must improve upon an already robust supply chain by keeping more recyclable batteries at home.
For lithium batteries, domestic recyclers need to prove their operations are viable.
For vanadium batteries, manufacturers must move from successful testing to building the foundation of a sustainable supply chain.
The success of America’s clean energy future hinges on continued collaboration and open lines of communication between energy storage manufacturers, suppliers, and policymakers.
The groundwork for the clean energy transition has been laid over the last decade.
2024 will see the energy storage industry leading the charge toward the development of more sustainable, environmentally friendly, and resilient energy technology.
Jeremy Furr serves as Senior Vice President of Strategic Sourcing at Stryten Energy.
source_title: Three Domestic Energy Storage Supply Chain Trends for 2024

facts: Hyundai Mobis plans to invest $926 million in an Electric Vehicle Power Electric system plant in Bryan County.
The plant will eventually employ at least 1,500 people.
Hyundai Mobis is one of the world's largest automotive suppliers.
Gov. Brian Kemp celebrated the groundbreaking of Hyundai's new electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facility in Bryan County.
Kemp stated the project would unleash transformational job creation and investment in that region of Georgia.
Hyundai Mobis is the second supplier to locate in that area in just two weeks.
The EV Power Electric system plant will expand Hyundai Mobis' current footprint in Georgia.
Hyundai Mobis has operated a manufacturing facility with almost 1,200 employees in West Point since 2009.
The West Point plant is the largest Tier 1 supplier to Kia Georgia.
The West Point plant supplies automotive manufacturers with complete cockpit modules, chassis modules, and bumper assemblies.
H.S. Oh is vice president of the Hyundai Mobis Electric Powertrain Business Unit.
Oh stated the investment reflects an acceleration in the development of the EV supply chain in Georgia's auto industry.
Oh stated they will be a major production player in the EV market, triggering more growth within the sector.
Mobis is looking forward to providing high-quality work opportunities to the growing local workforce.
Once at full production, the 1,200,000-square-foot facility will supply more than 900,000 EV Power Electric systems annually.
The facility will also supply 450,000 Integrated Charging Control Units annually.
These products will go to the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America factory in Bryan County.
These products will also go to the Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama factory in Montgomery.
These products will also go to the Kia Georgia plant.
Construction on the new facility is expected to begin as early as January 2023.
Production is expected to begin in 2024.
The facility is located at the Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development-certified Belfast Commerce Park in Richmond Hill.
Jon Seagraves is Chairman of the Development Authority of Bryan County.
Seagraves welcomed Mobis to the community.
Belfast Commerce Park has close proximity to available housing in nearby Heartwood.
The area has a top-performing public school system, interstate access, and rail.
The Development Authority purchased the site about two years ago.
Seagraves noted nearly $1 billion of investment and creation of over 1,500 good-paying jobs.
Seagraves expressed gratitude to Bryan County, the city of Richmond Hill, and Raydient Places for their support and partnership.
Alyce Thornhill is Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy.
Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development's Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project was in partnership with the Development Authority of Bryan County, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia Power.
Pat Wilson is GDEcD Commissioner.
Wilson stated suppliers like Hyundai Mobis are transitioning to the jobs of the future and becoming part of Georgia's growing EV ecosystem.
In May, they predicted that non-affiliated HMGMA suppliers in connection with the project would invest an additional $1 billion into Georgia.
They have already blown past that prediction in about two weeks.
Wilson stated they are excited to prepare Georgia's workforce for the jobs being created now with support from partners like Georgia Quick Start and sister agencies.
source_title: Automotive supplier to bring 1,500 additional jobs to Bryan County

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America is the key pillar of the Group’s $12.6 billion investment in Georgia.
It is the largest economic development project in Georgia's history.
The vehicle assembly and battery plant will produce up to 500,000 electric and hybrid vehicles annually for Hyundai, Kia and Genesis brands.
Hyundai Motor Group commits to an additional $21 billion investment from 2025 to 2028 to drive U.S. manufacturing growth.
Since entering the U.S., Hyundai Motor Group has invested $20.5 billion in the U.S., creating directly and indirectly more than 570,000 American jobs.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America hosted its Grand Opening celebration on March 26, 2025, in Ellabell, Ga.
The ceremony marks the completion of the largest economic development project in Georgia’s history, just two and a half years after breaking ground.

Euisun Chung, Executive Chair of Hyundai Motor Group, stated that HMGMA represents advanced manufacturing capabilities, commitment to innovation, and investment in relationships with partners and communities in Georgia.
Governor Brian P. Kemp attended the event and highlighted collaborations between HMGMA and Georgia’s universities and technical colleges, including Georgia Quick Start’s on-site training facility.

HMG entered into a landmark agreement with the State of Georgia in May 2022 to establish an electric and hybrid vehicle production and battery manufacturing facility in Ellabell, Georgia, located 20 miles outside of historic Savannah.
By June 2022, HMGMA was officially formed, and within four months, the company broke ground on the project.
On October 3, 2024, less than two years after breaking ground, the first vehicle – a Hyundai IONIQ 5 – rolled off the assembly line.
Hyundai’s IONIQ 9 three-row electric SUV is also now in production at HMGMA.
The first Kia model to be built at the facility will join production in 2026.
In addition to manufacturing electric vehicles for Hyundai, Genesis, and Kia, the plant is equipped to produce hybrid vehicles.
HMGMA has the capacity to initially produce 300,000 vehicles annually.
The Group's investment in Georgia related to HMGMA and joint battery ventures with LG Energy Solution and SK On is $12.6 billion.
This includes a total of 8,500 jobs at HMGMA by 2031.
The Group’s total investments in Georgia are expected to create nearly 40,000 direct and indirect jobs.
These investments are expected to generate $4.6 billion in individual earnings every year, according to the Center for Automotive Research.
As of July 2024, more than $2.5 billion in capital investment and approximately 6,900 jobs have been announced by HMGMA suppliers in 12 counties across the state.
This more than doubles the additional $1 billion investment by suppliers that was originally anticipated.
The Group announced a significant investment of $21 billion in the U.S. from 2025 to 2028, with $9 billion invested to establish an annual production capacity in the U.S. of 1.2 million vehicles.
The Group plans to expand HMGMA's annual production capacity to a total of 500,000 units, an increase of 200,000 units from the original plan.
The Group’s existing U.S. production sites – Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama and Kia Autoland Georgia – will also see improvements in production facilities.

A total of $6 billion will be allocated to enhance the Group’s parts and logistics business, as well as establishing an Electric Arc Furnace-based integrated steel mill in the U.S. through its affiliate, Hyundai Steel.
The steel mill will have an annual production capacity of 2.7 million tons and will supply high-quality automotive steel plates to HMGMA when commercial steel production begins in 2029.

The Group will invest an additional $6 billion to drive innovation and expand strategic partnerships with U.S. companies in areas including autonomous driving, robotics, artificial intelligence (AI), and advanced air mobility (AAM).
Through this $21 billion commitment, the Group anticipates creating 14,000 new direct full-time jobs in the U.S. by 2028.
The overall economic impact is expected to generate more than 100,000 direct and indirect job opportunities across related industries.

HMGMA has integrated proof-of-concept manufacturing technologies and systems from Hyundai Motor Group Innovation Center Singapore (HMGICS), scaling them to accommodate larger production volume at the Georgia facility.
All HMGMA’s processes – order collection, procurement, logistics and production – are optimized using AI and real-time data.
Cutting-edge robotics and vision systems ensure high-quality vehicle production and help create a human-centered work environment with robots assisting human workers.

Oscar Kwon, CEO of HMGMA, stated that the state-of-the-art smart plant is officially open and emphasized creating a comfortable and welcoming work environment for employees alongside an energy-efficient and technologically advanced plant.
All work zones, from office to production areas, have been designed to encourage communication and build team collaboration with open layouts, ample daylight, and indoor landscaping.
Production areas in the assembly building feature team centers with skylights delivering natural light at the center of the building for employee breaks and meetings.

The 3,050-foot-long front of the HMGMA building’s exterior is lit in two parallel lines inspired by a stream, outlining an area where interstate travelers can view vehicles traveling by conveyer from paint to general assembly.
A solar parking lot at the front of the site will generate 5.2 megawatts of energy, accounting for roughly five percent of the Metaplant’s total power consumption.
The solar panels are installed above 1,878 parking spaces, providing shade for vehicles while simultaneously producing electricity.

A 41-acre ecological park featuring trails, running tracks, sports fields, and picnic areas will be located in front of the Metaplant for employees to enjoy before or after work, with construction ongoing.
HMGMA has deployed Hyundai Motor XCIENT heavy-duty hydrogen fuel-cell electric trucks for clean logistics operations, with a total of 21 XCIENT trucks currently in operation.

These trucks represent more than a third of the Glovis America truck fleet at HMGMA and transport vehicle parts from suppliers across the region to the 2,906-acre Megasite where HMGMA is located on a daily basis.
Since 2022, Hyundai and Genesis, along with various social responsibility initiatives and nonprofit organizations, have donated nearly $4.5 million to local organizations in Georgia.

Kia's Autoland Georgia factory in West Point has provided over $22 million in support of Georgia communities since 2009, including more than $6 million to support educational programs such as the THINC College & Career Academy and SAE’s “A World in Motion.”

source_title: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America Celebrates Grand Opening, Powering U.S. Economic Growth AUTOJUNCTION - Autojunction

facts: Form Energy has begun construction on a factory expansion that will add nearly 300,000 square feet to the Weirton, West Virginia, facility where it manufactures long-duration energy storage systems.
The expansion announcement came less than a week after Form announced the closing of a $405 million Series F fundraising round.
Form signed a memorandum of understanding with GE Vernova involving strategic collaboration on manufacturing operations, supply chain operations, financing, and sourcing.
The collaboration will also involve leveraging GE Vernova’s Advanced Research Center capabilities.
The moves bring the company closer to its goal of a 1 million square-foot manufacturing footprint with 500 MW / 50 GWh in annual production capacity by 2028.
Form Energy aims to commercialize an iron-air battery system that can store electricity for 100 hours at system costs competitive with legacy power plants.
The technology uses atmospheric oxygen to oxidize (discharge) and deoxidize (charge) iron powder housed in a water-based electrolyte.

CEO and co-founder Mateo Jaramillo told Utility Dive in 2021 that the company’s technology is "in a different category" than other long-duration storage technologies, which typically discharge over periods of eight to 24 hours.
Form began trial production earlier this year at its 550,000 square-foot manufacturing facility in Weirton.
The company plans to begin commercial production by the end of 2024.
Form Factory 1 sits on 55 acres formerly occupied by a steel mill.
Form expects to complete the expansion project by the end of 2025.
The expanded facility will be approximately 850,000 square feet, within reach of Form’s longer-term goal of 1 million square feet by 2028.
The company has announced 14 GWh of long-duration storage projects to date, representing approximately 18 months of production at Form Factory 1.
The $405 million fundraise included investments from GE Vernova, Breakthrough Energy Ventures, and Energy Impact Partners.
CEO Mateo Jaramillo stated the fundraise will enable the company to boost manufacturing capacity and grow operations to deliver even more iron-air batteries to customers nationwide.
Form broke ground in August on its first commercial pilot, a 1.5 MW/150 MWh project developed in partnership with Minnesota utility Great River Energy.
The Great River Energy pilot facility is expected to begin operation by the end of 2025.
Form signed a memorandum of understanding with Puget Sound Energy to explore a potential 10-MW/1-GWh deployment in its service territory as soon as the end of 2026.
Form is pursuing a 5-MW/500-MWh project funded in part by a $30 million grant from the California Energy Commission at a Pacific Gas & Electric site in northern California.
Form is pursuing a 10 MW/1-GWh demonstration project in New York funded in part by a $12 million grant from the New York State Energy Research and Development Authority.
Form is developing a self-funded deployment of 85 MW/8.5 GWh in rural Maine, funded in part by a $147 million grant from the U.S. Department of Energy.
Form has formed a partnership with Xcel Energy’s Minnesota subsidiary for a 10 MW/1-GWh deployment paired with up to 710 MW of solar.
source_title: Iron-air battery developer Form Energy raises $405M, announces collaboration with GE Vernova | Utility Dive

facts: Ascend Elements is a Westborough, Mass. based company.
Ascend Elements is on the brink of becoming the top domestic provider of sustainable, engineered battery materials in North America.
Ascend Elements utilizes a revolutionary recycling process and patented cathode engineering technology.
These advancements position Ascend to become the leading supplier of high-nickel, NMC cathode precursor (pCAM) to the rapidly expanding lithium-ion battery industry in North America.
Ascend Elements recently inked a multi-year deal to provide around $1 billion worth of sustainable pCAM for a leading U.S. company’s battery manufacturing process.
The multi-year deal kicks off in Q4 2024.
The agreement terms provide the customer the option to expand the contract up to $5 billion.
Ascend Elements is constructing one of North America’s first large-scale NMC pCAM production facilities in southwest Kentucky.
Mike O’Kronley serves as the CEO of Ascend Elements.
Mike O’Kronley pointed out that almost 100% of the world’s pCAM is manufactured in Asia.

Mike O’Kronley believes the United States needs to manufacture its own critical battery materials to secure the supply chain in North America, cut down on carbon emissions, and maintain energy independence.
The U.S. Department of Energy granted Ascend Elements two matching funds totaling $480 million in October 2022 to speed up construction of the southwest Kentucky facility.
Ascend Elements intends to invest over $1 billion in this project.
The Hopkinsville, Kentucky-based Ascend Elements facility will serve as a unique, sustainable cathode manufacturing plant.
The facility will have the ability to produce NMC pCAM for up to 750,000 electric vehicles annually.

Ascend Elements uses its patented Hydro-to-Cathode® direct precursor synthesis process to manufacture NMC pCAM and cathode active material (CAM) from used lithium-ion batteries and battery gigafactory manufacturing scrap.
This closed-loop process eliminates several intermediary steps in the traditional cathode manufacturing process.

Peer-reviewed studies confirm that Ascend Elements’ recycled battery materials perform on par with materials derived from virgin or mined sources while significantly reducing carbon emissions related to mining.
The company's process aims to reduce the number of batteries ending up in landfills.
source_title: Ascend Elements Transforms Battery Industry with Sustainable, High-Nickel Cathode Manufacturing Technology - The EV Report

facts: Battery recycling startup Ascend Elements filed for bankruptcy on Thursday, according to the company.
Westborough, Massachusetts-based Ascend specializes in reprocessing old batteries into cathode material at a factory in Georgia.
The company has raised more than $1.1 billion in equity and grants since its founding in 2015.
Chief Executive Officer Linh Austin stated that financial difficulties were insurmountable and the company voluntarily initiated a Chapter 11 process.
CEO Linh Austin said creating a new industry to recycle old batteries is capital-intensive and complex, while the US seeks to break its dependency on battery materials from China.
Ascend saw a $316 million grant canceled by the administration last October.
Both grants were made under former President Joe Biden.
The company counts top investment houses, including Temasek, Qatar Investment Authority, and BHP Ventures, among its backers.
source_title: Battery recycler Ascend Elements files for bankruptcy

facts: The NEVI (National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure) program is a federally funded program allocating $5.4 billion to building EV charger infrastructure across the country.
The funds will be allocated at the state level.
Most states are allocating NEVI funds through an RFP process.
Installations must be located within one mile of designated corridors called Alternative Fuel Corridors, typically along main highways.
Approved installations cannot be more than 50 miles away from each other.
Charging stations must be capable of charging four vehicles at 150kW simultaneously.
The BTC GEN4 180 kW and the Split System offers NEVI compliant configurations.
Any organization interested in using NEVI funds must match 20%.
Some states do have alternative funding and grant opportunities available to help with additional costs.
NEVI funding covers eligible costs such as planning, acquisition, installation, and operation of the charging unit.
EV drivers will seek out and visit fast-charging stations giving your organization the ability to attract prospective customers.
This is a limited-time opportunity for one location at a time.
States are looking to participate in NEVI to meet EV promises and increase tourism.
For Georgia, NEVI information is handled by the Georgia Department of Transportation.
source_title: Applying for NEVI Benefits - BTC Power

facts: Rivian is slated to build a factory in Morgan and Walton counties in Georgia.
The factory is expected to produce 400,000 vehicles per year.
It will be located 45 miles east of Atlanta.
There is no definite timeline for the plant’s production.
Rivian hopes to employ about 2,000 people at its new factory.
The new facility will be nearly 20 million square feet and cover nearly two thousand acres.
The Georgia site will be five times the size of Rivian’s current facility in Normal, Illinois.
The company’s new Georgia factory is set to open in 2024.
It is anticipated that the Georgia factory will produce 400,000 cars a year.
The new factory is expected to have a significant impact on the environment.
The company will be required to acquire environmental permits for the new factory.
The permitting process will involve numerous committees and public comment sessions.
Georgia has a large, educated workforce that is interested in technology.
Rivian’s executives cite Georgia’s friendly attitude toward technology companies as a key factor in selecting this location.
The state is also ideally located for distribution and logistics.
The new site will allow Rivian to scale quickly while maintaining the highest quality standards in its products and facilities.
Rivian’s opponents cite various concerns about the plant, including the potential for groundwater contamination.
Opponents question the state’s incentive program for the project.
Opponents fear that the enormous factory will ruin the bucolic character of the area.
Former U.S. senator David Perdue has criticized Governor Kemp for accepting the project in Georgia.
Rivian is already in a partnership with Amazon, which aims to buy 100,000 Rivian delivery vans by 2030.
The company has received more than seven hundred orders for pickups and SUVs in the U.S. and Canada as of Dec. 15, 2018.
Amazon is a major shareholder in Rivian, having invested $700 million in the company this year.
Amazon led a series of funding rounds totaling $1.8 billion.
As of January 2019, Amazon holds 16.2% of Rivian’s shares.
Ford is the largest investor in Rivian.
Other financial institutions have also invested in Rivian.
The Inflation Reduction Act allocates $3 billion to electrify the delivery fleet of the Postal Service.
USPS has doubled its order of electric trucks for its new fleet, from 5,000 to 10,019 vehicles.
Ford says it plans to invest $30 billion in the production of electric vehicles through 2025.
By 2030, Ford expects to sell 1.5 million EVs a year.
Ford has invested $700 million in the creation of an electric vehicle center near the Rouge Complex where the F-Series truck is built.
The Lightning’s battery pack and frame are assembled at the Rouge Complex.
The pickup bed and cab are built in another location.
A conveyor belt transports the assembled Lightnings from one factory to the next.
source_title: How Truck Factory Became Lightning? [Answered 2026] | Prettymotors

facts: The U.S. Department of Energy gave final approval Thursday to electric vehicle maker Rivian for a $6.6 billion loan to support its stalled Georgia factory.
The loan provides financial backing to build the plant in southern Morgan and Walton counties, roughly an hour east of Atlanta.
The project was first proposed in late 2021.
The project was initially supposed to open in 2024.
Delays mounted as Rivian faced supply chain issues and other challenges.
Rivian also plans to expand its Georgia plant with a second line.
The T1R, S1R, and R2 models are expected to start production in the coming years for export.
The $5.1 billion project is key to Rivian's future.
source_title: $6B federal loan for Rivian’s Georgia plant finalized

facts: Rivian is an American-born, designed, programmed, and assembled electric vehicle brand founded in 2009 by RJ Scaringe.
The company's headquarters are located in Irvine, California.
Rivian's first production facility is a repurposed Mitsubishi plant in Normal, Illinois.
The Illinois plant assembles the R1T pickup truck, R1S SUV, and Amazon electric delivery vans.
Rivian plans to build a second U.S. manufacturing plant in Stanton Springs North, Georgia.
The new Georgia facility will produce the R2 and R3 models, which are smaller, more affordable electric SUVs aimed at mainstream consumers.
In January 2025, Rivian secured a $6.6 billion loan from the U.S. Department of Energy to fund the new Georgia plant.
The first phase of production at the Georgia facility is set to begin in 2028.
The initial capacity for the Georgia plant will be 200,000 vehicles per year.
A second phase at the Georgia facility will eventually double this output capacity.
By 2030, Rivian estimates the Georgia expansion will generate 7,500 permanent operational jobs in manufacturing, engineering, and logistics.
The Georgia expansion will also create 2,000 construction jobs.
Rivian's expansion strategy aims to increase domestic EV production capacity, create thousands of American jobs, strengthen the U.S. EV supply chain, and make EVs more accessible and affordable.
At its Normal, Illinois factory, Rivian stamps its own aluminum panels in-house to reduce reliance on third-party suppliers.
Each Rivian battery pack consists of 7,776 cylindrical cells that are assembled and tested in-house.
Rivian builds its own quad-motor drive units for superior off-road performance.
The R2 mid-size SUV is expected to start around $45,000 and production will begin at the new Georgia factory in 2026.
The R3 is Rivian's smallest and most affordable model, designed to compete with the Tesla Model Y and Ford Mustang Mach-E, and will be available in an off-road-focused "R3X" variant.
Some parts and materials for Rivian vehicles are globally sourced, but design, engineering, and final assembly take place on U.S. soil.
source_title: Rivian - the American-made Electric Vehicle: Designed and Assembled in the USA

facts: Work on electric vehicle manufacturer Rivian Automotive’s $5 billion plant will progress as planned despite an ongoing legal fight, officials confirmed.
Site grading began in the early fall.
The judge’s ruling does not affect progress on development.
Marie Gordon is a spokeswoman for the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD).

GDEcD and the Joint Development Authority (JDA) of Jasper, Morgan, Newton, and Walton Counties filed an appeal of Ocmulgee Superior Court Judge Brenda Trammell’s decision to deny bond validation for the Rivian project.
Economic development officials stated the bonds "are not a debt obligation" of the state or the local community.
Officials would not explicitly guarantee Georgia taxpayers would not be left holding the bag if the deal goes south.
Earlier this year, GDEcD and JDA announced a deal to give $1.5 billion in incentives to Rivian.
The company plans to build a plant along Interstate 20 in Morgan and Newton counties.
On July 8, Georgia officials filed a petition to validate $15 billion in taxable revenue bonds to finance the Rivian project.
Seven Morgan County residents subsequently filed a motion to intervene.
On Sept. 29, Trammell denied the bond validation for the project.
GDEcD and JDA stated they are confident in the strength of their appeal and have every intention of seeing the project through to completion for the benefit of all Georgians.

GDEcD and JDA stated the agreement is one of the strongest the State has secured in terms of clawbacks and that due diligence was performed, including a review of Rivian's financial records and outlook.
Economic officials said the debt obligation is solely Rivian’s responsibility.
Officials declined to elaborate on what might happen if Rivian declares bankruptcy, pointing to a Friday statement.

GDEcD and JDA stated the judge’s ruling improperly considered Rivian’s finances and disregarded its significant cash reserves, manufacturing performance at its existing facility in Normal, Illinois, and the rising market trend toward electric vehicles.
A spokesperson for Gov. Brian Kemp did not respond to a request to confirm Georgia taxpayers wouldn’t be on the hook for the project’s cost.
Officials previously said the plant would begin production in 2024.
source_title: Despite legal battle, $5B Rivian electric vehicle plant still in progress for Georgia | Georgia | thecentersquare.com

facts: The Biden administration avoided one of the more challenging early tests of its climate agenda Saturday, when two South Korean battery giants feuding over trade secrets reached a settlement to keep a massive Georgia factory complex open.

The White House faced a Sunday deadline to decide whether to veto a U.S. International Trade Commission decision from February that barred SK Innovation from importing the materials needed to build electric vehicle batteries at a $2.6 billion pair of plants in Commerce, Georgia.

The case illustrates the new administration’s dueling priorities as it seeks to jumpstart efforts to eliminate the U.S. economy’s planet-heating pollution while going head-to-head with China over intellectual property protections.

As late as Friday night, SK Innovation looked far from a deal with Seoul-based rival LG Energy Solutions, which convinced the federal trade court that SK Innovation destroyed evidence of stolen trade secrets.
In February, the ITC imposed a 10-year import ban on SK Innovation.
The import ban jeopardized the supply of batteries for Ford’s electric F-150 pickup and Volkswagen’s Crossover series.
The ruling threatened the 2,600 jobs the company planned to fill in Georgia over the next few years.
The ruling also risked delaying President Joe Biden’s plans to electrify the nation’s 276 million some-odd automobiles, the top U.S. source of climate pollution.
A $1.8 billion cash deal will end other ongoing lawsuits between SK Innovation and LG Energy Solutions in the U.S. and South Korea.
The cash deal includes ending a lawsuit in a federal court in Delaware that had been on pause until the ITC saga reached its completion.

SK Innovation CEO Jun Kim and LG Energy Solution CEO Jong Hyun Kim issued a joint statement Sunday morning saying they decided to settle and compete amicably for the future of the U.S. and South Korean electric vehicle battery industries.

The CEOs stated they will work together to strengthen an EV battery domestic supply chain and support the U.S. Biden Administration’s effort to advance green energy policies and environmental responsibility.
SK Innovation’s plant will comprise upward of 35% of the U.S. production capacity of electric vehicle batteries by the end of Biden’s first term.
By the end of Biden’s first term, the country is expected to have roughly 11 big powerpack-making factories online.
The settlement will likely help reverse the ITC ruling and allow SK Innovation to keep its plant open.
Leaders across the partisan spectrum pressed for an outcome that would allow the plant to remain open.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp (R) pleaded with Biden to veto the decision if a settlement wasn’t reached.
In recent weeks, Sen. Jon Ossoff (D-Ga.) held at least one meeting seeking to broker a deal.
The looming deadline and high stakes of a decision that should define the future of a U.S. set for near exponential growth in the next decade led to an eleventh-hour agreement.
Losing the factory without a deal or veto would have sent a chill through the U.S. battery market as it looks to start attracting more players, according to Caspar Rawles.
Caspar Rawles is an analyst at Benchmark Mineral Intelligence, a London-based research firm that specializes in lithium-ion batteries and electric vehicle supply chains.
Rawles stated before the deal was announced that shutting down a plant in the U.S. due to legal reasons does not send a good message.
Rawles noted it is not the most inviting investment environment for someone looking to spend several billion dollars with the risk of legal intervention causing them to lose it all.
A presidential veto would have been a rare move, with the last time used in 2013 when President Barack Obama blocked an ITC ruling that would have prevented Apple from importing some iPads.
Ronald Reagan set the record for vetoing four ITC decisions, including one that coincidentally dealt with batteries.
source_title: Biden Avoids Big Test As Battery Giants Reach Deal To Save Georgia Factories | HuffPost Latest News

facts: SK Battery America is the U.S. unit of South Korean battery maker SK On.
SK Battery America laid off 37 percent of its workforce at its Georgia plant, citing slowing electric vehicle (EV) sales and a shifting market landscape.
The company announced on Saturday that it cut 968 jobs out of its 2,566 employees at the manufacturing facility in Commerce, Georgia.
The restructuring comes as the global EV transition loses momentum.
The plant supplies batteries to automakers including Volkswagen and Hyundai Motor.
The plant was severely impacted by Ford Motor's recent decision to cancel production of its F-150 Lightning electric pickup truck.
Ford's move follows the Trump administration's rollback of EV tax credits, prompting the U.S. automaker to pivot toward more profitable hybrid and internal combustion engine vehicles.
In an emailed statement, SK Battery America called the job cuts a "difficult decision to adjust operations in line with market conditions."
The company added that it remains committed to Georgia and to building a robust U.S. supply chain for advanced battery manufacturing.
South Korean battery makers are facing broader headwinds as the transition from internal combustion engines to EVs decelerates in major markets like the United States.

Despite the setback, parent company SK Innovation is proceeding with a second battery plant in Georgia dedicated to supplying Hyundai Motor, with production scheduled to begin in the first half of this year.
Another facility in Tennessee, previously planned as a joint venture with Ford, is slated to open in 2028.
A company official indicated that the Tennessee plant may supply both automotive and energy storage system (ESS) batteries.
source_title: EV slump hits hard: SK On slashes 37 percent of Georgia plant staff | Aju Press

facts: Governor Brian Kemp and First Lady Marty Kemp joined President and Global COO of Hyundai Motor Company and President and CEO of Hyundai and Genesis Motor North America José Muñoz to celebrate Hyundai Motor Group’s historic impact on Georgia.
An update was provided on the progress of Hyundai Motor Group's Metaplant America facility.
Hyundai Motor Group's Metaplant America facility will officially start vehicle production in the fourth quarter of 2024.

Hyundai Motor Group has been a partner to Georgia for decades, helping recruit businesses, giving back to communities, and working with colleges and universities to connect Georgians with opportunities.
Hyundai and Genesis electric vehicles including the IONIQ 5, IONIQ 6, GV60, and Electrified GV70 were displayed outside the State Capitol.
The hydrogen-powered XCIENT Fuel Cell Truck was displayed outside the State Capitol and will be used for logistics between Hyundai’s manufacturing facilities and suppliers.
Governor Kemp officially declared February 26 as Hyundai Day throughout the state.
The Georgia General Assembly passed a Resolution commending Hyundai Motor Group’s dedication to Georgia and recognizing February 26 as Hyundai Day at the State Capitol.
Hyundai’s investments in Georgia will create tens of thousands of great-paying American jobs.
Hyundai’s investments in Georgia will bring tens of billions of dollars in economic impact for decades to come.
Since the Metaplant America project was announced in May 2022, LG Energy Solution was named as the joint venture partner in the manufacture of battery components.
An additional expansion of the LG Energy Solution battery facility was announced.
Hyundai and SK On are building an EV battery manufacturing facility in Bartow County that will supply Hyundai Motor Group’s U.S. plants.
Since the initial announcement, Hyundai Motor Group’s planned investment in Georgia has reached more than $12.6 billion.
The planned investment brings with it the creation of 12,000 direct, long-term jobs.

Lieutenant Governor Burt Jones, House Speaker Jon Burns, legislative leaders, GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson, and officials from the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority joined the celebration.
Partnerships with the University System of Georgia, especially Georgia Tech, have enabled Hyundai's positive impact and job creation in Georgia.
Up to 300,000 Hyundai, Genesis, and Kia EVs are expected to be produced annually at the Metaplant.
Seventeen HMGMA suppliers have announced projects across 10 Georgia counties spanning beyond southeast Georgia.
Further supplier announcements are expected.
Supplier facilities are expected to create more than 6,700 new jobs and more than $2.5 billion in investment.
Supplier facilities will be operational prior to the opening of HMGMA.
The Metaplant will have a generational impact as a force multiplier for additional investments in education, healthcare, housing, and transportation.
The nearly 3,000-acre Metaplant site has four building exteriors complete with outfitting for internal operations underway.
The Metaplant’s architecture incorporates technologically advanced elements, design features representing the Savannah area, Hyundai’s brand identity, nature, and clean energy.
HMGMA is hiring full-time employees rapidly for training and skills on machinery, office and clerical work, and numerous other employment roles.
HMGMA currently has 566 full-time employees.
Approximately 75 percent of the current workforce is from the Greater Savannah area.
An additional 37 employees are from Georgia but outside the local area.
Georgia Quick Start broke ground on its customized training center at the Metaplant site on February 8, 2024.
HMGMA partnered with local technical colleges to prepare students for entry-level employment at the Metaplant.

Electrical Vehicle Professional Technical Certificate of Credit (EVP TCC) training is available at Coastal Pines Technical College’s Baxley and Waycross campuses, Southeastern Technical College’s Vidalia campus, Savannah Technical College, and Ogeechee Technical College.
In September 2023, Georgia Institute of Technology and Hyundai Motor Company signed a memorandum of understanding to collaborate on research and applications for sustainable mobility.
The collaboration areas include developing a hydrogen economy, workforce development, and making cities smarter and more sustainable.
U.S. News & World Report ranked Georgia Tech as the top public university and No. 3 nationally in energy and fuel research.
Since 1998, Hyundai has directed over $6 million towards local Georgia communities through philanthropic programs and partnerships.
Hyundai is promoting STEM programs in local K-12 schools and hosting educational programs on alternative energy sources.
Hyundai supports local organizations dedicated to affordable homeownership and focuses on environmental stewardship in the region.
Hyundai has given more than $1 million to regional nonprofits and formed key partnerships with local educational institutions.
Hyundai committed to $7.59 billion in capital investment in Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
The project will create 8,500 jobs.
More than 550 Meta Pros have already been hired, with 75 percent coming from the Savannah region.
Beginning with mass production, the electricity used to power the Metaplant will come from 100 percent clean power sources.
The clean power initiative aligns with Hyundai’s commitment to carbon neutrality by 2045.
A 41-acre park will be featured in front of the plant, including trails, running tracks, environmental art displays, sports fields, and amenities for employee use.
source_title: State Leaders Celebrate Hyundai Motor Group’s Historic Impact on Georgia - Rome CEO

canonical_name: State of Georgia Business Climate Ranking
facts: Gov. Nathan Deal announced that Georgia's business climate has been ranked No. 1 in the nation for the sixth consecutive year by Site Selection on November 5, 2018.
Georgia is the first state to hold this top ranking for six consecutive years under leadership by the same governor.
Gov. Nathan Deal stated that people from all over the country have come to Georgia's communities to find opportunity, jobs, hope, and prosperity.
The six-year streak reflects the strength of Georgia's business-friendly environment and strategic investments made across the state.
The ranking resulted from exceptional collaboration between public and private sector partners creating economic opportunities in Georgia's communities.
Conservative policies have attracted more businesses to the state each year.
Georgia has cultivated an economic environment that rewards hard work and innovation, generates investments in local communities, and improves quality of life for families.
The state has cut taxes and made deeper investments in its workforce since first earning the distinction.

Gov. Nathan Deal expressed pride in leading the nation in economic development and looking forward to Georgia retaining its position as an exceptional place to do business, work, learn, and call home.
Site Selection releases rankings for the top states to do business each November.
Site Selection's business climate rankings are based on a survey of corporate real estate executives and site consultants.
The ranking index includes tax burden criteria from the Tax Foundation and KPMG's Location Matters analysis.
The index also considers qualifying projects resident in the Conway Projects Database, which tracks new and expanded business facility activity worldwide.
Georgia has been ranked among the top 10 states throughout the last decade.
Georgia held the top spot for five years prior to this sixth consecutive year.
Mark Arend, editor-in-chief of Site Selection, noted that a six-year top business climate winning streak is highly unusual.
Arend attributed the streak to Governor Deal's commitment to making Georgia a competitive location for business early in his administration and every year since.
Capital investors report that Georgia delivers fiscal soundness, logistics infrastructure, competitive business costs, and workers required today and in the future.
Gov. Deal was joined at the news conference by Mark Arend, GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson, former GDEcD Commissioners Attorney General Chris Carr, and Chris Cummiskey.
Pat Wilson stated that being recognized for having the best business climate in the nation for six years is a testament to the leadership of Governor Deal and the General Assembly.
Wilson highlighted that the last eight years have been unprecedented for Georgia in terms of economic development from job creation to located projects and investment.
Site Selection, published by Conway Data Inc., delivers expansion planning information to 48,000 executives of fast-growing firms.

Site Selection is available via Site Selection Online and publishes e-newsletters including The Site Selection Dispatch, Life Sciences Report, Aerospace Report, Energy Report, Site Selection International, The FDI Report, TrustBelt.com, and the OnSITE Travel blog.
source_title: World Trade Centers Association

facts: Georgia is turning into a manufacturing and logistics hub for electric mobility, with major investments by automakers and battery suppliers.
The state is rapidly stitching together a robust web of Level 2 and DC fast chargers between Georgia Power, federal NEVI funding, and private networks.
Georgia Power operates community Level 2 and DC fast chargers across the state with kWh-based pricing.

Under the Georgia Power Make Ready Infrastructure Program, for business, municipal, and multifamily sites installing six or more ports or at least one DC fast charger, Georgia Power will design, build, and own the electrical infrastructure up to (but not including) the chargers themselves.
The Georgia Power Make Ready program covers up to hundreds of thousands of dollars in make-ready costs and runs through at least 2028, while funding lasts.
Georgia Power offers separate rebates and managed charging pilots that can offset Level 2 hardware for businesses and pay residential customers to charge off-peak.
Under the Federal NEVI Formula Program, Georgia is slated to receive roughly $135 million over five years to build fast-charging hubs every ~50 miles along Alternative Fuel Corridors.
The state has already awarded funds for dozens of sites under the NEVI program, each with at least four DC fast chargers that must operate 24/7.

Under the Federal 30C Alternative Fuel Infrastructure Tax Credit, businesses, nonprofits, and municipalities that install charging in eligible census tracts can get a federal tax credit of up to 30% of project costs, capped at $100,000 per charger, through 2032.
The 30C tax credit is stackable with other programs when structured correctly.
Electrify America lists 30+ DC fast-charging stations in Georgia.
EVgo has sites in and around Atlanta and along key corridors.
Tesla’s Supercharger network blankets Metro Atlanta and major interstates, with more sites now including CCS compatibility or NACS access for non-Teslas.
I-75 serves as a corridor between Atlanta and Florida/Tennessee with strong DC fast coverage at Pilot/Flying J, Electrify America sites, and Tesla Superchargers.
GM-EVgo-Pilot truck-stop hubs are expanding along the I-75 route.
I-85 is already designated an EV corridor between Atlanta and Greenville/Charlotte, with a goal of fast chargers roughly every 50 miles and multiple private-network sites.
New NEVI-backed hubs are closing remaining gaps on I-85 between Atlanta and the South Carolina line.
I-20 serves as a corridor between Atlanta and Birmingham/Augusta with a growing mix of Electrify America, EVgo, utility-enabled sites, and Tesla coverage.
Coverage thins out on I-20 between major cities.
I-16 serves as a corridor between Macon and Savannah that is historically sparse but targeted in Georgia’s NEVI plan and recent funding rounds focused on rural gaps.
New four-stall DC hubs are expected at travel centers and highway exits along I-16.
Select towns and travel centers on US Highways like US 82 and US 441 will receive NEVI-funded hubs operated by companies like Pilot Travel Centers, Love’s, and regional providers.
Some NEVI projects funded in late 2025 will still be under construction through 2026.
Georgia Power community Level 2 chargers cost around $0.28/kWh.
Georgia Power community DC fast chargers cost around $0.48/kWh.
A 60 kWh session on Georgia Power DC fast charging (roughly 200 miles in many EVs) would be about $29 before any idle fees.
Third-party networks like Electrify America, EVgo, and Tesla use dynamic pricing that often ranges higher than utility-run stations, especially for pay-as-you-go users.
Membership plans or automaker partnerships can significantly lower per-kWh rates on third-party networks.
Metro Atlanta has a dense mix of Level 2 and fast charging around downtown, Midtown, Buckhead, and major job centers.
A growing presence of chargers exists in county buildings, libraries, and parking decks after local initiatives added public EV stalls.
Smaller cities and rural Georgia have coverage clustered at interstate exits, travel centers, and a few downtown Level 2 sites.
Long stretches of US highways in rural areas have no DC fast charging yet, with Level 2 potentially being the only fallback in some towns.
Many NEVI-funded sites in rural areas are in planning or construction phases through 2026.
Big brands like Pilot, Love’s, Waffle House, and Walmart are adding 400 kW-class chargers at highway-adjacent locations, often with both CCS and NACS connectors.
Automakers are rolling out more NACS-equipped EVs and adapters, making Tesla Superchargers a practical option for many non-Tesla drivers.
Between 2027 and 2030, there will be a noticeable shift from destination chargers to ambient charging, with chargers appearing at grocery stores, gyms, and apartment complexes.
Regulators and drivers are pushing networks to improve uptime, payment simplicity, and station maintenance.
More dynamic pricing tied to grid conditions and time-of-day is expected, especially as data centers and industrial loads grow in Georgia.
There will be a heavier focus on fleets (delivery, ride-hail, municipal) with dedicated depots that may reduce competition at public stations.
Level 2 chargers use 240V and typically add 25-50 miles of range per hour depending on power level and the EV's onboard charger.

New NEVI-funded DC fast charging sites are required to provide at least four DC fast chargers that can run simultaneously, often at 150-350 kW, which can add 150+ miles of range in 20-30 minutes for many EVs.
source_title: Georgia Electric Car Charging Network Guide 2025–2026 | Recharged

entity_name: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America EV Plant
canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America EV Plant
facts: St. Joseph’s/Candler and Hyundai have entered into an agreement for the health system to operate a medical center inside the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America EV Plant.
The medical center will be 7,700 square feet in size.
The facility is intended to help employees stay their healthiest.
Information regarding the agreement was provided via a press release on the St. Joseph’s/Candler website.
source_title: Hyundai briefs: Clinic announced, more hiring - Bryan County News

facts: Hyundai announced the release of the refreshed 2025 IONIQ 5, including a rugged new IONIQ 5 XRT variant.
The expanded lineup offers more driving range and innovative features than ever.
The IONIQ 5 will be the first model range manufactured at the brand-new Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) facility in Georgia.
Upgraded 2025 IONIQ 5 models, including the new XRT, arrive at Hyundai dealerships this fall.
The factory-built 2025 IONIQ 5 XRT brings additional off-road capability and a bolder, more rugged appearance to this Hyundai EV for the first time.
IONIQ 5 aims to further enhance customer satisfaction by being the first Hyundai model to come equipped with the Tesla North American Charging Standard (NACS) port.
This port opens 17,000+ chargers on Tesla’s Supercharger network without using an adapter, doubling the size of the DC fast-charging network available to new IONIQ 5 customers.
2025 IONIQ 5 models also will be sold with a Combined Charging System (CCS) adapter.
All-new or refreshed Hyundai EVs will come exclusively with a NACS port beginning in Q4 2024.
Models produced at HMGMA are expected to be eligible for a $3,750 U.S. tax credit at start of sale.
In the future, Hyundai anticipates the U.S.-built 2025 IONIQ 5 models to qualify for additional federal tax incentives.
Leases continue to qualify for the $7,500 credit that Hyundai fully passes onto consumers with more attractive lease offers.

José Muñoz, president and global COO of Hyundai Motor Company and president and CEO of Hyundai Motor North America, stated: "IONIQ 5 has been a sales success for us since day one. We’re continuing to give customers more of what they want by expanding the IONIQ 5 lineup with a dedicated off-road-capable XRT model, NACS port, design upgrades and a long list of features developed with customers in mind. The high-performance IONIQ 5 N shows how exciting EVs can be and has been a halo for our entire brand. We’re taking ‘America’s Most Awarded EV Lineup’ from strength to strength."

Brad Arnold, senior design manager of exteriors, Hyundai Design North America, stated: "We designed this IONIQ 5 XRT to offer more Protection, Connection, and Functionality. These will be hallmarks of our XRT models going forward. The digital camo pattern was created using our IONIQ pixel as the building block. The matte-black 18-inch wheels also display the same IONIQ pixel theme while providing strength and cooling."
NACS charging port and support for NACS networks including Tesla Superchargers are included.
Standard Range battery capacity increases from 58.0 to 63.0 kWh.
Long Range battery capacity increases from 77.4 to 84 kWh.
AWD Limited trim current range is 260, with a new targeted range of 250+ to 280+ depending on trim / wheel & tire size.
RWD SE/SEL/Limited current range is 303, with a new targeted range of 310+.
RWD SE Standard Range current range is 220, with a new targeted range of 240+.
Exclusive XRT Model Features include a dual motor (AWD only) + 84 kWh battery.
The XRT features a +23mm suspension lift with unique tuning, bringing ground clearance to seven inches compared to the standard IONIQ 5's 6.1 inches.
The XRT includes unique model-specific black 18-inch wheels and 235 / 60 R 18 all-terrain tires.

The XRT features XRT-exclusive unique front and rear fascias, etched digital camouflage cladding, black window surround trim, black side sills, and black Hyundai flying ‘H’ badges on hood and liftgate.

The XRT includes an HTRAC liftgate badge and IONIQ 5 lettering in black relief, an XRT logo embossed on the rear fascia, black mirror caps, XRT badged all-weather floor mats, H-Tex™ seating surfaces featuring XRT-unique pattern and logo, and a black headliner.
Exclusive XRT exterior colors are Ultimate Red and Cosmic Blue Pearl.
The XRT approach angle is 19.8 degrees compared to the non-XRT's 17.5 degrees, and the departure angle is 30.0 degrees compared to the non-XRT's 25.4 degrees.

Non-XRT exterior changes include restyled front and rear bumpers emphasizing IONIQ’s wide, confident SUV stance, a new rear spoiler, new more aerodynamic wheels, available flush-type roof side rails, and new exterior colors: Atlas White Matte, Ecotronic Gray Matte, Celadon Gray Matte.
Interior changes include redesigned HVAC controls, a new center console with physical heated front seat controls, a new steering wheel design, and heated rear seats for SEL and above trims.

New infotainment/convenience technology includes a 12.3-inch audio/video navigation (AVN) system, standard wireless Apple CarPlay® / Android Auto™ integration, Hyundai Digital Key 2 Premium (NFC + Bluetooth Low Energy + UWB) for SEL and above, controller Over-The Air (OTA) update support, Smart Cruise Control 2, and USB Type-C ports (1 USB-C data in front seat, 2 USB-C charging in front and 2 in rear).

Safety features include Blind-Spot Collision Warning for SE trim / Blind-Spot Collision-Avoidance Assist for SEL trim and above, Forward Attention Warning via In-Cabin Camera/Direct Driver Monitoring System, Parking Distance Warning with forward warning in addition to reverse warning, a rear windshield wiper and washer, Advanced Rear Occupant Alert (radar type) for SEL trim and above, Parking Distance Warning – Side for XRT and Limited, Parking Collision-Avoidance Assist – Reverse/(New)Forward/(New)Side for XRT and Limited, and Remote Smart Parking Assist 2 for XRT and Limited.

The IONIQ 5’s interior offers improved ergonomics and convenience, with the upper part of the Universal Island featuring physical buttons for frequently used functions including heating and ventilation controls for first-row occupants, a button for the heated steering wheel and parking assist function, and a relocated wireless charging pad to significantly enhance usability.

The 2025 IONIQ 5 features a more powerful next-generation infotainment system with standard wireless Apple CarPlay® / Android Auto™ integration across the range and wireless Over-The-Air (OTA) software updates for all models to ensure major controls remain up to date and clear the way for new features to be added remotely.

Hyundai engineers introduced new safety and convenience features including the Hands-On Detection (HOD) steering wheel, Lane Following Assist 2, Remote Smart Parking Assist 2 (RSPA 2), and Parking Collision-Avoidance Assist – Reverse / Forward / Side (PCA-R/F/S).
In response to customer feedback, Hyundai applied features including Hyundai Digital Key 2 Premium and a rear wiper/washer combo.

The body, front and rear doors, and B-pillar parts have been reinforced to improve side collision protection, and the vehicle features eight airbags including second-row side airbags for enhanced safety.

The 2025 IONIQ 5 XRT is a more capable variant positioned between the upgraded standard model and the high-performance IONIQ 5 N, designed to appeal to customers who want to play in the dirt and have all-electric adventures.

The IONIQ 5 XRT boasts a more aggressive front and rear design with unique bumpers, stylish side skirts, and dedicated 18-inch aluminum wheels, emphasizing heightened capability in rough terrain made possible by higher ground clearance and suspension tuning.
Hyundai Motor America offers U.S. consumers a technology-rich lineup of cars, SUVs, and electrified vehicles while supporting Hyundai Motor Company’s Progress for Humanity vision.

Hyundai has significant operations in the U.S., including its North American headquarters in California, the Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama assembly plant, the all-new Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (in development in Georgia), and several cutting-edge R&D facilities.
These operations, combined with those of Hyundai’s 835 independent dealers, contribute $20.1 billion annually and 190,000 jobs to the U.S. economy according to a recent economic impact report.
source_title: Hyundai Debuts U.S.-Built 2025 IONIQ 5 Range, Including New Adventure-Ready XRT - HMGMA
